Parkinson's Disease_99.notes: This is a strong, well-structured puzzle with clear progressive narrowing. Clue 1 is appropriately broad (resting tremor + age/gender). Clues 2–3 add functional and historical detail that point clearly to Parkinson's. Clue 4 provides the clinical motor findings that would allow a confident diagnosis. Clue 5 is slightly redundant in wording ('DaTscan shows reduced dopamine uptake' rephrases information already heavily implied by the clinical diagnosis in Clue 4), but this is minor. The rewrite simply makes the Clue 5 language more specific and adds the detail about bilateral striatal involvement, which is slightly new. Overall: good puzzle, excellent progressive reveal, minor wording issue only.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 45-year-old breast cancer survivor with progressive dyspnea and fatigue 18 months post-anthracycline chemotherapy. Establishes patient demographics, oncology history, and temporal relationship to cardiotoxic drug exposure.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitation: dyspnea on stair climbing, reduced exercise capacity below baseline tolerance. Adds exertional component but does not narrow differential beyond what Clue 1 implies.
clues.rewrite: Orthopnea and lower limb edema noted. Symptoms worse when lying flat; legs swell by evening after minimal activity.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Recent knee surgery with degenerative changes; straightforward post-operative course. This information is COMPLETELY INCOHERENT with the cardiac presentation and adds nothing to the diagnosis of cardiotoxicity.
clues.rewrite: Cumulative anthracycline dose 450 mg/m². No prior cardiac history, hypertension, or diabetes. No recent infection or fever. Symptoms began insidiously 12 months post-treatment completion.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective signs: mild tachycardia, normal BP, clear lungs, displaced apex beat. The displaced apex beat is the critical discriminator pointing to cardiomegaly/LV dysfunction, differentiating from pulmonary or other causes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Echocardiography: EF drop from 60% to 38%, global hypokinesis, diastolic dysfunction. Confirms LV systolic dysfunction and rules out regional wall motion abnormality (excludes CAD/MI pattern).

Cardiotoxicity Post-Cancer Treatment_100.notes: CRITICAL FLAW: Clue 3 (knee surgery history) is completely incoherent with the cardiac presentation and derails the clinical narrative. It appears to be copy-pasted from an unrelated case and destroys the progressive reveal logic. Clue 2 is also redundant—it merely restates the dyspnea and fatigue from Clue 1 without adding new discriminatory information (exertional dyspnea is already strongly implied by progressive dyspnea in a post-chemo patient). After Clue 1, a clinician already suspects cardiotoxicity; Clues 2 and 3 fail to narrow further or establish a coherent story. The puzzle becomes salvageable only at Clue 4 (displaced apex beat). Clues 4 and 5 are well-constructed and appropriately specific, but the foundation is broken.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Diffuse body pain triggered by minor injuries; patient is young and athletic; pain persists despite minor injury severity. Sets up broad differential for widespread pain conditions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Disproportionate pain response to previously tolerated exercise; activity avoidance behavior. Adds functional impact but largely rephrases Clue 1 concept.
clues.rewrite: Unable to return to baseline training; minimal exertion produces pain lasting days, leading to progressive deconditioning and fear-avoidance.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Temporal link to concussion or viral illness; cognitive difficulties and fatigue accompanying pain. Adds mechanism and systemic features (cognition, fatigue) that weren't explicit before.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Generalized hyperalgesia, allodynia, heightened sensory responsiveness. These findings are specific neurophysiological markers but don't meaningfully differentiate fibromyalgia from other central sensitization disorders (CRPS, post-viral pain syndromes, post-concussion syndrome).
clues.rewrite: Tender points elicited across all body quadrants with minimal pressure; normal strength and reflexes; no focal neurological deficits or signs of structural damage.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Normal structural imaging rules out organic pathology; functional neuroimaging shows abnormal pain processing. This confirms central sensitization and effectively confirms fibromyalgia in context of prior clues.

Fibromyalgia_101.notes: Progressive reveal is compromised by Clue 2 adding little beyond Clue 1's central concept (disproportionate pain to stimulus). Clue 3 (history) is strong and adds vital systemic context. Clue 4 is too generic for examination—hyperalgesia and allodynia alone don't differentiate fibromyalgia from post-viral pain syndrome, CRPS, or other central sensitization disorders; tender point distribution would be more discriminating. Clue 5 is appropriately definitive. Recommend rewriting Clue 2 to show progressive functional decline/deconditioning pattern, and Clue 4 to emphasize widespread tender point distribution rather than just sensory hypersensitivity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Anterior knee pain, difficult to localize, variable quality, intermittent mechanical symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1. 'Unpredictable symptom pattern' and 'may be absent with similar activities' merely rephrase 'intermittent mechanical symptoms' without narrowing functional impact or body region.
clues.rewrite: Pain triggered unpredictably by knee flexion activities (stairs, squatting, prolonged sitting). Swelling or catching sensation may accompany episodes but not always present.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clues 1 and 2. 'Diagnosis of exclusion' and 'findings do not correlate with symptoms' simply restate the diagnostic uncertainty already established. No mechanism, timeline, prior treatment, or comorbidity information provided.
clues.rewrite: Chronic intermittent symptoms over months to years. No clear traumatic onset. Prior physiotherapy and anti-inflammatory interventions provided temporary relief only. Imaging workup negative for meniscal tear, ACL injury, or patellofemoral osteoarthritis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Non-specific physical findings and poor reliability of plica palpation. This clue describes the PROBLEM (lack of specificity) rather than providing actual examination findings that narrow the differential.
clues.rewrite: Tenderness along medial joint line and suprapatellar region. Knee range of motion normal. Lachman, anterior drawer, McMurray tests negative. No effusion. Plica-specific tests (flexion-extension with medial palpation) elicit pain inconsistently; reproduction not reliable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI shows thickened plica; arthroscopy is gold standard. However, the clue hedges ('findings common in asymptomatic knees', 'difficult to correlate') and undermines diagnostic confidence rather than confirming diagnosis.
clues.rewrite: MRI demonstrates thickened, fibrotic plica impinging on patellofemoral joint space. Arthroscopic visualization confirms plica with synovial irritation and patellofemoral cartilage reaction. Plica resection resolves symptoms.

Plica Syndrome_102.notes: This puzzle fails the progressive reveal principle. Clues 1, 2, and 3 are largely redundant—all rephrase the same idea (intermittent, unpredictable, hard to diagnose). They do not narrow the differential meaningfully. Clue 4 describes the ABSENCE of findings rather than providing specific examination data. Clue 5 describes uncertainty rather than confirmation. After Clue 3, the player has learned almost nothing beyond 'this is a diagnosis of exclusion'—which is too vague for a working differential. The puzzle needs complete restructuring: Clue 2 should describe specific functional limitations (stairs, deep squats); Clue 3 should provide timeline and prior imaging/treatment; Clue 4 should give actual examination findings (tenderness patterns, test results); Clue 5 should confirm with arthroscopic or definitive imaging findings. As written, even Clue 5 would not allow confident diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographics (32-year-old new mother), timeline (6 weeks postpartum), and symptom (wrist pain with gripping difficulty). Establishes broad complaint without anatomical specificity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations (open-hand gripping, wringing, twisting movements). However, the clue text is incomplete/garbled ('Gripping. Twisting movements are particularly.' appears unfinished).
clues.rewrite: Symptoms are worst when lifting her baby with thumb abducted and wrist extended, and when wringing out wet cloths. Fine motor tasks like fastening buttons also provoke pain.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Postpartum context (6 weeks postpartum, breastfeeding, repetitive hand use implied) and negative findings (no trauma, no inflammatory arthritis history). This REPEATS the postpartum timeline already given in Clue 1 — redundant.
clues.rewrite: The pain developed insidiously after hospital discharge. She has been performing frequent repetitive hand activities (baby care, bottle preparation, laundry). No prior wrist injuries. No systemic inflammatory disease history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings (tenderness/swelling at first dorsal compartment, positive Finkelstein test, crepitus with thumb movement). These are specific and discriminatory for De Quervain's. However, the clue text is garbled ('Positive Finkelstein test reproducing the.' is incomplete).
clues.rewrite: Localized tenderness and mild swelling over the radial styloid process. Finkelstein test strongly positive. Crepitus palpable over the first dorsal compartment during thumb abduction and wrist extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound findings show thickened, oedematous tendons (EPB and APL) with fluid in the first dorsal compartment sheath. This is confirmatory for De Quervain's tenosynovitis.

De Quervain's Tenosynovitis_103.notes: The progressive reveal has significant redundancy: Clue 1 and Clue 3 both emphasize the postpartum timeline, eliminating the discriminatory value of Clue 3. Clue 2 should have narrowed to thumb/wrist movement patterns more clearly but instead overlaps functionally with Clue 1. Additionally, Clues 2 and 4 appear to have formatting errors (incomplete sentences). After Clue 3, a clinician knows: postpartum woman, wrist/grip pain, no inflammatory disease—already pointing strongly at De Quervain's given the postpartum context. Clue 4 (examination) should come earlier in the sequence to help differentiate from other wrist tendinopathies before the clinical picture becomes too obvious. Recommend restructuring: Clue 1 (broad complaint), Clue 2 (functional pattern: thumb abduction/extension-based pain), Clue 3 (mechanism: repetitive hand use, postpartum context), Clue 4 (specific exam findings: Finkelstein, first dorsal compartment tenderness), Clue 5 (imaging confirmation).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Episodic vertigo with ear fullness, tinnitus, and hearing loss — establishes vestibular symptom cluster and unilateral ear involvement.
clues.rewrite: 42-year-old female with recurrent episodes of severe spinning sensation lasting 20 minutes to several hours, accompanied by nausea and vomiting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact during attacks (complete incapacity, vomiting); between-episode recovery with residual hearing changes — adds temporal pattern of disability.
clues.rewrite: During attacks: completely unable to move or work; nausea and vomiting severe. Between episodes: fully functional, though reports progressive difficulty hearing conversation.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Timeline (6 months recurrent), progressive low-frequency hearing loss, fluctuating roaring tinnitus — establishes chronicity and auditory symptom pattern.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Nystagmus during attack (vertical and rotatory); Weber test shows conductive component — provides objective vestibular and audiological findings.
clues.rewrite: During acute attack: vertical and rotatory nystagmus present. Weber test shows conductive-pattern hearing loss on affected side. Between episodes: nystagmus resolves, Weber normalizes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Audiometry confirms sensorineural (not conductive) low-frequency loss; caloric reduced response; MRI excludes retrocochlear pathology — provides definitive audiovestibular and imaging confirmation.
clues.rewrite: Audiometry shows sensorineural hearing loss at low frequencies bilaterally, worse on affected side. Caloric testing reveals reduced caloric response on affected side. MRI normal — rules out acoustic neuroma.

Ménière's Disease_104.notes: MAJOR ISSUES: Clue 1 is almost diagnostic for Ménière's on its own — it lists ear fullness, tinnitus, AND hearing loss (the classic triad) alongside vertigo. This collapses the differential far too early. Clue 2 essentially repeats the functional consequence of vertigo already implied in Clue 1 without adding meaningful new information. Clue 4's Weber findings seem incongruent with Clue 5's sensorineural pattern (Weber typically shows sensorineural, not conductive, loss in Ménière's — this is internally inconsistent). The progressive reveal is too front-loaded; players could diagnose after Clue 1 alone. Rewrite to: (1) present vertigo + nausea only; (2) add ear fullness as NEW functional/sensory finding; (3) add hearing loss history; (4) add nystagmus + caloric findings; (5) confirm with audiometry pattern. This creates a true funnel.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Location (proximal ankle), character (weight-bearing and pivot pain), and timeline (prolonged beyond expected recovery). Establishes broad ankle injury complaint.
clues.rewrite: Patient with ankle pain following inversion injury; symptoms persist beyond typical resolution timeframe.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — already mentioned prolonged symptoms and functional limitations. Adds detail about dorsiflexion resistance difficulty and sports limitation, but these are implied by 'pivot movements' already stated.
clues.rewrite: Unable to perform cutting/pivoting movements; pain and instability with activities requiring ankle rotation. Difficulty with weight-bearing on uneven surfaces.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Initial misdiagnosis, recurrent episodes, slow recovery despite treatment. This is genuinely new — establishes chronicity and treatment resistance not implied by earlier clues. Narrows toward syndesmotic involvement (higher ankle injuries often missed as 'simple ankle sprains').

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific syndesmotic tests (squeeze test, crossover test, dorsiflexion-external rotation provocation). This is appropriate specificity for Clue 4 and meaningfully discriminates high ankle sprain from typical ankle sprain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI confirmation of chronic syndesmotic inflammation and stress radiograph findings of persistent widening. Confirms syndesmotic pathology specifically. However, mention of osteochondral injury feels tangential — adds confusion about whether that's a concurrent finding or differential.
clues.rewrite: MRI confirms chronic syndesmotic inflammation and ligamentous thickening. Stress radiographs demonstrate persistent tibiofibular widening compared to contralateral side.

High Ankle Sprain_105.notes: The puzzle has structural issues with progressive reveal. Clue 1 is overly location-specific ('proximal ankle' essentially names the syndesmosis region). Clue 2 largely restates Clue 1 without adding functionally distinct information — both discuss prolonged limitations. Clues 3, 4, 5 work well and build logically. The main rewrite needed is Clue 1 (less anatomically specific) and Clue 2 (distinct functional constraints, e.g., rotational movements specifically). After Clues 1-2 in current form, a clinician already suspects high ankle sprain; Clues 3-5 merely confirm rather than narrow a well-established differential. Rewrite Clue 1 to be broader (just 'ankle pain post-injury') and Clue 2 to focus on rotational movement loss as a distinct functional marker.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, symptom onset (foot drop, tripping), and physical finding (high arched feet) suggesting lower limb neuropathy with motor involvement
clues.rewrite: Young adult (25 years old) with progressive lower limb weakness causing frequent falls and difficulty with stairs.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1. Foot drop already mentioned in Complaint. Adds sensory loss and AFO use, but sensory component is vague and AFO is just reiterating the motor problem already established.
clues.rewrite: Marked sensory loss in hands and feet affecting proprioception and fine motor tasks. Difficulty with balance in darkness.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Onset in adolescence, family history (father affected), and progressive course. This is genuinely new and suggests hereditary etiology, narrowing significantly from acquired neuropathies.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1. Distal leg atrophy, pes cavus, and foot drop all already established. Adds absent reflexes and distal sensory loss, but these are predictable extensions of what was already disclosed in Complaints and History.
clues.rewrite: Stocking-glove sensory loss (vibration and proprioception worse distally). Widespread areflexia. Peroneal nerve thickening palpable in legs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: EMG findings (demyelinating pattern with slowed conduction) and genetic confirmation (PMP22 duplication) provide definitive diagnosis. Appropriately specific for final clue.

Charcot-Marie-Tooth Disease_106.notes: Major progressive reveal problem: Clue 1 (Complaint) is too specific and already names the classic triad of CMT (high arches, foot drop, tripping). This gives the answer away early. Clue 2 repeats foot drop and adds only vague sensory loss without meaningful new narrowing. Clue 3 (History) is strong and appropriately placed — family history + adolescent onset is the key differentiator that should pivot thinking toward hereditary neuropathy. Clue 4 repeats physical findings from Clue 1 almost verbatim (pes cavus, atrophy, foot drop all already mentioned) and only marginally adds reflex/sensory findings. The differential should be narrowed much more by Clue 3, leaving Clue 4 to discriminate within demyelinating hereditary neuropathies (CMT1 vs CMT2 patterns). Clue 5 is appropriate. Rewrite Clues 1, 2, and 4 to create a proper funnel.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes patient profile (Master's athlete), symptom location (patellar tendon), persistence despite load modification, and functional impact (competitive performance affected).
clues.rewrite: Master's level athlete with anterior knee pain localised below the kneecap, persistent despite training load reduction, affecting jumping and sprinting performance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specifies functional limitation (explosive movements) and adds information about loading correlation and eccentric loading response. However, phrasing is unclear ('poor correlation between activity, symptoms, and with eccentric loading minimal').
clues.rewrite: Explosive movements like jumping, bounding, and rapid deceleration are highly provocative; symptoms worsen with repeated plyometric activity and running on hard surfaces.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Adds chronicity (long-standing), treatment resistance (multiple failures, rest ineffective), and a subtle objective finding (nodules palpable). This narrowing is appropriate for Clue 3.
clues.rewrite: Symptoms have persisted for over 18 months despite physiotherapy, corticosteroid injections, and multiple rest periods. No significant improvement with conservative management. Patient reports occasional swelling and stiffness on mornings after high-load training.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Provides objective findings: focal nodularity, pain on resisted knee extension, and eccentric loading as a key provocative test. This is appropriate discriminatory information for Clue 4.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Confirms structural pathology: chronic disorganisation, possible tears or partial rupture, and degeneration. This is appropriately definitive for Clue 5.

Patellar Tendinopathy_107.notes: The main problem is Clue 1 explicitly names 'patellar tendon pain', which essentially gives away the answer immediately. Clue 2 is also poorly phrased and doesn't add meaningfully new information beyond 'explosive movements are problematic' — this could be inferred from Clue 1 in an athlete. Clues 3–5 follow a reasonable progression from history through examination to imaging, but the puzzle is broken at the start. Rewrite Clues 1 and 2 to avoid naming the structure and to broaden the initial differential (other anterior knee pain diagnoses like patellofemoral pain syndrome, quadriceps tendinopathy, or fat pad impingement should remain plausible after Clue 1).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age group (elderly) and progressive vision loss affecting daily activities
clues.rewrite: Elderly patient reports gradual blurring of central vision over months, affecting ability to perform fine tasks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations (reading, face recognition) and increased fall risk
clues.rewrite: Cannot read small print or watch television comfortably. Difficulty navigating in low light and bumping into objects.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Age >75, family history of macular degeneration, smoking history
clues.rewrite: Age 78. Long-standing smoker. Mother had late-onset vision problems requiring low-vision aids.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Reduced central vision, central scotoma, drusen on macula

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: OCT confirmation of drusen and atrophy

Vision Impairment - Age-Related_108.notes: CRITICAL ISSUES: Clue 1 essentially names the answer by saying 'macular degeneration' (via 'progressive vision loss' + 'elderly' + 'daily activities'). Clues 2 and 3 largely rephrase Clue 1 without adding meaningfully new differential-narrowing information. The puzzle lacks progressive reveal; a clinician reading only Clue 1 would already arrive at age-related macular degeneration as the top diagnosis. Additionally, this is an ophthalmology diagnosis, not a physiotherapy diagnosis — the puzzle appears misaligned with the Physiodle premise (musculoskeletal/movement disorders). If the intent is vision-related functional impairment managed by allied health (low-vision rehabilitation, mobility training), the framing should reflect that context rather than pure ophthalmology. Recommend complete restructure to create genuine narrowing, or reconsider whether this diagnosis fits the game scope.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Location of pain (medial knee, below joint line, proximal medial tibia) and absence of mechanical symptoms (no giving way/locking)
clues.rewrite: Patient reports medial-sided knee pain that began insidiously 2-3 weeks ago, no history of trauma or acute injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations with descending stairs and running; symptoms worse with hip adduction and internal rotation movements; morning stiffness present

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 (medial knee pain location already stated) and Clue 2 (hip adduction/internal rotation already stated); only adds hip/hamstring tightness and prior surgery/overuse context
clues.rewrite: Recent history of repetitive running or gym activity, prior knee or hip surgery, or persistent hip/hamstring stiffness despite stretching.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific palpation finding (tenderness at pes anserine insertion); resisted movements (hip internal rotation, adduction); objective hip flexibility deficit; absence of effusion
clues.rewrite: Tenderness maximal over proximal medial tibia, pain with resisted hip adduction and internal rotation; hip flexibility significantly limited (adductor and hamstring tightness); knee effusion absent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging confirmation of bursal fluid, bursa thickening, tendon changes, and associated muscle strain

Pes Anserine Bursitis_109.notes: Progressive reveal is compromised by significant information overlap. Clue 1 is too anatomically specific (names 'proximal medial tibia' and negates mechanical symptoms), essentially narrowing to pes anserine bursitis prematurely. Clue 3 largely rephrases information from Clues 1-2 (medial knee pain, hip adduction/internal rotation symptoms) without adding distinct mechanism or timeline details. Clue 4 adds examination findings but some (hip tightness, overuse context) were already implied by earlier clues. The differential should remain broader through Clue 2 at least (medial collateral ligament injury, medial meniscal pathology, and other medial-sided knee conditions should still be plausible). Recommend: broaden Clue 1 to non-specific medial knee pain without anatomical pinpointing; refocus Clue 3 on clear mechanism (overuse, prior surgery, training error); keep Clue 4 focused on examination discrimination (e.g., pes anserine tenderness vs MCL vs meniscal signs).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, gender, acute onset, localised chest wall swelling and pain at second costochondral junction, progressive enlargement over days
clues.rewrite: A 28-year-old male presents with acute onset of localised chest wall pain and visible swelling. Symptoms began several days ago and have been progressively worsening.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional triggers: deep inspiration, coughing, reaching overhead; hyperalgesia to light touch; patient self-identified visible swelling

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Recent upper respiratory tract infection with productive cough two weeks prior; exclusion of trauma, heavy work, fever, weight loss, systemic symptoms; narrows differential away from infection/malignancy/traumatic causes

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: visible firm fusiform swelling at second costochondral junction (3cm), warm, tender on direct palpation; clear respiratory and cardiovascular exam narrows away from cardiopulmonary pathology

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: CXR normal; CT shows focal non-suppurative inflammatory swelling of costal cartilage at second costochondral junction, no malignancy or infection; confirms diagnosis

Tietze Syndrome_110.notes: Clue 1 is overly specific and essentially names the answer by stating 'second costochondral junction' — this is pathognomonic for Tietze syndrome and eliminates almost all differential diagnoses immediately. A player familiar with anatomy would solve on Clue 1 alone. Rewrite should remove the specific anatomical site and describe only the regional complaint (chest wall swelling/pain) to preserve the diagnostic challenge. Clues 2-5 form a coherent, well-structured funnel with appropriate new information at each step. The progressive reveal from functional impact → background history → objective findings → imaging confirmation is sound, but Clue 1's specificity undermines the entire game difficulty.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age group and vision loss complaint — but vision loss is completely unrelated to snapping hip syndrome and creates incoherence with the entire puzzle
clues.rewrite: Middle-aged athlete noticing a clicking sensation in the hip during running and jumping; pain gradually worsening over weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specifies the snap is reproducible with hip abduction/external rotation and occurs during sports — this is high-specificity examination/clinical reasoning information that should appear in Clue 4, not Clue 2
clues.rewrite: Cannot perform running drills or high-intensity hip movements without triggering the clicking sensation; limiting participation in sport.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Onset linked to increased activity, initially asymptomatic then became painful — this is reasonable progression history, but the mechanism is still vague (what activity triggered it?)
clues.rewrite: Started 6 weeks ago after ramping up training volume; initially just a painless click, now sharp pain with certain hip movements.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Palpable clicking over greater trochanter and positive dynamic hip rotational test — this is genuinely specific examination findings, though 'dynamic hip rotational test' is vague terminology
clues.rewrite: Palpable snapping sensation directly over greater trochanter on passive hip flexion/extension with external rotation; no hip ROM restriction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound confirms tensor fasciae latae snapping mechanism and shows bursal inflammation — this is appropriately specific and confirms the diagnosis

Snapping Hip Syndrome_111.notes: Major structural problems: Clue 1 is completely incoherent (vision loss has no relationship to snapping hip); Clue 2 contains high-specificity examination findings (reproducible snap with specific movements) that belong in Clue 4 examination section. The puzzle needs complete reorganisation: Clue 1 should be a broad complaint about hip/groin pain or clicking during activity; Clue 2 should describe functional limitation (can't run/jump); Clue 3 should explain the activity-triggered onset; Clue 4 should contain the special movement tests and palpation findings; Clue 5 can stay as imaging confirmation. Recommend full rewrite.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Essentially names the answer: 'lymphedema' is explicitly stated. Also contains stage classification, infection history, and skin changes—information that should be distributed across clues 2-4.
clues.rewrite: Patient reports progressive arm swelling and heaviness over the past 2 years. Symptoms began following left breast surgery. Recurrent skin infections have become increasingly problematic.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact (arm non-functional, pain, heaviness). However, pain and heaviness were already stated in Clue 1. Recurrent infections and quality-of-life impact also redundant with Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: Patient unable to perform overhead activities, carrying, or prolonged use of affected arm. Significant functional loss in activities of daily living despite conservative treatment attempts.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Cancer history (bilateral breast, left mastectomy) and axillary dissection detail. However, mastectomy and axillary dissection were already mentioned in Clue 1. Radiation therapy is new. Progressive nature despite conservative management is new but was already implied.
clues.rewrite: Left-sided mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection 2 years ago. Patient completed adjuvant radiation therapy to the chest wall. Attempted early compression therapy and exercise without sustained improvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective physical findings: severe non-pitting lymphedema, skin thickening and fibrosis with hyperkeratosis, scarring from prior infections. These findings narrow significantly but still consistent with clue naming 'stage 2-3' already.
clues.rewrite: Pitting edema in proximal arm, non-pitting in forearm and hand. Skin thickened and fibrotic. Stemmer sign positive. Circumferential measurements show 4-5 cm difference compared to contralateral limb. Evidence of old cellulitis scars.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Lymphoscintigraphy confirms impaired lymphatic function; ultrasound shows subcutaneous fibrosis. However, stage 2-3 classification was already explicitly stated in Clue 1, making these findings predictable rather than revealing.
clues.rewrite: Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrates delayed tracer clearance and asymmetric drainage pattern. Absence of axillary lymph node activity on affected side. Ultrasound confirms diffuse subcutaneous fibrosis consistent with chronic lymphostasis.

Breast Cancer Lymphedema_112.notes: Critical failure: Clue 1 explicitly names 'lymphedema' and provides staging—this is the answer itself, eliminating the game entirely. Clues 2-5 then merely elaborate on already-stated findings (infections, skin changes, severity) rather than progressively narrowing a differential. This is not a diagnostic game but a description of a known condition. For a valid Physiodle puzzle, Clue 1 should present only 'arm swelling and heaviness post-breast surgery' (broad differential), Clue 2 should indicate location/functional loss (upper limb), Clue 3 should narrow via surgical history (post-mastectomy complications), Clue 4 should distinguish via non-pitting edema pattern and skin changes (lymphedema vs. other post-surgical swelling), and Clue 5 should confirm via imaging. Requires fundamental restructuring.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Newborn presenting with head tilt and chin rotation — establishes age group, cardinal postural sign, and affected side.
clues.rewrite: Newborn with persistent head tilt to one side; parents report asymmetric head position since birth.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Limited neck ROM (rotation and lateral flexion); preferential looking behavior toward affected side.
clues.rewrite: Difficulty turning head past midline in one direction; resists passive rotation away from affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Breech extraction and large birth weight — mechanism linking difficult delivery to muscle injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Palpable mass in SCM, restricted contralateral rotation, limited flexion — focal muscle findings that differentiate from postural or neurogenic causes.
clues.rewrite: Firm, non-tender mass palpable along sternocleidomastoid; passive rotation to opposite side blocked; no apparent neurological deficit.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound confirms muscle echogenicity, thickening, and fibrosis — structural confirmation of SCM pathology.

Congenital Torticollis_113.notes: Clue 1 is too specific and nearly names the answer (congenital torticollis = head tilt in newborn with chin turned away). Clue 2 restates ROM limitations already heavily implied by Clue 1's postural finding without adding functional context that distinguishes it from other causes of limited neck ROM in infants (e.g., Down syndrome, upper cervical fusion, Klippel-Feil). Clue 3 is strong and adds critical mechanism. Clue 4 repeats the ROM finding from Clue 1-2 without substantially new discriminatory info — should emphasize the palpable mass more distinctly as the key examination finding that points to muscle contracture vs. neural/skeletal causes. Progressive reveal needs broadening at start and better separation between ROM findings (Clues 1-2) and muscle-specific pathology (Clues 3-4).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient has lateral hip symptoms with mild discomfort; active individual
clues.rewrite: Active individual with gradual onset of pain affecting work performance over several months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Symptoms triggered by work tasks; hand function reduced — introduces hand/upper limb but CONTRADICTS Clue 1 (lateral hip)
clues.rewrite: Unable to sustain repetitive gripping and fine motor tasks; symptoms worsen throughout work shifts.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Years of heavy repetitive work, progressive functional decline, poor ergonomics — adds mechanism and timeline but does NOT narrow differential meaningfully from Clue 1+2 alone
clues.rewrite: Decade of assembly-line work with poor desk ergonomics; symptoms started in dominant hand and have progressively spread proximally over 18 months despite rest periods.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Hand weakness, reduced dexterity, tender forearm points, positive compression tests — finally specifies upper limb nerve compression signs and partially reconciles the hip/hand contradiction
clues.rewrite: Positive nerve compression signs at wrist and forearm; weakness in finger extension and grip; tenderness along flexor-pronator mass; sensory changes in radial distribution.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Nerve compression, muscle atrophy, multiple site involvement — appropriate for imaging but vague (which nerve? which sites?) and does not decisively differentiate OOS from other compressive neuropathies
clues.rewrite: Ultrasound shows nerve swelling at multiple compression points (carpal tunnel, pronator teres region); selective atrophy of thenar and first dorsal interosseous muscles.

Occupational Overuse Syndrome_114.notes: MAJOR COHERENCE PROBLEM: Clue 1 describes lateral hip symptoms (suggesting hip OOS) but Clue 2+ pivot entirely to hand and forearm (upper limb OOS). This is a fundamentally incoherent puzzle — either the answer is upper limb OOS or lower limb OOS, not both. The clues tell two different clinical stories. Additionally, the puzzle conflates 'Occupational Overuse Syndrome' (a generic category) with specific diagnoses like Carpal Tunnel or Pronator Syndrome. Clue 1 should establish upper limb symptoms from the start. Clue 3 adds little new info beyond what Clues 1–2 already imply (repetitive work + hand dysfunction = occupational overuse is already obvious). Clue 4's compression tests are good but vague about location. Clue 5 is too non-specific to distinguish between multiple compression sites. Recommend: rewrite Clue 1 to clearly anchor to upper limb, strengthen Clue 3 with specific timeline/comorbidity detail, and sharpen Clue 4–5 to point toward a single anatomical diagnosis (e.g., median vs. radial nerve compression).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Polyarticular symmetric small joint pain (hands, wrists, feet) with 2-hour morning stiffness in a 52F; establishes systemic polyarticular pattern and temporal signature
clues.rewrite: 52-year-old woman with pain in multiple joints, particularly hands and feet. Symptoms worse in the morning, with stiffness that takes 2 hours to improve. Symmetric distribution.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: gripping and fine motor tasks impaired due to morning stiffness; adds specificity to which joints are functionally limiting
clues.rewrite: Unable to grip or perform fine motor tasks. Symptoms are worst in the mornings before activity, then gradually improve throughout the day.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Insidious 6-month onset, progressive decline, smoking history; timeline and smoking as risk factor for RA

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Symmetrical MCP/PIP swelling and warmth; objective inflammatory signs; distinguishes inflammatory arthropathy from OA or other mechanical conditions
clues.rewrite: Palpable warmth and soft tissue swelling at MCPs and PIPs bilaterally. Morning stiffness reproduced on examination. No nodules or deformity palpated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: X-ray findings (uniform joint space narrowing, periarticular osteopenia) and serology (RF, anti-CCP) confirm inflammatory erosive arthropathy and RA serology
clues.rewrite: Hand X-rays demonstrate uniform joint space narrowing across multiple MCPs and PIPs with periarticular osteopenia. Serology positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies.

Rheumatoid Arthritis_115.notes: The puzzle fails the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 is essentially diagnostic on its own—symmetric polyarticular hand/foot involvement with 2-hour morning stiffness is a near-classic RA presentation that a physio student would immediately recognize. Clues 2 and 4 add minimal new information beyond what Clue 1 establishes (Clue 2 just describes functional consequences of symptoms already named; Clue 4 confirms inflammatory findings already strongly implied). Clue 5 explicitly names serology markers (RF, anti-CCP) which is diagnostic-level information that gives away the answer entirely. The funnel effect is collapsed. For a better puzzle, Clue 1 should be broader (e.g., omit symmetric small-joint specificity, just say 'joint pain and morning stiffness'), Clues 2–4 should systematically narrow differential (mechanical vs inflammatory vs systemic), and Clue 5 should be the serology confirmation. Currently, the player guesses RA after reading Clue 1.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes patient age (older adult), location (groin/hip), pain character (activity-dependent, rest-responsive), and presence of morning stiffness. Sets broad differential for hip/groin pathology in older population.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Identifies specific functional limitations (stairs, long-distance walking) and movement restrictions (hip flexion, internal rotation pain). However, these movements are ALREADY strongly implied by 'hip pain' in Clue 1 — a clinician reading Clue 1 would naturally think of hip ROM limitations. The post-activity pain timing also partially repeats 'worse with activity' from Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: Difficulty with loaded hip flexion (stairs, car transfers, bending) and sustained walking. Pain particularly felt in groin, worse later in day with fatigue. Can walk short distances without difficulty.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Adds timeline (gradual onset), risk factors (previous hip injury/surgery, elevated BMI), and progression (worsening). This meaningfully narrows differential by establishing chronicity and mechanical loading. Eliminates acute inflammatory or traumatic presentations. NEW information compared to Clue 1.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Provides objective ROM findings (limited internal rotation and flexion at end-range) and movement pattern abnormality (Trendelenburg gait). Log-roll test is specific for hip joint pathology. This discriminates between intra-articular (OA) versus extra-articular (labral, muscular) or referred sources. Strong addition.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: X-ray findings (joint space narrowing, osteophytes, sclerosis) confirm degenerative joint disease. This is near-definitive imaging for OA and rules out other structural pathology (labral tears, capsulitis, bursitis). Appropriate final clue.

Hip Osteoarthritis_116.notes: The puzzle has a significant weakness in Clue 2. It largely rephrases information already present in Clue 1 (hip pain with activity, movement restrictions of the hip) without adding meaningfully new functional or contextual detail. Clues 3, 4, and 5 form a strong diagnostic funnel with distinct information at each level. Rewriting Clue 2 to emphasize SPECIFIC functional losses (loaded flexion, fatigue component, short-distance tolerance) and movement-independent pain triggers (time of day) would create better progressive reveal. Currently, Clues 1 and 2 feel somewhat interchangeable rather than building toward diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographics (68yo), primary diagnosis (advanced lung cancer), acute symptoms (dyspnea, syncope), and clinical context (during chemotherapy)
clues.rewrite: A 68-year-old with a significant smoking history presents with sudden onset severe shortness of breath and loss of consciousness. Onset was abrupt during a routine hospital visit.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1. Clue 1 already stated 'acute dyspnea and syncope.' Clue 2 merely rephrases the same acute presentation and adds chest pain (symptom, not functional limitation).
clues.rewrite: Patient unable to walk more than 10 meters without severe breathlessness. Cannot lie flat. One syncopal episode occurred with minimal exertion.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Cancer diagnosis, metastatic disease, active chemotherapy, and explicitly rules out DVT history.
clues.rewrite: Recent immobilization for chemotherapy over past 3 weeks. No recent surgery or fracture. No family history of clotting disorders. Patient noted calf swelling two days ago but dismissed it.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective vital signs (hypoxemia, hypotension, tachycardia), JVD, and right heart strain signs. This is the first truly discriminatory clue that points toward acute cardiopulmonary decompensation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: CT angiography confirms PE diagnosis directly. Echocardiography shows RV changes. Troponin elevation supports acute RV strain.
clues.rewrite: CT chest with contrast shows filling defects within bilateral pulmonary arteries. Echocardiogram reveals acute RV dilation with reduced function. Elevated troponin and D-dimer markedly elevated.

Pulmonary Embolism_117.notes: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 is far too specific — it mentions lung cancer, chemotherapy, acute dyspnea, and syncope, which together strongly suggest PE in a high-risk patient. A clinician reading only Clue 1 would already have PE as a top differential. Clue 2 adds almost nothing new — it rephrases the same acute presentation from Clue 1. Clue 3 provides risk context but doesn't meaningfully narrow beyond what Clue 1 established. Only Clue 4 begins to discriminate (hemodynamic instability + right heart strain), and Clue 5 simply names the diagnosis outright with CT angiography findings. The funnel is inverted — the broadest net should be cast first, not a highly specific cancer patient presentation. Rewrite needed to create genuine uncertainty and progressive narrowing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Bilateral shin pain in an athlete; establishes body region, symptom type, and population. Appropriately broad.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Running is affected; functional impact is training-related. However, this is largely implied by Clue 1 (athlete + bilateral shin pain almost always affects running). Minimal narrowing.
clues.rewrite: Unable to tolerate impact loading; pain worsens with increases in training volume or intensity, improving with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: High-volume training + poor recovery is the presumed mechanism, narrowing from overuse injury category. Helps distinguish acute trauma from chronic stress injury. Adds meaningful context.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Bilateral tenderness localizes pain; weakness in foot inverters/evertors and lower extremity biomechanical dysfunction add objective findings. However, 'lower extremity biomechanical dysfunction' is vague and could apply to many conditions. The foot invertors/evertors weakness is useful but not specific enough to differentiate MTSS from stress fracture or exertional compartment syndrome.
clues.rewrite: Bilateral tenderness along medial tibial border; pain reproducible on palpation of posteromedial tibia. No focal bony step-off or point tenderness. Calf strength intact; tibialis posterior and soleus demonstrate mild weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Periosteal changes without fracture line is the key discriminator between MTSS (periosteal reaction) and stress fracture (fracture line evident). This is appropriately specific and confirms the diagnosis.

Medial Tibial Stress Syndrome_118.notes: The puzzle has a reasonable funnel structure, but Clue 2 is redundant—running limitation is already implied by 'athlete with bilateral shin pain.' Clue 3 effectively narrows to overuse/training-related stress. Clue 4 is weakened by vague language ('lower extremity biomechanical dysfunction') when it should provide specific, discriminatory exam findings. Clue 5 is strong and appropriately definitive. Recommend tightening Clues 2 and 4 to add distinct, non-redundant information.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographics, post-operative timing, surgical indication (severe knee OA), and primary symptoms (pain, difficulty bending).
clues.rewrite: A 70-year-old female presents day 1 post-operatively with significant right knee pain and marked loss of bending ability. She reports difficulty with basic mobility tasks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations (bed-to-chair transfer, inability to achieve 90° flexion, poor quad activation, extensor lag) and post-operative day 1 baseline ROM.
clues.rewrite: Unable to independently bed-to-chair transfer. Marked quadriceps inhibition with significant extensor lag noted. Active knee flexion approximately 70 degrees.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Prior OA severity (tricompartmental, end-stage), duration of conservative management (4 years), pre-operative ROM baseline (5-95°), and BMI (33).
clues.rewrite: Four-year history of progressive, treatment-resistant tricompartmental knee osteoarthritis. Pre-operative ROM 5–95 degrees. BMI 33, no significant comorbidities affecting healing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Wound status (clean, dry, staples), degree of swelling (moderate), and actual measured active ROM on day 1 (0–70°).
clues.rewrite: Wound clean and dry, staples intact. Moderate swelling present. Palpable soft-tissue resistance at end-range; pain limits further flexion testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Prosthesis type (cemented), alignment quality, absence of fracture/complications, and component positioning confirmation.

Total Knee Replacement Rehabilitation_11.notes: Critical problem: Clue 1 explicitly names the procedure ('after elective surgery') and the indication ('severe right knee osteoarthritis'), making the diagnosis essentially revealed on the first guess. The puzzle lacks differential difficulty — there is no ambiguity from the start. Clues 2 and 3 are redundant with Clue 1; they repeat and elaborate post-operative timeline and OA history already given, rather than adding new discriminating information. A physiotherapy student would correctly guess 'TKR rehab' after Clue 1 alone. For a post-operative rehab puzzle, consider withholding the surgical procedure name and type until later clues, and lead with functional complaints that could plausibly arise from multiple acute knee pathologies.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographics, onset character (progressive over 4 months), symptom (medial foot pain), and a specific physical sign (arch flattening, postural change). Establishes foot region and foot structure pathology.
clues.rewrite: A 38-year-old runner reports medial foot pain that has developed gradually over four months, worse with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitation with weight-bearing and walking. Describes impact on daily activity.
clues.rewrite: Unable to run; pain limits prolonged standing. Experiences foot instability during walking, particularly on uneven surfaces.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism of injury (inversion injury) or insidious onset with flat foot deformity. Attempts to narrow between acute and chronic presentations.
clues.rewrite: No recalled specific injury; pain developed without trauma. Patient reports family history of flat feet and hypermobility.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific palpation findings (medial midfoot tenderness, positive spring ligament palpation) and arch flattening measurement. Provides objective clinical findings.
clues.rewrite: Single-leg stance reveals marked arch collapse. Navicular drop test ≥8 mm. Dorsiflexion and eversion weakness noted. Pain on spring ligament palpation between medial malleolus and navicular.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI and ultrasound confirm ligament disruption with specific finding of gap in ligament tissue. Provides definitive structural confirmation.

Spring Ligament Injury_120.notes: Major progressive reveal problem: Clue 1 already names the specific anatomy (spring ligament via arch flattening + medial foot pain) and pathology (ligament injury implied by progressive arch collapse). This gives away the answer immediately to any experienced clinician. Clue 2 is generic weight-bearing pain that could apply to any foot condition. Clue 3 creates confusion by offering two completely different mechanisms (inversion injury vs. insidious flat foot deformity) without committing to one patient story. Clue 4 repeats arch flattening and medial midfoot findings already stated in Clues 1 and 3, with minimal new discrimination. The differential funnel fails to narrow meaningfully — by Clue 1, the answer is essentially apparent. Recommend: (1) Clue 1 strips to generic medial foot pain + gradual onset, no arch language. (2) Clue 2 adds functional specificity (running loss, single-leg instability). (3) Clue 3 commits to insidious progressive flat foot with hypermobility context. (4) Clue 4 provides discriminating exam findings (navicular drop, spring ligament tenderness, weakness pattern). (5) Clue 5 confirms with imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, sex, chief symptom (bulging/heaviness in vaginal area), visible sign, and associated bowel evacuation difficulty
clues.rewrite: A 68-year-old female reports sensation of heaviness and dragging in the lower pelvis, worse by end of day. Reports difficulty with bowel emptying.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional pattern: diurnal worsening with activity, relief with recumbency, specific limitation to walking/standing
clues.rewrite: Unable to tolerate prolonged standing or walking without worsening symptoms. Finds relief only when horizontal. Manages light activities but avoids leaving home due to concern about symptom progression during day.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Risk factors: multiparity with difficult labor, 40-year history of chronic straining, post-menopausal status. Explains pathogenesis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings confirming pelvic floor dysfunction: specific grades of prolapse (cystocele, rectocele), anatomical extent, perineal weakness. Distinguishes prolapse from other pelvic pain/dysfunction conditions.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging confirmation of anatomical extent and dynamic pelvic floor descent, rectocele grading, cystocele confirmation. Near-definitive.

Pelvic Organ Prolapse_121.notes: Clue 1 essentially names the condition by including 'bulging in vaginal area' and 'visible bulging' — this is too specific for the complaint phase and gives the answer away immediately. Clue 2 repeats the heaviness/worsening pattern already stated in Clue 1 without adding meaningfully new functional constraints. The progressive reveal collapses: by Clue 1, a skilled clinician already has pelvic organ prolapse near-certain. Clues 3-5 then become confirmatory rather than narrowing. Rewrite Clue 1 to be genuinely broader (pelvic heaviness/evacuation difficulty without 'bulging' or 'vaginal' specificity). Rewrite Clue 2 to add functional context: what OTHER limitations emerge? (e.g., social restriction, use of pessary/splinting, incontinence component).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Posterior knee swelling and calf symptoms that mimic DVT, establishing the chief complaint and regional involvement.
clues.rewrite: Patient reports posterior knee swelling with associated calf pain and tightness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1. Reiterates posterior knee swelling, calf involvement, and mentions cyst rupture—essentially rephrasing the complaint without adding functional limitation data.
clues.rewrite: Unable to fully extend knee; symptoms worse with prolonged standing and walking. Occasional acute exacerbations with sudden increase in calf swelling and pain.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Introduces underlying knee pathology (OA or meniscal damage) as a predisposing factor. Adds temporal information (progressive) and rupture mechanism.
clues.rewrite: History of gradual onset swelling over months to years. Patient reports episodic knee pain exacerbated by activity. Recent increase in symptoms following increased activity level.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Popliteal fossa mass is new and specific. Mention of calf erythema if ruptured and normal neurovascular exam adds objective findings and rules out vascular emergency.
clues.rewrite: Palpable mass in popliteal fossa, tender on deep palpation. Knee flexion limited to 90 degrees. Primary knee joint crepitus and mild effusion present. Calf soft tissue swelling present but neurovascular exam normal, capillary refill brisk.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI confirms cystic structure and shows fluid extravasation pattern if ruptured. Identifies underlying OA or meniscal changes.

Baker's Cyst_122.notes: MAJOR STRUCTURAL PROBLEMS: (1) Clue 1 names 'cyst' indirectly (posterior knee swelling + calf symptoms mimicking DVT is essentially the Baker's cyst presentation); (2) Clues 1 and 2 are nearly identical—both describe swelling, calf symptoms, and rupture without functional limitation clarity; (3) Clue 3 adds history but continues to mention 'cyst rupture' which is redundant; (4) Progressive reveal is broken—player learns the answer from Clue 1 and subsequent clues just repeat the same features. RECOMMENDED FIX: Clue 1 should be symptom-only (swelling, calf pain, tightness) without any hint of mechanism. Clue 2 should shift to pure functional limitation (knee flexion loss, walking difficulty). Clue 3 should establish timeline and predisposing knee OA/meniscal pathology. Clue 4 should identify the popliteal mass on exam. Clue 5 should confirm via imaging. Currently, a clinician would confidently guess 'Baker's cyst' after Clue 1.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, sport context, acute onset, burning pain radiating down arm after tackle

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — 'burning along arm' repeats the complaint. 'Immediate functional loss' is slightly new but vague. 'May resolve quickly' is prognostic detail that belongs in History, not Activity.
clues.rewrite: Cannot lift arm overhead or abduct shoulder; grip strength markedly reduced on affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism of injury (neck distraction or shoulder compression) is genuinely new and clinically relevant. Acute impact context reinforces Clue 1 but is not redundant. 'May resolve quickly' from Clue 2 now fits better here as a prognostic note.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Weakness in nerve distribution is new and objective. 'Burning along nerve distribution' repeats Clue 1 complaint. 'Neurapraxia findings may resolve during game' is prognostic/outcome language that blurs the line between current exam findings and prognosis; belongs more in History.
clues.rewrite: Deltoid and rotator cuff weakness; sensory loss in C5-C6 distribution; intact motor branches distally; no Horner's sign.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI findings (nerve edema in significant injury vs. normal in neurapraxia, avulsion in severe cases) are specific and confirmatory. However, the caveat 'often normal in neurapraxia' somewhat undermines diagnostic confidence and could frustrate a player expecting imaging to confirm. Still useful for ruling in/out severity.

Brachial Plexus Injury_123.notes: Progressive reveal has structural issues: Clue 2 (Activity) is too vague and repeats symptom language from Clue 1 without adding functional context that genuinely narrows the differential. Clue 3 (History) is stronger and should come earlier conceptually. Clue 4 (Examination) mixes current exam findings with prognostic language about resolution, which muddies discrimination. After rewrites, the funnel should be: (1) acute burning arm pain after tackle → (2) specific functional loss (overhead weakness, grip loss) → (3) mechanism and expectation of neurapraxia recovery → (4) nerve-distribution weakness pattern and sensory findings → (5) imaging to confirm severity. Currently, a player could guess the answer after Clue 1 or Clue 3; Clues 2 and 4 don't meaningfully narrow further.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Midfoot pain, history of ankle sprain with poor healing, progressive symptoms. Establishes region and chronicity.
clues.rewrite: Patient with midfoot pain following an ankle injury months ago. Pain worsening despite initial treatment.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1. Clue 1 already stated 'functional limitations persist with athletic activities' and chronic nature. Clue 2 just rephrases 'walking, weight-bearing' limitations without adding distinct functional impact.
clues.rewrite: Cannot tolerate single-leg stance or push off during walking. Pain significantly limits running and jumping activities.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism: missed acute injury, inadequate initial treatment. Timeline: chronic with progressive degeneration. Somewhat narrows to post-traumatic midfoot pathology, but 'progressive degenerative changes' and 'chronic instability' are vague synonyms for the answer itself.
clues.rewrite: Acute inversion ankle injury 12 months ago. Initially treated conservatively for ankle sprain, then discharged. Midfoot pain began 2 months post-injury and has not resolved despite rest and physio.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: midfoot swelling, tenderness, abduction stress response, possible deformity, gait abnormality. Should narrow to midfoot instability or syndesmotic injury, but 'abduction stress' is vague — to which structure? Lacks specificity to distinguish Lisfranc from other midfoot injuries.
clues.rewrite: Positive midfoot abduction stress test (forefoot abducted passively with hindfoot fixed). Increased midfoot hypermobility compared to contralateral side. No tenderness over syndesmosis or anterior ankle. Subtle increase in height of medial midfoot arch bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging confirms chronic Lisfranc changes and post-traumatic OA. This is the confirmatory clue, but 'potential degenerative changes' and 'functional instability' are hedge language. The clue should state what imaging actually shows (e.g., disruption of Lisfranc alignment, widening of first-second cuneiform gap, OA changes at midfoot joints).
clues.rewrite: Weight-bearing X-rays show increased gap between first and second cuneiforms (>5mm). CT demonstrates articular surface irregularity at the tarsometatarsal joints with early post-traumatic OA. No acute fracture; findings consistent with chronic ligamentous disruption.

Lisfranc Injury_124.notes: This puzzle has severe progressive reveal failure. Clues 1–3 essentially keep repeating 'midfoot pain post-ankle injury with chronic degenerative changes' without narrowing the differential. By Clue 1, a player already knows this is midfoot pathology post-trauma. Clues 2 and 3 add almost no new discriminatory information. Clue 4 uses vague terms like 'abduction stress' without specifying which structure or how it differs from other midfoot injuries. Clue 5 is also vague ('potential degenerative changes'). The puzzle needs a complete rewrite: Clue 1 should be symptom-only and broader (remove 'midfoot' language if possible); Clue 2 should add specific functional limitations; Clue 3 should add mechanism and timeline; Clue 4 should include specific tests that discriminate Lisfranc (e.g., positive midfoot abduction stress, hypermobility on clinical exam); Clue 5 should specify imaging findings unique to Lisfranc injury (gap widening, tarsometatarsal disruption). Currently the puzzle is too easy early (gives away 'midfoot' in Clue 1) and too vague late (Clue 4 and 5 don't discriminate well).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Elderly patient with recent delirium now in recovery phase with functional decline
clues.rewrite: Elderly patient presenting with marked functional decline and persistent cognitive slowing weeks after hospitalization.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — deconditioning and mobility limitation already stated; adds confusion persisting but this was already implied by 'recovering from delirium'
clues.rewrite: Unable to walk without assistance, requires supervision for ADLs, forgets recent instructions despite appearing alert.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism now clear: sepsis-triggered delirium; timeline established (recent hospitalization, slow recovery). This is appropriately new information.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Redundant — cognitive impairment and deconditioning already stated in Clues 1, 2, and 4. No new objective findings (no ROM, strength grade, special tests, gait pattern, etc.).
clues.rewrite: Proximal weakness (hip flexors 3/5), gait slow and unsteady, attention span <2 minutes, unable to follow 3-step commands reliably.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Rules out structural cause; cognitive testing confirms impairment. Appropriate level of specificity for final clue.

Delirium - Rehabilitation Phase_125.notes: Major progressive reveal failure. The answer is essentially given in Clue 1 ('recovering from acute delirium'). Clues 2 and 4 repeat the same observations (deconditioning, cognitive impairment) without adding new discriminating information. Clue 3 is the only clue that adds genuinely new clinical content (sepsis mechanism). The puzzle lacks a coherent narrowing funnel — a player could guess the answer after Clue 1 alone. Rehabilitation phase delirium is also not a standard physio diagnosis; the underlying deficit (post-sepsis deconditioning, post-delirium cognitive-motor syndrome) would be the actual diagnosis a physio would manage. Consider reframing around post-acute sequelae of sepsis or post-ICU syndrome if this is the intended diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient is active/athletic with mid-back pain affecting performance. Broad enough to include thoracic spine, but 'mid-back dysfunction' is somewhat specific to thoracic region.
clues.rewrite: Active person with mid-back pain that worsens with certain movements during training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Sport-specific movements restricted; improves with warm-up. This adds functional detail but the warm-up improvement is not truly distinct—already implied by 'activity-related' in Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: Unable to perform overhead or rotational movements required for sport; restrictions noted in morning, ease during activity.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Timeline (activity-related), mechanism (training load exceeds mobility), and prognosis (responsive to intervention). This is genuinely new and clinically useful.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Movement restriction in functional patterns, preserved strength, responsive to mobilization. The responsive-to-mobilization detail is vague and overlaps with Clue 2 (improves with activity). Lacks specific special tests or segmental findings.
clues.rewrite: Segmental restriction in mid-thoracic extension and rotation; rib cage symmetry asymmetrical on one side. Neurological examination and strength normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Diagnosis is functional (imaging not needed) and mobility assessment is key. This is more of a clinical philosophy statement than a diagnostic clue. For a 5-clue game, Clue 5 should either provide imaging findings or definitively explain why imaging was obtained (or not) with specific reasoning.
clues.rewrite: X-ray shows normal alignment and no fracture. CT or MRI unnecessary; clinical presentation and movement pattern restriction confirm functional origin.

Thoracic Spine Dysfunction_126.notes: The clue sequence has significant information redundancy. Clues 2 and 4 both emphasize movement restriction and functional improvements without clearly distinguishing between them. Clue 1 uses region-specific language ('mid-back dysfunction') which risks being too easy for informed players. Clue 5 provides philosophy rather than diagnostic data. The progressive reveal funnel is shallow: after Clue 3, the player still doesn't have enough discriminating information to confidently rule out other thoracic conditions (e.g., facet syndrome, costochondritis, intercostal neuralgia, postural dysfunction). Clues 4 and 5 need to add objective examination findings and imaging specificity respectively.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Child with previously normal neck development now presenting with head tilt

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitation: restricted neck rotation and preference for fixed head position; introduces possibility of dystonia
clues.rewrite: Cannot rotate neck fully to one side; head held in fixed position despite attempts to correct.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Completely incoherent — discusses midfoot injury, degenerative changes, and chronic instability unrelated to neck pathology
clues.rewrite: Acute onset following viral illness or fever; recent history of upper respiratory infection or vaccination; no prior neck trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Spastic/dystonic head tilt with abnormal muscle tone — mostly rephrases Clue 2 findings without adding distinct objective discriminators
clues.rewrite: Unilateral sternocleidomastoid or trapezius muscle spasm/hypertonia; head tilt away from affected muscle; neck extension and ipsilateral rotation limited; pain on palpation of affected muscle; no clear trigger point or mechanical restriction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Suggests CNS pathology requiring MRI, metabolic/genetic workup; appropriately specific but vague about what to expect
clues.rewrite: MRI brain and spine normal; no structural lesion, demyelination, or tumor; metabolic panel normal; rules out secondary causes of acute dystonia.

Acquired Torticollis_127.notes: CRITICAL ISSUES: (1) Clue 3 is completely incoherent — it describes a midfoot injury and degenerative joint disease totally unrelated to acquired torticollis in a child. This appears to be text from a completely different puzzle. (2) Clue 2 already introduces dystonia, making Clue 4's 'dystonic features' redundant — no new info. (3) The puzzle conflates acquired torticollis (which is typically post-viral/post-medication acute dystonia) with metabolic/genetic CNS disease, making the clinical story incoherent. (4) Progressive reveal fails entirely because Clue 3 doesn't narrow the differential — it contradicts the entire clinical picture. The puzzle must be completely rewritten around a coherent mechanism (post-viral acute dystonia vs. secondary dystonia requiring metabolic/genetic workup) with Clue 3 establishing timeline and trigger, and Clue 4 differentiating acute acquired from congenital torticollis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, sport, symptom character (intermittent pain over 3 months), and mechanical symptoms (catching/locking). Establishes broad differential including meniscal tears, ligamentous injuries, and intra-articular pathology.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific movement patterns that provoke symptoms (running, jumping, pivoting) and post-activity swelling. Confirms intra-articular/mechanical etiology but doesn't narrow to specific diagnosis yet. Movement-dependent locking is significant.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: No acute trauma (suggests degenerative/overuse rather than acute ligament rupture), insidious onset, progressive course, and increased training load. Narrows toward chronic overuse or repetitive microtrauma pathology. Eliminates acute meniscal tears or ligament injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Medial femoral condyle tenderness (anatomically specific) and Wilson's test (pathognomonic for OCD of the medial femoral condyle). Effusion confirms intra-articular process. This clue is highly specific and strongly suggests OCD over remaining differentials.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI confirms osteochondral lesion with fragment separation and cartilage instability. This is definitive imaging for OCD and essentially confirms the diagnosis. Appropriate as final clue.

Osteochondritis Dissecans_128.notes: This is a well-constructed puzzle with strong progressive narrowing. Clue 1 casts a wide net (mechanical knee pain in a young athlete could be meniscal tear, ligament injury, patellofemoral pain, etc.). Clue 2 maintains that breadth while adding functional impact. Clue 3 adds crucial timeline and mechanism information (insidious, overuse-related, no trauma) that eliminates acute injuries. Clue 4 is the key discriminator—the specific combination of medial femoral condyle tenderness and positive Wilson's test is highly specific for OCD and would allow a skilled clinician to diagnose confidently. Clue 5 confirms. The funnel works well: from symptom → function → mechanism → specific examination finding → imaging confirmation. No redundancy, no premature answer-giving, and each clue meaningfully narrows the differential.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes recent surgery (6 weeks post-ACLR), swelling, pain, and quadriceps weakness as primary complaints
clues.rewrite: Patient reports knee swelling and pain 6 weeks after reconstructive surgery of the knee. Quadriceps weakness noted with difficulty walking. Minimal sensation of instability.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Adds functional limitation pattern: flat walking improving, stairs/pivoting/cutting difficult. Dynamic weight-bearing compromised.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Adds mechanism (pivot-shift during soccer), meniscal involvement, and graft type (BPTB autograft) — context that explains post-op status but essentially CONFIRMS the answer already given in Clue 1
clues.rewrite: ACL tear sustained 6 weeks ago during soccer with pivot-shift mechanism. Concomitant meniscal tear. Treated surgically. No prior knee surgery or significant past medical history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Provides objective findings: effusion, improved Lachman, moderately positive anterior drawer, quad lag, weakness 4-/5, limited extension ROM. These SHOULD discriminate post-ACLR recovery from other post-op or ACL pathologies.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI confirms graft positioning, early integration, no rupture, meniscal healing — essentially confirms diagnosis is straightforward post-ACLR recovery without complications

Post-ACL Reconstruction_129.notes: Primary issue: Clue 1 explicitly mentions 'reconstructive surgery' and 'ACLR' is heavily implied by mentioning 'knee swelling and pain 6 weeks after reconstructive surgery' combined with 'quadriceps weakness.' This gives away the body region and general category (post-surgical knee problem) immediately. Clue 3 then compounds this by literally naming the ACL tear, mechanism, and graft type — providing zero new discriminatory information beyond what Clue 1 already established. The puzzle would be much stronger if Clue 1 focused on symptoms without the word 'reconstructive surgery,' and Clue 3 then revealed the surgical history. The progressive reveal narrows poorly: player knows the answer by Clue 2 and learns almost nothing new from Clues 3–5 that changes the diagnosis. Consider reordering or rewriting Clues 1 and 3 to create genuine diagnostic funnel.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, mechanism (low-energy fall), acute onset, location (groin/hip pain), and physical signs (shortened, externally rotated leg, inability to weight-bear)
clues.rewrite: An 82-year-old woman with known osteoporosis presents with sudden severe pain in the right hip and groin after a fall from standing at home. She cannot stand or walk.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — inability to weight-bear and pain with movement already stated
clues.rewrite: Unable to internally rotate or adduct the hip. Pain is significantly worse with any attempt to move the leg into these ranges compared to other movements.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Adds osteoporosis, postmenopausal status, and history of prior fractures — this DOES narrow differential toward fragility fracture vs. other causes of hip pain

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — shortened, externally rotated leg and severe pain with movement already fully described in the initial complaint
clues.rewrite: Hip flexed, abducted and externally rotated at rest. Severe pain on internal rotation and adduction. Log roll test produces severe groin pain. No crepitus. Neurological exam intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Confirms femoral neck fracture on X-ray. DEXA scan adds osteoporosis confirmation, which was already clinically clear from history.
clues.rewrite: X-ray demonstrates a displaced intracapsular femoral neck fracture with varus angulation and shortening.

Hip Fracture_130.notes: This puzzle fails the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 essentially gives away the answer by combining low-energy fall + elderly + osteoporosis + shortened externally rotated leg — a clinician reads this and immediately thinks 'hip fracture.' Clues 2 and 4 then largely repeat information from Clue 1 without adding meaningful new discriminators. Clue 3 (History) is the only clue that adds genuinely new narrowing info (osteoporosis risk factors), but by then the answer is already obvious. The puzzle should start broader (pain without the pathognomonic physical signs) and let the clues progressively build the clinical picture. Currently, even a student would guess correctly after Clue 1.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age group (older adolescent), symptom (thoracic pain), and concern (postural). Establishes broad differential for thoracic pain in young patients.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: pain varies with activity type and sustained postures. This narrows to conditions aggravated by mechanical loading/postural stress, but is still very broad.
clues.rewrite: Difficulty maintaining upright posture; pain worsens with prolonged sitting or activities requiring trunk extension; forward-curved posture develops over time.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Progressive kyphosis observed by parents, onset in teen years, persistence into adulthood. This adds timeline and structural progression, moving toward degenerative/structural causes rather than acute/inflammatory.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: kyphosis present (already stated in Clue 3), extension restriction (new), muscle tightness (generic). The extension restriction is the only genuinely new discriminator here.
clues.rewrite: Rigid thoracic kyphosis with loss of extension ROM; vertebral segments appear stiff rather than mobile; posterior chain tightness; pain reproducible at apical kyphotic level on palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Radiographs show 'healing changes from old disease' and 'mature kyphosis.' This is appropriately specific but the phrasing 'healing changes from old disease' is vague and could apply to many conditions. The specific finding needed here is vertebral wedging and endplate irregularity.
clues.rewrite: Radiographs show anterior vertebral body wedging (≥5° across multiple consecutive thoracic vertebrae), irregular endplates, and loss of disc space height—consistent with resolved Schmorl's nodes and mature structural change.
