Scheuermann's Disease_131.notes: Progressive reveal has structural problems. Clue 2 (Activity) is too generic and doesn't narrow meaningfully from Clue 1—both just say 'pain in certain situations.' Clue 3 adds critical information (progressive kyphosis over years) that should come earlier to establish the structural/degenerative nature. Clue 4 mostly restates kyphosis from Clue 3 with only extension loss as new info. Clue 5 is vague ('healing changes from old disease') and doesn't give the reader the specific vertebral body features that would confirm Scheuermann's. After Clues 1–2, a clinician has almost no narrowing; the diagnosis becomes clear mainly from Clue 3 onwards. Reorder recommended: Clue 3 should move closer to Clue 1 to establish the progressive structural pattern early.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, occupation (golfer), symptom onset (gradual), and body region (inner arm pain). Establishes broad differential for elbow/forearm pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations tied to wrist flexion and gripping. Narrows to flexor-pronator origin pathology rather than lateral elbow or other regions.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: MAJOR PROBLEM: This clue describes an acute knee ACL/meniscal injury and reconstruction 6 weeks ago. This is completely incoherent with the elbow complaint in clues 1–2. It is a different body region, different timing, and unrelated to medial epicondylitis. This clue adds NO relevant history to the elbow diagnosis.
clues.rewrite: Repetitive gripping activities at work over the past 3 years, with pain onset following a weekend golf tournament. Previous left shoulder impingement treated conservatively 2 years ago, now resolved.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Tenderness over medial epicondyle + pain with resisted wrist flexion + mild weakness. Confirms flexor-pronator origin pathology and distinguishes from lateral epicondylitis or other differentials.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound shows tendon inflammation and thickening; X-rays exclude fracture. Confirms soft tissue origin and rules out bony pathology. Consolidates diagnosis.

Medial Epicondylitis_132.notes: This puzzle has a critical incoherence: Clue 3 (History) describes a knee ACL/meniscal injury and reconstruction, which is entirely unrelated to the elbow complaint and diagnosis. This is either a copy-paste error or a corrupted puzzle. Clue 3 must be rewritten to contain relevant elbow history (e.g., onset mechanism, prior treatments, occupational/activity factors) that advances the differential reasoning toward medial epicondylitis. Clues 1, 2, 4, and 5 form a coherent clinical narrative, but Clue 3 breaks it completely. Overall progressive reveal would be good if Clue 3 were fixed.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographics (68F), post-operative timeline (3 weeks), and chief symptoms (pain, swelling, reduced ROM)
clues.rewrite: 68-year-old female presenting with pain and swelling at the knee, with difficulty regaining movement after recent major surgery.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1. Clue 1 already stated 'limited knee range of motion'; Clue 2 just rephrases as 'knee extension and flexion very limited.' Clue 1 already implied weight-bearing difficulty.
clues.rewrite: Difficulty achieving heel-to-buttock flexion and full knee extension. Struggles with stairs and sit-to-stand transfers.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Provides mechanism (severe OA refractory to conservative treatment) and surgical timeline confirmation. 'Standard surgical approach' is vague and adds minimal specificity.
clues.rewrite: Severe osteoarthritis resistant to 6+ months of physiotherapy and injections. Open surgical approach used. Patient reports good pre-operative functional level relative to disease severity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective measurement of ROM (flexion 60°, extension lag 10°), presence of effusion, and quadriceps inhibition. This is the first clue to provide measurable discriminators.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Confirms prosthetic alignment and component positioning are appropriate; rules out mechanical complications (malalignment, instability, loosening).

Post-Total Knee Replacement_133.notes: Major structural flaw: Clue 1 essentially names the diagnosis by stating 'major knee surgery' and 'severe osteoarthritis' in a post-operative context. A player can guess Total Knee Replacement from Clue 1 alone. Clue 2 is almost entirely redundant with Clue 1 — both describe the same ROM limitations and pain with movement. Progressive reveal collapses early; Clues 3-5 are confirmatory rather than narrowing a differential. The puzzle needs Clue 1 rewritten to focus on post-operative symptoms WITHOUT the surgery detail, and Clue 2 rewritten to describe specific functional deficits (e.g., stair climbing, transfers) not already implied by ROM loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient reports skin breakdown and tissue loss; establishes basic complaint
clues.rewrite: Older patient reports a painful area of skin that has become red, swollen, and tender over the past week.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — immobility already implied by 'breakdown' and explicitly stated; 'high risk patient' is vague and adds little
clues.rewrite: Patient unable to reposition or change position independently; spending 12+ hours daily in same position on a single surface.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Adds timeline context (prolonged immobility), nutritional factors, comorbidities (diabetes, infection risk) — these begin to narrow differential

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific wound characteristics (open, partial/full thickness, exposed dermis, purulent drainage) — this is appropriate specificity for Clue 4 and does narrow differentials meaningfully

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging depth assessment and culture results confirm infection/tissue involvement; appropriate for final confirmation

Pressure Injury Stage 2-3_134.notes: Major problem: Clues 1 and 2 together are essentially redundant — both state immobility and skin breakdown explicitly, which gives away 'pressure injury' far too early. Clue 1 names the condition directly ('skin breakdown and tissue loss'). The differential should be much broader at Clue 1 (could be dermatitis, infection, trauma, surgical complication, etc.). Clue 2 should add NEW functional/positional context (prolonged single positioning) rather than repeating immobility. By Clue 3, history of diabetes + poor nutrition + immobility should make pressure injury the obvious leading diagnosis. Clues 4–5 are appropriately specific. Rewrite Clues 1–2 to widen the initial funnel and make the progressive narrowing felt by the player.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Mid-thoracic location, age/occupation context, gradual worsening, posture-dependent pain

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant. 'Worse with prolonged sitting' already stated in Clue 1. 'Worsens with certain movements/postures' rephrases Clue 1 without adding specificity. 'Improved with gentle mobilization' is the only new detail, but vague.
clues.rewrite: Unable to maintain posture for extended periods; pain increases with reaching forward or overhead activities; some relief with manual therapy.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Redundant. 'Insidious onset' already stated in Clue 1 ('worsened over six months'). 'Related to posture' restates Clue 1. 'May follow immobility' adds minimal new detail and contradicts the context (office worker with constant symptoms).
clues.rewrite: No specific traumatic event; symptoms coincided with new desk setup and remote work three months ago; no previous spinal issues; denies trauma or falls.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: restricted thoracic rotation, midthoracic tenderness, absence of neuro signs. This does narrow differential (excludes radiculopathy, myelopathy) and adds concrete examination data.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Normal imaging rules out significant structural pathology (disc herniation, fracture, spondylolisthesis). Confirms functional diagnosis. Appropriate for final clue.

Thoracic Spine Dysfunction_135.notes: The puzzle has a critical flaw in the middle tier: Clues 1, 2, and 3 all convey the same essential information (posture-related stiffness, gradual onset, worse with sitting/inactivity). A player gains almost nothing new from moving through these clues—the differential doesn't meaningfully narrow. Clues 4 and 5 are reasonable, but the cumulative redundancy in 1–3 makes the game feel flat. Rewrite Clues 2 and 3 to add orthogonal information: Clue 2 should specify functional limitations (e.g., overhead reach, sustained posture, specific movement directions), and Clue 3 should add distinct history (trigger event, comorbidities, or prior treatment response) that differentiates from other postural/mechanical thoracic conditions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Elderly patient with hip pain post-operatively after fracture surgery

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 - simply restates post-operative failure to recover. Does not specify functional limitation pattern (e.g., weight-bearing vs. ROM vs. specific movement). Names the problem ('mechanical failure of fixation') rather than describing functional impact.
clues.rewrite: Unable to weight-bear or ambulate without pain and instability; functionally immobile.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Adds timeline (12 weeks post-op) and introduces 'instability' as emerging feature. Specifies proximal femoral fracture location. However, 'instability' is already named in Clue 2 and should not reappear.
clues.rewrite: Proximal femoral fracture fixed 12 weeks ago with internal fixation. Recent onset of increasing hip pain and giving way despite apparent initial healing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Reiterates 'mechanical instability and functional decline' already stated in Clues 1, 2, and 3. No new objective findings provided (no specific tests, ROM values, palpation, neurovascular status, etc.).
clues.rewrite: Marked pain with hip flexion/abduction; positive Trendelenburg sign; audible clunk on hip rotation under load; no neurovascular compromise.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Specifies imaging findings (loss of reduction, screw penetration) and notes osteoporosis as comorbidity. This is genuinely the first clue to provide concrete imaging detail, making it appropriate for Clue 5. However, 'loss of reduction or screw penetration' is a near-synonym for 'mechanical failure of fixation' from Clue 2.
clues.rewrite: X-ray shows lateral cortical perforation of proximal femur; loss of fracture line reduction; severe osteoporosis limits revision options.

Subtrochanteric Fracture_136.notes: Major progressive reveal failure. Clues 1–4 all circle around the same idea ('post-op failure/instability/mechanical breakdown') without narrowing the differential. Clue 1 could plausibly apply to infection, DVT, neurovascular compromise, or hardware failure. Clues 2–4 all reinforce 'instability' without discriminating mechanism. Clue 2 names the problem rather than describing function. Clue 3 merely restates Clue 2 with a timeline. Clue 4 adds no objective clinical signs. Only Clue 5 provides actual imaging data. The puzzle needs a complete resequencing: Clue 1 should remain broad complaint; Clue 2 should isolate weight-bearing dysfunction; Clue 3 should add timeline and mechanism (fixation vs. bone healing); Clue 4 should provide specific exam findings distinguishing hardware failure from osteoporotic collapse; Clue 5 should confirm with imaging. As written, this puzzle would frustrate players because the information plateau stops them from reasoning clinically.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Progressive leg weakness and breathing difficulty that worsens toward evening — establishes symptom onset and pattern of fatigue.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: cannot climb stairs, requires mechanical ventilation at night. NEW: weakness improves after rest — this is a key distinguishing feature (fatigability and recovery) that narrows toward neuromuscular junction disorder.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Eye drooping onset 1 year ago, progression to generalized weakness, recent myasthenic crisis hospitalization. NEW: establishes localized-to-generalized pattern and acute decompensation, but 'myasthenic crisis' is almost a direct synonym for the answer.
clues.rewrite: Started with eye drooping 1 year ago, progressed to generalized weakness over months. Recently hospitalized for acute respiratory decline requiring intubation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Fatigability, ptosis, nasal speech, positive tensilon test, normal sensation. These are examination findings that confirm neuromuscular junction pathology, but the clue is poorly formatted and 'positive tensilon test response' is highly specific and diagnostic.
clues.rewrite: Ptosis and ophthalmoplegia bilaterally. Weakness of facial and bulbar muscles (nasal speech, difficulty with chewing). Strength deficits worse after sustained muscle contraction. Tensilon test shows transient improvement in strength.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Serological and electrophysiological findings: seronegative antibodies, repetitive stimulation shows >10% decrement. This is appropriately specific for final confirmation, but 'seronegative MG' names the answer directly.
clues.rewrite: Repetitive nerve stimulation demonstrates >10% decremental response in proximal muscles. Anti-acetylcholine receptor and anti-MuSK antibodies negative.

Myasthenia Gravis_137.notes: The puzzle has a coherent clinical narrative (ocular → generalized → respiratory crisis), but MAJOR FLAW: Clues 2, 3, and 5 either name the answer ('myasthenic crisis', 'seronegative MG') or come very close. Clue 3's 'myasthenic crisis' and Clue 5's explicit 'seronegative MG' essentially give away the diagnosis. Clue 4 is also examination-specific but formatted as prose list, making it hard to parse. The progressive reveal principle is compromised because experienced clinicians would identify MG well before Clue 5. Rewrite all three major clues to describe findings and history without naming or heavily implying the diagnosis. Clue 4 needs cleaner presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes age group (school-age child), diagnosis category (autism), and two symptom domains (coordination difficulties, sensory sensitivities)
clues.rewrite: School-age child reports feeling clumsy and uncoordinated. Struggles with balance and dislikes certain clothing textures, bright lights, and loud noises.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specifies functional limitations: clumsiness, poor balance, avoidance of team sports, texture avoidance
clues.rewrite: Cannot participate in organised team sports due to coordination difficulties. Prefers solitary or structured activities. Trips frequently and seems to lack spatial awareness.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Adds formal diagnosis timeline (age 3), concurrent therapy (speech and OT), implying prior assessment and multi-system involvement
clues.rewrite: History of developmental delay identified at age 3. Currently receiving occupational therapy and speech pathology support. No specific injury or recent change in function reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Adds objective findings: motor planning deficits, proprioceptive deficits, abnormal gait, sensory defensiveness
clues.rewrite: Gait pattern irregular and energy-inefficient. Difficulty coordinating bilateral limbs during crossing midline activities. Hyper-responsive to light touch; defensive reactions to textures. Proprioceptive testing shows reduced body awareness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Brain imaging findings (atypical connectivity) and developmental motor assessment (delays)
clues.rewrite: Functional MRI demonstrates altered connectivity in motor coordination and sensory integration networks. Standardised motor development scales confirm delays in gross and fine motor milestones.

Autism Spectrum Disorder_138.notes: CRITICAL ISSUES: (1) Clue 1 names the diagnosis ('autism') — this is a fundamental violation of Wordle format and makes the puzzle unsolvable as intended. The answer should be discoverable through clinical reasoning, not stated upfront. (2) Clues 1–5 are largely redundant; all five clues essentially rephrase the same core observation: 'child with autism has motor and sensory issues.' There is NO progressive narrowing of differential diagnosis because the diagnosis is already named in Clue 1. (3) This puzzle is not suitable for Physiodle because autism spectrum disorder is a neurodevelopmental psychiatric diagnosis, not a mechanical/structural physiotherapy condition. Physiodle should focus on conditions a physiotherapist diagnoses and manages (e.g., rotator cuff tear, ACL injury, plantar fasciitis), not primary psychiatric/developmental diagnoses. Recommend rejection of this puzzle and reframing toward a motor coordination dysfunction that mimics ASD presentations (e.g., developmental coordination disorder, cerebellar ataxia) — if that is the intent.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, sex, symptom character (room-spinning vertigo), episode duration (seconds to minutes), and trigger type (positional: lying down, rolling in bed). Establishes vertigo as the chief complaint and hints at positional nature.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1. Clue 1 already specified triggers (lying down, rolling in bed, looking up implied). Clue 2 simply elaborates the same positional triggers without adding functional limitation insight beyond what Clue 1 established. Does not meaningfully narrow differential beyond 'positional vertigo'.
clues.rewrite: Patient avoids head movements and certain positions to prevent vertigo. Between episodes, able to walk, stand, and perform ADLs normally. Hesitant to lie flat or turn in bed independently due to fear of triggering attack.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Proximal femoral fracture 12 weeks prior with current pain and instability. This is INCOHERENT with the BPPV diagnosis — introduces a lower-limb musculoskeletal injury that has no causal or clinical link to benign paroxysmal positional vertigo. The clue should establish timeline/mechanism for vertigo (e.g., recent head trauma, prolonged bed rest, prior vestibular events, or idiopathic onset), not unrelated orthopedic history.
clues.rewrite: Vertigo onset insidious, 3 weeks ago. No recent head trauma or significant illness. No prior balance problems or vestibular disorders. Lives alone; symptoms causing anxiety about falling and social withdrawal.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Positive Dix-Hallpike maneuver producing brief vertigo and rotatory nystagmus resolving within 60 seconds. This is the key discriminator for BPPV and differentiates it from other vestibular causes (e.g., vestibulitis, Menière's disease, central causes). Confirms normal gait/balance between episodes, supporting peripheral rather than central cause.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Clue 5 repeats information already given in Clue 4. Rotatory nystagmus < 60 seconds on Dix-Hallpike was already stated in Examination. The phrase 'otoconia from utricle confirmed by characteristic nystagmus' is not imaging — it is clinical diagnosis. True imaging (MRI, CT, video-oculography) is not typically needed for BPPV; the clue should either acknowledge this explicitly or provide a finding that rules out mimics (e.g., 'MRI of brain/internal auditory canal normal, excludes central/retrocochlear pathology').
clues.rewrite: MRI of brain and internal auditory canal normal. No evidence of central nervous system lesion, retrocochlear pathology, or other structural cause. Diagnosis confirmed clinically by Dix-Hallpike maneuver; imaging performed to exclude red flags given age and initial presentation.

Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo_139.notes: Major structural problem: Clue 3 (History) is incoherent — introduces a proximal femoral fracture with no link to BPPV. This breaks the clinical narrative. Clue 2 (Activity) adds no new information beyond Clue 1 and should be rewritten to describe functional impact and disability pattern. Clue 5 (Imaging) repeats examination findings from Clue 4 instead of providing imaging confirmation or exclusion of mimics. After Clue 1, the differential should include multiple causes of positional vertigo (BPPV, vestibulitis, Menière's disease, migraine-associated, central causes); Clue 2 fails to narrow this meaningfully. Clue 3 should establish timeline/mechanism for BPPV; instead it introduces orthopedic irrelevance. Clue 4 is strong. Clue 5 should confirm with imaging but currently just restates clinical findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Middle-aged woman with hip pain; age and gender establish demographic context.
clues.rewrite: Middle-aged woman with groin and anterior hip pain, worse with prolonged sitting and certain movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Pain pattern with flexion/adduction and limited external rotation in flexion — defines movement restriction and pain provocation pattern.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mentions increased acetabular coverage as an anatomical feature — but this is structural imaging finding, not history. Should cover mechanism and timeline instead.
clues.rewrite: Insidious onset over months; no clear traumatic event; symptoms gradually worsened with running and hip-heavy activities; no prior hip surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Anterior hip pain provocation (likely FABER/anterior impingement test), tight adductors, and limited external rotation — these are objective findings distinct from the complaint and activity clues.
clues.rewrite: FABER test reproduces anterior hip pain; hip external rotation in flexion is limited to 15°; adductor tightness; pain with log-roll test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Over-coverage of acetabulum with coxa profunda or acetabular retroversion — imaging confirmation of structural pathology that explains the clinical picture.

Femoroacetabular Impingement_140.notes: Clue 1 explicitly names the anatomical basis of the answer ('acetabular anatomy'), essentially giving away FAI immediately — this is a critical failure. Clue 2 is solid. Clue 3 incorrectly places imaging/structural information in the History slot; it should focus on mechanism and timeline. Clue 4 is good but slightly redundant — the limitation in external rotation was already stated in Clue 2. Clue 5 is appropriately specific. The progressive reveal is compromised because Clue 1 does too much work; a player reading only the complaint already knows the answer is hip anatomy-related and impingement-like.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, setting (residential aged care), progressive unsteadiness and reluctance to mobilise over months. Establishes a broad elderly patient with mobility decline.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional dependence for transfers/walking, forgetfulness with walking frame, confusion about direction mid-task. NEW: cognitive/behavioural component affecting mobility.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Progressive neurodegenerative diagnosis three years ago with accelerating cognitive decline. Multiple falls over six months. NEW: specific diagnosis category (neurodegenerative) and timeline, eliminating acute/primary structural pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Balance impairment (eyes open/closed), shuffling gait with reduced step length/arm swing, difficulty with multi-step commands. NEW: specific motor pattern (parkinsonian features) and executive dysfunction during assessment.
clues.rewrite: Marked postural instability with backward tendency on perturbation. Parkinsonian features: rigid trunk, reduced arm swing, shuffling steps with freezing tendency. Minimal arm swing despite instruction to swing arms. Difficulty turning; requires multiple steps to redirect.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: CT brain shows generalised atrophy and ventricular enlargement (non-specific neurodegenerative pattern). Osteoporosis noted. NEW: secondary finding (osteoporosis risk) but imaging is generic and doesn't discriminate between dementia subtypes or confirm diagnosis.
clues.rewrite: CT brain shows marked generalised cerebral atrophy with disproportionate frontosubcortical involvement and substantial ventricular dilatation. Basal ganglia appear hypodense. No acute findings. DaTscan shows reduced striatal dopamine uptake bilaterally.

Dementia-Related Mobility Decline_141.notes: The puzzle has a coherent clinical story: elderly patient with known neurodegenerative disease, cognitive decline, and progressive mobility decline. Clues 1-3 work well to narrow from 'elderly with mobility decline' to 'neurodegenerative dementia with motor features.' However, Clue 4 (Examination) largely rephrases information already covered in Clues 1-2 (unsteadiness, confusion, forgetfulness affecting mobility) rather than adding new discriminatory objective findings. Clue 5 is problematic: generalised atrophy and ventricular enlargement are non-specific across multiple dementias (Alzheimer's, vascular, Lewy body, etc.) and don't confirm dementia-related mobility decline over other neurodegenerative causes with parkinsonian features. To improve, Clue 4 should include specific examination findings (postural instability, freezing, rigidity) that point toward Lewy body or parkinsonian-variant dementia, and Clue 5 should include imaging findings specific to the subtype (e.g., basal ganglia changes, striatal hypodensity, or DaTscan findings for Lewy body).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Sudden loss of leg function and bowel/bladder control following lumbar spine trauma
clues.rewrite: Patient experienced sudden onset paraplegia following a crush injury to the lower back at work.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Bilateral leg weakness and perineal sensory loss — adds anatomical specificity of sensory distribution
clues.rewrite: Unable to walk or stand; complete loss of lower limb motor control bilaterally.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism (crush injury from heavy equipment) and urgency of presentation (emergent trauma admission)
clues.rewrite: Crushed between heavy machinery at work. Mechanism suggests high-energy axial or compressive force to the lower spine.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific reflex and sacral examination findings (absent anal wink, absent bulbocavernosus reflex, saddle anesthesia, flaccid paralysis grade 1/5)

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging confirms conus medullaris compression with hemorrhagic infarction and edema — definitive diagnosis

Lumbar Spinal Cord Injury_142.notes: Major problems: Clue 1 essentially names the answer by explicitly stating 'sudden loss of leg function and bowel/bladder control' + 'lumbar spine trauma' — this is nearly pathognomonic for acute spinal cord injury and leaves almost no differential. Clue 2 mostly repeats the same information (bilateral weakness, sensory loss in sacral distribution) without adding meaningful functional or anatomical distinction. Clue 3 adds mechanism and urgency but these should have been in Clue 1, and the history clue doesn't narrow differential further. Clues 4 and 5 are appropriately specific and useful. The progressive reveal is broken early; by Clue 1, an experienced clinician has already diagnosed the answer. Rewrite Clues 1-2 to be much broader and withhold the mechanism until Clue 3.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Involuntary jerking movements and mood changes in middle-aged patient
clues.rewrite: Middle-aged patient with progressive involuntary movements and behavioral deterioration over the past 2 years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: unable to sit still, personality changes affecting social/work life
clues.rewrite: Tremor and uncoordinated limb movements worsening with intentional tasks; difficulty with fine motor control and balance.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Family history (mother, similar presentation, died age 60); symptom timeline (behavioral 2 years ago, choreiform 1 year ago); sequential progression

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific movement pattern (choreiform), tone abnormality (hypotonia), oculomotor dysfunction, cognitive slowing
clues.rewrite: Generalized dance-like involuntary movements, difficulty with smooth eye tracking, mild slowing of thought and speech, preserved strength.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Genetic confirmation (CAG repeat >39 in HTT gene); structural finding (caudate atrophy) - pathognomonic for Huntington's disease

Huntington's Disease_143.notes: Major problem: Clue 1 is too leading. 'Involuntary jerking movements' + 'mood changes' in a middle-aged patient nearly names Huntington's disease outright for anyone with neurological knowledge. Clue 2 repeats the movement dysfunction and behavioral issues from Clue 1 without adding functional specificity — it just rephrases symptoms as activity limitations. Clue 3 is the first clue that truly narrows the differential (family history is critical). Clue 4 describes choreiform movements, which was already heavily implied by Clue 1. The puzzle collapses after Clue 1 and doesn't follow a true clinical funnel. Rewrites aim to: (1) make Clue 1 broader and less disease-specific, (2) make Clue 2 focus on actual functional tasks and motor control deficits rather than repeating symptom language, and (3) preserve Clues 3, 4, 5 which are reasonably specific.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Sudden onset arm pain with visible deformity in biceps region — establishes acute mechanism and anatomical region (upper arm/shoulder)
clues.rewrite: 55-year-old male with sudden onset severe upper arm pain and visible lump or swelling in the biceps region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional loss: weakness with elbow flexion and supination — narrowing to shoulder/elbow flexor or rotator involvement

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism (lifting overhead), sudden pop sensation, timeline (3 days), and negative history (no prior surgery/rotator cuff disease) — confirms acute traumatic event and excludes chronic or post-surgical causes

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 and Clue 2. Loss of flexion/supination power was already stated in Clue 2. Visible muscle retraction is a rephrase of 'visible deformity' from Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: Marked weakness with elbow flexion and supination strength testing (grade 2/5). Visible bulge or absence of normal biceps contour in upper arm on inspection.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging confirmation: absent tendon on ultrasound and MRI-confirmed rupture with retraction — definitive confirmation of diagnosis

Long Head of Biceps Rupture_144.notes: The puzzle suffers from a significant overlap between Clues 1 and 3–4. Clue 1 already names 'visible deformity in the biceps,' which is too anatomically specific and essentially gives away the answer. Clue 3 adds good mechanism and timeline, but Clue 4 merely restates weakness findings from Clue 2 and rephrases the visual finding from Clue 1. The progressive reveal is weak: after Clue 1, the differential is already extremely narrow (essentially long head biceps rupture or proximal biceps strain). A better sequence would: (1) focus Clue 1 on pain/weakness without naming biceps, (2) keep Clue 2 functional, (3) add mechanism in Clue 3, (4) use Clue 4 to discriminate between partial tear vs. complete rupture via specific strength grades, and (5) confirm with imaging. Rewrite Clue 1 to be less anatomically revealing and Clue 4 to add genuinely new examination data (e.g., specific strength grades, palpation findings, or special tests that differentiate from other shoulder pathology).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient is post-operative and experiencing pain improvement. However, the clue immediately names 'knee surgery' and gives a specific timeline (4 weeks), which is too specific and anchors too strongly to post-surgical context.
clues.rewrite: Patient reports significant pain in the knee with variable symptoms depending on activity level and time of day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations with walking, sit-to-stand, and knee movement patterns (flexion progressing, extension lag). Narrows to knee pathology requiring gait aids.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Explicitly names 'Total knee arthroplasty performed 4 weeks ago' and surgical approach. This is essentially naming the answer. The clue should provide context without stating the diagnosis directly.
clues.rewrite: History of severe knee osteoarthritis refractory to conservative management. Recent surgical intervention within the past month.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings including well-healed incision, effusion, specific ROM measurements (90° flexion, 15° extension lag), and quadriceps weakness. These findings differentiate post-surgical states and clarify the timeline and recovery stage.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Radiographic confirmation of prosthesis positioning, alignment, and absence of loosening. Definitively confirms arthroplasty and rules out mechanical complications.

Post-Total Knee Replacement_145.notes: Major problem: Clues 1, 2, and 3 collectively make the diagnosis obvious far too early. Clue 1 shouldn't mention 'knee surgery' specifically — it should describe post-operative pain patterns generically. Clue 3 essentially names the diagnosis by stating 'Total knee arthroplasty performed 4 weeks ago'. The progressive reveal collapses because a player reading Clue 3 knows the answer with certainty. Clues 4 and 5 are appropriately specific but become redundant. Restructure Clue 1 to describe pain complaints only, and Clue 3 to reference recent surgery without naming the procedure explicitly.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes respiratory symptoms (fever, rapid breathing, cough) in a 3-year-old; sets broad differential for lower respiratory tract infection.
clues.rewrite: 3-year-old child with high fever, persistent cough, and rapid breathing for one week. No improvement with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Adds functional limitation: dyspnoea at rest and with minimal exertion (walking); indicates severity and respiratory compromise.
clues.rewrite: Child is breathless even at rest and cannot walk short distances without increased respiratory distress.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: CRITICAL INCOHERENCE: Clue 1 states '3-year-old'; Clue 3 states '3-month-old infant, unwell for four days'. These are contradictory and cannot be the same patient. Also, Clue 1 mentions 'one week' of illness; Clue 3 says 'four days'.
clues.rewrite: Born at term with no perinatal complications. No prior hospital admissions. Currently unwell for four days.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Provides objective findings that strongly suggest lower respiratory tract infection with hypoxaemia and focal consolidation (left lower lobe crackles and reduced air entry). Narrows differential and indicates severity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Confirms consolidation pattern and etiology (RSV); elevated WCC supports infection. Clinches diagnosis of viral pneumonia.
clues.rewrite: Chest X-ray shows left lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms. Nasopharyngeal swab positive for respiratory syncytial virus.

Pneumonia_146.notes: This puzzle has SERIOUS INCOHERENCE problems. The patient age and timeline change between Clue 1 ('3-year-old...one week') and Clue 3 ('3-month-old infant...four days'), making it impossible for these clues to describe the same patient. This is a fundamental failure of puzzle design. Beyond this critical issue, the clinical progression is reasonable (broad respiratory complaint → functional dyspnoea → history → examination findings → imaging confirmation), but the contradictions make the puzzle unplayable. The answer 'pneumonia' is also somewhat generic for a physio-focused game (pneumonia is a medical diagnosis, not typically a physiotherapy diagnosis), and clues 4–5 are highly specific to medical (not physio) examination and imaging. Rewrite required with consistent patient details throughout.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Post-operative timeline (10 days) and visible wound separation with drainage.
clues.rewrite: Increasing discharge from surgical site with visible gap opening at wound edges, beginning second post-operative week.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: activity restrictions and delayed rehabilitation due to wound care complexity.
clues.rewrite: Unable to bear weight or perform upper limb loading activities; prone positioning contraindicated due to wound location.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Surgical context, timing, and multiple risk factors (obesity, diabetes, poor nutrition, immunosuppression) that predispose to wound complications.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective wound assessment: degree of separation, drainage type (serosanguineous), surrounding tissue signs (induration, minimal erythema), and systemic stability.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound findings showing fascial layer separation, fluid collection, and early abscess formation risk—confirms deep wound involvement beyond skin/subcutaneous layer.

Wound Dehiscence Post-Surgical_147.notes: Clue 1 is too specific and names the answer outright—'wound separation' explicitly describes dehiscence. It should describe only symptoms (drainage character, timing, patient concern) without the anatomical diagnosis. Clue 2 adds minimal new discriminatory information; activity restrictions and delayed rehab are predictable consequences of any major post-surgical complication. Rewrite Clue 2 to specify the anatomical location or movement pattern limitation (e.g., weight-bearing vs. upper limb), which would narrow toward specific surgical sites and aid differential reasoning. Clues 3, 4, 5 follow a logical progression and add meaningful new information. The puzzle is currently too easy after Clue 1 alone.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Acute impact injury with transient visual disturbance and altered mental status in a young athlete; establishes mechanism, population, and broad symptom picture.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Cognitive/memory impairment and 'fogginess' — adds post-injury neurocognitive symptoms beyond immediate complaint.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Specifies temporal impact zone and brief loss of awareness (30 sec) — adds precision on mechanism and severity of initial injury.
clues.rewrite: Second head impact to same region 3 weeks prior; reported worsening of fogginess and concentration issues since first incident despite return to light training.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective neuro findings (GCS 15, normal cranial nerves, normal pupils, mild headache, no focal deficits) — establishes absence of severe intracranial pathology or focal neurological injury.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: CT head normal; confirms clinical diagnosis is based on symptom cluster and mechanism rather than structural lesion.
clues.rewrite: CT head normal. ImPACT baseline neurocognitive testing shows decline from pre-season scores in processing speed and reaction time; score consistent with concussive injury.

Concussion_148.notes: The puzzle has good progressive structure overall but suffers from a critical redundancy issue in Clue 3. Clues 1 and 2 already establish the impact mechanism, transient confusion, and post-concussive symptoms. Clue 3 ('moment of impact to temporal region' + '30 sec loss of awareness') merely elaborates on what Clue 1 already told us ('heading the ball forcefully' + 'brief confusion'). It should add genuinely new discriminating information — e.g., prior head injury history or progressive symptom trajectory — to narrow the differential toward concussion vs. other acute neuro conditions. Clue 5 is also weak; 'normal CT + clinical diagnosis' is essentially just confirming the absence of structural pathology, which Clue 4 (normal neuro exam) already strongly suggested. A specific neurocognitive finding or vestibular impairment would better seal the diagnosis. The puzzle works clinically but violates the progressive-reveal principle at Clue 3 and misses an opportunity at Clue 5 for true diagnostic confirmation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Young patient, gym-related activity context, groin location, persistent discomfort. Establishes broad hip/groin differential.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific movement pattern (squats, lunges) that triggers pain. This is distinct from Clue 1 — it narrows to compound lower limb movements and hip-dominant exercises, helping differentiate hip pathology from other groin conditions.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Gradual onset over months, no trauma. This rules out acute injury and post-traumatic causes, but adds relatively little beyond what Clue 1 already implied (persistent, progressive issue in a young person).
clues.rewrite: Insidious onset over 6 months; no prior hip injury or surgery. Symptoms began after increasing deadlift volume at the gym.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Combined movement test reproduces pain; hip external rotation preserved. The movement reproduction is valuable, but 'external rotation preserved' is odd phrasing — it suggests ROM testing but doesn't strongly differentiate FAI from other hip intra-articular pathology. This clue feels weak.
clues.rewrite: Positive anterior hip impingement test; pain on flexion-internal rotation combined movement. Hip flexion ROM limited to 100° and internal rotation to 10° in flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: 3D CT with cam and pincer morphology. This is specific and definitional for FAI — appropriate for final clue.

Femoroacetabular Impingement_149.notes: The puzzle has a reasonable overall structure but suffers from weak differentiation in Clue 3 and Clue 4. Clue 3 (History) is generic — 'gradual onset, no trauma' applies to many hip conditions (labral tears, osteoarthritis, muscle strains) and doesn't meaningfully narrow beyond Clue 1. Clue 4 (Examination) mentions 'external rotation preserved' which is vague and doesn't discriminate well; FAI is classically defined by limitation of combined flexion-internal rotation, and specific tests like the FABER test or anterior impingement test would be much stronger differentiators. Rewriting Clue 3 to include sport/activity history and Clue 4 to include ROM restrictions and specific impingement test results would create a much tighter diagnostic funnel.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes respiratory symptoms (chest tightness, cough, wheezing) with occupational/temporal pattern (work-triggered, symptom-free on weekends). Suggests occupational or environmental trigger.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1. The occupational trigger (aerosols/dust) was already stated in Complaint. Adds no new functional limitation or body region clarity beyond what Clue 1 established.
clues.rewrite: Patient is unable to perform sustained physical exertion; symptoms improve markedly with rest and are worse in the evening after activity.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Recent major surgery 10 days ago with unremarkable post-op course. This introduces a timeline and context (post-operative state) that could suggest post-operative complications (PE, pneumonia, atelectasis, acute heart failure) rather than pure occupational asthma.
clues.rewrite: Patient has a childhood history of atopy and seasonal allergies. No prior respiratory diagnoses. Symptoms began 6 weeks ago and have progressively worsened. No recent infections or surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings of airway obstruction: bilateral expiratory wheeze, severely reduced peak expiratory flow (52% predicted), accessory muscle use, tachycardia. These findings differentiate obstructive airway disease from other causes of chest tightness and support lower airway pathology.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Spirometry confirms obstructive pattern (FEV1/FVC 0.62) with significant bronchodilator reversibility (18%), confirming asthma as primary diagnosis. Hyperinflation on CXR supports chronic airway obstruction. Elevated FeNO (42 ppb) indicates eosinophilic airway inflammation, highly specific for asthma.

Asthma_150.notes: Major structural problem: Clue 2 (Activity) is completely redundant with Clue 1 — both state the same occupational trigger without adding new functional or discriminatory information. More critically, Clue 3 introduces a recent major surgery 10 days ago with an 'unremarkable' post-op course, which is INCOHERENT with the symptom timeline in Clue 1 (occupational pattern over weeks/months). A patient with acute post-operative symptoms would not show a clear occupational/weekday pattern; this suggests either occupational asthma (chronic) or post-operative complication (acute), not both. The history clue should establish baseline asthma risk (atopy, childhood wheeze, smoking) and a gradual occupational onset, NOT recent surgery. Clues 4 and 5 are clinically sound and specific, but the early clues fail to create a coherent, progressive differential. Recommend rewriting Clue 2 to establish different functional limitations (e.g., exercise intolerance), and Clue 3 to establish relevant asthma risk factors and a proper symptom timeline.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographics (office worker, adult), primary complaint (lower back pain), and secondary symptom (shooting leg pain). Establishes broad differential for lumbar/lower limb pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional pattern: worse with flexion/prolonged sitting, better with extension/supine. This narrows to mechanical lumbar conditions but is already heavily implied by Clue 1's 'shooting pain down leg' (radicular pattern).
clues.rewrite: Worse with prolonged sitting and forward bending; improves with lying down. Unable to bend to pick up light objects.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism (lifting), onset timing (acute), and NEW discriminator: 'positive lower limb radiation below the knee.' This is crucial — it shifts from non-specific leg pain to radicular/dermatomal pattern, narrowing significantly toward nerve root compression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Three specific findings: SLR+, diminished ankle reflex, foot dorsiflexion weakness. These distinguish L5 root involvement from other levels and from non-neural mimics. Provides solid objective discrimination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI findings explicitly describe disc herniation with posterolateral location and nerve root compression. This is definitive and confirms the diagnosis completely.
clues.rewrite: MRI shows posterolateral disc protrusion at L5-S1 level with nerve root compression.

Lumbar Disc Herniation_151.notes: The puzzle is fundamentally sound — it follows a logical clinical funnel from symptom → function → mechanism/radiculopathy → neuro exam → imaging. However, Clue 2 is weak: the mechanical pattern (worse flexion, better extension) is already heavily implied by Clue 1's description of 'shooting pain down the leg,' which in an office worker context strongly suggests radicular pain. Clue 2 should add a distinct functional layer (e.g., specific activity limitations) that Clue 1 didn't establish. Clue 5 could be slightly more specific regarding level/root, but overall this is a well-structured puzzle that requires genuine clinical reasoning to solve.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes three cardinal symptoms: profound fatigue unrelieved by rest, myalgia, and cognitive dysfunction. Casts a reasonably wide net across systemic/neurological/psychiatric conditions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Introduces post-exertional malaise (PEM) as a specific functional pattern — disproportionate symptom worsening after minor activities. This is a key distinguishing feature of CFS and narrows the differential meaningfully.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Viral trigger (14 months ago), progressive worsening trajectory, and explicit exclusion of fibromyalgia and mood disorder. The post-viral timeline is clinically important and helps differentiate CFS from primary mood/psychiatric causes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Adds objective findings: reduced exercise tolerance, neuropsychological impairment, lymphadenopathy. However, these findings are relatively generic to CFS — they don't meaningfully distinguish CFS from other post-viral conditions, autoimmune diseases, or systemic infections at this stage.
clues.rewrite: Orthostatic intolerance on tilt-table testing, reduced cardiac output with minimal exertion, cervical lymphadenopathy, and absence of motor weakness or focal neurological signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Provides laboratory/biomarker evidence: low NK cells, elevated cytokines, inflammatory markers, and brain imaging changes. These are specific biological findings consistent with CFS pathophysiology.

Chronic Fatigue Syndrome_152.notes: The puzzle has a solid overall structure and follows the clinical funnel well. Clue 1 is appropriately broad, Clues 2–3 narrow effectively with PEM and post-viral history, and Clue 5 confirms with biomarkers. The main weakness is Clue 4 (Examination): it lists generic findings (exercise intolerance, cognitive impairment, lymphadenopathy) that could fit many systemic post-viral conditions, autoimmune diseases, or even severe depression. The clue should include more distinctive examination findings — specifically orthostatic intolerance and haemodynamic derangement with exertion, which are much more characteristic of CFS and would actively discriminate it from fibromyalgia, depression, or early autoimmune disease. As written, Clue 4 doesn't meaningfully narrow the remaining differential after Clue 3; it mostly elaborates on what is already implied.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes acute onset leg pain triggered by heavy lifting in a young worker. This is appropriately broad — leg pain post-lifting could suggest nerve root involvement, but doesn't specify mechanism or anatomical level.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Adds functional pattern: pain worsens with flexion/sitting and Valsalva, relieved by side-lying. This narrows from generic 'leg pain' to a movement-sensitive presentation consistent with nerve root compression or disc pathology. Rules out many acute injuries (e.g., simple hamstring strain).

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Adds temporal context: recurrent back pain without prior leg involvement suggests degenerative/structural predisposition. Smoking and sedentary lifestyle are risk factors for disc degeneration. NEW element: distinguishes this from first-time acute injury, suggesting chronic underlying pathology now manifesting with radiculopathy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Provides objective evidence of nerve root compression at a specific level (L5). SLR at 35° is highly sensitive for lower lumbar radiculopathy. Weak great toe extension and L5 sensory changes confirm L5 nerve root involvement. This narrows differential to L4/L5 or L5/S1 disc herniation, excluding central canal stenosis, piriformis syndrome, or upper lumbar involvement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Confirms disc herniation at L4/L5 compressing the left L5 nerve root — this is essentially definitive for the diagnosis. The posterolateral location and specific nerve root compression align perfectly with examination findings. Imaging confirms rather than discovers the diagnosis.

Lumbar Disc Herniation_153.notes: This is a well-constructed puzzle with excellent progressive reveal. Each clue adds distinct, clinically meaningful information. Clue 1 is appropriately broad (acute leg pain post-lifting). Clue 2 adds movement pattern (flexion-sensitive, Valsalva-positive). Clue 3 adds history and degenerative context. Clue 4 provides the key discriminator: specific neurological findings confirming L5 nerve root involvement at a particular spinal level. Clue 5 is confirmatory imaging. A competent clinician would be highly confident after Clue 4 and essentially certain after Clue 5. The differential is progressively narrowed at each stage without redundancy. No issues detected.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes knee pain with specific mechanism (dashboard/forced flexion) and swelling pattern. Broadly applies to multiple knee ligament injuries.
clues.rewrite: Adult patient with acute knee pain after forced flexion or direct anterior tibial blow; minimal to moderate swelling over initial hours.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations (kneeling, squatting, stairs) and notably LESS instability than ACL. This is valuable — differentiates from ACL.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism detail (high-energy, MVC/fall) and swelling timeline. However, mechanism is already mentioned in Clue 1; swelling was also mentioned in Clue 1. Minimal new information.
clues.rewrite: Injury from high-energy mechanism; patient often unaware of complete injury details. May report feeling or hearing a pop at time of injury, with rapid effusion development.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Posterior sag sign and positive posterior drawer at 90° are PCL-specific findings. Quadriceps strength assessment is relevant. This is genuinely discriminatory.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI findings directly confirm PCL disruption. Associated injuries (collateral/meniscal) add clinical context but are non-specific to PCL diagnosis.
clues.rewrite: MRI demonstrates PCL fiber disruption with increased T2 signal throughout ligament; proximal or distal avulsion may be present. Associated posterolateral corner injury or medial meniscal tear common.

Posterior Cruciate Ligament Tear_154.notes: Clue 2 is strong — it explicitly contrasts with ACL (less instability) and provides functional constraints that narrow the differential well. Clue 3 is the main weakness: mechanism and swelling are already established in Clue 1, so Clue 3 adds little new diagnostic value. Reframing Clue 3 around mechanism unawareness and pop sensation would provide fresh information. Clue 4 is appropriately specific (posterior sag, posterior drawer). Clue 5 could be more specific about PCL fiber signal changes rather than non-specific 'signal changes'. Overall progressive reveal is reasonable but Clue 3 breaks the funnel principle.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age (older patient) + symptom (progressive difficulty reading small print). Establishes presbyopia as leading differential.
clues.rewrite: Older patient reports gradual-onset difficulty focusing on near objects.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: requires correction for near work. Confirms near-vision problem but adds minimal new discrimination.
clues.rewrite: Patient reports difficulty maintaining clear focus during sustained reading; symptoms worsen with dim lighting and small print sizes.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Timeline (18 months), age-correlation, exclusion of diabetes/hypertension/ocular surgery. Excludes secondary causes but mostly rephrases Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: Onset coincided with patient's mid-50s; she has no prior eye disease, trauma, or systemic comorbidities. Reading glasses were never needed before this.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Reduced accommodation + normal retina. This is useful but 'reduced accommodation' is almost synonymous with presbyopia diagnosis already strongly suggested by Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: Near-point accommodation reduced to 33 cm (normal for age ~25 cm). Distance visual acuity normal. Pupillary reflexes intact; no visual field defect.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Refraction confirms presbyopia; normal age-related changes. This confirms the diagnosis but is somewhat redundant after Clue 3 (already named 'presbyopia').
clues.rewrite: Cycloplegic refraction shows hyperopic shift relative to patient's prior prescription from 5 years ago; lens power insufficient for near-distance focusing.

Vision Impairment - Age-Related_155.notes: This puzzle fails the progressive reveal principle catastrophically. Clue 1 essentially names the answer ('presbyopia') by combining 'older patient' + 'difficulty reading small print' — this is a textbook description. Clues 2–5 largely rephrase or elaborate on the same finding without adding genuine new discriminators. A player would guess presbyopia after Clue 1 alone. The puzzle needs a complete rewrite: Clue 1 should describe symptoms without age anchor; Clue 2 should narrow to near-vision; Clue 3 should add age/timeline/exclusions; Clue 4 should provide accommodation/refraction specifics; Clue 5 should confirm with objective refraction data. The current design does not teach differential diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Recurrent acute vertigo episodes with unilateral ear fullness, tinnitus, progressive course over 18 months, fluctuating hearing loss between attacks. Establishes vestibular + auditory involvement and chronicity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Severe functional impact during episodes (unable to ambulate, nausea/vomiting, visual disturbance). Environmental triggers (noise, crowding). Work capacity reduced due to unpredictability.
clues.rewrite: During acute episodes, she experiences severe nausea with vomiting and is unable to walk safely. She has noticed that exposure to loud noise and crowded environments tends to trigger or worsen symptom clusters. Her symptoms are unpredictable in timing.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Rules out head trauma and viral prodrome. No family history. Hypertension as comorbidity. Salt sensitivity and stress-related clustering noted.
clues.rewrite: Hypertension is managed on medication. She reports that high salt intake and periods of stress seem to cluster her symptom episodes. She denies recent head injury or upper respiratory infection. No known family history of similar vestibular or hearing disorders.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific objective signs during acute attack: horizontal nystagmus with rotatory component, Romberg instability, negative Dix-Hallpike (rules out BPPV), reduced caloric response unilaterally, audiometry pattern (low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss). These are strong discriminators for Ménière's vs other vestibular conditions.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI with gadolinium demonstrates endolymphatic hydrops (enlarged endolymphatic space in cochlea and vestibule) on affected side. Rules out retrocochlear mass or other structural pathology. This is confirmatory for Ménière's disease.
clues.rewrite: MRI of the temporal bones with high-resolution sequences shows asymmetric enlargement of the membranous labyrinth (endolymphatic hydrops) on the affected side. No mass at the cerebellopontine angle. No demyelinating lesions or other structural abnormalities.

Ménière's Disease_156.notes: The puzzle has a solid clinical narrative but suffers from redundancy between clues 1 and 2, and between clues 2 and 3. Clue 1 already establishes 'recurrent episodes with nausea/vomiting implications' (Complaint clue is detailed); Clue 2 then merely describes the functional consequence of those same episodes without adding mechanistic or timing information that wasn't already implied. Clue 3 adds useful history (hypertension, salt sensitivity, stress), but this feels like it should have been woven into Clue 1 or given more weight earlier. The examination findings (Clue 4) are excellent and properly discriminatory. Clue 5 is appropriate but uses the term 'endolymphatic hydrops' which is essentially synonymous with Ménière's disease and may be too revealing. Rewrites should: (1) streamline Clue 2 to focus on FUNCTIONAL PATTERN and environmental sensitivity WITHOUT restating the nausea/vomiting already in Clue 1; (2) enhance Clue 3 to emphasize modifiable risk factors (salt, stress) more explicitly; (3) soften Clue 5 language to describe the hydrops finding without naming the mechanism quite so directly.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age (68), progressive bone pain in low back and hip over 2 years, impact on mobility, weight-bearing exacerbation. Establishes age group and anatomical region (spine/hip) with slow progression.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional decline pattern (2 miles → 200 meters), gait abnormality (widened gait), rest-responsive but rapid recurrence of pain. Adds functional severity and gait characteristics not implied by complaint alone.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Family history of Paget's disease (strongest clue), gradual increase in shoe/hat size over decade (pathognomonic for bone remodeling), elevated alkaline phosphatase and urinary hydroxyproline (biochemical markers of high bone turnover). This significantly narrows differential.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Localized warmth over affected bones (increased vascularity specific to Paget's), percussion tenderness, palpable bone deformity (anterior bowing of femur), mild hip flexor weakness. These add specific palpation findings and deformity confirmation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Mixed lytic-sclerotic pattern, coarse trabecular architecture, cortical thickening, increased bone density, high radionuclide uptake in multi-site distribution. Imaging is pathognomonic for Paget's disease and confirms diagnosis.

Paget's Disease of Bone_157.notes: This puzzle demonstrates excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 is appropriately broad (could include osteoarthritis, metastatic disease, multiple myeloma, ankylosing spondylitis). Clue 2 adds functional/gait specificity without naming diagnosis. Clue 3 is the critical narrower—family history + shoe/hat size increase + specific biochemical markers (alkaline phosphatase, hydroxyproline) make Paget's disease highly likely. Clue 4 provides examination findings consistent with Paget's (bone warmth, deformity, vascularity signs) that distinguish from other conditions. Clue 5 confirms with pathognomonic imaging. Each clue meaningfully reduces the differential; no redundancy or information placed in wrong category. Clinical reasoning funnel works well.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographics, symptom onset (3 months, gradual), symptom timeline (worse throughout day), and specific symptoms: eye strain, blurred vision, dry eye, frontal headaches
clues.rewrite: A 32-year-old office worker presents with eye discomfort and occasional blurred vision that has developed gradually over 3 months. She reports the symptoms worsen as her workday progresses and describes mild frontal headaches in the afternoons. Symptoms are worse on working days than weekends.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional correlation with screen time duration (2-3 hours threshold), relief with distance viewing and breaks, and symptom pattern (evening/weekend improvement)
clues.rewrite: Her symptoms are tightly correlated with continuous near-work activity, improving rapidly with breaks and distance viewing. She also reports significantly fewer symptoms during periods away from work, such as weekends and vacation. This pattern has been consistent and reproducible.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Occupational detail (desk work, dual monitors, suboptimal ergonomics), behavioral factor (reduced blinking during screen work), and medical history (recent normal eye exam, no systemic disease)
clues.rewrite: Transition to full-time remote work 3 months ago coincided with symptom onset. She has noticed reduced blinking when concentrating on her screens. Recent optometric assessment was normal, and she denies any systemic autoimmune or rheumatological history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific objective findings: near acuity reduction (20/40 at 40 cm vs 20/20 distance), reduced tear break-up time (4 sec vs normal >5), punctate epithelial erosions on staining, reduced accommodation amplitude (10 vs expected 12-14)

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Structural/morphological findings on OCT: decreased tear meniscus height, thin tear film layer, meibomian gland dropout on meibography. Importantly, excludes retinal pathology

Computer Vision Syndrome_158.notes: This puzzle has SEVERE issues with progressive revelation. Clues 1-3 are redundant with each other and collectively name or nearly name the answer ('eye strain + blurred vision + dry eye + screen time + reduced blinking + occupational near-work'). A clinician reading Clue 1 alone—especially the phrase 'eye strain...blurred vision...dry eye sensation...worsens throughout workday...transition to full-time remote work'—has essentially already identified Computer Vision Syndrome. Clues 2 and 3 then labor the same point with different wording (screen time makes it worse, breaks help, weekends are fine, she doesn't blink much, works at desk). The clue ordering is also problematic: Clue 1 bundles symptom onset, timeline, and strong occupational context that arguably belong in History (Clue 3). By the time a player reaches Clue 4 (examination findings), the diagnosis is nearly certain, so the examination data—while clinically correct—arrives too late to meaningfully narrow a differential that was already collapsed. Clues 4 and 5 are good individual clues but are wasted here because the answer was already obvious. To salvage this puzzle: (1) Strip Clue 1 down to pure symptoms without timeline or context ('eye discomfort, blurred vision, mild headaches'); (2) Move occupational/temporal context to Clue 3; (3) Use Clue 4 examination findings to differentiate Computer Vision Syndrome from other causes of blurred vision/eye discomfort (dry eye syndrome, presbyopia, refractive error, accommodation spasm).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, occupation (desk work), timeline (8 months gradual onset), symptom character (central LBP + cervical tightness, no trauma). Establishes broad musculoskeletal complaint without naming mechanism.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional pattern (worse sitting >2hrs, better with movement/stretching/posture correction). Morning stiffness resolving with activity. Absence of radicular features and movement-specific aggravation. Narrows to postural/muscular vs neurogenic/mechanical.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Workstation ergonomics (low monitor = forward head posture mechanism). High daily volume (8-10 hrs). Rules out trauma, degenerative disease, neurological involvement. Confirms occupational postural etiology.
clues.rewrite: Patient works 8-10 hours daily at a desk with minimal breaks. Recent ergonomic assessment identified workplace risk factors contributing to sustained postural deviation. No history of significant spinal trauma, prior disc pathology, or neurological symptoms. General health unremarkable.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective postural findings (forward head posture 3cm, thoracic kyphosis, anterior pelvic tilt). ROM pattern (full but tight, reversal of lumbar curve on flexion without radiculopathy). Muscle palpation findings (sustained tension paraspinals). Negative neurological screens (SLR 85°, slump negative). Confirms postural/muscular diagnosis vs nerve root involvement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging findings (normal disc heights, no degeneration, preserved curves, no neural compression, normal signal intensity, normal facet joints). Rules out structural/degenerative pathology.
clues.rewrite: Plain radiographs show preserved spinal alignment and disc heights without degenerative changes. MRI demonstrates normal disc signal, intact facet joints, normal canal diameter, and no neural compression. Imaging is structurally normal.

Postural Syndrome - Work-Related_159.notes: The puzzle has a fundamental progressive reveal problem: Clue 3 explicitly names the mechanism ('forward head posture from low monitor') and directly identifies the working diagnosis by describing the ergonomic cause. This is too early and removes the fun of clinical reasoning. Clue 2 already provided strong functional clues (posture-responsive, activity-responsive, no radiculopathy) that narrow the differential significantly. Clue 3 should omit the specific ergonomic finding (that belongs in clinical reasoning once diagnosis is narrowed) and instead focus on occupational volume/timeline context. Clue 5 is somewhat redundant — once Clues 2-4 establish a postural muscular syndrome with normal neuro signs, normal imaging becomes somewhat predictable. Consider whether Clue 5 needs to be more specific (e.g., spinal imaging shows loss of normal cervical lordosis or flattened lumbar curve, which would be more diagnostically meaningful than just 'normal'). Overall: solid clues 1, 2, 4; major rewrite needed for Clue 3; minor polish for Clue 5.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 27-year-old overhead throwing athlete with progressive medial elbow pain over 6 weeks, activity-related, no acute trauma. Establishes age, mechanism (throwing), body region (elbow), onset character (gradual worsening).

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Pain specifically triggered by throwing phases (late cocking/deceleration) with decreased velocity and control; non-throwing activities pain-free. Adds functional specificity and narrows to throwing-related elbow pathology, distinguishes from general upper limb or non-activity-dependent conditions.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Recent training load increase without progression; insidious onset without acute event; played through mild pain. Adds mechanism of overuse/repetitive strain and timeline details. Eliminates acute traumatic etiologies and suggests cumulative microtrauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Point tenderness over medial collateral ligament; moving valgus stress test positive; milking maneuver positive; increased valgus laxity with soft end-feel; reduced grip strength; no neurological signs. Provides specific ligamentous findings and differentiates from nerve compression or other medial elbow pathology (e.g., flexor-pronator strain alone, medial epicondylitis without ligamentous involvement).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI shows partial thickness tear of anterior bundle UCL with edema; high signal intensity indicates ligamentous disruption; small effusion; flexor-pronator edema; no osteochondral lesion. Confirms ligamentous tear (vs. sprain or pure muscle strain) and specifies location/severity. Essentially definitive for UCL injury.

Ulnar Collateral Ligament Injury_160.notes: Excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 casts appropriate net (medial elbow pain in throwing athlete — could be UCL, flexor-pronator strain, medial epicondylitis, nerve compression). Clue 2 adds functional specificity to throwing mechanics, narrowing differential toward ligamentous or muscle-tendon pathology of medial elbow. Clue 3 adds overuse mechanism, making acute trauma unlikely and supporting chronic ligamentous injury. Clue 4 provides specific examination findings (valgus laxity, special tests) that strongly differentiate UCL injury from other medial elbow conditions. Clue 5 is confirmatory with MRI showing specific tear. Each clue meaningfully narrows the differential without redundancy. No naming of answer or anatomical giveaways in early clues. Strong puzzle.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 72-year-old woman, 8 weeks post-THR, groin and lateral hip pain, functional mobility loss
clues.rewrite: A 72-year-old woman reports persistent hip region pain and difficulty with basic mobility tasks including walking and stairs. Onset was gradual over recent weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Antalgic gait, hip flexion limitation >90°, hip abduction weakness, difficulty with ADLs (dressing, bathing, toileting)
clues.rewrite: Patient uses a cane and demonstrates significant weakness with hip abduction tasks. She struggles particularly with activities requiring hip flexion beyond 90 degrees or weight-bearing through the affected leg.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Anterolateral surgical approach, 8-week timeline post-op, completed standard protocol, comorbidities (HTN, DM2), denies instability symptoms
clues.rewrite: Patient underwent hip surgery via anterolateral approach 8 weeks ago for osteoarthritis. Completed standard rehabilitation protocol. No episodes of catching, locking, or sudden instability.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Hip flexion 3/5 weakness, hip abduction 2+/5 weakness, flexion ROM 85°, abduction ROM 25°, extension 15°, no instability on special tests, mild anterior swelling, sensation intact

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Cup positioning normal (45° abduction, 20° anteversion), femoral stem neutral alignment, no varus/valgus deformity, no early loosening, maintained joint space, CT confirms no malposition
clues.rewrite: Plain radiographs show appropriate component positioning with no signs of aseptic loosening, malalignment, or early failure. Imaging does not explain the ongoing weakness and functional loss.

Post-Total Hip Replacement_161.notes: Critical flaw: Clue 1 names the answer ('post-total hip replacement') and reveals the surgical procedure, approach, and diagnosis immediately. This eliminates the entire progressive reveal. Clues 1 and 2 are heavily redundant — both emphasize pain, mobility loss, and weakness without adding distinct information. The puzzle essentially collapses after Clue 1. For a post-surgical diagnosis, Clue 1 should describe only the patient's functional complaint (pain, weakness, stiffness with mobility tasks) without revealing that surgery was performed. The surgical history belongs in Clue 3. Clue 4 is well-structured and appropriate. Clue 5 is overly detailed but acceptable as a confirmatory imaging clue. Rewrite needed for Clues 1 and 2 to preserve clinical uncertainty and create a genuine diagnostic funnel.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, gender, mechanism (fall), chronicity (2-year history with acute worsening), symptom location (lateral shoulder), and functional impact (difficulty overhead). Sets up broad differential for shoulder pain post-trauma in middle-aged patient.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations (overhead reaching, self-care, sleep) and movement-specific weakness pattern (active ROM much worse than passive). The passive/active discrepancy and weakness-dominant picture (not just pain) begins to narrow toward rotator cuff or neurological pathology.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Chronic overuse background (carpenter, 2 years intermittent pain) clarifies this is not acute-only injury but chronic pathology acutely worsened. Rules out acute traumatic tear in young patient; confirms degenerative/chronic load context. No prior surgery or significant trauma history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Highly specific muscle-level deficits. Marked external rotation weakness (2/5), positive empty can test (2/5 supraspinatus strength), positive drop arm test, and visible infraspinatus atrophy are near-pathognomonic for supraspinatus-infraspinatus rotator cuff tear. Negative apprehension/relocation rules out instability. This clue essentially clinches the diagnosis—rotator cuff tear is now the only reasonable answer.
clues.rewrite: On examination, abduction is weak and painful at 3/5. External rotation strength is reduced compared to internal rotation (which is relatively preserved). Drop arm test cannot be completed due to weakness. Supraspinatus tenderness is present on palpation. Apprehension test is negative.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Confirms full-thickness supraspinatus tear with specific dimensions, retraction, and fatty infiltration stage. Acromion anatomy adds structural context. This clue provides imaging confirmation but diagnosis is already essentially certain from Clue 4.

Rotator Cuff Tear_162.notes: The puzzle has a significant pacing problem: Clue 4 is far too specific and essentially names the answer (drop arm test + empty can test weakness + infraspinatus atrophy = rotator cuff tear). By the time a player reaches Clue 4, there should still be 2–3 reasonable candidates on the differential—this clue eliminates all alternatives at once. Clue 4 needs substantial weakening to preserve game difficulty. The rewritten Clue 4 removes the pathognomonic combination (drop arm + empty can + atrophy) and focuses only on ROM/strength patterns and tenderness—still pointing toward rotator cuff but not conclusive. Clue 5 then provides definitive imaging. Progressive reveal is now preserved: Clues 1–3 narrow to shoulder/overuse context → Clue 4 suggests rotator cuff (but could still be advanced tendinopathy or adhesive capsulitis) → Clue 5 confirms tear.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 64-year-old male with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presenting with progressive bilateral foot pain, burning sensation, and numbness over 18 months
clues.rewrite: A 64-year-old man with a 12-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with progressive bilateral lower limb pain and sensory loss over 18 months. He describes a burning sensation in his feet and difficulty with fine sensory perception.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations: reduced walking tolerance, balance impairment in darkness, sleep disruption from nighttime pain, avoidance of barefoot walking, reduced standing tolerance (15-20 min)

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Long disease duration (12 years diabetes), poor glycemic control, hypertension, elevated cholesterol, normal B12, no trauma/prior nerve injury, no chemotherapy exposure
clues.rewrite: Poor medication adherence has resulted in suboptimal glycemic control (HbA1c 9.2%) throughout his diabetes course. Associated comorbidities include hypertension and hyperlipidaemia. Notably, B12 level is normal and there is no history of chemotherapy, malignancy, or significant trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective neurological findings: diminished ankle reflexes bilaterally (1+/4), markedly reduced vibratory sense (2/8 sec), impaired proprioception, diminished two-point discrimination (>1 cm), reduced sensation to 5.07 monofilament distally, distal lower limb weakness 4+/5 in dorsiflexors

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: NCS/EMG demonstrates reduced conduction velocities and amplitudes in peroneal and tibial nerves with prolonged distal latencies, abnormal/absent F-waves, and denervation potentials in intrinsic foot muscles. Normal lumbar spine MRI excludes central causes.

Peripheral Neuropathy_163.notes: The puzzle has a critical flaw in Clue 1: it essentially names the diagnosis by combining 'poorly controlled type 2 diabetes' with 'progressive bilateral foot pain and burning' over 18 months — this is diagnostic of diabetic peripheral neuropathy and eliminates 95% of the differential before any active guessing. Clue 2 is strong and adds genuine functional context.
