Clue 3 is redundant; it merely elaborates on risk factors already established in Clue 1 (diabetes duration, control, comorbidities) without narrowing the differential further or introducing new pathophysiological insights. Clues 4 and 5 are appropriately specific but arrive too late — the diagnosis is already obvious. Rewrite Clue 1 to be more generic (age, symptom character, timeline, without naming diabetes as the cause) and restructure Clue 3 to add comorbidities or lab findings that meaningfully differentiate from other neuropathies.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes persistent fatigue (4 years), lack of relief with rest, functional limitation, and post-exertional pattern. Very specific to CFS.
clues.rewrite: A 44-year-old woman reports persistent fatigue lasting 4 years that significantly impairs her ability to work and engage in daily activities. She notes that physical exertion, even mild, triggers worsening of symptoms that can last for days.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Describes post-exertional malaise (PEM) and functional limitation (housebound, mostly bedbound). This is the hallmark feature of CFS.
clues.rewrite: She is now largely housebound and unable to sustain any consistent activity level. Even brief periods of light activity (walking to the mailbox, preparing a meal) result in disproportionate exhaustion lasting 24-48 hours, forcing her to rest significantly afterward.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Adds timeline (post-viral onset 2 months before presentation), cognitive symptoms, excludes psychiatric illness, and shows normal standard labs. Narrows away obvious organic causes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Excludes objective neurological deficits, myopathy, orthostatic intolerance, and major CNS dysfunction. Cognitive testing shows mild impairment in attention/processing—supports functional disturbance without structural disease.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Excludes demyelinating disease, significant metabolic/endocrine derangement, sleep apnea, and structural CNS pathology. Confirms diagnosis of exclusion.
clues.rewrite: MRI brain is normal with no white matter abnormalities or structural lesions. Metabolic panel, CBC, thyroid, and autoimmune markers (ANA, RF, anti-CCP) are all normal. Sleep study demonstrates non-restorative sleep architecture without obstructive apnea.

Chronic Fatigue Syndrome_164.notes: MAJOR PROBLEM: Clue 1 is far too specific and essentially names the diagnosis ('post-exertional malaise' and 'profound persistent fatigue not relieved by rest' in a 4-year duration are nearly pathognomonic for CFS). A player reading only Clue 1 would likely guess CFS immediately or within 1-2 attempts. Clue 2 then largely repeats the same hallmark feature (PEM) without adding substantially new information—both emphasize the disproportionate symptom flare after activity. The differential should still be broad after Clue 1 (e.g., depression, hypothyroidism, fibromyalgia, malignancy, persistent viral syndrome, CNS lesion, sleep disorder). Clues 3-5 function well as a 'ruling out' sequence, but the game is essentially solved by Clue 1. The progressive reveal principle is broken in the first two clues.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, occupation, onset pattern (insidious, 3 months, no trauma), primary symptom (deep groin pain), and provocative feature (clicking). Establishes broad differential for groin pain in active adult.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional pattern that narrows to hip joint pathology: flexion >90° aggravates; cutting/pivoting (sport-specific) aggravate; straight-line activities tolerate. This distinguishes hip joint from pure muscle/nerve injury and localizes to intra-articular or FAI-spectrum problem.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Training load escalation over 6 months (NEW information: repetitive microtrauma context rather than acute event). Explicitly rules out prior FAI symptoms and osteoarthritis risk. No surgical history. This refines the mechanism toward repetitive stress injury in young fit athlete.
clues.rewrite: He has no prior hip injuries or surgery. Training volume increased markedly 6 months ago with shorter recovery between matches. Denies any family history of joint disease. Generally fit with no other musculoskeletal complaints. No previous groin or hip symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: High-value discriminators: FADIR+, McCarthy+ (specifically reproduces clicking—cardinal labral sign), log-roll negative (rules out intra-articular loose body/severe capsular), mild IR ROM loss. Neurological intact rules out nerve. This trio strongly suggests labral pathology over FAI or pure capsular.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Definitive: anterior-superior labral tear at 1-2 o'clock position, labral fraying and signal change, no effusion, intact cartilage, no FAI morphology, no osteonecrosis. This confirms labral tear diagnosis and excludes mimics (FAI, OCD, AVN, osteoarthritis).

Hip Labral Tear_165.notes: Progressive reveal is strong overall. Clue 1 casts wide net (groin pain, many differentials possible). Clue 2 narrows to hip joint/FAI-spectrum (functional pattern). Clue 3 refines mechanism (repetitive stress, young athlete) but has slight overlap with Clue 1's onset narrative—rewrite removes the 'no prior FAI' redundancy and focuses purely on training load and risk factors. Clue 4 is excellent: McCarthy test + FADIR + normal log-roll is the key discriminator for labral tear vs FAI. Clue 5 confirms with imaging specificity. By Clue 3, a skilled clinician would reasonably narrow to labral tear vs FAI; Clue 4 differentiates; Clue 5 confirms. The puzzle works well.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 35-year-old woman with 6-month progressive weakness in left arm/leg post-viral, with coordination difficulty, tremor, and psychological distress. Establishes timeline, laterality, and hint of non-organic features.
clues.rewrite: A 35-year-old woman reports progressive weakness and clumsiness affecting her left arm and leg over the past 6 months. Onset followed a minor respiratory infection. She describes episodes of tremor and difficulty with fine motor control. She is significantly distressed about her symptoms and reports high levels of anxiety.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional inconsistency explicitly described: can't do fine motor tasks but shows intact use during natural observation; variable gait; improvement with distraction; clear mismatch between reported and observed function.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Psychosocial stressors (relationship/work) temporally linked to symptom onset; personality profile (perfectionist, high-achiever); exclusion of familial neurological disease; multiple specialist visits without organic diagnosis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific non-anatomical weakness pattern (give-way, effort-dependent); positive Hoover sign; inconsistent Romberg; variable strength on retesting. These are objective signs that actively differentiate FND from organic neurological disease.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Complete structural/physiological normality: normal brain MRI (no lesions, demyelination, infarct), normal cord signal, normal NCS/EMG. Definitively excludes organic neurological pathology (MS, stroke, motor neuron disease, neuropathy).

Functional Neurological Disorder_166.notes: Clue 1 is the main problem: it explicitly states 'psychological distress' and 'considerable psychological distress related to her symptoms' in the complaint itself, which is a major giveaway for FND and significantly narrows the differential on first guess. The phrase 'following a minor viral illness' is clinically reasonable, but combined with the psychology language, it essentially names the answer. Rewording to focus on the motor/sensory complaint and anxiety as independent symptoms (rather than linked as cause/effect) would preserve the clinical picture while keeping the differential broader. Clues 2–5 form an excellent funnel: Clue 2 adds functional inconsistency (non-organic pattern), Clue 3 adds psychosocial context and personality factors, Clue 4 adds non-anatomical examination signs, Clue 5 excludes all organic disease. After Clue 1 rewrite, this would be a solid puzzle.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes forefoot pain, third-fourth web space location, burning/sharp quality with paresthesias, 8-month progressive course, and footwear relationship. Casts wide net: differential includes Morton's neuroma, metatarsalgia, interdigital nerve compression, stress fracture, capsulitis.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: NEW: Specifies functional limitation pattern (heels/tight shoes worse, loose shoes/elevation better; 30-min walking threshold). This is distinct from complaint and narrows toward biomechanical/compressive forefoot pathology. Confirms reversibility with footwear change, reducing likelihood of structural damage or inflammatory arthropathy.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: NEW: Occupational history (prolonged standing, 25 years hairdresser), absence of trauma/prior surgery, exclusion of diabetes and inflammatory disease. This strengthens compressive/mechanical etiology and rules out secondary causes. However, these details are somewhat supportive rather than highly discriminatory at this stage.
clues.rewrite: Worked as hairdresser for 25 years with prolonged standing. No prior foot trauma, surgery, or orthopedic intervention. No diabetes, inflammatory arthropathy, or peripheral neuropathy. Pain progressive despite conservative footwear modifications over 8 months, suggesting mechanical compression not responding to load reduction alone.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: NEW and CRITICAL: Mulder's click is pathognomonic for Morton's neuroma—this is the single most discriminating finding. Positive Tinel's and sensory loss in web space confirm nerve involvement. Tenderness in third web space localizes lesion. Absence of deformities/calluses excludes structural foot deformities. This clue should make the diagnosis nearly certain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: NEW: Imaging confirms structural diagnosis—8 mm hypoechoic nodular mass in third intermetatarsal space on ultrasound, MRI T2-hyperintense fusiform mass with perineural fibrosis. Imaging is confirmatory and eliminates mimics. Appropriate as final clue.

Morton's Neuroma_167.notes: Overall progressive reveal is solid. Clue 1 is appropriately broad (forefoot pain with paresthesias could be several diagnoses). Clue 2 adds functional pattern (footwear dependency, activity limitation). Clue 3 provides occupational/exclusionary history. Clue 4 is the key discriminator with Mulder's click—this is genuinely pathognomonic and moves confidence from ~40% to ~95%. Clue 5 confirms with imaging. The sequence follows good clinical reasoning. Minor suggestion: Clue 3 could be slightly tightened to avoid feeling redundant with earlier clues (footwear modification is mentioned in both Clue 1 and 2)—rewrite focuses on what's mechanistically new rather than repeating adjustment details.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes symptom pattern (gradual onset, unilateral hip/buttock pain radiating posterolateral thigh), timeline (4 months), and functional impact (sitting difficulty). Broad enough to encompass hip pathology, sacroiliac, lumbar radiculopathy, and other posterior hip conditions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Identifies movement pattern discriminator: sitting and hip IR aggravate, but walking and ER ease symptoms. This meaningfully narrows from broad hip pathology to specifically posterior hip/buttock conditions exacerbated by piriformis engagement. Eliminates anterior hip OA, labral pathology, and sacroiliac dysfunction (which would not typically improve with ER).

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Sedentary lifestyle, no trauma, no prior hip/LB pathology, negative red flags (bowel/bladder, weight loss). This contextualizes presentation as acquired muscular/nerve compression rather than structural or systemic. Eliminates malignancy, inflammatory arthropathy, and radiculopathy from disc herniation. However, the statement 'no previous hip or lower back pathology' is somewhat redundant with the gradual onset in Clue 1 — a patient with prior pathology would likely have been mentioned in Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: No recent trauma or falls reported. The patient has a sedentary lifestyle with minimal physical activity and denies regular stretching or conditioning. No bowel/bladder dysfunction or weight loss reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: FAIR and Freiberg's tests positive (both load piriformis and test for sciatic compression); weak hip ER; tenderness at greater sciatic notch; negative SLR and Kernig's (rules out significant nerve root tension from central disc herniation). This directly discriminates piriformis syndrome from lumbar radiculopathy and sacroiliac dysfunction. The specific test results are the key differentiator at this stage.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Rules out structural hip pathology (OA, labral tear, fracture), lumbar pathology (disc herniation, facet arthrosis), and confirms the suspected diagnosis with piriformis hypertrophy, signal changes, and sciatic nerve compression. This is appropriately specific and near-definitive for piriformis syndrome.

Piriformis Syndrome_168.notes: This is a well-constructed puzzle with good progressive narrowing. Clue 1 establishes a broad posterolateral hip/thigh pain pattern plausible for 8-10 conditions. Clue 2 narrows meaningfully with movement-pattern specificity (sitting/IR vs. walking/ER). Clue 3 adds history and red-flag screening, with only minor redundancy in the phrasing about 'no prior pathology' (could be tightened). Clue 4 provides the crucial examination findings that differentiate piriformis syndrome from radiculopathy and SI dysfunction. Clue 5 confirms with imaging. The differential at each stage feels realistic: after Clue 1, a clinician might consider hip OA, labral tear, SI dysfunction, L5 radiculopathy, or piriformis syndrome; after Clue 2, posterior hip/piriformis-related conditions rise; after Clue 3, systemic and structural causes are largely ruled out; after Clue 4, piriformis syndrome becomes the leading diagnosis; after Clue 5, it is confirmed. Minor suggestion: tighten Clue 3 to avoid the redundant phrasing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, chronicity (18 months progressive), bilateral lower back/buttock pain with radiation into both legs triggered by walking. Establishes age-appropriate presentation for degenerative lumbar pathology.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Distance-dependent claudication pattern (>100m triggers pain/numbness), rapid resolution with sitting or forward bending, improved with spinal flexion (trolley-leaning). This is the KEY finding that differentiates spinal stenosis from other lumbar conditions.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Long-standing degenerative history (since 1990s), no acute trauma, negative red flags (no cauda equina signs: bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, weakness). Eliminates acute disc herniation and serious pathology; supports chronic degenerative process.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Diminished reflexes (1+ bilaterally), reduced sensation L5/S1 bilaterally, full painless SLR, postural findings. These objective neurological signs confirm multilevel nerve root irritation consistent with stenosis; full SLR rules out significant single-level disc herniation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Structural confirmation: multilevel degenerative changes, central stenosis L4-L5, ligamentum flavum hypertrophy, disc bulging, facet arthrosis, severely narrowed canal. Directly correlates imaging pathology to clinical presentation.

Lumbar Spinal Stenosis_169.notes: This is a well-constructed puzzle with strong progressive narrowing. Clue 1 casts a wide net (could be several lumbar conditions). Clue 2 adds the critical functional finding—claudication with flexion relief—which is nearly pathognomonic for stenosis and dramatically narrows the differential. Clue 3 adds degenerative history and rules out red flags. Clue 4 provides objective confirmation of bilateral nerve root involvement. Clue 5 seals the diagnosis with imaging. The clinical story is coherent and each clue adds meaningfully distinct information. No rewrites needed.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, gender, location (pelvic/perineal), duration (3 years), character (constant but fluctuating), impact on quality of life/sexual function/work. Establishes broad complaint but too specific about diagnosis-revealing features.
clues.rewrite: A 48-year-old woman with persistent pelvic pain over 3 years. Pain is constant but variable in intensity, worsening with her menstrual cycle. Significant impact on daily activities and quality of life.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations specific to prolonged sitting, sexual activity, pelvic floor contraction; relief with position changes and heat. Narrows to pelvic/lower abdominal region but overlaps significantly with Clue 1's already-stated impact.
clues.rewrite: Pain is worse with prolonged sitting and sexual intercourse. Patient improves with position changes, lying down, and heat. Has significantly modified activities to avoid triggers.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Negative traumatic history, prior UTIs and endometriosis, extensive negative workup (imaging normal, gynecology unremarkable), negative systemic findings. This is appropriate for position 3 — clarifies that organic pathology has been ruled out and establishes chronicity of presentation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Palpable pelvic floor guarding and tenderness (left-sided), pain with pelvic floor and obturator internus palpation, limited hip internal rotation, normal SLR and general hip ROM. Discriminates myofascial/neuromuscular pelvic floor involvement from other causes. Appropriate specificity for position 4.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound and MRI negative for ovarian, uterine, bladder, and bowel pathology. Confirms absence of structural disease. Appropriate confirmation clue for position 5.

Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome_170.notes: The main weakness is Clue 1, which is overly specific and diagnosis-revealing for an opening clue. The phrase 'persistent pelvic pain and deep perineal discomfort' combined with 'significantly impacted her quality of life, sexual function, and work productivity' and the specific menstrual cycle pattern are collectively too narrow — they point heavily toward chronic pelvic pain syndrome before the player has made any guesses. Clue 1 should be broader (remove sexual dysfunction detail, remove menstrual cycle specificity, simplify the complaint). Clue 2 partially overlaps Clue 1's description of functional impact (quality of life, sexual function already mentioned) — rewrite to focus more purely on the activity-specific triggers and improvements. Clues 3, 4, and 5 are well-structured and show good progressive narrowing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Unilateral neck pain with occipital headaches, chronic (6 months), aggravated by computer work, non-radiating. Establishes body region (cervical spine/occipital) and rules out radiculopathy.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific movement patterns that provoke pain (extension, rotation, side-bending) and relieve it (position changes, heat). Afternoon fluctuation pattern. Narrows to mechanical/postural dysfunction affecting multi-planar cervical movement.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Gradual onset tied to ergonomics (not trauma), no radiculopathy symptoms (no radiation/tingling/weakness), sleep disruption with specific positional trigger. Eliminates acute injury, nerve compression, and systemic causes. Refines to chronic mechanical/degenerative process.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Ipsilateral ROM restriction (rotation), pain on contralateral movement (right lateral flexion—unusual finding), facet joint palpation tenderness at C4-C5 specifically, normal neuro (rules out myelopathy/radiculopathy). This is the critical discriminator pointing to facet joint involvement.
clues.rewrite: On examination: right cervical rotation restricted to 60 degrees (left side 80+ degrees). Right lateral flexion reproduces localized neck pain at the C4-C5 level. Palpation directly over the right cervical facet joints at C4-C5 elicits marked tenderness. Upper extremity strength, reflexes, and sensation are intact in all dermatomes; no findings of nerve root compression.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Structural confirmation: facet joint arthrosis and osteophytes at C4-C5 on the right, facet capsular thickening. Absence of canal stenosis or nerve root compression confirms mechanical facet pathology, not neuropathy. Definitively confirms cervical facet joint syndrome.

Cervical Facet Joint Syndrome_171.notes: Excellent progressive reveal structure. Clue 1 casts a wide net (neck pain + headaches, could be facet, discogenic, myofascial, migraine-related, etc.). Clue 2 narrows via movement patterns (mechanical derangement). Clue 3 eliminates trauma/radiculopathy/systemic causes. Clue 4 directly points to facet joint involvement via palpation tenderness and movement provocation pattern. Clue 5 confirms with imaging. The only minor note: Clue 4 phrasing could be slightly clearer to emphasize the facet palpation finding as the key discriminator. The puzzle is well-constructed and follows the clinical funnel principle effectively.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 42-year-old sedentary office worker with progressive lower back pain over 2 years, worsened by prolonged sitting
clues.rewrite: A 42-year-old office worker presents with progressive lower back pain over 2 years. Pain is localized to the lower lumbar and sacroiliac region, worsened by prolonged sitting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitation: pain with sitting >30 min, difficulty rising from chair, limited forward bending; postural observation includes increased lordosis and anterior pelvic tilt
clues.rewrite: Patient reports pain with sitting for more than 30 minutes and difficulty rising from a chair. Pain is relieved by lying down and walking. Forward bending is limited and uncomfortable.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: No trauma; sedentary job with minimal exercise; morning tightness in hip flexors and lower back; no radicular or systemic symptoms
clues.rewrite: No history of acute trauma or injury. Patient reports morning stiffness and tightness in hip flexors and lower back. Minimal exercise activity. No radicular symptoms or systemic features.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Thomas test positive (hip flexor tightness); lumbar extension limited and painful; poor core stability on prone bridge and plank; hip abductor weakness 4/5; palpable anterior pelvic tilt with exaggerated lordosis
clues.rewrite: Thomas test markedly positive. Hip abductor strength 4/5 bilaterally. Core stability poor: cannot maintain neutral spine in prone bridge. Lumbar extension painful and limited. Palpable anterior pelvic tilt with increased lumbar lordosis.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI rules out disc herniation, stenosis, neural compromise; mild degenerative changes insufficient to explain symptoms; confirms muscular/postural origin

Lower Crossed Syndrome_172.notes: MAJOR PROBLEMS: (1) Clue 1 explicitly names the postural deformity 'anterior pelvic tilt' — this is essentially naming the answer. Remove this. (2) Clues 1 and 2 heavily overlap: both mention sitting limitation, both mention postural deformity (lordosis, pelvic tilt). Clue 2 adds almost nothing new beyond restating that posture is wrong. (3) Clue 2 contains examination-level specificity ('marked postural dysfunction with increased lumbar lordosis') that belongs in Clue 4, not the activity section. (4) Clue 3 is generic and repetitive — 'tightness in hip flexors' is already heavily implied by the sitting pain and postural findings in earlier clues. (5) Clue 4 doesn't meaningfully distinguish between possible diagnoses at that point; it just confirms what was already obvious from posture/activity patterns. The differential narrowing effect is weak throughout. The puzzle reads as a description of ONE patient's presentation told six different ways, rather than a progressive clinical reasoning funnel.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, acute onset with audible pop during weightlifting, sharp anterior shoulder pain, immediate weakness and functional loss.
clues.rewrite: A 58-year-old recreational weightlifter presents with acute onset sharp anterior shoulder pain and an audible 'pop' during bench press. He reports immediate arm weakness and functional loss. Pain persists at rest and with movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific movement limitations: flexion and internal rotation weak against resistance; unable to lift or carry heavy loads; pain at rest worsened by active flexion and horizontal adduction.
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot lift overhead or carry loads. All shoulder movement, particularly reaching across the body, reproduces anterior pain. Symptoms present even at rest but substantially worse with active use.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: 15-year history of weightlifting with progressive intensity increase over 2 years; no prior shoulder injury; injury mechanism was loaded lifting movement; no systemic/neck symptoms.
clues.rewrite: Long history of heavy weightlifting with progressive training intensity over recent years. No prior shoulder pathology. Injury occurred during a heavy loaded lift. No neck involvement or constitutional symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Anterior tenderness, ecchymosis on proximal arm, weak flexion/supination (3+/5), positive Speed's and O'Brien's tests, mild subscapularis/infraspinatus weakness.
clues.rewrite: On palpation, anterior shoulder is tender with visible ecchymosis along proximal arm. Shoulder flexion strength is 3+/5. Speed's test and O'Brien's test reproduce anterior shoulder pain. Rotator cuff strength testing is intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI shows complete rupture of long head of biceps at intertubercular groove with 5 cm proximal retraction, hemorrhage and edema in groove, intact rotator cuff.

Long Head of Biceps Rupture_173.notes: The puzzle has significant redundancy in the functional/history/examination clues. Clue 1 already establishes acute onset, weakness, and difficulty moving the arm—so Clue 2's information about weak flexion/internal rotation against resistance is largely implied. Clue 3 adds mechanism (loaded lift) but this was already stated in Clue 1. Clue 4 contains specific special tests and strength grading that are appropriately detailed for examination, but the weakness described is not meaningfully new. The progressive reveal is compromised: by Clue 2, a clinician has already strongly suspected acute LHB rupture, making Clues 3 and 4 feel confirmatory rather than narrowing. Rewrite Clue 2 to focus on pain behavior and ROM restrictions (not just strength), and Clue 4 to emphasize the *ecchymosis pattern* and specific muscle sparing (e.g., infraspinatus intact = suggests LHB, not full rotator cuff tear). This creates better differentiation from other acute shoulder injuries like subacromial impingement or rotator cuff tears.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 54-year-old woman with 6-month progressive bilateral symmetrical distal lower extremity pain, numbness, burning; onset 4 weeks into docetaxel chemotherapy for breast cancer
clues.rewrite: A 54-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of progressive bilateral symmetrical distal lower extremity pain, numbness, and burning sensation starting in her toes. Symptoms began approximately 4 weeks into a chemotherapy regimen and have steadily worsened.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations: difficulty walking and balance (especially low-light), worsening with prolonged standing/walking, relieved by sitting/elevation; fine motor deficits (buttoning, writing)

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: 12 cycles of docetaxel chemotherapy with cumulative dose >360 mg/m²; excludes diabetes, prior chemotherapy, alcohol use, hereditary neuropathy, recent infections
clues.rewrite: Patient reports no diabetes, significant alcohol use, or family history of hereditary neuropathy. She denies recent infections or medication changes aside from chemotherapy. No prior chemotherapy exposure.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Bilateral stocking distribution sensory loss (fine touch, vibration to ankle), absent bilateral Achilles reflexes, preserved proprioception in feet, intact motor strength 5/5 throughout lower extremities
clues.rewrite: On examination, bilateral lower extremity sensation is diminished distally in a stocking distribution. Vibration sense is reduced to the ankle bilaterally. Achilles reflexes are absent bilaterally. Motor strength in lower extremities remains intact at 5/5.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: NCS/EMG: sensory-predominant axonal polyneuropathy with reduced bilateral sural and superficial peroneal nerve sensory action potentials, no demyelination. Lumbar MRI normal (excludes structural pathology).

Chemotherapy-Induced Peripheral Neuropat.notes: Major problems: Clue 1 explicitly names the chemotherapy agent (docetaxel) and cancer type, which essentially gives away CIPN immediately to anyone familiar with oncology — this violates the progressive reveal principle severely. Clue 3 adds almost nothing new beyond what Clue 1 already established (chemotherapy history); it should focus on excluding mimics (diabetes, hereditary causes, alcohol) more clearly. Clue 4 has examination-level detail (stocking distribution, absent reflexes) that could be moved slightly earlier or compressed. Overall coherence is good, but the differential is never meaningfully narrow — by Clue 1, an informed clinician already knows this is CIPN. Recommend: strip Clue 1 to remove drug name and cancer type; consolidate Clue 3 to focus purely on exclusions; tighten Clue 4 description.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, demographics, painless progressive weakness over 12 months, overhead functional loss, attribution to aging rather than acute injury. Establishes broad differential: neurogenic weakness, chronic rotator cuff pathology, cervical radiculopathy, motor neuron disease, myopathy.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional pattern: overhead activities failed, gravity-resisted abduction collapses, passive ROM preserved but active ROM lost, hand grip and elbow strength normal. NEW: This localizes to shoulder abductors/external rotators and distinguishes from cervical radiculopathy or generalized neuromuscular disease. Narrows to 5-6 shoulder-specific diagnoses (rotator cuff tear, suprascapular nerve compression, axillary nerve injury, C5 radiculopathy, shoulder arthropathy).

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Denies acute trauma (rules out acute tear from known mechanism), remote impingement 8 years ago (suggests chronic degenerative process), 15-year corticosteroid use (increases tear risk), no prior surgery. NEW: Duration (12 months + history of impingement) and chronic corticosteroid exposure strongly suggest degenerative full-thickness tear rather than acute traumatic tear or neurogenic compression. Differential now ~3 conditions: chronic rotator cuff tear, suprascapular nerve compression (less likely given full passive ROM), C5 radiculopathy (very unlikely given negative trauma and preserved hand grip).

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Drop arm test positive, severe abduction weakness (1/5), severe external rotation weakness (0/5), bilateral infraspinatus atrophy (right > left), supraspinatus atrophy present, preserved grip and elbow strength, pain-free passive ROM. NEW: The combination of marked bilateral atrophy and zero external rotation strength with preserved passive ROM is pathognomonic for chronic rotator cuff tear (not acute tear, not neurogenic compression which would show more focal single-muscle atrophy). Eliminates suprascapular nerve compression (would show infraspinatus atrophy only, less severe) and cervical radiculopathy (would show hand weakness). At this point, diagnosis is essentially confirmed before imaging.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Full-thickness tear supraspinatus + infraspinatus (2.8 x 1.5 cm), significant retraction, Goutallier grade 3-4 fatty infiltration, hooked acromion. NEW: Confirms full-thickness (not partial), defines tear size/location (both muscles), quantifies chronicity (grade 3-4), excludes arthropathy. Provides definitive anatomic confirmation and prognosis markers (fatty infiltration, hooked acromion).

Rotator Cuff Tear_175.notes: Excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 casts wide net (painless weakness in elderly = broad differential). Clue 2 narrows to shoulder region and movement pattern. Clue 3 adds chronicity, corticosteroid exposure, and mechanism (degenerative vs acute). Clue 4 provides the clinching objective findings (atrophy pattern, drop arm, preserved passive ROM) that differentiate rotator cuff tear from mimics. Clue 5 confirms with imaging specifics. Each clue genuinely eliminates possibilities. The puzzle respects the clinical reasoning funnel and provides meaningful new information at each step. Difficulty is appropriate: players could reasonably guess after Clue 3-4 but are guided progressively rather than given away early.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, mechanism (mechanical fall), onset (immediate), symptom (severe hip/groin pain), and inability to bear weight. Establishes elderly patient with acute hip pain post-trauma.
clues.rewrite: A 78-year-old woman with known osteoporosis fell at home and was unable to get up. She has severe pain in the hip and groin region with immediate onset. She was found on the floor 2 hours later.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: inability to weight-bear, unable to walk or transfer, pain with any hip movement, constant severe pain unrelieved by position or analgesia.
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot tolerate any weight on the left leg. Pain is constant and severe; even passive hip movements (flexion, rotation) are acutely painful. She is bed-bound and requires full assistance.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: 8-year history of osteoporosis with poor medication adherence, lives alone, baseline walker use, denies head injury or loss of consciousness.
clues.rewrite: Patient has had osteoporosis for 8 years but reports poor adherence to therapy. She lives independently and ambulated with a walker at baseline. No head injury, confusion, or loss of consciousness reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: left leg shortened and externally rotated, marked hip tenderness, severe pain on passive ROM (flexion/rotation), hip strength 0/5 due to pain, intact sensation and distal pulses. These are classic signs of hip fracture and differentiate from non-bony injury.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Confirms displaced intracapsular femoral neck fracture (subcapital), 90-degree angulation, osteopenia, soft tissue swelling. Specifies fracture type and location, confirming the diagnosis.

Hip Fracture_176.notes: The puzzle suffers from premature specificity in Clues 1 and 2. Clue 1 explicitly names 'severe hip pain,' 'mechanical fall,' 'unable to bear weight,' and even specifies the joint region (hip/groin), which is nearly pathognomonic for hip fracture in an elderly patient with osteoporosis. A player reading only Clue 1 would immediately think 'hip fracture' — this clue should cast a much wider net. Clue 2 adds functional detail but repeats the mechanism and pain severity already stated in Clue 1 without meaningfully narrowing from other hip pathologies (e.g., hip dislocation, severe labral tear, stress fracture). Clues 3–5 are well-structured and appropriately specific. The progressive reveal principle breaks down early; the game becomes too easy by Clue 2.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Asymptomatic spinal curvature detected on screening; physical sign of asymmetry (shoulder/breast height). Establishes no pain, no functional complaint, and screening detection context.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1. Clue 1 already stated 'no functional complaints' and 'no pain.' Clue 2 merely elaborates that sports and school are unaffected — this adds nothing about activity limitations that wasn't already established.
clues.rewrite: Patient denies any activity limitations, pain, or breathing difficulty even with volleyball. Truncal asymmetry is visually evident; patient and parents have noticed progressive postural changes over the past 6–12 months.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Rules out secondary scoliosis causes (trauma, infection, systemic illness, neurofibromatosis signs). Establishes no significant family history and that she is past PHV (menarche 2 years ago). This genuinely narrows the differential toward idiopathic.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Adam's forward bend test result (asymmetrical rib prominence, specific measurement of rib hump ~1.5 cm) and confirmation of shoulder height inequality. Skin examination rules out syndromic features (NF1, Marfan, etc.). This is the first objective, measurable clinical finding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Specific Cobb angle (35°), location (thoracic, apex T7–T8), and structural absence of bony abnormalities. Confirms structural scoliosis and rules out other spinal pathology. However, 35° is still in the idiopathic range, and imaging alone doesn't exclude other diagnoses (e.g., early Marfan, mild EDS) without clinical correlation already provided.
clues.rewrite: Spinal X-rays demonstrate a right thoracic curvature measuring 35 degrees Cobb angle with apex at T7–T8. Vertebral bodies are normal height, rotation is mild, and there is no kyphosis, spondylolisthesis, or dysplasia. No evidence of skeletal dysplasia, marfanoid features on imaging, or other structural abnormality.

Idiopathic Adolescent Scoliosis_177.notes: The main problem is Clue 2: it adds NO new information about functional activity limitations because Clue 1 already explicitly states 'no functional complaints' and 'no pain.' Clue 2 just restates and elaborates this in different words. The progressive reveal stalls here — a player learns nothing new that helps narrow the differential further. Suggest replacing Clue 2 with information about timeline of change or progressive postural asymmetry, which would genuinely narrow toward idiopathic vs. secondary causes. Clues 1, 3, 4, and 5 are clinically sound and build a logical hierarchy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, gender, symptom type (involuntary urine leakage), trigger pattern (exertional events), onset/timeline (5-year history, progressive), and functional impact (social/professional). Establishes broad differential for urinary incontinence.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: REDUNDANT WITH CLUE 1. Clue 1 already specified triggers: 'coughing, sneezing, and laughing.' Clue 2 merely elaborates the same triggers (coughing, sneezing, jumping, exercise) and adds that walking with full bladder may trigger leakage—which is IMPLIED by the exertional nature already established. The one genuinely new detail is the TREATMENT response (relief with pelvic floor contraction and training), but that belongs in Clue 3 (History/context) or should be saved for Clue 4 (Examination findings). The functional impact here does NOT narrow the differential beyond Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: Patient reports she can manage symptoms by contracting pelvic floor muscles proactively (before coughing/sneezing) and has noticed improvement with dedicated pelvic floor muscle training over the past 6 months. She has not sought other treatments (pessary, medications, or injections) to date.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Obstetric history (3 vaginal deliveries, largest baby 4.2 kg—risk factors for pelvic floor trauma), menopausal status (8 years post-menopause, no HRT—relevant to tissue elasticity and urethral support), and exclusion of confounders (no frequency/urgency/nocturia, no neuropathy, no diabetes, no culprit medications). This narrows differential: stress incontinence likely; urge or mixed incontinence less likely.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective pelvic floor weakness (3/5 Oxford, poor endurance/control), reproducible leakage on cough test with full bladder, normal neuro/abdominal exam, and negative urinalysis. These findings confirm intrinsic sphincter and support dysfunction; rule out UTI or neurological cause. Discriminates stress from urge incontinence.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound demonstrates urethral hypermobility with bladder neck funneling and proximal urethral descent (1.5 cm on Valsalva)—the anatomical hallmark of stress urinary incontinence. Normal PVR excludes retention. Absence of prolapse rules out alternative pelvic pathology. This is confirmatory and specific.

Stress Urinary Incontinence_178.notes: Overall structure is sound: Clue 1 casts a wide net (exertional incontinence in older woman), Clues 3–5 narrow progressively. However, Clue 2 is problematic. It repeats the trigger pattern from Clue 1 (coughing, sneezing, jumping, exercise) without adding new differential information—players learning Clue 2 discover nothing they couldn't infer from Clue 1 alone. The treatment response is interesting clinically but does not narrow diagnosis and should be repositioned or replaced. Rewrite Clue 2 to focus on functional capacity/limitations or contextual factors (e.g., impact of activity level, severity thresholds, management strategies tried) that differentiate stress from other incontinence types WITHOUT repeating triggers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes acute onset lower back + leg pain with radicular pattern (buttock to foot), associated neuro symptoms (numbness/tingling), mechanism (lifting injury), and timeline (2 weeks ago).
clues.rewrite: A 45-year-old manual laborer presents with acute onset lower back and leg pain following a lifting injury at work 2 weeks ago. Pain radiates down the right leg with associated numbness and tingling.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations: worse with flexion/sitting/lifting; better with extension/walking/lying. Describes pain character (dull back ache vs sharp radiating leg pain) and adaptive behaviors (lumbar belt, modified work).
clues.rewrite: Pain is significantly worse with forward bending and prolonged sitting. Walking and extension-based movements provide relief. Patient has stopped heavy lifting and adopted a lumbar support belt.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Progressive worsening over 3 weeks; prior episode 5 years ago (resolved conservatively); RED FLAG screening negative (no bowel/bladder, saddle anesthesia, progressive neuro decline); sleep disturbance due to leg pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings that LOCALIZE to nerve root level: SLR 40° (typical radiculopathy), ankle dorsiflexion weakness 4/5 (L5 myotome), diminished ankle reflex 1+ (S1 reflex arc), sensory deficit in L5 distribution. Normal knee reflex and hip flexor strength help exclude higher root involvement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Confirms structural pathology and EXPLAINS clinical findings: right-sided disc herniation at L5-S1 with extrusion, compression of right L5 nerve root with edema, axillary zone location (explains lateral distribution). Excludes cauda equina syndrome.

Lumbar Radiculopathy_179.notes: The puzzle is undermined by Clue 1 being TOO SPECIFIC and essentially naming the answer (mentions 'right leg pain radiating to foot with numbness and tingling in the same distribution' — a non-clinician would immediately think 'radiculopathy'). Clue 2 adds almost no new functional information beyond what Clue 1 already established; it mostly rephrases and elaborates the same pain pattern without narrowing the differential further. The player sees radiculopathy in Clue 1 and has little reason to guess anything else. Clues 3, 4, and 5 are solid and well-ordered, but they come too late to save the puzzle. Rewrite Clue 1 to be symptom-focused WITHOUT mentioning radiation pattern or neuro symptoms explicitly, and rewrite Clue 2 to focus on functional impact (what movement/position helps/hurts) as a distinct piece of new information.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, gender, activity context (recreational runner), onset timeline (6 weeks progressive), pain location (medial tibia), associated swelling and morning stiffness, functional impact (reduced running).
clues.rewrite: A 26-year-old recreational runner presents with progressive lower leg pain over 6 weeks that has forced her to reduce running frequency and distance. She reports morning stiffness and tightness in the lower leg that improves with activity, and localized swelling.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific aggravating factors (running, hard/uneven surfaces, impact activities), pain pattern during activity (first 10-15 min, improves mid-run, worsens post-activity), walking tolerance, activity modification (non-impact switching).

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Recent training load increase (50% over 8 weeks), shoe change timing (3 months ago), absence of acute trauma/prior injury history, absence of systemic symptoms, training frequency (4-5 days/week).

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Localization of tenderness (posteromedial tibia, middle third, 5 cm span), mild swelling and erythema, reproducibility on palpation, full painless ankle ROM, subtle pronation at foot. Distinguishes from compartment syndrome, fracture, and other lower leg pathologies.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI-specific findings (cortical stress reaction, periosteal reaction, bone marrow edema at posteromedial middle third tibia), associated soft tissue inflammation (tibialis posterior, soleus fascia), absence of fracture line, staging as early-stage stress injury. Definitively confirms stress reaction pattern.
clues.rewrite: MRI reveals cortical stress reaction at the posteromedial tibia in the middle third with periosteal reaction and bone marrow edema. Tibialis posterior and soleus fascia show inflammation. No fracture line is visible, consistent with early-stage stress injury rather than frank fracture or other pathology.

Medial Tibial Stress Syndrome_180.notes: MAIN ISSUE: Clue 1 explicitly names the medial tibia as the pain location ('Pain is localized to the medial tibia'), which is essentially the answer itself for MTSS. This removes the entire diagnostic challenge from the puzzle. The complaint should say 'anterior/lower leg pain' without specifying medial tibia. Clues 2–4 then progressively narrow to that location. Clue 5 is well-structured but slightly redundant after Clue 4 has already pinpointed the posteromedial tibia on examination. The progressive reveal principle is undermined by the early anatomical specificity in Clue 1.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, chronic productive cough, dyspnea on exertion, progressive symptoms, frequent infections, declining functional capacity over one year
clues.rewrite: A 16-year-old with a one-year history of progressive dyspnea on exertion and chronic productive cough. Recently experiencing fatigue and recurrent infections requiring treatment.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: exercise intolerance, fatigue with minimal activity, difficulty with stairs, school absenteeism due to cough, postural effect on sputum production (worse when recumbent)

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Key new information: diagnosed with genetic pulmonary condition in early childhood (narrows to inherited/congenital lung disease). Pancreatic insufficiency requiring enzyme supplementation is a major discriminator — this constellation (lung disease + pancreatic involvement) is pathognomonic.
clues.rewrite: Genetic pulmonary condition first diagnosed in early childhood. Multiple courses of antibiotics for recurrent respiratory infections. No pancreatic or gastrointestinal issues reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Bilateral crackles throughout lung fields, productive cough with thick discolored sputum, hypoxemia on exertion (88%) with borderline baseline (92%), digital clubbing

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: HRCT findings: hyperinflation, bronchial wall thickening, bronchiectasis (upper lobe predominant), bronchial secretion impaction, air trapping

Cystic Fibrosis_181.notes: MAJOR PROBLEM: Clue 3 (History) essentially names the answer by combining 'genetic pulmonary condition' with 'pancreatic insufficiency requiring enzyme supplementation.' This is pathognomonic for CF and dramatically over-constrains the puzzle. By Clue 3, any competent clinician would have CF as the only reasonable answer. Clue 1 is also too specific — chronic productive cough + dyspnea + frequent infections + functional decline in a 16-year-old is already highly suggestive of CF. The puzzle violates the progressive reveal principle: Clue 3 should narrow from a shortlist, not eliminate all alternatives. Recommend removing pancreatic insufficiency from Clue 3 and saving that discriminator for Clue 4 or 5 (e.g., as a clinical finding of malabsorption, or as imaging/labs showing pancreatic changes).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes age, lower extremity weakness and sensory loss, wheelchair use, and transfer difficulties
clues.rewrite: 8-year-old with chronic lower limb weakness and reduced sensation. Uses wheelchair for distance but family reports increasing difficulty with positional transfers and balance activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Adds bladder/bowel dysfunction and inability to manage stairs or uneven terrain
clues.rewrite: Patient manages self-care with assistance but has poor trunk control in sitting. Cannot participate in playground activities; bladder and bowel management requires scheduled catheterization and bowel routine.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Clarifies congenital origin, prenatal detection, neonatal surgery, tethered cord, hydrocephalus with VP shunt
clues.rewrite: Detected prenatally; emergency spinal closure within 48 hours of birth. Subsequent surgeries for progressive deformity. VP shunt placed for hydrocephalus. Multiple surgical episodes across childhood.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Provides motor grades (0-1/5), sensory level at umbilicus, palpable scar, visible spinal curvature

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI shows segmental cord abnormality with partial agenesis; X-ray confirms 45-degree scoliosis and lateral recess stenosis

Spina Bifida_182.notes: Critical failure in progressive reveal. Clue 1 explicitly states 'spinal cord defect' and 'loss of sensation below mid-back' — this is essentially naming the answer (spina bifida) for an 8-year-old with wheelchair use and sensory loss. Clue 3 then literally says 'congenital spinal cord defect identified prenatally' and 'tethered spinal cord' — making the diagnosis painfully obvious. By Clue 3, any physiotherapy student would have written 'spina bifida'. The differential should feel open through Clue 1 (could be cerebral palsy, spinal cord injury, muscular dystrophy, or many other causes of paraplegia in a child), narrow significantly by Clue 2-3 (congenital/developmental origin with cord involvement), and crystallise by Clue 4-5. Instead, the clues give away the pathology by Clue 1 and confirm it redundantly in Clues 2-3. The puzzle is unsalvageably easy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Progressive swelling of upper extremity post-cancer treatment with heaviness and tightness; establishes timeline (weeks), mechanism (cancer treatment), and body region (arm/hand). Broad enough to include multiple post-treatment complications.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact of swelling: fine motor impairment, inability to wear jewelry, exacerbation with use/heat, sleep disruption. NEW information is the activity-dependent worsening pattern and specific functional losses (gripping, keyboard work) not yet mentioned.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Specific cancer surgery (mastectomy + axillary lymph node dissection 18 months prior), adjuvant chemo/radiation, and recent cellulitis. NEW information is the lymph node dissection (direct mechanism for lymphedema) and temporal relationship. This significantly narrows differential to lymphatic complications.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective measurements (3.5 cm forearm difference, 2.8 cm wrist difference), pitting edema, skin thickening with nodularity, and Stemmer sign positive. NEW information is the palpable skin/tissue changes and a specific lymphedema test result that discriminates from simple post-operative fluid retention or venous insufficiency.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Lymphoscintigraphy findings (absent axillary uptake, delayed transit), ultrasound honeycomb pattern with dermal thickening, and absent/hypoplastic deep nodes. NEW information is direct visualization of lymphatic system dysfunction and structural changes specific to lymphedema staging/confirmation. Near-definitive.

Lymphoedema_183.notes: This is a well-constructed puzzle with strong progressive reveal. Clue 1 establishes symptom and context without naming the condition. Clue 2 adds functional pattern (activity-dependent worsening in heat). Clue 3 provides the mechanistic link (lymph node dissection). Clue 4 provides discriminatory clinical signs (Stemmer, skin changes, measurements). Clue 5 confirms with imaging. Each clue meaningfully narrows the differential: after Clue 1 (10+ post-treatment complications), after Clue 2 (lymphatic/vascular issues), after Clue 3 (lymphedema vs venous insufficiency), after Clue 4 (lymphedema highly likely), after Clue 5 (definitive). The differential is never accidentally given away, and a skilled clinician would converge on the answer by Clue 3-4.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, athlete status, location (shoulder blade), and presence of visible scapular winging. Establishes broad complaint of shoulder girdle pain with postural asymmetry.
clues.rewrite: A 28-year-old competitive athlete with 3 weeks of progressive shoulder blade pain. Notices the shoulder blade on one side looks different when training, and overhead activities feel uncomfortable.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations (push-ups, bench press, throwing) and movement patterns that reproduce symptoms (flexion, horizontal adduction). Adds fatigue/heaviness component.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism (heavy bench pressing), acute onset timing, progressive weakness pattern, and exclusion of neurological referral or neck involvement. Narrows from general shoulder pain to force-related scapular dysfunction.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: medial scapular winging on wall push-up, long thoracic nerve weakness on testing, scapular dyskinesis with arm elevation. This directly implicates serratus anterior and/or long thoracic nerve pathology.
clues.rewrite: Marked scapular winging with medial border prominence; winging increases with wall push-up. Weakness of scapular protraction on manual testing. Inability to maintain scapular position during forward arm raise.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Confirmatory neurophysiological and radiological findings (EMG denervation, MRI signal change, NCS evidence of axonal injury). Definitively confirms long thoracic nerve injury and serratus anterior denervation.

Serratus Anterior Injury_184.notes: Overall progression is sound: broad complaint → functional impact → mechanism/timeline → objective findings → imaging confirmation. PRIMARY ISSUE: Clue 1 is too specific and essentially names the answer by mentioning 'visible asymmetry of scapular position' and 'abnormal scapular winging' — these are pathognomonic findings that a physio student would immediately recognize as serratus anterior injury or long thoracic nerve palsy. Clue 1 should be rewritten to present only the patient's subjective complaint (shoulder blade pain with overhead activities) and demographic info, without revealing the visible winging or postural asymmetry — that should emerge from Clue 4 examination. Clue 3 also borders on too specific by explicitly stating 'progressive weakness,' which further narrows early. Minor issue in Clue 4: the long thoracic nerve testing finding is somewhat anticipated after Clues 1–3 have already implied nerve involvement; rewrite to focus on pure motor findings without naming the nerve testing itself.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient is 6 weeks post-arthroscopic shoulder surgery (rotator cuff repair). Presenting with persistent pain and ROM limitation despite physiotherapy.
clues.rewrite: 35-year-old with shoulder pain and stiffness for 6 weeks following a sports-related overhead injury. Pain worsens with movement and at night. Currently attending physiotherapy with minimal improvement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations: unable to lift arm overhead or away from body, unable to sleep on affected side, difficulty with dressing and self-care. Uses protective positioning.
clues.rewrite: Unable to reach overhead or out to side beyond 45 degrees. Sleeping on affected shoulder not tolerable. Avoids pushing/pulling activities. Pain with dressing and grooming tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism: overhead sports injury causing rotator cuff tear confirmed on MRI. Surgical intervention performed 6 weeks ago via arthroscopic approach. Good protocol adherence. Previously active without limitation.
clues.rewrite: Injury occurred during overhead throwing sport. Underwent surgical repair 6 weeks ago. Patient reports good adherence to post-operative restrictions and early physiotherapy. No prior shoulder pathology or surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific ROM measurements (active flexion 60°, abduction 45°, passive flexion 110°). Muscle guarding present. Pain 6/10 with movement.
clues.rewrite: Significant discrepancy between passive (110° flexion) and active ROM (60° flexion, 45° abduction). Marked muscle guarding and apprehension. Pain-limited movement. Deltoid strength difficult to isolate due to guarding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound confirms intact rotator cuff repair with appropriate healing. No re-tear, fluid collection, or significant structural complications. Mild reactive bursal inflammation noted.
clues.rewrite: Post-operative ultrasound demonstrates successful repair integrity with no evidence of re-tear, gap formation, or fluid collection. Findings exclude major structural failure and support mechanistic cause of persistent stiffness as adhesions or inadequate post-operative mobilization rather than surgical complication.

Shoulder Arthroscopy_185.notes: Critical structural problem: This puzzle reveals the diagnosis in Clue 1 (explicitly states 'post-shoulder surgery for rotator cuff repair'). The answer is not a diagnosis to be inferred—it's stated outright. The subsequent clues do not narrow a differential; they merely elaborate on a single, already-named condition. The progression fails the core Physiodle principle: there is no differential diagnosis to collapse. Clues 2-5 are consistent with each other but add no new discriminatory information—they just provide more detail about the same already-identified post-operative scenario. For a valid Physiodle puzzle, Clue 1 must not name the intervention or specific pathology; it should describe symptoms broadly (e.g., 'shoulder pain, stiffness, loss of motion') and allow the player to build a differential. The diagnosis should emerge only after the full clinical picture is assembled.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes broad symptom profile: bilateral headache, pressing/squeezing quality, chronic daily pattern (hours to full day), no complete resolution. Age and occupation provided as context. Differential could include tension-type headache, chronic migraine, cervicogenic headache, medication overuse headache, or other chronic headache disorders.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: NEW: Functional pattern and triggers. Worsens with sustained computer work and poor posture; improves with breaks and neck stretching. Clear stress/fatigue relationship. Critically, absence of migrainous features (no nausea, no photophobia) and presence of neck stiffness at onset. This narrows differential significantly away from migraine and toward postural/cervical-origin headache or tension-type headache.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: NEW: Timeline and mechanism. Onset 2 years ago coinciding with job change and desk work — establishes clear temporal relationship and ergonomic trigger. Denies head trauma, neurological red flags, and medication overuse (eliminates medication overuse headache and secondary causes). Good response to rest and simple analgesics suggests primary headache disorder without structural pathology. Still leaves tension-type vs. cervicogenic in differential.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: NEW: Objective physical findings. Bilateral upper trapezius and suboccipital tenderness (muscular component). Forward head posture with chin jut (postural dysfunction). Preserved cervical ROM (argues against significant cervical pathology or severe restriction). Symptom reproduction with sustained cervical flexion (suggests muscular/postural mechanism, not neurological compression). No focal neuro deficits (rules out radiculopathy, myelopathy). This strongly supports tension-type or postural headache and argues against cervicogenic headache requiring nerve root involvement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: NEW: Confirmatory imaging. Loss of cervical lordosis on X-ray (objective postural finding). MRI explicitly rules out structural/intracranial pathology, spondylosis, stenosis, mass — confirms primary headache disorder. Imaging eliminates cervicogenic headache (which typically requires structural nerve involvement) and confirms musculoskeletal/postural dysfunction as mechanism. Combined with earlier clues, diagnosis is essentially confirmed as tension-type headache with postural/muscular component.

Tension-Type Headache_186.notes: Excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 casts a wide net across chronic headache differentials. Clue 2 narrows by ruling out migraine features and implicating posture/stress. Clue 3 establishes mechanism and excludes red flags. Clue 4 provides physical findings that differentiate tension-type from cervicogenic (preserved ROM, muscular tenderness, postural findings rather than neurological). Clue 5 confirms with imaging. Each clue meaningfully narrows the differential. The puzzle successfully guides a player from ~6-8 initial possibilities down to one. No redundancy, good order, appropriate specificity at each stage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Acute hand injury from boxing with localized pain, swelling, and visible deformity at knuckle level. Establishes mechanism (blunt trauma), timeline (2 weeks), and body region (hand/knuckle).
clues.rewrite: A 24-year-old male with acute hand pain and swelling sustained during contact sport training 2 weeks ago. Localized symptoms at knuckle level with visible hand deformity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: grip weakness, inability to make full fist, pain on flexion beyond 60°. Activity-dependent swelling. Narrows to conditions affecting flexor mechanics and metacarpophalangeal joints.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Direct punch impact mechanism (already stated in Clue 1), home management approach, no prior fracture history, halted training. Largely redundant with Clue 1 details; minimal new discriminatory value.
clues.rewrite: No prior hand fractures or injuries. Initial management with ice and elevation only; no imaging obtained acutely. Patient remains concerned about hand alignment and residual prominence at knuckle.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific objective findings: visible prominence at fifth metacarpal head with depression proximal to knuckle, ecchymosis pattern, palpation findings, neurovascular status intact. This is the key clinical discriminator—the 'boxer's knuckle' deformity (prominence + depression) strongly suggests metacarpal neck fracture with angulation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: X-ray confirms fifth metacarpal neck fracture with 35° angulation, minimal displacement, intact metacarpal head, no intra-articular involvement. This is definitive and consistent with the examination findings.

Boxer's Fracture_187.notes: The puzzle has a functional progressive reveal, but Clue 1 is overly specific and essentially names the answer ('visible deformity of hand' + acute boxing injury + knuckle location + '2 weeks' strongly suggests boxer's fracture to an experienced clinician). Clue 3 then largely repeats Clue 1's mechanism and timeline without adding meaningful discriminatory history. The rewrite for Clue 1 removes the word 'deformity' and boxing context to broaden the differential, and Clue 3 is repositioned to focus on absence of prior injury and initial conservative management, which adds real clinical context. Clues 4 and 5 work well as written and appropriately narrow to the specific diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 52-year-old woman, 5-year history of chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, diffuse pelvic pain, urinary frequency, pelvic heaviness
clues.rewrite: A 52-year-old woman presents with a 5-year history of chronic pain in the pelvic region. She reports dyspareunia and urinary frequency but denies fever, weight loss, or systemic symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: prolonged standing/sitting exacerbate pain, perineal pressure intolerant, bowel/bladder function causes discomfort, relieved by supine rest with hip flexion and pelvic floor relaxation
clues.rewrite: She avoids prolonged standing and sitting due to symptom exacerbation. She reports difficulty with normal bowel and bladder function. Symptoms ease with rest in a supine position with hips flexed.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Hysterectomy 8 years ago without pain resolution, multiple prior negative gynecological and urological consultations, significant psychological distress associated with chronic pain

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Pelvic floor hypertonia and tenderness bilaterally, normal abdominal exam, diminished hip ROM, normal lower limb neuro but decreased light touch sensation in S2-S4 distribution
clues.rewrite: Pelvic floor muscles are hypertonic and tender on palpation. Hip abduction and internal rotation produce discomfort at end-range. Lower limb reflexes and motor strength are intact, but sensory testing reveals decreased light touch sensation in a dermatomal pattern corresponding to S2-S4.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI shows no masses, adhesions, or structural abnormalities of bladder, rectum, or pelvis; essentially negative imaging

Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome_188.notes: PROGRESSIVE REVEAL PROBLEM: Clue 1 is too specific—it names the key clinical features (chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, urinary frequency, pelvic heaviness) that essentially define CPPS, leaving little diagnostic uncertainty for a clinician. Clue 2 then repeats and reinforces these same features functionally (perineal sensitivity, bladder/bowel involvement, relief with relaxation techniques) without adding NEW discriminating information. By Clue 2, the diagnosis is already obvious. Clue 3 adds helpful context (hysterectomy history, failed prior workup, psychological distress) but this comes too late and doesn't narrow further because the diagnosis was already evident. Clue 4 introduces neurological findings (S2-S4 sensory change) that actually seem incongruent with the primary diagnosis and muddy the picture rather than clarify it. The clues do not form a proper diagnostic funnel. Rewrite Clue 1 to be symptom-general (pain, frequency, dyspareunia only, without pelvic-specific language if possible). Rewrite Clues 2 and 4 to avoid redundancy and ensure each adds distinct, non-overlapping clinical information.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographics (19yo), mechanism (MVC), timeline (4 weeks post-injury), and symptom cluster (headache, dizziness, concentration difficulty, fatigue). However, this clue explicitly names the injury type ('traumatic brain injury') and provides injury severity, which is far too specific for Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: 19-year-old student with persistent headaches, dizziness, and significant difficulty concentrating. Reports fatigue that interferes with daily tasks. Symptoms began 4 weeks ago following a motor vehicle collision.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations: unable to attend school full-time, concentration lapses, vertigo with rapid head movements, balance difficulty walking, photosensitivity. This does narrow toward vestibular/balance/neuro involvement, but some of this was already implied by 'dizziness' and 'concentration difficulty' in Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: Unable to tolerate prolonged visual tasks or bright environments. Vertigo triggered specifically by rapid head movements. Gait unsteady, particularly on uneven surfaces or when turning.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Loss of consciousness (3 min), secondary brain swelling requiring ICU, imaging findings (contusions, subdural fluid, no intubation). This is appropriate historical detail that narrows differential toward moderate-to-severe TBI, but it repeats the imaging detail that should belong in Clue 5. Also, the ICU course and LOC duration heavily imply TBI diagnosis prematurely.
clues.rewrite: Loss of consciousness for approximately 3 minutes at scene. Admitted to hospital and required ICU-level monitoring in acute phase. No mechanical ventilation needed. Previously no significant medical or neurological history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific neuro examination findings: compensatory arm swing in gait, positive Romberg test (20-sec instability), Dix-Hallpike triggers vertigo, Fukuda test with significant deviation, CN II-XII intact, no focal deficits. This genuinely discriminates between diffuse vs focal injury and indicates vestibular involvement. Appropriate level of specificity for Clue 4.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI findings: resolving frontal contusions, minimal residual subdural fluid, microhemorrhages in white matter on SWI, normal CSF spaces, no mass effect. This confirms diffuse axonal injury pattern consistent with moderate TBI. Appropriate confirmation-level detail for Clue 5.

Traumatic Brain Injury_189.notes: Major problem: Clue 1 explicitly names 'traumatic brain injury' in the opening phrase, which completely defeats the guessing game. This is an immediate giveaway. Clue 2 largely restates vestibular and cognitive symptoms already mentioned in Clue 1 without substantial new discriminatory information. Clue 3 mixes historical timeline (appropriate) with imaging findings that belong in Clue 5, and the LOC + ICU course further solidifies the TBI diagnosis too early. The differential narrowing is broken: by end of Clue 1, the answer is already stated. A well-designed puzzle would withhold the injury name, allow Clue 1 to suggest multiple possibilities (post-concussion syndrome vs vestibular disorder vs anxiety vs chronic fatigue), and use Clue 3 to establish mechanism/timeline, Clue 4 to identify vestibular findings, and Clue 5 to confirm via imaging. Rewrites needed for Clues 1, 2, and 3 to restore playability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, acute-onset, knee swelling localized to anterior knee/front of kneecap, visible fullness, progression over days
clues.rewrite: 45-year-old with acute-onset knee swelling over the past week. Reports visible fullness in front of the knee that has progressively worsened. Denies significant trauma or mechanical symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional pattern: kneeling exacerbates symptoms; walking/stairs tolerated; direct pressure pain. Reveals occupational exposure (kneeling).

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Recent job change (3 weeks); repeated mechanical irritation from prolonged kneeling on hard surfaces; excludes acute trauma, prior knee pathology, systemic inflammation

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Fluctuant quality (fluid), anterior to patella, ~4cm diameter, localized erythema/warmth, normal joint mechanics, normal special tests. Distinguishes bursal fluid collection from joint effusion or solid mass.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound confirms simple fluid collection anterior to patella (1.2 cm depth), no internal debris/septations, normal patellar tendon/joint space, no bursal wall enhancement. Rules out infection, complex bursal pathology, and joint-space pathology.

Prepatellar Bursitis_190.notes: Overall progressive reveal is sound and clinically coherent. Clue 1 is the main weakness: it explicitly names 'localized to the front of the kneecap' and 'visible fullness anterior to patellar tendon' — this anatomy-specific language (anterior patella, patellar tendon) is too direct and essentially pins down prepatellar bursitis immediately for an experienced clinician, even before Clue 2. Rewritten Clue 1 should remove anatomical specificity and describe only the symptom and timeline. Clues 2–5 show good progressive narrowing: Clue 2 adds mechanism/occupation; Clue 3 adds timeline and excludes systemic causes; Clue 4 adds physical findings that distinguish fluid from other pathology; Clue 5 confirms with imaging. The puzzle is playable but slightly front-loaded.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, timeline (8 months post-injury), symptom type (chronic LBP), and key observation that pain severity exceeds clinical findings. Establishes mismatch between subjective complaint and objective presentation.
clues.rewrite: A 38-year-old reports persistent low back pain 8 months after a work-related strain injury. Pain level has remained high despite gradual functional improvement and medical clearance to gradually resume normal activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations (avoidance of bending, lifting, sitting) and behavioral pattern (catastrophizing, self-imposed restrictions exceeding physiology, delayed recovery). Shifts focus from 'what hurts' to 'what they avoid' and 'how they think about pain'.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism (work injury), treatment history (passive therapies only, no progressive exercise), healthcare interactions (multiple providers, varying diagnoses, increased anxiety), and environmental factors (family overprotection, reinforced avoidance). Explains *why* patient developed and maintains avoidant behavior.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings are *inconsistent* and *fear-limited* rather than pain- or stiffness-limited (SLR improves with distraction). FABQ score quantifies severity of kinesiophobia. Demonstrates discrepancy between observed and reported function.
clues.rewrite: Objective findings minimal and inconsistent: straight leg raise limited by guarding but improves markedly when patient is distracted during conversation. Able to flex lumbar spine 60 degrees when attention diverted, but reports severe pain with intentional forward bending. Fear-avoidance beliefs questionnaire indicates elevated fear-related cognitions with score in severe range (67/96).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Structural imaging findings (minor disc protrusion, no cord compression, stable over time) definitively do NOT explain the severity of reported disability. Confirms mismatch between pathology and clinical presentation observed throughout clues 1–3.

Kinesiophobia_191.notes: MAJOR ISSUES: Clue 1 explicitly names 'excessive fear of movement and reinjury' and 'kinesiophobia' (the answer) in the very first clue, eliminating all mystery and making the puzzle trivial. Clue 4 directly references the FABQ (a kinesiophobia-specific tool) and quantifies 'fear-related cognitions,' again naming the diagnosis via proxy. The puzzle structure is inverted: Clues 1 and 4 together essentially give away the answer before the player has a chance to reason through the differential. A proper version should: (1) start with LBP as a generic complaint without mentioning fear; (2) let Clue 2 reveal the behavioral pattern; (3) use Clue 3 to establish the reinforcing factors; (4) use Clue 4 to show inconsistency and fear-limitation on exam without explicitly naming FABQ or 'kinesiophobia'; and (5) use Clue 5 to confirm structural imaging doesn't match disability (ruling out serious pathology). The rewrite of Clue 1 removes the word 'fear' and focuses on the mismatch between pain and findings. The rewrite of Clue 4 avoids naming FABQ and instead describes the behavioral findings (distraction-responsive, fear-limited movement) that would lead a clinician to suspect kinesiophobia.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, occupation (recreational runner), symptom location (Achilles region), temporal pattern (morning stiffness, improves with activity, worsens evening/after running). Establishes broad differential for lower leg pain in active adult.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations (running distance <3km, uphill/speed work aggravate, stairs—particularly downstairs—cause focal pain, stiffness after sedentary periods). Narrows to ankle/foot region and load-dependent pathology. Rules out systemic/inflammatory causes.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Training load progression (8-week volume increase, hills/tempo added without grading), tight calves, no prior Achilles issues, prior ankle sprain (5 years ago), no inflammatory comorbidities. Establishes overuse mechanism and rules out systemic/chronic inflammatory tendinopathy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Palpation tenderness at 3-4 cm proximal to insertion with nodularity (localises to midportion); pain on resisted plantarflexion despite 5/5 strength; single leg calf raise limited (8 reps, progressive pain). Discriminates Achilles tendinopathy from ankle instability, plantarfasciitis, or peroneal tendinopathy.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound: midportion thickening, hypoechoic intratendinous degeneration, neovascularity on Doppler, retrocalcaneal bursitis. Confirms Achilles tendinopathy with secondary bursitis and rules out insertional pathology alone.

Achilles Tendinopathy_192.notes: Excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 is appropriately broad (symptom + temporal pattern in active adult—could be Achilles, peroneal, plantarfasciitis, ankle instability, stress fracture). Clue 2 narrows powerfully with functional specifics (stairs, especially downstairs = Achilles-loading activities; short running distance = load-dependent). Clue 3 adds critical history (overuse mechanism, no prior Achilles disease, no systemic flags)—rules out insertional pathology, inflammatory tendinopathy, or ankle instability.
