Clue 4 provides examination findings that differentiate (midportion tenderness + nodularity + pain on plantarflexion resistance = Achilles, not plantarfasciitis or peroneal). Clue 5 confirms with imaging specificity (hypoechoic degeneration, neovascularity, retrocalcaneal bursitis). Each clue adds distinct, non-redundant information. Clinician after Clue 3 should confidently narrow to Achilles tendinopathy; Clues 4–5 confirm. No giveaway terminology. Excellent difficulty calibration.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, symptom character (sharp, well-localized), anatomical location (lateral elbow prominence), onset mechanism (repetitive gripping), and timeline (6 weeks progressive). Establishes broad differential of lateral elbow pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations (gripping, lifting, twisting reproduce pain; difficulty carrying); weakness (grip strength deficit); movement-specific reproducers (pronation under load); radiation pattern (lateral forearm to wrist). Narrows from generalized elbow pain to lateral-sided, load-dependent pathology affecting grip and forearm function.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism of injury (tool use, forceful gripping at work); occupational context (tradesperson); absence of confounders (no prior injury, no rheumatological disease); timeline of gradual worsening. Solidifies link between repetitive occupational load and lateral elbow syndrome; eliminates systemic or traumatic etiologies.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: point tenderness over lateral epicondyle (anatomical confirmation); positive special tests (Mill's test, Cozen's test—both specific to extensor mechanism); resisted wrist extension reproduction; reduced grip strength (15% deficit quantified); absence of neurological signs (rules out nerve compression). Confirms tendon-origin pathology at lateral epicondyle and excludes nerve involvement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasonographic confirmation of common extensor tendon pathology: hypoechoic thickening, heterogeneous echotexture, neovascularity on Power Doppler, surrounding edema. Absence of calcification/cortical irregularity (rules out bony spur or enthesopathy). Near-definitive imaging signature of acute-to-chronic lateral epicondylitis.

Lateral Epicondylitis_193.notes: Excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 casts a wide net on lateral elbow pain. Clue 2 adds functional and movement-specific discriminators. Clue 3 anchors the mechanism and occupational context, eliminating systemic disease. Clue 4 provides physical examination findings that confirm tendon-origin involvement and exclude nerve compression. Clue 5 provides imaging confirmation of tendinopathy with specific ultrasound signatures. Each clue narrows the differential meaningfully. The puzzle is appropriately challenging—a skilled clinician would likely arrive at the answer after Clue 3 or 4, but Clue 5 locks it in. No redundancy, no naming of the answer, no out-of-order information. High-quality puzzle.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographics (28F runner), symptom character (localized foot pain, gradual onset 3 weeks, activity-dependent, worsened by weight-bearing), and establishes broad differential (overuse vs acute trauma).

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific triggers (running, walking on hard surfaces) and response to intervention (relief with rest/ice). Progressive functional decline adds temporal pattern and severity.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism of injury (40% mileage increase without recovery—classic overuse pattern). Female-specific risk factors (irregular menses, low calcium) establish predisposition to stress fracture over other overuse injuries (tendinopathy, plantar fasciitis). Absence of acute trauma rules out traumatic fracture.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Focal palpation point (2nd/3rd metatarsal shaft) localizes anatomy precisely. Load-bearing reproduction and single-leg stance/hopping results are classic for stress fracture—distinguish from other midfoot overuse injuries (e.g., midfoot sprain, cuboid dysfunction) which would not typically show reproducible pain with hopping.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Radiographic confirmation (fracture line + periosteal reaction) and MRI specificity (bone marrow edema, stress reaction pattern in trabecular bone). Definitively distinguishes stress fracture from mimics (muscle strain, metatarsalgia, fasciopathy).

Stress Fracture - Metatarsal_194.notes: Excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 casts a wide net (foot pain in runner—could be PF, metatarsalgia, stress fracture, tendinopathy, etc.). Clue 2 adds functional specificity (activity-dependent, response to rest/ice). Clue 3 introduces the critical discriminator: female + mileage overload + dietary/hormonal risk factors = stress fracture pattern, eliminating most other differentials. Clue 4 provides examination findings (focal tenderness, load-bearing reproduction) that are pathognomonic for stress fracture in this context. Clue 5 is definitive imaging. Each clue meaningfully narrows the differential. Clinician would be confident after Clue 3, near-certain after Clue 4. The puzzle has good difficulty: not solvable on Clue 1–2 alone, but solvable by a competent physio after Clue 3–4 without imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Acute onset lateral elbow pain and swelling following FOOSH injury. Mechanism establishes upper extremity trauma but is still compatible with multiple diagnoses (radial head fracture, coronoid fracture, elbow dislocation, ligamentous injury, etc.).
clues.rewrite: A 32-year-old presents with acute onset elbow pain and swelling after a fall on an outstretched hand while rollerblading. Pain is laterally localized and worsens with movement. The mechanism involved falling from standing height onto an extended arm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Inability to supinate forearm without marked pain; pronation relatively preserved; intolerance of weight-bearing through upper extremity in push position. This is NEW — it identifies supination as specifically painful and hints at forearm rotational involvement.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Direct impact to lateral elbow; no prior injury; valgus stress during fall. However, valgus stress during a FOOSH is already heavily implied by Clue 1. This clue largely rephrases the mechanism rather than adding new historical context (e.g., no comorbidities, no medication use, no prior instability mentioned). The direct lateral impact is somewhat redundant with 'lateral localized pain' from Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: No prior elbow injuries or instability. No inflammatory history. Impact occurred at the lateral aspect of the elbow during the fall. Immediate swelling and inability to continue activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Palpable point tenderness over radial head; pain with resisted supination; negative varus/valgus stress tests; rotational movements painful. This narrows significantly — radial head pathology is now strongly suggested over ligamentous injury (stress tests negative). Supination pain with resisted movement is a key discriminator.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: CT reveals fracture through radial head with minimal displacement, articular involvement at radiocapitellar joint, no significant comminution. This is definitive — it confirms the diagnosis and rules out other fracture patterns (e.g., coronoid, capitellum) or pure soft tissue injury.

Radial Head Fracture_195.notes: The puzzle has a strong overall structure, but Clue 3 (History) is the weak point. It largely rephrases the fall mechanism already established in Clue 1 ('direct to lateral elbow', 'valgus stress') without adding genuinely new historical information that would narrow the differential further at that stage. After Clue 2, a clinician knows pain is lateral, supination is painful, and there's a FOOSH injury — all of which already point toward radial head/neck involvement or lateral collateral ligament pathology. Clue 3 should add NEW context (e.g., age-related factors, time since injury, prior instability, radiation of symptoms, night pain, functional demand changes) to help rule out alternatives. The revised Clue 3 focuses on what hasn't been covered: background/comorbidities and the immediate functional consequence. Clues 1, 2, 4, and 5 are solid and maintain good progressive narrowing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Systemic fatigue disproportionate to activity, not relieved by rest, 6-month duration, declining functional capacity
clues.rewrite: A 52-year-old patient reports a 6-month history of persistent, overwhelming tiredness that is disproportionate to physical activity level and not relieved by rest. Daily activities that were previously manageable now require significant effort.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional decline: work capacity reduced from full-time to part-time, light household tasks cause multi-hour exhaustion, social/recreational activities curtailed

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Chemotherapy completed 4 months ago for newly diagnosed malignancy; fatigue persists beyond typical post-treatment recovery; sleep disruption with early morning awakening
clues.rewrite: Patient underwent chemotherapy 4 months ago for a newly diagnosed systemic illness. Although fatigue was expected during treatment, it has persisted beyond the typical recovery window. Sleep quality remains disrupted with early morning awakening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: elevated resting heart rate (88 bpm), normal manual muscle strength throughout, disproportionate cardiovascular/respiratory response to light exertion

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Laboratory: persistent anemia (Hb 10.2 g/dL), elevated inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR). Thyroid function normal. Imaging shows no recurrent malignancy or metastases.
clues.rewrite: Laboratory analysis shows persistent anemia (hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL) and elevated inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR). Thyroid function studies are normal. Imaging surveillance shows no evidence of disease recurrence.

Cancer-Related Fatigue_196.notes: Major issues: Clue 1 is too clinically specific for a 'Complaint' clue — it contains interpretation ('systemic presentation warrants investigation'), duration, and mechanism detail that reads like a clinical summary rather than patient report. Clue 3 explicitly names 'chemotherapy' and 'newly diagnosed malignancy,' essentially revealing the answer category before Clue 5. The progressive reveal collapses here: by Clue 3, any experienced clinician knows this is cancer-related fatigue. Clue 4 adds genuine new examination data but comes too late. Clue 5 adds only supporting lab/imaging confirmation with limited new discriminative value. Restructure: Clue 1 should be simpler (fatigue complaint only, age/duration). Clue 2 (functional impact) is appropriate. Clue 3 should hint at a systemic trigger (prior serious illness, recent treatment) WITHOUT naming cancer. Clue 4 should provide exam findings that point toward post-treatment fatigue. Clue 5 should confirm cancer history and lab abnormalities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, chronicity, progressive nature, overhead aggravation, morning stiffness. Explicitly eliminates acute/inflammatory/traumatic etiologies.
clues.rewrite: A 64-year-old with several years of progressive shoulder pain and stiffness. Pain worsens with overhead activities and is notably stiff in the mornings. Gradual onset over many years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations: overhead reaching, swimming strokes, dressing. Confirms glenohumeral joint involvement affecting multiple planes of motion.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Remote dislocation (15 years), recurrent impingement syndrome, 30 years occupational overhead work. Establishes traumatic and mechanical risk factors supporting degenerative cascade.
clues.rewrite: Remote shoulder dislocation treated conservatively 15 years prior. Multiple episodes of subacromial impingement over the past decade. Thirty years of occupational overhead work.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific ROM loss (ER 45°), painful arc 70–120°, glenohumeral crepitus, pain-limited weakness. Differentiates osteoarthritis from isolated rotator cuff disease or impingement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Radiographic joint space narrowing, osteophytes, cartilage thinning, chondral defects, bone marrow edema. Confirms primary osteoarthritis rather than secondary impingement or soft-tissue pathology.

Shoulder Osteoarthritis_197.notes: Clue 1 is the main problem: it explicitly states 'degenerative rather than inflammatory or traumatic etiologies' and describes classic osteoarthritis presentation (chronic, progressive, morning stiffness, overhead pain). This essentially names the answer category before any guessing. A physio student reading only Clue 1 would immediately narrow to shoulder OA. Clue 1 should be much broader—describe only the complaint without diagnostic reasoning. Clue 3 adds history that explains the pathogenesis (trauma leading to OA) but is largely implied by Clue 1's already-strong steer toward degeneration. Clues 4 and 5 are well-ordered and appropriately specific. Overall progression is sound from Clue 2 onwards, but Clue 1 undermines the game difficulty severely.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, sport context, acute onset, lateral ankle pain/swelling, mechanism (cutting/direction change)
clues.rewrite: A 22-year-old basketball player presents acutely with ankle pain and swelling after a sudden cutting movement during a game. The injury occurred approximately 2 hours ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations: inability to weight-bear, no running/jumping, pain with inversion and push-off
clues.rewrite: Patient reports inability to bear weight on the affected side. Attempted ambulation produces severe pain. Running, jumping, and any lateral weight shift are impossible.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Prior sprain 8 months ago with similar mechanism, incomplete rehabilitation, recurrent 'giving way' episodes. This significantly narrows differential by establishing chronic ankle instability pattern and previous ligamentous injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Anterior drawer test positive (talus displacement), inversion stress test positive with excessive motion, tenderness over ATFL, proprioceptive deficits on single-leg stance. This is highly specific for ligamentous injury and distinguishes from other ankle pathologies.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI confirms grade II ATFL tear with partial CFl disruption and edema. Syndesmotic involvement noted. Provides definitive structural diagnosis.

Lateral Ankle Sprain_198.notes: Clue 1 is problematic: it explicitly states 'lateral ankle injury' and describes it as 'consistent with multiple soft tissue pathologies' but also specifies the exact anatomical location and injury type, which severely compromises the game difficulty. It reads like the answer is already partially revealed. The phrase 'lateral ankle injury' is a near-synonym for the answer. Clue 2 largely reiterates functional consequences already implied by Clue 1 (acute ankle injury → can't walk/run). The progression from Clues 3-5 is solid, with good discrimination between possibilities (history of instability, then specific ligamentous findings, then imaging confirmation). Recommend Clue 1 be substantially broadened by removing anatomical specificity and injury-type language.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, insidious groin pain over 6 months, medial hip/proximal inner thigh localization, broad differential acknowledged
clues.rewrite: A 58-year-old avid golfer presents with insidious-onset groin pain that has progressively worsened over 6 months. Pain is localized to the medial hip and inner thigh region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: golf swing (especially follow-through) and walking >15 min trigger pain; hip internal rotation provokes sharp pain

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: 30-year golf history with poor swing mechanics emphasizing deep hip rotation; recent increased tournament activity with minimal rest; no acute trauma
clues.rewrite: 30-year golf history with chronic poor swing mechanics emphasizing deep hip rotation. Recently completed extended tournament schedule over 4 weeks with minimal rest. No acute traumatic event. Progressive activity-related presentation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: FABER positive in groin; IR limited to 20°; adductor strength pain-limited at 4/5; point tenderness at adductor longus origin — distinguishes adductor pathology from other hip conditions

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI confirms adductor longus tendinopathy with partial tearing, pubic symphysis edema, and concurrent mild hip OA — definitively rules out isolated intra-articular pathology
clues.rewrite: MRI reveals adductor longus tendinopathic changes with mild partial tearing at the pubic symphysis insertion. Concurrent mild hip joint cartilage thinning and edema at pubic symphysis noted.

Golfer's Hip_199.notes: MAIN ISSUES: Clue 1 is too specific and effectively names the answer ('golfer' + 'groin pain' + 'medial hip' + '30-year golf history' is nearly diagnostic before any guessing). Remove the explicit reference to golf mechanics and tournament schedule from Clue 1 — those belong in Clue 3 (History). Clue 3 and Clue 5 have substantial overlap: both emphasize the adductor pathology. Clue 5 should more sharply differentiate from other causes (e.g., psoas strain, FAI, labral pathology) by confirming the specific tendinopathic + partial tear picture. After rewrite, the progressive reveal should be: Clue 1 (broad groin pain in a golfer), → Clue 2 (functional pattern of pain with rotation/loading), → Clue 3 (chronic repetitive stress + recent overload), → Clue 4 (localized adductor origin tenderness and weakness), → Clue 5 (imaging confirmation of adductor tendinopathy with tear).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient is 3 weeks post-arthroscopic meniscectomy with persistent knee pain, swelling, and functional limitations in the operated compartment.
clues.rewrite: A 35-year-old with a recent knee injury presents with persistent joint pain and swelling that has not improved with conservative management over the past 3 weeks. Pain is localized to the knee and worsens with activity. Some loss of motion is noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Walking >10 minutes and descending stairs are difficult; knee flexion >90 degrees causes discomfort. This narrows to knee-based limitation affecting gait and flexion tolerance.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Medial meniscectomy for degenerative tear (not locking); previously highly active with running and tennis. Expected return to sport is 8-12 weeks.
clues.rewrite: Patient sustained a medial meniscal injury requiring surgical intervention 3 weeks ago. Prior to injury, patient was training at a competitive level with high-impact demands. Recovery is slower than anticipated at this stage.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Reactive effusion in suprapatellar pouch; flexion limited to 110 degrees; quadriceps weakness 4/5 with atrophy; McMurray's negative; medial joint line tenderness resolved. Distinguishes from ongoing meniscal pathology and clarifies tissue healing status.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI confirms complete meniscal resection, reactive synovitis, and intact ligaments without chondral damage. Confirms surgical success and rules out structural complication.
clues.rewrite: Post-operative imaging demonstrates successful surgical resection with evidence of reactive inflammatory changes within the joint. No evidence of iatrogenic ligament injury or articular cartilage involvement. Structural integrity of remaining knee anatomy is preserved.

Post-Meniscectomy_200.notes: Major problem: Clue 1 essentially names the answer by stating 'post-meniscectomy' explicitly. This should begin as a broader complaint (e.g., persistent knee pain after recent injury) without disclosing surgery type. Clue 2 is solid. Clue 3 redundantly restates that meniscectomy was performed—this fact was already given in Clue 1. Clue 3 should instead focus on mechanism of initial injury, symptom timeline trajectory, or comorbidities that explain delayed recovery. Clue 5 (imaging) adds minimal discriminatory value beyond confirming what Clues 3 and 4 already established. Consider replacing with imaging findings that distinguish post-meniscectomy inflammation from other post-operative complications (e.g., specific effusion volume, synovial characteristics). The progressive reveal funnel collapses too early due to premature disclosure in Clue 1.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, heel pain location, morning severity with gradual improvement, end-of-day recurrence
clues.rewrite: A 48-year-old presents with heel pain that is worst upon rising in the morning and taking the first steps, then gradually improves with activity before returning at end of day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitation: pain with prolonged standing/walking on hard surfaces; relief with supportive footwear; barefoot walking exacerbates symptoms
clues.rewrite: Activities requiring prolonged standing or walking on hard surfaces significantly exacerbate pain. Patient notices barefoot walking and light footwear worsen symptoms; pain is minimal when wearing supportive shoes with cushioning.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Insidious onset 4 months ago without injury; recent exercise increase; BMI 32; standing job 6-8 hours daily; flat shoes without arch support

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific palpation finding (medial calcaneal tubercle tenderness along plantar fascia insertion); positive dorsiflexion + toe extension test; calf tightness with limited dorsiflexion (5°)

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound confirms fascia thickening (4.2 mm vs normal <3 mm) with hypoechoic inflammatory change at medial insertion; radiographs negative for heel spur

Plantar Fasciitis_201.notes: MAJOR ISSUES: Clue 1 explicitly names 'plantar fascia' and describes 'inflammation of the weight-bearing structures of the plantar aspect' — this gives away the diagnosis far too early. A physio student reading this immediately knows the answer. Clue 1 should describe ONLY symptoms (heel pain, location, timing pattern) without anatomical attribution. Clue 2 largely restates information already in Clue 1 (morning pain, activity-related relief, barefoot/flat shoes make it worse) without adding meaningfully new functional context. The differential narrowing is weak between Clues 1 and 2. Clues 3, 4, and 5 are strong and progressively specific, but the early clues undermine the game. Rewrite Clue 1 to remove anatomical language and be genuinely broad (heel pain could be plantar fasciitis, calcaneal stress fracture, nerve compression, insertional Achilles, heel pad syndrome, etc.). Rewrite Clue 2 to emphasize specific functional activities or contextual relief patterns not already obvious from symptom timing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient identity, age, time since injury, anatomical location, TBSA percentage, burn depth classification, and current wound status (epithelialization phase)
clues.rewrite: A 34-year-old manual laborer presents 4 weeks after a thermal injury to the hand and forearm. Pain and functional loss are significant. Wound care is ongoing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations (gripping, fine motor, self-care), pain-driven barriers to activity, sleep disruption from dressing changes
clues.rewrite: Unable to grip or perform fine motor tasks. Self-care activities including bathing and dressing are severely limited. Sleep is disrupted by pain and frequent dressing protocol requirements.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism of injury (hot liquid), social context (lives alone), occupation (manual labor), prior treatment received (cleaning, antibiotics, dressings), grafting status (none to date)
clues.rewrite: Injury occurred 4 weeks ago from hot liquid exposure while cooking. Lives alone and relies on manual work. No surgical intervention (grafting) has been performed. Treated conservatively with dressings and topical care.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific wound bed appearance (granulation tissue, patchy epithelialization), edema magnitude, passive ROM findings (flexion contracture development), pain-limited grip strength
clues.rewrite: Wound bed shows granulation tissue with epithelialization beginning at margins. Moderate persistent edema in hand and forearm. Early finger flexion contractures evident on passive range of motion. Grip strength severely limited by pain on testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Thermal imaging showing variable perfusion patterns, ultrasound confirming tissue edema, exclusion of deep structure involvement (tendons)

Burns Rehabilitation_202.notes: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 already names the diagnosis explicitly ('Burns Rehabilitation') and provides exhaustive clinical detail (TBSA %, burn depth classification, epithelialization status) that would be diagnostic information revealed much later in a clinical funnel. Clues 2-4 then redundantly re-state the same functional, historical, and examination findings without adding materially new differentiating information. By Clue 1, a player has no meaningful differential diagnosis to construct — the answer is already given. The puzzle reads like a case summary rather than a progressive diagnostic game. Rewrite needed: Clue 1 should mention only acute hand/forearm pain and functional loss post-injury, without naming injury type or providing pathological detail. Clues 2-4 should each introduce genuinely distinct information (mechanism → timeline/comorbidity → examination findings) that progressively narrows from 'acute hand injury' to 'thermal burn' to 'specific burn rehabilitation phase.' Clue 5 can then confirm with imaging correlation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, gender, sport, acute mechanism (lateral contact with planted foot), medial knee pain and swelling, localized to medial joint line
clues.rewrite: A 29-year-old female soccer player reports acute onset medial knee pain and swelling sustained during a game. Injury occurred with non-contact mechanism during dynamic movement. Pain is localized around the knee joint.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitation pattern: cutting and pivoting reproduce pain; straight running tolerated; direction changes impossible. Valgus stress exacerbates symptoms.
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot perform cutting or rapid directional changes. Straight-line running is tolerated. Activities requiring knee stability with rotation are significantly limited.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: No prior knee injuries; prior ankle sprains on opposite side; patient has generalized ligamentous laxity and hypermobility; competitive athlete with good training history
clues.rewrite: No prior knee injuries. Patient has generalized ligamentous hypermobility affecting multiple joints. Competitive training history without prior significant lower limb pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Valgus stress test at 30° positive with increased lateral opening compared to contralateral knee; Lachman negative (rules out ACL); tenderness over MCL at tibial attachment; localized medial swelling without effusion

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI confirms grade I-II MCL strain with partial tearing of fibular and tibial components; ligamentous edema; ACL and menisci intact

Medial Collateral Ligament Sprain_203.notes: Clue 1 is diagnostically premature — it names 'medial stabilizers' and 'soft tissue pathology' which severely narrows the differential before the player has guessed. Clue 2 adds valgus stress mechanism which is essentially implicit in Clue 1's description of lateral contact with planted foot — this is redundant. Clue 3 adds hypermobility context which is useful for understanding risk, but doesn't meaningfully narrow between MCL sprain and other ligamentous injuries at this stage. Clues 4 and 5 are appropriately specific and discriminatory. The progressive reveal funnel is weakened by early over-specification and redundancy between Clues 1 and 2.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient with depression who started exercising 6 weeks ago and reports mood improvement, reduced anxiety, and decreased depression score
clues.rewrite: 36-year-old reports gradual improvement in mood and anxiety over the past 6 weeks. Symptoms were previously stable despite pharmacological intervention. Patient attributes change to lifestyle modification.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional gains: now able to perform 45-minute brisk walking 5x/week (previously avoided), resumption of hobbies like gardening, improved sleep quality and daytime energy
clues.rewrite: Patient has resumed cardiovascular exercise at moderate intensity (45 min, 5x/week). Secondary gains include restored ability to engage in previously abandoned recreational activities and normalized sleep architecture.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Prior SSRI trial without response. Exercise initiation was motivated by community health education. No concurrent medication changes during improvement period.
clues.rewrite: One-year history of depression partially refractory to SSRI monotherapy. Exercise program initiated independently following health promotion event. Timeline of mood improvement correlates precisely with sustained physical activity adherence.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: improved affect/mood on interview, reduced blood pressure and resting heart rate, improved cardiovascular efficiency on stress testing
clues.rewrite: Observed affect is euthymic with appropriate emotional responsiveness. Cardiovascular parameters show improvement: resting heart rate reduced by approximately 8-10 bpm, systolic blood pressure decreased 10-12 mmHg from baseline. Exercise capacity on treadmill testing increased 15-20%.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Neurobiological correlates: increased prefrontal/striatal activation on fMRI reward processing, elevated serum BDNF, no structural pathology
clues.rewrite: Functional neuroimaging reveals altered activation in regions associated with reward processing and emotional regulation. Biomarker panel demonstrates elevation in neurotrophic factors consistent with neuroplastic adaptation. Structural imaging unremarkable.

Exercise-Induced Mood Improvement_204.notes: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. The answer ('Exercise-Induced Mood Improvement') is explicitly named in Clue 1, making Clues 2-5 redundant elaborations rather than a narrowing diagnostic funnel. In a real Physiodle, players should generate a differential diagnosis from Clue 1 (e.g., major depressive disorder, anxiety disorder, bipolar depression, adjustment disorder) and progressively narrow it through functional impact, history, examination, and imaging. Instead, each clue merely restates 'exercise improved mood' with different terminology. This is not a valid Physiodle puzzle—it reads as a case study description rather than a diagnostic challenge. The clue sequence needs complete restructuring: Clue 1 should present only mood/anxiety complaint without mentioning exercise; Clue 2 should reveal the temporal relationship to activity; Clue 3 should provide medication/treatment history; Clue 4 should offer examination findings that demonstrate physiological changes; Clue 5 should confirm neurobiological mechanism.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 14-year-old female with 6-month progressive hip pain and gait asymmetry, insidious onset, no trauma. Establishes age, timeline, and that symptoms are mechanical/structural in nature.
clues.rewrite: A 14-year-old adolescent female presents with progressive hip pain over the past 6 months. She reports a limp that her parents have noticed worsening gradually. No history of trauma or acute incident. Pain is felt deep in the hip joint.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations: sprinting provokes sharp pain, prolonged hip flexion causes discomfort, hip abduction activities are limited. Identifies hip as the region and movement patterns that provoke symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Family history positive for hip dysplasia (maternal aunt); patient had Pavlik harness treatment as infant for suspected developmental hip dysplasia but was cleared. This is crucial: suggests previous DDH diagnosis that may have been inadequately resolved. Narrows differential significantly to hip dysplasia spectrum.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: hip abductor weakness (4/5), FABER pain, limited painful internal rotation, subtle Trendelenburg sign. These findings support femoroacetabular impingement, acetabular dysplasia, and labral pathology. Discriminates between various hip structural pathologies.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Plain film radiographs show increased acetabular index (28° vs normal <20°) and diminished center-edge angle (18°), confirming acetabular dysplasia. MRI shows early labral chondral damage. These findings definitively establish developmental dysplasia of the hip with secondary labral damage.

Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip_205.notes: The main issue is Clue 1: it explicitly states 'structural hip pathology' and contains language ('developmental hip dysplasia' mentioned indirectly via 'adolescent-onset symptoms suggests consideration of structural hip pathology') that approaches naming the answer. For a Wordle-style game, Clue 1 should cast a much wider net — hip pain in adolescents could be femoroacetabular impingement, slipped capital femoral epiphysis, iliopsoas tendinopathy, labral pathology, or many other conditions. The phrase 'structural hip pathology' prematurely narrows the field. Clues 2-5 form a strong progressive reveal: Activity narrows to hip movement patterns, History reveals prior DDH workup (major new info), Examination shows impingement/labral signs, and Imaging confirms dysplasia with labral damage. Rewrite Clue 1 to be deliberately non-specific about pathology type.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Localized lower sacral pain, gradual onset, weeks duration, worse with sitting/standing, unclear trauma history
clues.rewrite: A middle-aged adult presents with localized pain in the lower back and buttock region that has worsened over recent weeks. Pain is aggravated by prolonged sitting and standing, with no clear injury recalled.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Sitting on firm surfaces is most provocative; lying supine relieves; forward bending worsens symptoms
clues.rewrite: The patient reports that sitting exacerbates pain dramatically, particularly on hard surfaces, whereas lying flat provides significant relief. Forward bending movements also provoke discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Gradual onset, occupational sitting, prior low back pain history, recent weight gain, failed conservative treatment

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Direct sacrococcygeal palpation reproduces pain; passive coccygeal flexion elicits sharp pain; hip ROM and lower limb neurology intact

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Anterior coccygeal deviation with increased angulation; mild disc degeneration at S5 noted but no neural compression
clues.rewrite: X-ray imaging shows anterior deviation of the coccyx with increased angulation at the sacrococcygeal joint. No fracture, subluxation, or neural compression evident on imaging.

Coccydynia_206.notes: Clue 1 is too anatomically specific—saying 'lower sacral region' and mentioning sacrococcygeal pain on opening narrows heavily. Clue 2 largely rephrases Clue 1's sitting provocativeness without adding meaningfully new functional detail. Clue 5 mentions 'S5' (which doesn't exist anatomically—should be S1 or refer to coccygeal segments) and 'intervertebral disc at S5' which is inconsistent with coccydynia and introduces confusion. The rewrite removes anatomical naming from Clue 1, strengthens Clue 2 with specific positional relief, and corrects Clue 5 to focus on coccygeal anatomy without contradictory spinal disc language.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Presents acute chest discomfort with radiation to left arm/jaw, pressure/heaviness quality, diaphoresis, dyspnea. Age 62, male, cardiovascular risk factors noted.
clues.rewrite: A 62-year-old male with a history of hypertension presents with acute-onset chest discomfort described as pressure and heaviness. Associated symptoms include sweating and shortness of breath. Pain began during exertion.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Symptom onset during physically demanding task, no relief with rest or position changes, worsening with attempted activity, prompted emergency call.
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot tolerate any physical exertion without symptom escalation. Rest and positional changes provide no relief. Symptoms persist at rest and are progressive.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Hypertension, hyperlipidemia on treatment, former smoker, family history of premature CAD, recent work stress preceding event by days.
clues.rewrite: Former smoker with 10-year history of hypertension and high cholesterol. Family history of early coronary disease in father (age 58). Symptoms began 3 days after major workplace stress.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Diaphoretic, HR 110, BP 150/95, fourth heart sound, pulmonary congestion signs, reduced peripheral perfusion, cool extremities.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: ST elevation in anterolateral leads with reciprocal changes, elevated troponin, LAD thrombotic occlusion on angiography.

Myocardial Infarction_207.notes: This puzzle is fundamentally unsuitable for Physiodle. Myocardial infarction is a medical emergency and medical diagnosis, not a physiotherapy diagnosis. Physiodle should feature musculoskeletal, neurological, or other conditions within the scope of physiotherapy practice (e.g., rotator cuff tear, ACL injury, cervical radiculopathy, stroke, Parkinson's disease). Additionally, Clue 1 is extremely leading—the combination of chest pressure radiating to left arm with diaphoresis in a 62-year-old male with cardiovascular risk factors essentially names the answer immediately. Clues 2 and 3 are largely redundant, merely elaborating details already implied by Clue 1 (exertional onset, risk factors, stress) without narrowing a differential that was already collapsed. Clues 4 and 5 are appropriate for their positions but are wasted because the game is already won. For a valid Physiodle puzzle, select a physiotherapy diagnosis and ensure Clue 1 is genuinely broad (e.g., 'patient with acute-onset shoulder pain and reduced overhead reaching').

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, gender, activity level (recreational runner), symptom onset (gradual, 6 weeks), location (deep buttock), pain character (deep ache), and provocative factor (sitting). Establishes broad differential including proximal hamstring, piriformis, gluteal tendinopathy, sacroiliac joint dysfunction, and referred pain from lumbar spine.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact narrows to lower limb — specifically activities that load hip extensors and flexors differentially. Running/sprinting exacerbate, walking/swimming ease symptoms. Hip flexion + knee extension is provocative. This pattern begins to distinguish proximal hamstring from piriformis (which would worsen with external rotation activities) and sacroiliac dysfunction (which typically has different loading patterns).

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Recent training error (volume increase without progression) directly implicates overuse tendinopathy over acute injury or structural pathology. Prior hip flexor treatment failure and ineffective stretching suggest the problem is not simple muscle tightness. Desk job adds sedentary loading factor. This history significantly narrows to proximal hamstring tendinopathy or lesser extent other hip extensor tendinopathies, and makes referred pain or inflammatory conditions less likely.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Resisted hip extension and adduction are reproducibly positive — this is HIGH specificity for proximal hamstring (which extends and adducts the hip). Slump test with hip flexion exacerbates pain, suggesting neural sensitisation or direct tendon stress with knee extension. SLR mildly restricted (not severely, ruling out acute nerve root compression). Palpation of ischial tuberosity tenderness localises pathology to origin. These findings strongly differentiate proximal hamstring from piriformis (which would be more provocative on resisted external rotation/abduction) and gluteus medius tendinopathy (lateral hip pain, different resistance pattern).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound shows tendon thickening and hypoechoic partial disruption at musculotendinous junction. MRI confirms proximal hamstring tendon pathology with peritendinous edema, no complete rupture. This directly visualises the proximal hamstring and rules out complete rupture, other tendon pathologies at this location, and confirms degenerative/inflammatory tendinopathy pattern. Essentially definitive for the diagnosis.

Proximal Hamstring Tendinopathy_208.notes: This is a well-structured puzzle with excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 is appropriately broad (buttock pain in a runner could be many things). Clue 2 functionally narrows to hip extensor/flexor involvement. Clue 3 adds mechanism (overuse) and rules out simple muscle tightness. Clue 4 provides examination findings (resisted hip extension/adduction, ischial palpation) that strongly distinguish proximal hamstring from differential candidates. Clue 5 confirms with imaging. Each clue adds materially new information in the appropriate order. The puzzle maintains clinical coherence throughout and would challenge an intermediate-level physio student without being unfair at any stage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Mechanism (acceleration-deceleration MVC), immediate onset, neck/head/jaw pain, variable daily symptoms
clues.rewrite: A 38-year-old reports neck pain with headache and jaw discomfort that began immediately after a motor vehicle collision. Symptoms vary throughout the day and are worsened by stress.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Head turning (especially toward affected side) aggravates pain; sustained postures and overhead activity provocative; supine resting eases symptoms; avoidance behavior (driving)

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Low-speed rear-end collision at 25 km/h, ten days prior, no prior neck history, no LOC, symptom fluctuation linked to stress and activity
clues.rewrite: The collision occurred ten days ago. Patient had no prior cervical complaints. Symptoms fluctuate with emotional stress and activity levels, raising consideration of central sensitization or psychosocial factors.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Global ROM reduction with pain on combined extension-rotation; upper trap/SCM guarding and tenderness; normal neurology

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: No fracture/malalignment, cervical lordosis straightening, MRI shows posterior muscle edema and C5-C6 disc bulging without neural compromise
clues.rewrite: MRI reveals edema in posterior cervical musculature and mild disc bulging at C5-C6 without neural involvement. X-ray shows loss of cervical lordosis but no fracture.

Whiplash_209.notes: The puzzle suffers from weak progressive reveal. Clue 1 names the mechanism (acceleration-deceleration) and immediate post-collision onset too explicitly—a student immediately suspects whiplash. Clue 3 adds timeline and prior history but doesn't meaningfully narrow from Clue 2 (already established neck/postural dysfunction). Clue 4 is solid (objective findings distinguish from other cervical conditions). Clue 5 repeats similar information between X-ray and MRI findings without clear differentiation. The sequence needs: (1) broader symptom complaint without mechanism detail, (2) functional/activity limitations, (3) clarification of mechanism and timeline (this is where whiplash context becomes relevant), (4) specific examination findings that rule in muscular vs. neural vs. discogenic patterns, (5) imaging confirmation. Reorder and rewrite Clues 1 and 3 to improve the funnel effect.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, gender, pattern of ocular/facial muscle fatigue worsening throughout day, with specific symptoms (ptosis, facial expression difficulty, speech impact)
clues.rewrite: A 35-year-old female reports progressive weakness and drooping of the eyelid that worsens as the day progresses. She also notices her face feels tired when talking or smiling for sustained periods.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Fatigue pattern with use and improvement with rest; specific functional limitations added (reading, conversation, chewing difficulties)
clues.rewrite: She has particular difficulty with activities requiring sustained muscle contraction—prolonged reading, extended conversations, and chewing become progressively harder as the day goes on. Symptoms improve markedly after sleeping.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Timeline (3 months, insidious onset, progressive); past medical history (unremarkable); recent viral illness; stress and sleep quality context

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Sustained gaze test with progressive bilateral ptosis; fatiguing response on repetitive contraction; Edrophonium test result (rapid improvement)

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Mediastinal thymoma identified; antibody serology (elevated anti-AChR); EMG finding (decremental response)

Myasthenia Gravis_210.notes: Clue 1 is too specific and diagnostic for a starting clue—mentioning ptosis, ocular/facial muscle involvement, and daily fatigue pattern essentially names the condition for anyone with neuromuscular knowledge. This collapses the differential from 10+ broad conditions to MG almost immediately. Clue 2 then largely restates the fatigue-with-use pattern already established in Clue 1, adding functional examples (reading, chewing) but not substantially narrowing further. The progressive reveal principle breaks down early. Rewrite Clue 1 to be symptom-only without specifying facial/ocular anatomy, and rewrite Clue 2 to emphasize the temporal fatigue pattern and rest-response as NEW information. Clues 3, 4, and 5 are appropriately specific and follow a logical diagnostic sequence.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes patient demographics, symptom character (sharp, radiating, burning), distribution (neck to shoulder/arm/hand), and neurological features (numbness/tingling in C8/T1 dermatome). Appropriately broad opening.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Adds functional context and movement-pattern information: aggravating factors (sitting, overhead reach, extension, ipsilateral rotation) and relieving factors (flexion, pillow support). Narrows to cervical spine pathology affecting nerve root irritation.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Provides timeline (gradual onset 2 months ago), mechanism (no trauma, occupational posture, recent keyboard work increase), and prior episode history. Adds occupational context that refines aetiology but does not meaningfully narrow differential beyond what clues 1+2 already suggest.
clues.rewrite: Symptoms began gradually two months ago without clear injury. Patient works in finance with heavy keyboard use and poor ergonomic setup. Previous neck pain episodes responded to conservative care. Current exacerbation followed a prolonged period of working from home with a makeshift desk setup.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Provides specific objective findings: upper limb tension test reproduction, cervical ROM restriction (extension/rotation), motor weakness (grip), and sensory loss (C7 dermatome). These findings support nerve root compression but C7 dermatome loss is INCONSISTENT with C8/T1 symptoms described in Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: On examination, the right upper limb tension test reproduces arm pain and dysesthesia in the fourth and fifth fingers. Cervical extension and right rotation are restricted and painful. Grip strength is reduced on the right. Sensation to light touch is diminished over the medial forearm and small finger (C8 dermatome).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI findings confirm disc herniation at C5-C6 with lateral recess stenosis compressing the C6 nerve root, foraminal narrowing, and root irritation signal changes. However, C6 root compression is INCONSISTENT with C8/T1 neurological findings (4th and 5th finger symptoms, C8 dermatome loss) described in earlier clues.
clues.rewrite: Magnetic resonance imaging of the cervical spine demonstrates a left-sided herniated disc at C6-C7 with lateral recess stenosis compressing the C8 nerve root. The nerve root shows signal changes consistent with irritation, and moderate foraminal narrowing is present.

Cervical Radiculopathy_211.notes: The puzzle has significant internal inconsistencies in dermatome/myotome correlation. Clues 1 and 2 describe C8/T1 distribution (4th and 5th fingers, burning in hand), but Clue 4 references C7 dermatome loss and Clue 5 references C6 root compression. A C6 root lesion would cause C6 dermatome (lateral arm/thumb/index finger) and C6 myotome findings, not C8/T1. The puzzle's clinical story is incoherent across clues. Additionally, Clue 3 adds only occupational elaboration without narrowing the differential further—it could apply to multiple cervical conditions. Clues 1+2 already strongly suggest cervical radiculopathy; the history in Clue 3 mostly confirms what is already implied. Rewrite Clues 3, 4, and 5 to establish anatomical consistency (either C6 root or C8 root, but not both), and ensure Clue 3 adds genuine new discriminatory information (e.g., progressive neurological deficit over time, failed conservative therapy, or comorbidities that affect prognosis).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, sport (elite soccer), insidious onset over 3 weeks, midline lower abdominal/groin pain with bilateral medial thigh radiation, variable intensity

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Worsening with high-impact, twisting, kicking; adduction against resistance reproduces sharp pain; relative rest/stretching provide relief; 50% training reduction

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Recent training load increase without progression; prior similar episode 6 months ago resolved conservatively; acute trigger was 5-day intensive camp with explosive movements

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Pubic symphysis palpation tenderness; adductor squeeze test at 45° reproduces pain; bilateral crossed adductor palpation tenderness; reduced hip abduction strength
clues.rewrite: On examination, hip adduction strength is markedly reduced bilaterally, and pain is reproduced with resisted hip adduction in standing. Single-leg stance on the affected side produces groin discomfort. No neurological deficits detected; lower limb strength otherwise intact except adductors.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: X-ray shows pubic symphysis irregularity, sclerosis, bilateral marginal osteophytes; MRI shows bone marrow edema at pubic rami and inflammatory changes around symphysis
clues.rewrite: MRI of the pelvis demonstrates bone marrow edema localized to the superior pubic rami with reactive inflammatory changes at the symphyseal margins. CT imaging confirms degenerative changes with early osteophyte formation and symphyseal joint space narrowing, distinguishing this from acute soft tissue strain alone.

Osteitis Pubis_212.notes: Clues 1–3 form a coherent, well-structured clinical narrative with excellent progressive narrowing. However, Clues 4 and 5 both heavily emphasize pubic symphysis pathology and imaging findings that directly confirm osteitis pubis—they are largely redundant with each other and with the location/mechanism already established by Clues 1–3. Clue 4 should differentiate osteitis pubis from adductor-dominant pathology (e.g., adductor longus tendinopathy, gracilis syndrome) or other groin conditions by highlighting functional motor deficits or special tests that point to symphyseal involvement rather than pure muscle injury. Clue 5 should then use imaging to confirm symphyseal degenerative/inflammatory pathology. As written, the puzzle narrows too early and becomes obvious after Clue 3; by Clue 4, there is minimal additional diagnostic value.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 31-year-old baseball pitcher with acute sharp medial elbow pain during throwing 3 weeks ago, progressively limiting throwing ability
clues.rewrite: A 31-year-old athlete presents with acute onset sharp pain on the inside of the elbow that started 3 weeks ago during a throwing activity. The pain is severe and has progressively worsened, preventing him from returning to his sport.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Throwing activity (especially high-velocity) reproduces pain; non-throwing activities pain-free; unable to return to competitive sport; rest provides mild improvement
clues.rewrite: Pain is strictly activity-dependent and occurs with throwing at any velocity. Non-throwing upper extremity activities—strength training, daily tasks, overhead reaching—are completely pain-free. The pattern is consistent and reproducible with every throwing attempt.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: 20 years throwing experience; no specific traumatic event; normal pre-season training; no prior elbow injury/surgery; team physicians suspect ligament injury
clues.rewrite: No acute traumatic event or fall preceded the pain—it emerged during routine training in an athlete with decades of throwing experience. No prior significant elbow pathology. Interestingly, the onset was insidious despite normal pre-season conditioning, suggesting a cumulative overuse mechanism rather than acute injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Point tenderness over medial collateral ligament; positive moving valgus stress test (valgus at 90° elbow flexion + external rotation reproduces sharp pain); positive milking maneuver; increased ligamentous laxity with soft end-feel; intact neurology

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Complete full-thickness tear of anterior bundle of ulnar collateral ligament with fiber discontinuity and edema; posterior bundle intact; joint effusion; no osteochondral lesions; no flexor-pronator tendinopathy; tear at proximal origin

Ulnar Collateral Ligament Injury_213.notes: Progressive reveal fails at Clues 1–3. Clue 1 almost names the diagnosis outright (medial elbow pain + baseball pitcher + acute throwing phase = UCL injury). Clue 2 simply elaborates on throwing mechanics already stated in Clue 1 without meaningfully narrowing the differential—a player already knows it's throwing-related medial elbow pain, so learning 'throwing reproduces it' adds minimal new information. Clue 3 adds historical context (no trauma, decades of experience) but this is mostly expected background and doesn't differentiate UCL injury from other medial elbow conditions (flexor-pronator strain, medial epicondylitis, etc.). Clues 4–5 are excellent: Clue 4 provides specific clinical tests that differentiate ligament injury from tendinopathy, and Clue 5 confirms the exact anatomy and tear pattern. The puzzle is front-loaded and needs significant rebalancing. Clue 1 should be vaguer (remove 'medial', remove 'pitching phase'); Clues 2–3 should add distinct, narrowing information (e.g., Clue 2 could emphasize that daily activities are pain-free while throwing is not—this rules out epicondylitis; Clue 3 could emphasize insidious onset vs. traumatic, suggesting overuse vs. acute).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, sport, posterior shoulder pain with apprehension, specific mechanism (anterior dislocation 2 weeks prior)
clues.rewrite: A 31-year-old recreational basketball player presents with posterior shoulder pain and apprehension triggered by abduction combined with external rotation. Onset was acute two weeks ago during competition, requiring emergency room evaluation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitation: abduction + external rotation movements cause pain and instability sensation; overhead throwing and defensive positioning provoke symptoms; rest provides relief
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot perform overhead throwing or defensive positioning maneuvers required in basketball. Symptoms are reproducible and episodic rather than constant; pain-free periods occur with activity modification.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: First major shoulder injury; no post-reduction imaging obtained; premature return to sport; prior minor strains without functional impact
clues.rewrite: No prior significant shoulder pathology. Patient was immobilized briefly post-reduction and returned to sport within two weeks. Post-reduction imaging was deferred at the time.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Positive apprehension and relocation tests; loss of posterior humeral head bone contour on palpation; resisted external rotation at 90° abduction reproduces apprehension

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI confirms posterolateral humeral head compression fracture, 20% of articular surface involvement; anterior labral damage noted

Hill-Sachs Lesion_214.notes: Major problems: Clue 1 includes the precipitating dislocation detail which, combined with the posterior pain location and apprehension in a specific pattern, makes Hill-Sachs lesion highly obvious even to a novice clinician—this is near-diagnosis-level specificity for a Complaint clue. Clue 2 largely reiterates the movement pattern already established in Clue 1 (abduction + ER) without adding substantially new functional insight. Clue 3 adds context but doesn't meaningfully narrow beyond what dislocation + no imaging already implies. Clues 4 and 5 are appropriately specific. Rewrite Clues 1 and 2 to delay the mechanism reveal and separate the movement pattern from the functional impact more clearly.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, gender, specific pelvic/perineal symptoms (heaviness, bulging), bowel dysfunction (difficulty evacuating, manual splinting), timeline (2-year progression)
clues.rewrite: A 58-year-old female reports progressive sensations of heaviness and fullness in the perineal region, accompanied by difficulty emptying her bowels. She has begun using manual pressure around the perineum during defecation to facilitate evacuation, a compensatory strategy that has become increasingly necessary over two years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional worsening with standing/exertion; symptom relief supine; persistent incomplete evacuation despite straining
clues.rewrite: Symptoms worsen significantly with prolonged standing and physical exertion; they resolve completely when lying flat. The patient reports incomplete evacuation despite 15+ minutes of straining, necessitating repeated evacuation attempts or manual assistance.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Three vaginal deliveries with a 4.2 kg baby; long-standing constipation (>10 years); chronic straining; recent weight gain and abdominal weakness

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective posterior wall bulge on Valsalva (3 cm beyond hymenal ring); pelvic floor weakness (3/5); perineal descent (4 cm during strain)
clues.rewrite: Vaginal examination reveals a smooth, mobile bulge of the posterior wall that protrudes visibly with Valsalva, measuring approximately 3 centimeters distal to the hymenal ring. Pelvic floor muscles are weak (3/5 on digital contraction). Perineal descent during straining measures 4 centimeters. No palpable masses or tenderness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Defecography shows posterior rectovaginal septum displacement (3.5 cm) with barium retention during evacuation; dynamic MRI confirms anatomy and altered anorectal mechanics

Rectocele_215.notes: Major issue: Clue 1 is far too specific and essentially names the diagnosis ('sensation of bulging in posterior vaginal wall' + 'manual splinting of perineum during defecation' = rectocele). This undermines the entire puzzle. A physiotherapy student reading only Clue 1 would immediately arrive at the answer without needing subsequent clues. Clue 2 largely rephrases Clue 1's functional information without adding discriminatory value — both mention heaviness, incomplete evacuation, and the postural improvement. Clue 4 adds minimal beyond what Clues 1–3 already established: the bulge and splinting behavior already implied the anatomical finding. Rewrite Clue 1 to focus only on the complaint of pelvic heaviness and defecatory dysfunction, removing 'bulging' and 'manual splinting.' This keeps the differential broad (pelvic floor weakness, pelvic organ prolapse, sacroiliac dysfunction, etc.). Then let Clue 2 narrow to the posterior compartment and positional nature. Clue 3 establishes risk factors. Clue 4 visualizes the posterior wall bulge and measures descent. Clue 5 confirms the rectovaginal involvement. This would create a proper diagnostic funnel.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Widespread multi-regional pain, fatigue, sleep disturbance, cognitive symptoms, 2-year duration. Establishes systemic, non-localized presentation with neurovegetative symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Inconsistent, disproportionate pain response to activity; emotional stress triggers; poor response to escalating treatment. Introduces the critical concept of central sensitisation (pain disproportionate to stimulus) and treatment resistance.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Insidious onset post-injury with negative investigations; multiple failed treatments; preceding psychosocial stressor (work stress). Adds temporal context and establishes that structural pathology does NOT explain symptom severity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Widespread allodynia and tender point criteria met; otherwise normal neurology. Confirms central sensitisation sign (allodynia) but this is largely redundant with Clue 2's assertion of 'pain disproportionate to stimulus'—allodynia is just the examination-level manifestation of that same concept.
clues.rewrite: Widespread allodynia is demonstrated with pain to light touch across multiple body regions. Tender point count meets diagnostic criteria with significant pain provocation. Notably, strength, reflexes, and motor coordination are fully intact; no focal neurological deficits. Two-point discrimination and proprioception are normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Minimal structural pathology on conventional imaging; fMRI shows altered central pain processing with amplified responses in pain-sensitive brain regions; negative systemic/inflammatory workup. Definitively confirms central (CNS) mechanism rather than peripheral pathology.

Central Sensitisation Syndrome_216.notes: Overall structure is sound and follows the progressive reveal principle well. Clue 1–2–3 funnel the differential effectively: from 'widespread multi-system pain' (many causes) → 'pain disproportionate to stimulus + treatment resistance' (narrows to central sensitisation/psychosomatic spectrum) → 'negative imaging, prior stressor, insidious onset' (strongly suggests central sensitisation over other chronic pain syndromes). Clue 4 has a minor redundancy issue: allodynia on examination is essentially the clinical sign that Clue 2 already conceptually introduced ('pain disproportionate to stimulus'). Rewrite suggested to add genuinely new discriminatory information (intact motor/sensory modalities that rule out neuropathy). Clue 5 is appropriately definitive and uses advanced imaging (fMRI) to confirm central mechanism. The puzzle is well-constructed but Clue 4 could be strengthened.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Swelling and redness over posterior elbow, acute onset over 2 days, mild-moderate discomfort, mild restriction of elbow flexion
clues.rewrite: A 34-year-old male presents with acute swelling and redness at the back of the elbow that developed over two days. He reports mild to moderate discomfort and denies significant functional loss. His occupation involves repetitive contact of the elbows with work surfaces.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional pattern: symptoms worse with elbow flexion and direct pressure to posterior elbow; improved with extension and ice
clues.rewrite: Leaning or applying direct pressure on the elbow during work significantly exacerbates swelling. Resting the arm in extension and applying ice provide temporary relief. Most other arm activities are minimally affected.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Occupational mechanism (frequent elbow contact), prior self-resolving episodes, recent increase in work demands triggering acute exacerbation

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Localized swelling over olecranon with warmth and mild erythema; non-fluctuant; mild ROM restriction in flexion; extension pain-free

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound confirms fluid in olecranon bursa (~1.5 cm) with echogenic particles; MRI confirms serous inflammation without infection; minimal surrounding soft tissue edema

Olecranon Bursitis_217.notes: Clue 1 is severely compromised by explicitly naming 'olecranon' and describing 'bursitis' symptoms so directly that the answer is nearly given away. A physiotherapy student would have olecranon bursitis as their top guess before even seeing Clue 2. Clue 2 then largely restates the mechanism and symptom pattern already established in Clue 1 without adding substantively new discriminatory information — the player learns nothing about what actually distinguishes this from other posterior elbow swellings. Clues 3–5 are appropriately structured and progressive, but the early clues fail to create a proper diagnostic funnel. The puzzle is too easy and needs significant rewriting of Clues 1 and 2 to maintain clinical credibility.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Demographics (47-year-old office worker), symptom character (progressive numbness and tingling in median nerve distribution), temporal pattern (worst at night/early morning, sleep disruption), associated mild forearm pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact tied to specific activities (keyboard/mouse work, driving, gripping aggravate; wrist splinting and stretching improve symptoms). This adds the critical functional-activity nexus not yet established.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Occupational timeline (18 years as typist), onset timing and progression (3 months, worsening despite ergonomic changes), comorbidities (recent weight gain and hypothyroidism). These contextualize the mechanism and add risk factors that narrow differential toward CTS vs. other neuropathies or systemic causes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective clinical findings: positive Phalen test (30 seconds, reproduces symptoms), positive Tinel sign at wrist, preserved grip strength, impaired two-point discrimination in index finger. These are median nerve-specific special tests and sensory findings that confirm nerve involvement and exclude motor weakness, differentiating CTS from motor neuropathy or cervical pathology.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Electrodiagnostic and sonographic confirmation: prolonged median motor distal latency (5.8 ms), reduced median sensory conduction velocity, median nerve swelling (12 mm²) at carpal tunnel inlet. This provides objective electrophysiological and structural evidence of median nerve compression at the wrist, confirming diagnosis and excluding other compressive or systemic neuropathies.

Carpal Tunnel Syndrome_218.notes: This is a well-structured puzzle with strong progressive reveal. Clue 1 establishes broad symptom picture with median nerve distribution and night symptoms (classic CTS presentation, but patient might initially consider cervical radiculopathy, thoracic outlet, or other upper limb neuropathies). Clue 2 narrows by showing activity-dependent aggravation and response to wrist-specific interventions, eliminating cervical/systemic causes. Clue 3 adds occupational risk and comorbidities (hypothyroidism is a CTS risk factor), further crystallizing mechanical compression over neuropathy. Clue 4 provides specific median nerve special tests and sensory findings that differentiate CTS from ulnar neuropathy, cervical radiculopathy, or other upper limb nerve lesions. Clue 5 confirms with electrophysiology and ultrasound. Each clue meaningfully narrows without naming the diagnosis. Differential funnel works as intended: broad → activity-specific → mechanism/risk factors → median nerve signs → imaging confirmation. No redundancy or information leakage between clues.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient age, symptom character (dull at rest, sharp with movement), and functional limitations (overhead activities, hand-to-mouth). Establishes shoulder region.
clues.rewrite: A 68-year-old retired teacher presents with shoulder pain and stiffness. Pain is dull at rest but sharp with movement attempts. Reports difficulty with overhead activities and hand-to-mouth movements. Onset was acute, following a surgical procedure 6 weeks ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific ADL limitations (dressing, grooming, reaching) and movement pattern (external rotation and abduction worse). Passive > active movement already mentioned in Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot don upper clothing or reach behind head. Pain significantly limits external rotation and abduction. Passive motion is notably better tolerated than active attempts.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Surgical intervention 6 weeks ago for end-stage osteoarthritis with bone-on-bone degeneration. This is the critical discriminator — the answer is hidden in plain sight here. Comorbidities are generic filler.
clues.rewrite: Recent surgical procedure 6 weeks ago for end-stage joint degeneration unresponsive to conservative management. Pre-operative imaging showed bone-on-bone articulation and severe cartilage loss. Medical history includes polyarticular osteoarthritis and controlled hypertension. No prior shoulder surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific ROM measurements (passive ER 35°, passive FF 110°, active ABD 30°), muscle grades (2/5 due to pain), and end-feel quality. These are objective findings consistent with early post-operative phase.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Confirms prosthetic positioning, stable fixation, and absence of complications (loosening, subluxation, fracture, cement issues). This confirms the surgical repair is sound but does not distinguish post-TSR from other post-operative shoulder conditions.
clues.rewrite: Post-operative radiographs demonstrate humeral and glenoid prosthetic components in appropriate anatomical alignment. No evidence of component subsidence, loosening, or periprosthetic fracture. Cement mantle is intact without gaps or radiolucencies.

Post-Total Shoulder Replacement_219.notes: CRITICAL ISSUE: The diagnosis (Total Shoulder Replacement) is essentially revealed in Clue 1 where 'glenohumeral region' combined with 'dull ache at rest, sharp with movement, difficulty overhead' could suggest primary shoulder pathology, but more importantly in Clue 3 which explicitly states 'surgical intervention 6 weeks ago for end-stage joint degeneration' — this is effectively announcing the answer to any clinician. The puzzle fails the progressive reveal principle because the answer is embedded in Clue 3's history rather than being deducible from objective findings. A proper progression would: (1) present post-operative shoulder pain/stiffness symptoms generically, (2) reveal functional limitations, (3) hint at recent surgery without naming it, (4) provide examination findings showing pain-limited ROM and guarding consistent with early post-op recovery, (5) confirm with imaging that shows prosthetic components. As currently structured, a player could guess TSR after Clue 3 without needing Clues 4-5.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes age (52), occupation (tradesperson), symptom location (base of great toe), character (progressive pain, stiffness), and aggravating factors (weight-bearing, push-off). Broadly consistent with forefoot pathology but not anatomy-specific.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Adds functional limitation details (difficulty with propulsion, inclines, stairs) and introduces specific MOV limitations (plantarflexion AND dorsiflexion of great toe affected). Mentions footwear preference (rocker shoes better). Narrows from general forefoot to first MTPj-specific dysfunction.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Timeline (2-year progressive), swelling pattern (post-work), prior mechanism (awkward landing 3 years ago), and family history (OA). Suggests degenerative/post-traumatic pathology rather than inflammatory or acute injury. Supports OA differential.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Provides specific ROM values (dorsiflexion 15°, plantarflexion 45°, both with direction-specific findings), end-feel quality (firm), palpable findings (osteophyte at medial 1stMTPj), and movement quality (crepitus). Differentiates hallux rigidus (limited dorsiflexion with firm end-feel) from other 1stMTPj conditions. Highly specific discriminator.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Confirms OA diagnosis with osteophytes, joint space narrowing, surface flattening, and subchondral sclerosis at 1stMTPj. Differentiates hallux rigidus from hallux limitus (no structural changes) or capsulitis (no degenerative changes). Near-definitive confirmation.

Hallux Rigidus_220.notes: Excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 casts appropriately wide net (age, occupation, forefoot pain, stiffness). Clue 2 narrows to first MTPj dysfunction via functional and ROM language. Clue 3 establishes degenerative/post-traumatic context, ruling out inflammatory or acute causes. Clue 4 provides the critical exam discriminator: specific dorsiflexion limitation with firm end-feel and palpable osteophytes—this is what separates hallux rigidus from hallux limitus or other 1stMTPj conditions. Clue 5 confirms with imaging. Each clue eliminates a distinct set of differentials. No redundancy, no premature naming, appropriate difficulty arc.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographics (34F, third trimester), symptom onset (8 weeks ago, progressive with pregnancy), location (unilateral lower back/buttock), character (sharp and aching), impact on function and sleep

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations (walking, stairs, rolling in bed) and positions that worsen symptoms (single-leg stance, leg crossing); diurnal pattern (worse at end of day, with prolonged standing/walking)

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Parity (second pregnancy, first uncomplicated 6 years ago), current pregnancy status (normal progression, no complications), explicit attribution to pregnancy-related hormonal factors and weight gain, absence of prior pelvic/back pain history

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific sacroiliac-region clinical findings: long dorsal ligament test positive on affected side, Patrick's test and FABER positive, SIJ palpation tenderness, midline sacral tenderness absent, hip abductor weakness (pain-inhibited)

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Rules out fetal/obstetric pathology (normal development, fluid, pelvic anatomy), rules out structural/degenerative changes if MRI performed; confirms diagnosis by elimination
clues.rewrite: Pelvic ultrasound shows normal fetal development with no structural abnormalities. No signs of sacroiliac joint hypermobility or inflammatory changes on imaging. Diagnosis confirmed by clinical presentation and exclusion of alternative structural pathology.

Antenatal Pelvic Girdle Pain_221.notes: Progressive reveal is well-structured. Clue 1 establishes pregnancy-related unilateral lower back/buttock pain. Clue 2 narrows to movement-dependent sacroiliac pattern. Clue 3 confirms the antenatal context and hormonal contribution, eliminating non-pregnancy etiologies. Clue 4 provides localizing examination signs specific to sacroiliac dysfunction. Clue 5 has minor weakness: it's somewhat generic in ruling out fetal issues (which weren't really on the differential for a physio diagnosis) and could be more specific about what imaging confirms regarding the diagnosis itself. The puzzle is solid and clinically coherent overall.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient is post-amputation with pain in absent limb; character of pain (burning, cramping, shooting); pain is spontaneous and unrelated to residual limb pressure.
clues.rewrite: A 45-year-old manual laborer presents with pain in a region of his body that no longer exists. He describes burning, cramping, and shooting sensations that arise spontaneously without clear external trigger. The pain is not relieved by pressing or manipulating the area where he feels it.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: pain worsens with prosthetic use and weight-bearing; temporal pattern (worse morning/evening); exacerbating factors (stress, weather, barometric pressure); ameliorating factor (distraction).

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism (traumatic crush injury with infection); timeline (8 months post-amputation, phantom pain emerged gradually weeks post-op after initial pain settled); absence of prior nerve or chronic pain history.
clues.rewrite: Amputation followed a crush injury with subsequent infection. Initial post-operative pain resolved within weeks, but then a different pain syndrome emerged and has persisted. No prior history of peripheral nerve injury or chronic pain conditions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific sensory findings in residual limb (hypersensitivity, allodynia medially); absence of trigger points; palpation over distal end reproduces phantom sensations; preserved adjacent joint ROM.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging rules out structural complications: normal bone healing, no neuroma, normal vascularization, normal scar maturation.
clues.rewrite: Imaging workup is notably unremarkable: no neuroma formation on ultrasound or MRI, no local vascular insufficiency, normal bone healing, and scar tissue maturation as expected. No structural or vascular pathology identified.

Phantom Limb Pain_222.notes: The main problem is Clue 1 explicitly names 'phantom limb pain' by describing pain in an 'absent limb'—this is too direct and removes the diagnostic challenge. A skilled physiotherapist reading only Clue 1 would immediately know the answer. Rewrite Clue 1 to describe the clinical presentation without explicitly referencing amputation or absence. Clue 3 mostly restates information already established in Clues 1–2 (post-operative timeline, gradual emergence). Clue 5 is defensive rather than diagnostic—it rules things out but doesn't actively narrow a differential between remaining candidates.
