Consider rewriting to focus on what IS present neurophysiologically (e.g., cortical mapping changes, altered sensory pathways) rather than what is NOT present structurally. The progressive reveal would be stronger if Clue 1 cast a wider net (could be any neuropathic pain condition), Clue 2 narrowed to post-operative pain syndrome, Clue 3 clarified the timeline and mechanism (amputation with delayed onset), Clue 4 confirmed phantom-specific examination findings, and Clue 5 provided imaging or neurophysiology that ruled out confounders and confirmed central sensitization or cortical reorganisation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient presentation: severe dyspnea, hypoxemia, on mechanical ventilation, rapid onset over hours post-sepsis from pneumonia
clues.rewrite: 58-year-old admitted to ICU 3 days ago with acute-onset severe breathlessness and low blood oxygen levels. Deteriorated rapidly over 24-48 hours despite starting antibiotics. Now requires mechanical ventilation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — restates that patient is on mechanical ventilation and cannot perform activities. No NEW functional distinction.
clues.rewrite: Even small positional changes (head of bed adjustments) or brief ventilator weaning attempts trigger rapid oxygen desaturation and respiratory distress. Oxygen requirements remain high despite maximum support settings.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Preceding infection (pneumonia/sepsis 5 days prior), comorbidities (COPD, diabetes), elevated inflammatory markers, worsening trajectory despite antibiotics

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Bilateral crackles, reduced chest wall compliance, specific ABG values (PaO2 65 mmHg on FiO2 0.8), bilateral infiltrates on auscultation. Adds objective physiological data not in prior clues.
clues.rewrite: Bilateral fine crackles throughout lung fields. Markedly reduced chest wall compliance requiring high ventilatory pressures. Refractory hypoxemia: PaO2 remains 60-70 mmHg despite FiO2 >0.6 and appropriate PEEP titration. No cardiac murmurs or signs of heart failure on examination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Bilateral diffuse infiltrates, non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema, ground-glass opacities, normal cardiac silhouette. Excludes cardiogenic cause.
clues.rewrite: Chest X-ray: bilateral diffuse infiltrates with ground-glass pattern and consolidation. CT confirms extensive bilateral opacities sparing dorsal-dependent zones. Cardiac silhouette normal; BNP negative. Imaging findings consistent with acute diffuse alveolar damage.

Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome_223.notes: Critical failures: Clue 1 essentially names the answer ('acute respiratory failure' post-sepsis with bilateral infiltrates = ARDS). Clue 2 adds almost nothing new beyond restating mechanical ventilation dependency. Clue 3 is solid but Clue 4 and 5 largely rephrase and reinforce the same findings (bilateral infiltrates, hypoxemia, non-cardiogenic) without clear discrimination between remaining differentials. The puzzle lacks a meaningful funnel effect. By Clue 1, a clinician has already locked onto ARDS as the top diagnosis. Progressive reveal is essentially non-existent. For a physio context, this is also problematic: ARDS is an ICU medical diagnosis with limited direct physio-specific differential — the puzzle should focus on what physio assessment (e.g., secretion clearance, ventilator weaning readiness, positioning effects) distinguishes this from other acute respiratory conditions that might present to a physio in acute care.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Lateral knee pain, giving way sensation, unpredictable episodes with certain movements, apprehension. Establishes region (lateral knee) and primary symptoms (instability).
clues.rewrite: A 38-year-old recreational athlete presents with episodes of lateral knee instability and giving way during activity. Pain is localized below the lateral knee line and occurs unpredictably with certain movements, causing apprehension.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific movement patterns that trigger symptoms: pivoting, lateral bounding, direction changes, running on uneven surfaces, single-leg standing, jumping. Narrows to rotational/dynamic instability rather than static pain.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Direct blow to lateral knee 2 years ago with delayed onset of instability; initial swelling resolved but chronic instability persists. No systemic ligamentous laxity. Establishes traumatic mechanism and rules out generalized hypermobility.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Positive anterior drawer test of fibula with subluxation; fibular head prominence and anterior glide on passive flexion; intact LCL; no anterolateral rotatory instability. Specifically identifies tibiofibular pathology and excludes ACL/LCL injuries.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI shows anterior/posterior tibiofibular ligament disruption; ultrasound documents 8mm fibular head translation; CT confirms anterior subluxation. Provides definitive confirmation of PTFJ instability with quantified translation.
clues.rewrite: MRI demonstrates disruption of the anterior and posterior tibiofibular ligaments consistent with chronic instability. Ultrasound shows fibular head translation of 8mm compared to the contralateral side.

Proximal Tibiofibular Joint Instability_.notes: The puzzle has a sound progressive reveal overall. Clue 1 is too specific—mentioning 'below the lateral knee joint line' and 'sensation of knee giving way' strongly points toward PTFJ instability before clue 3 is even revealed. Clue 5 has minimal new diagnostic value beyond what Clue 4 already established—all three imaging modalities confirm the same finding (anterior subluxation/ligament disruption) without adding differential discrimination. The player's path to diagnosis should rely on Clue 4's examination findings to distinguish PTFJ instability from other lateral knee instabilities; Clue 5 should confirm rather than merely restate. Rewrite Clue 1 to be broader (remove anatomical specificity) and Clue 5 to avoid redundancy with Clue 4.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, sport context, sudden sharp posterior ankle pain on push-off, immediate fall, rapid swelling, inability to bear weight
clues.rewrite: A 42-year-old recreational badminton player reports sudden sharp pain in the posterior ankle during a push-off movement. Patient immediately fell and was unable to bear weight. Rapid swelling developed over the following hours.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific loss of plantarflexion (0/5 strength implied), inability to push off, inability to stand on toes, pain on dorsiflexion stretch
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot walk without assistance. Plantarflexion against gravity is completely absent. Attempts to stand on toes are impossible. Passive dorsiflexion stretch produces sharp pain in posterior ankle.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Recent training load increase (4 weeks prior), no prior ankle injuries, no chronic tendon symptoms before rupture, patient generally fit
clues.rewrite: Patient had increased training volume over 4 weeks prior to injury. No prior ankle injuries, chronic ankle pain, or Achilles complaints. No medical comorbidities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Palpable gap 5 cm above insertion, positive Thompson test (calf squeeze does not plantarflex), 0/5 plantarflexion strength, swelling and ecchymosis
clues.rewrite: Palpable gap in Achilles tendon approximately 5 cm proximal to insertion. Thompson test positive: manual calf squeeze produces no ankle plantarflexion. Swelling and ecchymosis present posterior ankle.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Complete discontinuity with 2 cm separation, full-thickness tear at musculotendinous junction, edema and hemorrhage, sagittal imaging detail

Achilles Tendon Rupture_225.notes: This puzzle has a critical flaw: Clue 1 is far too specific and essentially names the answer for an experienced physiotherapist. The phrase 'sudden sharp pain in posterior ankle during push-off' combined with 'immediate fall, unable to bear weight, rapid swelling' is essentially diagnostic of Achilles rupture before any subsequent clues. Clues 2–4 are severely redundant, each elaborating on the same functional deficit (loss of plantarflexion) and examination findings (Thompson test, strength 0/5) already heavily implied by Clue 1. The differential narrowing is non-existent: a clinician reading only Clue 1 would immediately think 'Achilles rupture' and all subsequent clues merely confirm this without ruling out alternatives or adding meaningfully new information. The puzzle should: (1) broaden Clue 1 to remove 'posterior ankle' specificity and 'push-off' context if possible, or at least make it plausible for 5–10 diagnoses (acute ankle ligament injury, gastrocnemius strain, plantaris rupture, calcaneal fracture, compartment syndrome, etc.); (2) use Clue 2 to narrow to posterior ankle/plantarflexion pattern; (3) use Clue 3 to further discriminate (no prior chronic symptoms vs. chronic tendinopathy history); (4) use Clue 4 to distinguish rupture from strain/tear using Thompson test and gap findings; (5) use Clue 5 to confirm rupture over other injuries. As written, this is too easy by Clue 1 and has no genuine progressive reveal.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient context (56F, 2mo post-chemotherapy for breast cancer) + broad symptoms (fatigue, weakness, functional decline, scar pain, upper extremity pain)
clues.rewrite: A 56-year-old woman presents 2 months after completing cancer treatment with progressive fatigue, generalised weakness, and reduced functional capacity. She reports pain in a surgical scar region and the ipsilateral upper extremity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations: overhead/lifting loss, dramatic walking tolerance decline (2 miles → 10 min), stair difficulty, requiring frequent rest, morning shoulder stiffness 30-45 min
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot perform overhead activities or lifting with affected arm. Walking tolerance has declined markedly to 10 minutes. Stairs precipitate significant fatigue and she requires frequent rest breaks throughout the day.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Treatment timeline specificity (neoadjuvant chemo, surgical reconstruction 8 weeks ago, post-op radiation 4 weeks ago), comorbidity (hypertension), and CIPN in feet/lower legs
clues.rewrite: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy completed 8 weeks ago, followed by surgical reconstruction. Post-operative radiation therapy completed 4 weeks prior. Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy developed in bilateral lower legs.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: shoulder ROM (85° abduction), grip strength asymmetry (18 vs 28 kg), axillary cording with restriction, sensory changes (glove-like paresthesia), ankle weakness (4/5 bilaterally)
clues.rewrite: Shoulder abduction limited to 85 degrees due to pain and tissue tightness. Axillary cording evident with palpable restriction and pain. Grip strength asymmetry: 18 kg affected vs 28 kg unaffected side. Ankle dorsiflexion strength 4/5 bilaterally with glove-like sensory loss in feet.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Structural imaging confirms: adequate surgical reconstruction with normal perfusion, no recurrence on chest CT, MRI excludes nerve compression and lymphedema
clues.rewrite: MRI upper extremity shows no evidence of lymphedema, nerve compression, or structural recurrence. Chest imaging obtained during cancer surveillance shows no distant metastases.

Cancer Rehabilitation_226.notes: CRITICAL FAILURES: (1) Clue 1 essentially names the answer by stating 'post-chemotherapy for breast cancer' — the diagnosis 'Cancer Rehabilitation' is transparently implied from the complaint alone. This violates the core game mechanic. (2) Clues 2–5 are heavily redundant with Clue 1; they elaborate on already-established facts (post-treatment, upper extremity pain, functional decline, CIPN) without introducing meaningfully distinct clinical information that would force the player to narrow a differential. After Clue 1, a player already knows: oncology patient, post-chemo/surgery/radiation, fatigue, weakness, upper limb and foot pain — this already points squarely at 'cancer rehab.' Clues 2–5 simply add granular detail (e.g., 'walk 10 min not 2 miles', 'grip 18 not 28 kg', 'MRI normal') but do not narrow from a broad differential to a specific one because there IS no differential; the answer was given in Clue 1. For a playable puzzle, Clue 1 must strip away the cancer context and present only the functional complaint (fatigue, weakness, reduced mobility, upper extremity pain). Clues 2–5 would then work to systematically reveal: (2) upper limb functional loss + systemic fatigue → upper limb + systemic condition; (3) post-surgical/treatment timeline + CIPN → post-cancer treatment; (4) cording, ROM loss, strength asymmetry → post-cancer shoulder syndrome; (5) imaging normal → rules out structural recurrence/compression, confirming rehabilitation-focused diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Head trauma mechanism, acute symptom onset (headache, dizziness, photophobia, phonophobia), and retrograde amnesia establish head injury with neurological effects. Broad differential includes concussion, post-concussive syndrome, traumatic brain injury, intracranial hemorrhage, or other acute CNS injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: NEW: Symptom exacerbation with cognitive load (reading, schoolwork, typing) and vestibular provocation (head movements, balance on uneven surfaces). This adds functional impact beyond simple complaint and begins narrowing toward concussion-spectrum disorder vs. vestibular dysfunction vs. migraine.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: NEW: First head injury, no LOC but witnessed daze immediately post-impact, and anxiety about timeline. This timeline (5 days post-injury, symptoms still present) and acute presentation without prior history support acute post-concussive state. However, this clue adds MINIMAL new discriminatory power — concussion vs. other acute intracranial pathology is not meaningfully distinguished here.
clues.rewrite: No prior head injuries. The incident occurred during competitive play; witness noted 20–30 seconds of apparent confusion and disorientation immediately after impact, but no documented unconsciousness. Symptoms have persisted unchanged over 5 days despite rest; athlete was not permitted to continue playing. No vomiting, seizure, or loss of bowel/bladder control reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: NEW: King-Devick slowed reading, tandem stance impairment, mild incoordination, normal pupils and cranial nerves. These objective findings (cognitive-motor and vestibular dysfunction without focal neurological deficits) support concussion diagnosis and effectively rule out hemorrhage, focal lesion, or isolated vestibular disorder.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: NEW: Normal CT excludes acute intracranial hemorrhage, contusion, or fracture. This is near-definitive for concussion diagnosis (imaging-normal, symptom-evident head injury with cognitive/vestibular dysfunction). Confirms diagnosis and rules out structural pathology.

Concussion_227.notes: The puzzle is structurally sound overall and follows the progressive reveal principle well. Clue 1 establishes head trauma with neurological signs (broad differential). Clue 2 narrows with cognitive and vestibular symptom exacerbation. Clue 3 is the weak link — it mostly elaborates on the timeline and psychology already implicit in Clues 1–2 without adding diagnostic discrimination (concussion vs. other intracranial pathology). Clue 4 provides examination findings that strongly differentiate concussion from hemorrhage/focal lesion. Clue 5 confirms with normal imaging. Rewrite Clue 3 to add clinically distinct history elements (e.g., sequence of post-impact confusion, absence of vomiting/seizure, recovery trajectory) that further narrow the differential before examination findings appear.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age (16, athlete), onset pattern (insidious), location specificity (unilateral, off-midline), movement trigger (hyperextension/backward arching), activity context (high-intensity training). Establishes broad differential: spondylolysis, facet syndrome, muscle strain, disc herniation, stress fracture.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations tied to SPECIFIC STROKES (butterfly and backstroke worsen; freestyle spared) and LOADING PATTERN (training intensity > prolonged static postures). This is NEW — Clue 1 said 'hyperextension worsens' but didn't link it to which activities in the patient's sport. This narrows to extension-loaded repetitive activities. Also: day-end fatigue pattern suggests cumulative microtrauma rather than acute mechanical block.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: DURATION of competitive training (5 years, high volume), TIMING of onset (8 weeks ago, coinciding with intensity escalation, NOT acute trauma), family history (parental low back pain). This is NEW — Clue 1 said 'insidious' but didn't give timeline or training load context. This strongly implicates repetitive overuse/stress mechanism and suggests genetic/biomechanical predisposition. Narrows to chronic overuse: spondylolysis, chronic facet syndrome, or stress reaction. Acute disc herniation or traumatic fracture now less likely.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: UNILATERAL single-leg hyperextension (Stork test) reproduces pain — this is the classic spondylolysis sign. Extension + ipsilateral rotation worsens symptoms. Paraspinal tenderness at the level. NORMAL neuro exam rules out radiculopathy/nerve root compression. This is NEW and DISCRIMINATING — these findings differentiate spondylolysis from facet-only syndrome, discogenic pain, or neurological causes. Combined with previous clues, spondylolysis is now the leading diagnosis; bilateral facet arthropathy or central stenosis largely ruled out.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: CT shows PARS INTERARTICULARIS DEFECT (unilateral, L5) — the pathognomonic finding. SPECT shows active uptake confirming bone stress/metabolic activity. Plain films may be normal (common in early spondylolysis). This is NEW and DEFINITIVE — imaging confirms the structural lesion underlying all previous clinical findings. No other diagnosis fits this constellation.

Spondylolysis_228.notes: Excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 casts a wide net (young athlete, insidious, extension-dependent pain — could be many lumbar diagnoses). Clue 2 narrows functionally by linking pain to specific extension-loaded strokes, ruling out simple muscle strain or static postural issues. Clue 3 adds the critical training history and timing (overuse, not trauma), pushing toward chronic remodeling/stress injuries. Clue 4 provides the pathognomonic examination finding (single-leg extension test, unilateral reproduction) that strongly confirms spondylolysis and rules out discogenic/neurological mimics. Clue 5 seals it with imaging of the pars defect. The funnel works: each clue eliminates ~30-50% of remaining candidates. A skilled clinician would have high confidence after Clue 4 and be certain after Clue 5. No redundancy, no giveaways, appropriate sequencing. This is a well-designed puzzle.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 28-year-old runner with 6-week gradual-onset plantar forefoot pain under great toe; aching character worsened by running and standing
clues.rewrite: A 28-year-old runner presents with gradual-onset pain in the forefoot that has worsened over 6 weeks. Pain is described as aching discomfort under the ball of the foot and is triggered by running and prolonged standing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Pain specifically with push-off, acceleration, forefoot-strike running, sprinting, and single-leg plantarflexion movements; minimal pain with walking in supportive shoes
clues.rewrite: Pain is reproducible with activities requiring forefoot propulsion: push-off during running acceleration and sprinting provoke symptoms. Single-leg plantarflexion (rising on toes) causes significant discomfort. Walking in supportive footwear is tolerated.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Recent training load increase (15 to 30 mpw over 4 weeks); prior gait alteration from heel blister 2 months ago; running primarily on hard surfaces (concrete/asphalt)

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Palpation over medial sesamoid reproduces pain; localized tenderness to sesamoid region; reduced plantarflexion strength of first MTPJ; single-leg plantarflexion positive
clues.rewrite: Palpation directly over the medial sesamoid elicits sharp tenderness and pain reproduction. Pain is strictly localized to the sesamoid region with no surrounding plantar tissue involvement. First metatarsophalangeal plantarflexion strength is diminished due to pain inhibition.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Normal sesamoid alignment and no fracture on X-ray; MRI shows bone marrow edema in sesamoid and surrounding soft tissue inflammation; ultrasound shows increased echogenicity consistent with inflammation
clues.rewrite: Weight-bearing radiographs exclude fracture or displacement of the sesamoid bones. MRI reveals bone marrow edema within the medial sesamoid and plantar soft tissue inflammation. Ultrasound shows increased echogenicity of the sesamoid consistent with inflammatory change.

Sesamoiditis_229.notes: Critical problem: Clue 1 is far too specific—'plantar forefoot pain localized under the great toe ball' + 'medial sesamoid' language essentially names the diagnosis. Clues 2 and 4 are redundant; both detail push-off/plantarflexion pain and reproducibility. Clue 5 names 'sesamoid' directly multiple times, which is a giveaway. The differential should not be narrowed to one diagnosis until Clue 4 or 5. Rewrite Clues 1 and 5 to remove anatomical giveavays, and consolidate Clues 2 and 4 into distinct functional vs. examination findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Mechanism (fall from scaffolding), timing (2 weeks ago), anatomical region (cervical spine), and immediate neurological deficit (bilateral sensory/motor loss below shoulders). Also includes that emergency stabilization was performed.
clues.rewrite: A 34-year-old construction worker sustained a traumatic injury 2 weeks ago with immediate onset of bilateral weakness and numbness. The patient reports loss of sensation and motor control affecting both arms and legs. Emergency hospitalization and surgical intervention were required.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional scope: unable to walk, stand, sit; requires assistance with ADLs; absent grip bilaterally; absent trunk control; respiratory dependency on positioning.
clues.rewrite: The patient cannot walk, stand, or sit without external support. Hand grip is absent bilaterally. Respiratory function varies with body position and trunk stability.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism detail (contusion and edema, no complete transection), timeline clarification (immediate post-stabilization rehabilitation), and contrast with pre-injury function.
clues.rewrite: Imaging at time of injury revealed spinal cord contusion and edema without complete transection. The patient was previously employed in heavy manual labor with normal neurological function prior to the incident.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific motor grades (shoulder/elbow 2-3/5, bilateral paralysis elsewhere), sensory level (C6 dermatome), and presence of hyperreflexia/spasticity in lower extremities.
clues.rewrite: Motor testing reveals paralysis in bilateral hands and lower extremities, with preserved but weak shoulder and elbow function (2-3/5). Sensory loss is complete below C6 level. Lower extremity reflexes are brisk and hyperreflexic, suggesting spasticity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Specific MRI findings (C5-C6 contusion with edema and hemorrhage) and CT findings (fracture-dislocation with posterior ligamentous disruption). Post-stabilization confirmation of alignment and decompression.

Cervical Spinal Cord Injury_230.notes: This puzzle has critical issues with the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 contains far too much specificity — it identifies the spinal cord region ('cervical spine region'), the mechanism, emergency treatment, AND bilateral sensory/motor loss, which collectively nearly name the answer. Clues 2 and 3 largely rephrase information already provided in Clue 1 without adding meaningfully new discriminating information. By Clue 3, a player already knows the diagnosis is a traumatic cervical spinal cord injury; Clues 4 and 5 simply elaborate details rather than narrow a differential. A well-designed puzzle should make Clue 1 broad (e.g., 'bilateral weakness and numbness affecting arms and legs') so that Clue 2 (functional impact), Clue 3 (mechanism and imaging findings), and Clue 4 (specific motor/sensory levels) progressively narrow the diagnosis from a wide differential to cervical SCI specifically. The current version collapses too much information into Clue 1, eliminating any meaningful clinical reasoning funnel.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes age, social difficulty, sensory sensitivities, repetitive behaviors
clues.rewrite: An 8-year-old child with parents reporting clumsiness in everyday activities and difficulty keeping up with peers during movement-based games. Onset in early childhood. Parents also note the child becomes distressed in busy, loud environments.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations: poor group participation, inconsistent PE, motor planning clumsiness, difficulty with peer initiation
clues.rewrite: Cannot tolerate unstructured group activities or busy social settings. Avoids participation in team sports and group games. Strength and basic motor control are intact, but coordination during complex or bilateral tasks is visibly impaired.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Developmental timeline, language pragmatics delay, inflexible thinking, pervasiveness across settings
clues.rewrite: Symptoms began in early childhood and persist across home and school. Parents report difficulty with transitions and changes to routine. Some difficulty with understanding social cues and nonverbal communication, though formal language development was age-appropriate.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific objective findings: reduced eye contact, restricted conversation topics, mild dyspraxia on bilateral coordination, normal fine/gross strength
clues.rewrite: Gross motor coordination testing reveals mild dyspraxia particularly with bilateral coordinated movements and motor sequencing tasks. Fine motor strength is intact. Gait pattern is within normal limits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Standardized psychological assessment confirms diagnostic criteria; structural brain imaging normal; EEG unremarkable
clues.rewrite: Standardized neuropsychological assessment demonstrates deficits consistent with neurodevelopmental disorder affecting motor planning and social-communicative function. Brain MRI structurally normal without white matter abnormalities. EEG unremarkable.

Autism Spectrum Disorder_231.notes: This puzzle fundamentally misunderstands the Physiodle format. ASD is a neurodevelopmental/psychiatric diagnosis, not a physiotherapy diagnosis. Physiodle should focus on musculoskeletal, neuromotor, or movement disorders that physiotherapists treat. The clue sequence also fails the progressive reveal principle: Clue 1 essentially names ASD through social difficulties and sensory sensitivities; Clues 2-4 largely repeat and rephrase the same social-communication-repetitive-behavior triad without adding genuinely new discriminatory information. By Clue 3, a clinician already knows this is ASD. A physiotherapist wouldn't use ADOS-2 or neuropsych testing as a diagnostic tool. If the intent is to test a movement disorder associated with autism (e.g., dyspraxia, hypermobility, motor coordination delay), the answer should reflect that specific condition, not the overarching diagnosis. Recommend: either replace with a legitimate physiotherapy diagnosis, or redesign the entire clue sequence to narrow progressively from general motor/coordination complaints toward a specific movement disorder.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, positional vertigo trigger (bed positioning, bending), episode duration, associated nausea, and exclusion of hearing loss/tinnitus

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact (avoids rapid head movements, morning routine difficulty, cautious gait) — adds context of movement-specific triggers and daily impact beyond complaint alone

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: No head trauma, comorbidities (diabetes/hypertension), fall history, and crucially — NO PRIOR VERTIGO — distinguishes this from recurrent vestibular disorder

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Dix-Hallpike reproduces nystagmus with latency (classic BPPV signature), normal head impulse test (rules out peripheral vestibular loss), mild gait/balance deficits
clues.rewrite: Dix-Hallpike maneuver reproduces brief rotatory nystagmus with 2-3 second latency. Head impulse test normal bilaterally. Gait base is narrow with mild weight transfer asymmetry. Romberg test elicits mild sway.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Rules out structural abnormality of semicircular canals, retrocochlear pathology, and CNS causes — confirms BPPV is likely functional/age-related, not secondary to mass or demyelination
clues.rewrite: CT temporal bones shows normal semicircular canal anatomy without canalith debris or hydrops. MRI excludes retrocochlear mass, demyelination, and central pathology. Findings consistent with age-related otolith dysfunction.

Vertigo - Age-Related_232.notes: The puzzle is broadly coherent and follows a reasonable clinical trajectory. Clue 3 is strong — the 'no prior vertigo' detail effectively narrows to age-related/new-onset vestibular dysfunction rather than chronic recurrent BPPV or Ménière's. However, Clue 4 has a subtle redundancy problem: the Dix-Hallpike finding (positive nystagmus with latency) is practically diagnostic of BPPV by itself, especially paired with the positional complaint from Clue 1 and the normal head impulse test. This makes Clue 5 feel less impactful — imaging just confirms 'no other pathology' rather than actively differentiating between remaining candidates. The puzzle would be stronger if Clue 4 focused on a subtle or mixed examination pattern that could fit multiple diagnoses (e.g., ambiguous nystagmus direction, mild vestibulo-ocular reflex abnormality, postural instability without clear vertigo reproduction), forcing the player to seek imaging confirmation. Clue 5's imaging should then reveal the specific structural finding (e.g., canal debris on high-resolution imaging, or age-related changes to vestibular nuclei on MRI) that clinches the diagnosis. As written, once Clue 4 is revealed, BPPV is nearly certain, leaving little mystery for Clue 5.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, unilateral lower leg swelling, progressive nature, functional impact (walking/standing difficulty). Establishes limb region and chronicity but doesn't differentiate between venous, lymphatic, or mixed pathology.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Diurnal worsening pattern, postural relief with elevation, triggering by prolonged activity and long flights. Adds temporal/provocation pattern but still compatible with venous insufficiency, lymphoedema, or combined pathology.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Prior DVT, post-thrombotic syndrome diagnosis, chronic venous insufficiency explicitly stated, recurrent cellulitis, obesity. This heavily weights the differential toward post-thrombotic/venous aetiology and away from primary lymphoedema, but NAMES post-thrombotic syndrome which is very close to the answer pathway and reduces diagnostic uncertainty too early.
clues.rewrite: History of left lower limb deep vein thrombosis 8 years prior, treated with anticoagulation. Persistent leg swelling despite treatment completion. Multiple episodes of cellulitis requiring antibiotics over the past 3 years. Obesity and sedentary lifestyle. No previous limb trauma or cancer history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Stemmer sign and Kaposi-Stumpe sign positive — these are pathognomonic for lymphoedema and essentially confirm the diagnosis before imaging. Brawny induration and fibrosis suggest chronic/secondary lymphoedema rather than simple venous oedema. This clue is TOO SPECIFIC and gives away the answer almost entirely.
clues.rewrite: Left lower leg pitting oedema from midthigh to ankle. Skin appears thickened and dimpled in places; subcutaneous tissues feel firm and non-compressible on deep palpation. No acute erythema or warmth. Mild restriction in ankle dorsiflexion. Calf circumference asymmetry 4 cm compared to right side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Lymphoscintigraphy showing delayed transit and collateral patterns is the definitive lymphatic imaging finding. Duplex ultrasound findings (venous reflux, perforators) are redundant with history already established in Clue 3. The venography result (no obstruction) is helpful for ruling out recurrent DVT but doesn't add specificity beyond confirming the pathway to lymphoedema.
clues.rewrite: Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrates severely delayed radiolabelled tracer clearance from the left lower limb with evidence of collateral lymphatic drainage pathways and dermal backflow. No significant deep venous obstruction on repeat compression ultrasound.

Lymphoedema_233.notes: The puzzle has a critical structural problem: Clue 3 essentially names 'post-thrombotic syndrome' (which is the diagnosis pathway), and Clue 4 includes Stemmer and Kaposi-Stumpe signs which are pathognomonic for lymphoedema and resolve the case almost entirely. This collapses the differential far too early and eliminates the diagnostic reasoning challenge. A well-constructed puzzle should require all 5 clues; here, Clue 4 alone would enable confident diagnosis. Recommend rewriting Clues 3 and 4 to delay the specific lymphatic examination findings and the explicit naming of post-thrombotic syndrome, so that the progression feels like genuine clinical narrowing rather than a handoff of the diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, occupation (sedentary), generalized fatigue, exertional dyspnea, gradual onset over months. Establishes functional complaint and timeline.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional decline in household tasks, need for frequent rest, reduced walking tolerance (two blocks), exertional dyspnea pattern. Quantifies impact on ADLs.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Prior hospitalization and prolonged bed rest six months ago, persistent low activity despite medical clearance, no new cardiopulmonary diagnoses. KEY: establishes immobility as preceding factor and rules out acute organic disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Elevated resting HR (92 bpm), normal BP, reduced 6-minute walk distance (280m is significantly impaired), dyspnea on exertion (4/10), generalized lower limb weakness (4/5), normal cardiopulmonary auscultation. Demonstrates objective deconditioning signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Redundant with prior clues. Imaging findings add NO discriminatory value—they simply confirm absence of cardiopulmonary pathology that was already strongly implied by normal auscultation, normal exertional response pattern, and absence of cardiac symptoms. This clue only 'rules out' rather than 'confirms in'.
clues.rewrite: Resting energy expenditure by indirect calorimetry is 15% below predicted for age and body composition. Maximal oxygen uptake (VO₂ max) estimated at 18 mL/kg/min, consistent with 'poor' cardiovascular fitness category for age. Skeletal muscle cross-sectional area reduced by 12% compared to age-matched normative data on thigh MRI.

Deconditioning_234.notes: The clue sequence establishes a strong clinical story of post-immobilization deconditioning through clues 1–3, and clue 4 provides confirmatory objective findings. However, clue 5 is problematic: it only confirms the ABSENCE of alternative diagnoses (cardiac disease, anemia, infiltrates) rather than CONFIRMING deconditioning itself. In a well-designed puzzle, clue 5 should provide direct, positive evidence of the answer—e.g., documented reduced cardiorespiratory fitness, muscle atrophy on imaging, or metabolic markers of deconditioning. As written, clue 5 feels like defensive medicine rather than diagnostic confirmation. Rewrite clue 5 to include direct physiological or structural markers of deconditioning (VO₂ max, muscle atrophy, resting metabolic rate) that affirmatively point to the diagnosis rather than just ruling out mimics.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 45-year-old manual laborer with 4-week progressive shoulder weakness, drooping appearance, effortful elevation, asymmetrical shrugging. No trauma or neck pain. Establishes broad differential for upper limb weakness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: overhead work and load-carrying affected. Shrug weakness against resistance is the discriminating finding. Neck ROM functionally preserved. This narrows to trapezius/scapular pathology rather than general shoulder or cervical origin.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Insidious onset without precipitant, progressive course, no surgical history or neuromuscular family history. Adds timeline and rules out iatrogenic/traumatic cause, narrows toward primary nerve or degenerative process.
clues.rewrite: Symptom onset was insidious over four weeks without preceding neck trauma, surgery, or focal nerve injury event. No family history of neuromuscular disease. Patient works as manual laborer with repetitive overhead demands.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Shrug strength 2/5 asymmetrical, upper trapezius atrophy, scapular medial winging, but neck flexion/lateral flexion 5/5 bilaterally. This isolates motor deficit to accessory nerve territory (trapezius) while sparing sternocleidomastoid/neck flexors. Highly specific.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: EMG denervation in trapezius with reduced motor recruitment (confirms neurogenic pattern). MRI rules out compressive lesion (mass, foraminal stenosis, canal involvement). Confirms accessory nerve pathology; excludes structural cord/root compression.

Accessory Nerve Palsy_235.notes: This is a well-constructed puzzle with appropriate progressive narrowing. Clue 1 is suitably broad (could initially suggest rotator cuff, C5 radiculopathy, brachial plexus, or nerve palsy). Clue 2 adds the trapezius-specific weakness pattern and rules out cervical ROM involvement. Clue 3 has minor redundancy — the 4-week timeline and no-trauma history were already established in Clue 1; rewriting it to emphasize the manual labor context and rule out iatrogenic causes adds more distinct value. Clues 4 and 5 are strong discriminators: examination isolates accessory nerve anatomy, and imaging confirms denervation while ruling out mass/compression. By Clue 4, a competent clinician should confidently suspect accessory nerve palsy. Clue 5 seals it. The sequence works well overall.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 52-year-old office worker with insidious onset posterior neck and upper back pain worsening during workday, plus skull-base headaches. No trauma or recent illness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitation with sustained computer work and overhead activities. Forward head posture evident and symptom-exacerbating. Postural correction provides temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: 10-year desk-based work without ergonomic setup. Recent job transition increased postural demands. No prior neurological symptoms or imaging. Sleep disrupted by nighttime neck stiffness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Bilateral upper trapezius and levator scapulae tightness on palpation. Cervical extension ROM reduced to 35° (normal 50°) with upper back pain reproduction. Scapular elevation and protraction noted. Normal neurology.
clues.rewrite: On examination, cervical extension is limited to 35 degrees (normal 50 degrees) with reproduction of upper back pain. Scapular positioning demonstrates bilateral elevation and protraction relative to the thorax. Manual muscle testing of lower trapezius and serratus anterior shows weakness (4/5). Upper limb neurological testing is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Cervical spine X-rays show loss of cervical lordosis and forward head positioning. MRI rules out disc herniations or stenosis. Soft tissue imaging confirms bilateral upper trapezius and levator scapulae hypertrophy.
clues.rewrite: Cervical spine X-rays demonstrate loss of cervical lordosis with anterior translation of the head relative to C7. MRI shows no neural compromise. Soft tissue imaging reveals marked bilateral upper trapezius hypertrophy with corresponding atrophy of lower trapezius and serratus anterior, consistent with scapular dyskinesis.

Upper Crossed Syndrome_236.notes: The puzzle has a critical flaw: Clues 3 and 4 together already establish upper crossed syndrome (desk work + postural findings + tight upper traps + weak lower traps = UCS textbook). Clue 5 then merely confirms what has already been narrowed. The clues don't create a true diagnostic funnel—a player with good knowledge would likely guess UCS after Clue 3 or Clue 4. The suggested rewrites for Clues 4 and 5 add muscle weakness and scapular dyskinesis as distinct findings that would only be revealed at examination and imaging stages, rather than being predictable from the history alone. This preserves the progressive reveal principle and prevents premature diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographics, symptom location (lateral forearm, proximal, not hand), activity triggers (gripping, pinching), and onset pattern (gradual, no trauma). Establishes broad differential for lateral forearm pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations specific to movement patterns (supination/pronation, tool use, grip weakness asymmetry). Narrows to nerve compression or motor dysfunction affecting forearm muscles. Writing tolerance suggests not severe neuropathy.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Occupational mechanism (decades of carpentry with pronation/gripping emphasis), timeline (insidious 2 months), and exclusions (no cervical radiculopathy, no prior injury). Rules out acute trauma, cervical referred pain, and systemic neuropathy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Motor findings (weak resisted supination pain, weak wrist extension 4/5) and focal tenderness at radial tunnel. Preserved sensation in dorsal web space distinguishes from pure sensory nerve compression or C5-C6 radiculopathy. Actively narrows to PIN involvement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Confirms soft tissue impingement compressing the posterior interosseous nerve specifically, with supporting muscle edema. Rules out fracture, mass, or structural anomaly. Near-definitive for radial tunnel syndrome.

Radial Tunnel Syndrome_237.notes: This is a well-constructed puzzle with clear progressive narrowing. Clue 1 establishes lateral forearm pain with occupational context (plausible for 8–10 conditions: RTS, lateral epicondylitis, extensor carpi radialis syndrome, C5-C6 radiculopathy, brachial plexus irritation, etc.). Clue 2 adds movement-specific reproducibility and asymmetric grip weakness, narrowing to 4–5 motor-dominant conditions. Clue 3 excludes cervical/neurological causes and confirms chronic occupational mechanism. Clue 4 is the key discriminator: resisted supination pain + point tenderness at the radial tunnel + preserved sensation = PIN compression, not C-spine or pure sensory neuropathy. Clue 5 seals it with imaging confirmation of PIN compression. The clinical story is coherent, each clue adds distinct information, and a skilled clinician would be confident by Clue 4.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes acute onset shoulder pain in lower scapular region with lateral radiation. Rules out numbness/tingling (nervy symptoms). Swimmer context provided.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Identifies pulling/rowing movements and overhead as provocative. Specifies swimming pull-through phase as most symptomatic. Rest relieves pain.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Rapid onset within intensive training after volume increase. No prior history. Adds flexibility deficits (extension, horizontal adduction).
clues.rewrite: Injury occurred during sprint set in week 6 of periodized training block after progressive volume build. Patient denies prior shoulder injuries. No relevant comorbidities or inflammatory history. Training load increased 40% over preceding 4 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Resisted adduction reproduces pain (muscle contraction test). Palpable tenderness at latissimus dorsi insertion. Flexion ROM loss (120°). Weakness on MMT (4/5 adduction).
clues.rewrite: Resisted shoulder adduction and internal rotation provoke sharp pain. Palpation over posterolateral shoulder at mid-scapular border is exquisitely tender. Scapular dyskinesis noted during arm elevation. Forward flexion limited to 120° due to muscle guarding; extension restricted to 20°.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI confirms latissimus dorsi strain with edema at musculotendinous junction. Rules out rupture, rotator cuff pathology, labral involvement.
clues.rewrite: MRI demonstrates hyperintense signal within muscle belly at the junction of middle and lower thirds, consistent with edema and microtearing. No complete disruption of muscle-tendon continuity. Supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and labral structures are intact. No subacromial impingement.

Latissimus Dorsi Strain_238.notes: Clue 1 and 2 are solid progressively. Clue 3 adds timeline/volume context which is new, but the flexibility deficits mentioned (extension/horizontal adduction limitation) are already partially implied by 'pull-through' and pulling activities. The major problem is Clue 4: it only elaborates on findings already suggested by Clues 1-2 (pain with adduction = latissimus involved; posterior shoulder location + pulling activities = latissimus). Clue 4 should differentiate latissimus strain from other posterior shoulder pathology (infraspinatus strain, teres major strain, rhomboid injury) with more specific examination maneuvers. Clue 5 explicitly names 'latissimus dorsi' and states the exact diagnosis, which violates the answer-naming rule. A sophisticated player reading only Clue 1 might already suspect latissimus given the mechanism and location; Clue 5 removes all ambiguity by naming it outright. Rewrite Clue 4 to differentiate latissimus from rotator cuff and other scapular muscles. Rewrite Clue 5 to describe MRI findings without naming the muscle.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Chronic cough, respiratory symptoms since childhood, airway clearance difficulties, dyspnea on exertion
clues.rewrite: 22-year-old with chronic productive cough and progressive dyspnea on minimal exertion. Symptoms began in early childhood. Reports recurrent chest infections and persistent sputum production despite frequent clearance attempts.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Requires daily airway clearance devices; severely limited exercise tolerance; functional decline over time
clues.rewrite: Severely limited walking distance (100–200 m before requiring rest). Unable to sustain any aerobic activity. Requires multiple daily airway clearance sessions with percussion/oscillatory devices to manage sputum. Functional capacity declining year-on-year.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Genetic pulmonary disorder (newborn screening); recurrent exacerbations; endocrine involvement (glucose intolerance); sinus involvement
clues.rewrite: Known genetic disorder diagnosed via newborn screening. Two or more respiratory hospitalizations in past 2 years. History of recurrent sinusitis and nasal polyps requiring surgical intervention. Concurrent glucose intolerance managed with dietary modification. No smoking exposure.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Bilateral crackles, decreased air entry at bases, desaturation with exertion (284 m walk → SpO2 84%), barrel chest, thick greenish sputum

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Hyperinflation, bronchial wall thickening, bronchus-to-artery ratio >1.5, focal bronchiectasis

Cystic Fibrosis_239.notes: The puzzle has a critical flaw: Clue 1 explicitly states 'airway clearance difficulties' and Clue 3 explicitly names it a 'known genetic pulmonary disorder diagnosed via newborn screening,' which directly identifies cystic fibrosis. A player familiar with CF screening protocols will solve this on Clue 1 or Clue 3 alone. Clue 1 should focus only on symptoms (cough, dyspnea, sputum) without mentioning airway clearance as a current therapy (that's functional/activity info). Clue 3 should describe the history without naming it a 'genetic pulmonary disorder' — instead describe the clinical trajectory and systemic features. Clues 4 and 5 are appropriately specific but become redundant given the early reveal.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age (6 years), parental/teacher concern about clumsiness and motor delay relative to peers. Establishes developmental motor delay as chief concern.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specifies functional deficits: bilateral coordination (skipping, cycling), fine motor (handwriting, grip), object manipulation (catching, throwing). Narrows to motor coordination/control impairment.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Developmental timeline (milestones met but awkwardly acquired, slower than siblings), lifelong/persistent course, rules out cerebral palsy and intellectual disability. Distinguishes primary motor coordination disorder from neurological disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective clinical findings: dysdiadochokinesia (slow, dysrhythmic RAMs), postural control deficit (increased sway), gait abnormality (wide-based, asymmetrical arm swing). Normal strength/reflexes rules out lower motor neuron or pyramidal signs.
clues.rewrite: On examination, rapid alternating movements are slow and irregular bilaterally. Tandem stance reveals significant loss of balance. Gait assessment shows asymmetrical arm swing with trunk instability. Strength and deep tendon reflexes are normal throughout.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Movement ABC-2 score (below 5th percentile) confirms motor proficiency impairment. Brain MRI normal excludes structural/white matter/cerebellar pathology. Together these confirm primary coordination disorder without organic CNS disease.
clues.rewrite: Movement ABC-2 testing shows motor proficiency score at the 3rd percentile. Standardized assessment of coordination, fine motor control, and balance all fall below expected range for age. Brain MRI is structurally unremarkable.

Developmental Coordination Disorder_240.notes: Clues 1–3 are well-structured and show good progressive narrowing: complaint → functional limitation → history/exclusions. However, Clues 4 and 5 suffer from overlapping information. Clue 4 describes clinical examination findings (RAMs, balance, gait) that are consistent with DCD but not diagnostic by themselves — cerebellar ataxia, mild cerebral palsy, or even gross developmental delay could show similar signs. Clue 5 then pivots to formal testing (Movement ABC-2) and imaging (normal MRI), which should be the clincher. The problem: Clue 4 already stated 'normal strength and reflexes' (organic disease excluded), so Clue 5's 'normal MRI' feels redundant. Rewrite Clue 4 to focus purely on abnormal motor signs without the organic disease exclusion; let Clue 5 provide the formal diagnostic confirmation (standardized testing score) plus the structural imaging that definitively rules out mimics. This creates a clearer funnel: clinical signs → diagnostic test + imaging confirmation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Acute severe pain in superior chest wall after fall onto shoulder; visible deformity in clavicular region; severely limited shoulder movement.
clues.rewrite: A 35-year-old cyclist reports acute onset of severe pain in the shoulder girdle region following a fall. Visible deformity is present over the anterior shoulder. Shoulder movement is severely limited and guarded.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Cannot bear weight through upper extremity or support arm without pain. Arm elevation and adduction cause sharp pain. Passive movements limited by pain and guarding.
clues.rewrite: The patient cannot bear weight through the upper extremity or support the arm without severe pain. Horizontal adduction across the chest reproduces sharp pain at the injury site.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Direct trauma to anterior shoulder on curb; immediate onset with rapid swelling and bruising; no prior clavicular pathology.
clues.rewrite: The injury occurred from direct impact to the anterior shoulder striking a curb. Symptom onset was immediate with rapid swelling. No prior shoulder girdle injuries or surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Visible deformity with superior displacement of lateral fragment; point tenderness over fracture site; severe ROM limitation in all planes; normal neurovascular status.
clues.rewrite: Palpation elicits sharp point tenderness over the injury site with a palpable step deformity. The clavicle appears shortened. Neurovascular examination is intact with normal distal pulses and sensation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: AP chest X-ray shows displaced fracture of distal clavicle with superior angulation; CT shows fracture through lateral third with mild superior displacement and soft tissue swelling.
clues.rewrite: AP chest X-ray demonstrates a fracture line through the lateral clavicle with superior displacement and angulation of the fragments. Associated soft tissue swelling is present.

Clavicle Fracture_241.notes: This puzzle has severe progressive reveal problems. Clue 1 explicitly mentions 'clavicular region' and 'visible deformity,' essentially naming the diagnosis for anyone with basic anatomical knowledge. Clues 2-5 then redundantly reinforce the same information (pain with movement, direct trauma, deformity on exam, fracture on imaging) without adding meaningfully distinct information. After Clue 1, a student already knows the answer is almost certainly a clavicle fracture. The puzzle fails the fundamental principle that each clue should narrow the differential by revealing NEW information. Clue 1 should be rewritten to describe shoulder girdle region pain without anatomical giveaway. Clues 2-5 currently repeat that there's 'deformity' and 'fracture'—they need to differentiate the LOCATION and TYPE of fracture (distal vs. middle vs. proximal; with vs. without displacement; with vs. without neurovascular compromise). The game should not be solvable with confidence until at least Clue 4.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, comorbidity (osteoporosis), acute onset, severe midline thoracic pain, constant nature, no positional relief, functional impact on mobility.
clues.rewrite: A 68-year-old woman with a known history of osteoporosis presents with acute onset of severe midline thoracic back pain. Pain began without clear precipitant and is constant in nature, not relieved by positional changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional restrictions: limited walking tolerance, forward bending intolerance, poor sitting tolerance. Movement pattern that reproduces pain.
clues.rewrite: Forward bending sharply exacerbates pain at the injury site. The patient can tolerate standing and walking for only brief periods before pain escalates significantly.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: 15-year osteoporosis history, medication-treated risk factors, minor fall from standing height as potential trigger, acute symptom onset following low-energy mechanism.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific palpation finding (point tenderness at mid-thoracic vertebrae), quantified ROM limitation (thoracic flexion ~20 degrees), intact neurology (normal strength and sensation throughout lower extremities).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Specific radiographic findings: wedge-shaped compression deformity at T7, 30% height loss, acute MRI signal change on STIR, intact posterior vertebral line without retropulsion.

Vertebral Compression Fracture_242.notes: Overall progression is sound and follows the clinical funnel well. Clue 1 is overly specific for a 'complaint' level—it already names the body region (midline thoracic back), age group (68, elderly), and comorbidity (osteoporosis), which together make compression fracture the dominant hypothesis immediately. This violates the principle that Clue 1 should cast a wide net. The rewrite removes the explicit mechanism hint ('without preceding trauma') to preserve clinical ambiguity. Clue 2 largely reiterates functional limitations already implied by Clue 1 ('significantly restricted mobility'). The rewrite focuses solely on movement patterns and their specific pain triggers, adding genuine functional discrimination. Clues 3–5 are well-sequenced and appropriately specific for their positions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, gender, sport (distance running), symptom location (deep buttock/sacral), character (progressive, insidious), timeline (8 weeks), and absence of radiculopathy.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitation pattern: reproducible with running/sitting, tolerated with walking. Indicates load-dependent mechanical pain but adds minimal new information beyond what 'worsens with running' already established in Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot tolerate any high-impact activity. Running and jumping are completely prohibited. Interestingly, pain does not ease with typical rest and NSAIDs despite reduced activity, suggesting a slower-healing pathology.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Training error (40% increase), female athlete triad (irregular menses, low bone density), prior stress fracture history. This substantially narrows differential to overuse injury in context of compromised bone health.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific palpation site (sacral ala), single-leg stance test result, hip abductor weakness (4/5), neurologically intact. Localises pathology to sacral region and identifies motor deficit. Meaningfully distinguishes sacral stress fracture from other buttock pain sources.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI shows fracture line in sacral ala with marrow edema; CT confirms stress fracture orientation and rules out displacement. X-rays negative (typical early finding). This is confirmatory and specific to diagnosis.

Sacral Stress Fracture_243.notes: The puzzle has a solid clinical funnel overall. Clue 1 casts a reasonably wide net (buttock/sacral pain in a runner could be several conditions). Clue 2 is the weak point—it largely rephrases what Clue 1 already established ('worsens with running') without adding clear new functional information that eliminates alternatives. Rewriting Clue 2 to emphasize slow healing despite activity modification (a hallmark of stress fracture) would better distinguish this from muscular strain or enthesopathy. Clues 3–5 are well-structured: Clue 3 adds critical risk factors (athlete triad, prior stress fracture, training error), Clue 4 provides discriminating exam findings, and Clue 5 seals the diagnosis with imaging. Minor revision to Clue 2 would strengthen the progressive reveal.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Asymmetric shoulder positioning (elevated, medially displaced) noted in a 12-year-old without pain. Establishes age, body region, and key observation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Restricted overhead mobility and scapular asymmetry during functional tasks; compensatory patterns in sports. Should narrow to shoulder/scapular pathology.
clues.rewrite: Despite minimal functional limitation, the child reports difficulty reaching overhead on the affected side and avoids butterfly strokes in swimming due to restricted shoulder abduction.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Progressive worsening since early childhood, familial pattern (maternal lineage), no trauma or nerve involvement. This is the critical clue suggesting CONGENITAL rather than acquired.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Medially and superiorly positioned scapula with inferior angle displacement; normal strength. Confirms scapular malpositioning but largely restates Clue 1 findings in examination language.
clues.rewrite: Palpation reveals the scapula is abnormally small and lacks the normal inferior angle prominence. Scapular winging is absent. Clavicle and upper trapezius are tethered in an elevated position. Strength testing is intact throughout, including serratus anterior and lower trapezius.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Hypoplastic scapular body and underdevelopment of scapular wing with embryological abnormality. Confirms congenital developmental pathology.
clues.rewrite: Plain films confirm a small, underdeveloped scapula with the characteristic 'high scapula' positioning. Ommovertebral bone (accessory bone between scapula and vertebral column) is visible on CT, a pathognomonic finding in this condition.

Sprengel's Deformity_244.notes: The puzzle's main weakness is redundancy between Clues 1 and 4—both describe elevated medial scapular positioning, making Clue 4 feel like a restatement rather than a discriminatory examination step. Clue 2 also overlaps with Clue 1's functional implications without adding clear new distinction. Clue 3 is strong (introduces congenital/familial history as the key differentiator). Clue 5 is appropriately specific but could be strengthened with a pathognomonic finding (ommovertebral bone) rather than generic hypoplasia language. Reorder examination to focus on palpable scapular structure and accessory anatomical findings rather than position.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, gender, symmetrical polyarticular hand/wrist pain, insidious onset, morning stiffness pattern, functional impact on grip

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations (fine motor tasks, buttoning, jar opening); morning stiffness duration (>60 min) and response pattern (activity/heat); fatigue as limiting factor

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Progressive timeline (6 months); absence of trauma/infection trigger; family history of autoimmune disease; systemic symptoms (malaise, low-grade fevers) accompanying joint symptoms

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective symmetrical swelling and warmth in MCP/PIP joints; specific grip strength reduction (15 kg); positive squeeze test; ROM limitation (wrist extension/flexion)

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Radiographic findings (periarticular soft tissue swelling, juxta-articular osteopenia, marginal erosions at MCPs); ultrasound confirmation of synovial hypertrophy with power Doppler vascularity—objective evidence of active inflammatory synovitis

Rheumatoid Arthritis_245.notes: This is a well-constructed puzzle with strong progressive reveal. Clue 1 appropriately casts a wide net with symmetrical polyarticular pain, morning stiffness, and functional impact—plausible for RA, SLE, other inflammatory arthropathies, OA, or even viral arthritis. Clue 2 adds functional specificity and emphasizes the morning stiffness duration/pattern and systemic fatigue, narrowing toward inflammatory polyarthritis. Clue 3 introduces the critical progressive timeline, family autoimmune history, and systemic symptoms (malaise, fevers)—this substantially narrows the differential toward autoimmune causes (RA, SLE, other connective tissue disease). Clue 4 provides examination specificity: symmetrical MCP/PIP involvement with warmth, reduced grip strength, positive squeeze test, and ROM limitation—these objective findings favor RA over SLE or other mimics. Clue 5 confirms with imaging: marginal erosions and power Doppler vascularity are hallmark RA findings that differentiate from other inflammatory conditions. Each clue meaningfully narrows the differential without naming the diagnosis. The clinical story is coherent and follows a logical clinical reasoning pathway.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Chronic productive cough with dyspnea, purulent sputum, recurrent infections requiring antibiotics. Establishes respiratory system involvement and chronicity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Exercise intolerance with dyspnea on moderate exertion; increased cough/sputum with sustained breathing; fatigue limiting conversation and daily activities.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: 30-year smoking history; recurrent lower respiratory infections since childhood; slow recovery from pneumonia; current management with bronchodilators and mucolytics; no significant hemoptysis.
clues.rewrite: Significant 30-year smoking history. Recurrent lower respiratory infections since childhood with slow recovery after pneumonia episodes. Currently managed with bronchodilators and mucolytics but no significant hemoptysis reported. History of structural lung changes not yet evident clinically.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Bilateral coarse crackles in basilar fields (non-clearing); prolonged expiration; elevated RR 24; low O2 sat 92% at rest. Provides objective pulmonary findings that suggest significant parenchymal/airway involvement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: High-resolution CT findings: bronchus-to-artery ratio >1 indicating bronchial dilation; bronchial pooling of contrast; traction bronchiectasis in lower lobes bilaterally; thickened bronchial walls without normal tapering. This is the diagnostic confirmation.

Bronchiectasis_246.notes: The puzzle structure is sound overall, with a clear clinical funnel from symptoms → function → history → examination → imaging. However, Clue 3 (History) is problematic: it largely reiterates information already established in Clues 1 and 2. The smoking history, recurrent infections, and slow recovery after pneumonia are already strongly implied by the presentation in Clues 1–2. The clue should either: (a) add NEW historical details that shift the differential (e.g., specific prior diagnosis, exposures, immunological events), or (b) be rewritten to emphasize details that genuinely narrow possibilities. The rewrite above attempts to refocus on the chronicity and structural implications without redundancy. Clues 4–5 work well as discriminators. The puzzle difficulty is moderate-to-challenging, which is appropriate for this diagnosis. Progressive narrowing is generally effective, though Clue 3 weakens the funnel slightly.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographic (34F, third trimester), symptom location (unilateral lower back/buttock), onset timing (8 weeks, progressive), character (sharp and aching), impact on function and sleep. Establishes broad differential for pregnancy-related lower back/pelvic pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations (walking, stairs, rolling over) and positional provocations (single-leg stance, leg crossing). Daily pattern (worse at end of day, after prolonged activity). Narrows to weight-bearing and pelvic stability-dependent movements; rules out pregnancy-related thoracic or upper limb pain.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Second pregnancy (prior uncomplicated pregnancy 6 years ago), normal current pregnancy course, acknowledgment of hormonal/weight factors, NO prior pelvic/back pain history. NEW information here is the absence of baseline pelvic pathology and the pregnancy-specific onset—rules out chronic recurrent conditions and suggests pregnancy-induced etiology rather than pre-existing disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific sacroiliac joint (SIJ) provocation tests (long dorsal ligament test positive, Patrick's/FABER positive), localized SIJ palpation tenderness with absence of midline sacral tenderness, hip abductor weakness (pain-limited). Definitively identifies SIJ as source and localizes pathology; narrows differential to SIJ-mediated conditions (PGP, SIJ dysfunction, sacroiliitis). Rules out central sacral pathology and buttock pain from other sources.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound for fetal/amniotic assessment (normal), reassurance that no structural pelvic anomalies exist. Hypothetical MRI would show no degenerative changes or inflammatory SIJ pathology. Confirms pregnancy-related mechanical dysfunction rather than structural, degenerative, or inflammatory pathology. In antenatal context, essentially confirms PGP as diagnosis of exclusion.
clues.rewrite: Pelvic ultrasound reveals normal pregnancy progression; no anatomical abnormalities. SIJ imaging would demonstrate preserved joint space and ligamentous integrity without edema, inflammatory changes, or osteoarthritis. No evidence of nerve root compression or other structural pathology explaining symptoms.

Antenatal Pelvic Girdle Pain_247.notes: Clues 1–4 form a strong progressive funnel with genuine narrowing at each step. Clue 5 is adequate but somewhat generic for imaging in pregnancy (fetal assessment is clinically appropriate but not discriminatory for PGP diagnosis). The puzzle correctly avoids naming 'pelvic girdle pain' or 'sacroiliac joint dysfunction' until the end. Clue structure is sound and would guide a competent clinician through differential diagnosis logically.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Direct blunt trauma to anterior thigh in young athlete; immediate pain, swelling, bruising, and functional loss
clues.rewrite: A 19-year-old collegiate soccer player reports sudden-onset anterior thigh pain, swelling, and bruising following a direct impact during play. Immediate functional impairment noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations: unable to weight-bear normally, cannot flex knee/hip, cannot climb stairs or transfer without pain. Reinforces severity but doesn't narrow differential beyond anterior thigh region.
clues.rewrite: Unable to bear weight on the affected limb; pain is reproducible with passive knee flexion and restricts walking distance to a few metres. Limb feels swollen and tense.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Timeline (5 days post-injury), progressive swelling despite conservative care, no anticoagulation use, no prior thigh injuries. Moderately helpful but still doesn't differentiate between contusion, strain, compartment syndrome, or haematoma adequately.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Palpable haematoma, significant edema distribution, reduced passive ROM, strength deficit (3/5), ROM limit to 60° knee flexion. This is more specific but still largely describes consequences of the initial injury rather than discriminating between contusion and other blunt trauma injuries.
clues.rewrite: Firm, tender swelling palpable over anteromedial quadriceps extending proximally. Passive knee flexion markedly painful at end-range (60°). Quadriceps strength 3/5 with pain-guarding pattern. Compartments firm but no clinical signs of compartment syndrome.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound confirms intramuscular haematoma with dimensions, partial muscle fibre disruption, surrounding edema, and rules out compartment syndrome. This is appropriately specific and definitive.

Quadriceps Contusion_248.notes: The primary problem is Clues 1 and 2 are nearly identical in message: both describe the same acute blunt trauma with pain, swelling, and functional loss. Clue 1 names the mechanism explicitly ('direct blow'), which is too leading for the 'Complaint' stage. Clue 2 adds little beyond restating the functional impact already obvious from Clue 1. The differential funnel never truly narrows—a clinician reading Clue 1 already suspects contusion/haematoma/possible strain, and Clues 2-3 don't meaningfully rule out competing diagnoses (e.g., acute compartment syndrome, significant muscle strain). Clue 4 describes expected physical signs of the injury but again doesn't actively discriminate. Only Clue 5 (imaging) genuinely confirms the diagnosis. Recommend rewriting Clue 1 to be more generic (symptom-focused, less mechanism detail) and Clue 2 to add a new functional or temporal dimension that starts narrowing the differential (e.g., tense compartments, progressive nature, specific ROM pattern).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Postpartum status (6 months), stress incontinence pattern (cough/sneeze/exercise), vaginal delivery history, failed conservative management
clues.rewrite: A 34-year-old female reports involuntary urinary leakage triggered by coughing, sneezing, and exertion. Symptoms began 6 months ago and have persisted despite conservative management.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: avoidance of high-impact exercise, inability to run/jump, secondary sexual dysfunction and psychological distress
clues.rewrite: Patient avoids high-impact activities and reports difficulty with running, jumping, and childcare activities involving movement. Reports pain during intercourse and reduced sexual enjoyment secondary to symptoms and self-consciousness.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Delivery mechanism (vaginal with episiotomy, instrumental assistance, 8-hour labour), absence of significant perineal trauma, no prior pelvic floor symptoms, absence of pelvic pain despite symptoms

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective pelvic floor findings: weakness (3/5 strength), reduced tone, positive cough stress test with demonstrable leakage, normal organ support (no prolapse)

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Dynamic findings during cough showing insufficient urethral mobility and inadequate urethrovesical angle closure; ultrasound evidence of neuromuscular dysfunction (reduced echogenicity); rules out prolapse

Postnatal Pelvic Floor Dysfunction_249.notes: Clue 1 provides too much specificity too early — it essentially describes stress urinary incontinence in a postpartum woman, which is nearly diagnostic before any functional context. Remove 'postpartum', 'vaginal delivery', and 'conservative measures' from Clue 1 to broaden the differential. The revised Clue 1 should allow differential consideration of other causes of stress incontinence (neurogenic, chronic cough, obesity, etc.). Clue 2 is largely redundant with Clue 1 — it adds activity-specific examples but doesn't meaningfully narrow from 'postpartum stress incontinence' to anything else. Clue 2 should focus on unique functional impacts (sexual dysfunction, psychological impact) that Clue 1 didn't mention. Clues 3–5 are well-ordered and progressively specific. The puzzle would benefit from reversing the specificity gradient in Clues 1–2.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, smoking history, dyspnea, chronic productive cough, progressive onset over 1 year, functional impact
clues.rewrite: A 68-year-old male with a significant smoking history reports gradually worsening shortness of breath and a persistent productive cough over the past 12 months. He describes a regular pattern of coughing, particularly in the mornings. Daily activities are becoming increasingly difficult.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations: dyspnea on minimal exertion, cannot climb stairs, limited self-care, nocturnal positional symptoms

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Continued smoking, multiple cessation attempts, recurrent respiratory infections requiring antibiotics, occupational dust exposure, no cardiac history, recent weight loss and anorexia
clues.rewrite: Long smoking history with continued active smoking despite cessation attempts. Recurrent chest infections managed with antibiotics over recent years. Previous occupational dust exposure. No known cardiac disease. Recent unintentional weight loss noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Bilateral crackles and wheezes, prolonged expiration, pursed-lip breathing pattern, reduced percussion resonance, limited diaphragmatic excursion, hypoxemia (88% on room air, 94% on O2)

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Chest X-ray: hyperinflation, flattened diaphragms, increased AP diameter, bronchial wall thickening. HRCT: centrilobular emphysema in upper lobes, reduced parenchyma, small airways disease

Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease_25.notes: PRIMARY ISSUE: Clue 1 (Complaint) is far too specific and essentially names the diagnosis. A patient reporting 'progressive dyspnea, chronic productive cough, 40-year pack history' with functional decline to a physio student reads as COPD before any guessing begins. The combination of age, smoking pack-years, and the pattern of dyspnea + productive cough is nearly diagnostic on its own. For comparison, asthma, bronchiectasis, and interstitial lung disease would all present with dyspnea and cough, but 40 pack-years smoking heavily weights toward COPD. Rewrite Clue 1 to remove pack-year quantification and sputum character specificity. Clue 3 has minor redundancy with Clue 1 (smoking history is already established); restructure to emphasize NEW information: infection recurrence pattern, weight loss, and occupational exposure. The progressive reveal becomes much stronger if Clue 1 is broadened and Clue 3 adds the clinical context of infections + systemic decline. Clues 4 and 5 are well-sequenced and appropriately specific.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Unilateral headache distribution (frontal + occipital), female age 38, onset trigger (prolonged desk work), positional aggravation pattern.
clues.rewrite: A 38-year-old female reports unilateral headaches that began after starting a new office-based role. Pain worsens with certain neck movements and sustained postures. Analgesic medications provide minimal relief.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitation: specific neck movements (flexion, rightward rotation, extension) reproduce pain; relief with rest; computer work aggravates; poor medication response.
clues.rewrite: Symptoms worsen during prolonged computer work and morning stiffness. Patient reports difficulty with work tasks requiring sustained posture or repeated neck turns to the right. Symptoms partially resolve with periodic breaks and postural changes.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Timeline (6 months, recurrent), trigger context (screen time + poor ergonomics), absence of red flags (no head trauma, no photophobia/phonophobia), posture observation (forward head posture noted).

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective ROM restriction (right rotation 60°), headache reproduction with extension, positive cervical flexion-rotation test, palpable asymmetrical muscle tension upper trapezius/cervical paraspinals.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging findings: mild degenerative changes C3-C4/C4-C5, facet hypertrophy, loss of cervical lordosis, spondylotic changes at symptomatic levels, no nerve root compression.
