clues.new_info: Objective findings discriminate from dermatologic disease and serious pathology: erythema and excoriation present but no purulent drainage/infection signs (rules out cellulitis/abscess), superficial ulceration at nipple tip (localizes injury mechanism to friction point), no masses/discharge/lymphadenopathy (rules out malignancy/systemic disease), vital signs normal (rules out infection/systemic illness)

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: CXR unremarkable with normal cardiac silhouette and lung fields—this rules out cardiopulmonary pathology that could present with anterior chest pain, confirms diagnosis is superficial/mechanical rather than systemic
clues.rewrite: Chest X-ray unremarkable (normal cardiac silhouette, clear lung fields) — confirms mechanical/superficial etiology and excludes cardiac or pulmonary involvement. Clinical diagnosis based on physical examination findings.

Runner's Nipple_308.notes: This is a well-constructed progressive funnel. Clue 1 casts a net wide enough to include various chest wall conditions; Clue 2 narrows to friction-based mechanism and distance/material triggers; Clue 3 solidifies the timeline and mechanical cause; Clue 4 discriminates from infection, malignancy, and dermatologic disease through specific exam findings; Clue 5 rules out systemic pathology. The only minor weakness is Clue 5 — it doesn't add much beyond what Clue 4 already established (that this is localized superficial trauma, not serious pathology). The rewrite simply makes this exclusionary function explicit and slightly more concise. Overall, the puzzle demonstrates good clinical reasoning progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographics (22yo runner), symptom character (localized sharp pain in lower leg), anatomical region (anterior tibia/fibula), onset pattern (progressive over 3 weeks), and triggering activity (running). Establishes broad differential: stress fracture, shin splints, compartment syndrome, tendinopathy, muscle strain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact narrows differential: high-impact activities cause sharp pain while low-impact (swimming, cycling) are pain-free. This distinguishes stress fracture and shin splints (load-dependent) from nerve entrapment. Morning stiffness is a new detail. Gait compensation (limping) noted.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Training error (50% increase in volume over 1 month, hard surfaces only) is the KEY NEW mechanism. This is classic stress fracture aetiology and strongly differentiates from acute muscle strain or tendinopathy. No prior injury history. Specific mileage detail (40→60 miles/week) solidifies the overuse mechanism.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Palpable point tenderness over specific osseous landmarks (anteromedial tibia/lateral fibula) is NEW and localises to bone. Single-leg hop test eliciting sharp pain (functional stress test) confirms load-induced pain at bone. Normal flexibility and intact neurovascular status rule out compartment syndrome, nerve entrapment, and severe muscle pathology. No swelling argues against acute inflammation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: CT and MRI findings (cortical thickening, periosteal new bone, marrow edema, endosteal thickening WITHOUT complete fracture line) are the definitive NEW information. This is pathognomonic for stress fracture and rules out other differentials (shin splints would show only muscle/fascia changes; acute fracture would show a fracture line). Near-certain diagnosis after this clue.

Stress Fracture_309.notes: This is a well-structured puzzle with excellent progressive narrowing. Clue 1 casts a wide net (lower limb pain in young athlete). Clue 2 eliminates non-load-dependent conditions. Clue 3 (training error mechanism) is the critical differentiator that moves stress fracture to top of differential. Clue 4 (point tenderness over bone + hop test) confirms osseous involvement. Clue 5 (imaging) seals it. Each clue meaningfully eliminates possibilities. The clinical story is coherent and realistic. No giveaway language. Difficulty is appropriate for Physiodle: a physiotherapy student could plausibly narrow to stress fracture by Clue 3-4, but Clue 5 confirms. No major issues.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age (4 years), presenting observation (collapsed arches when standing), minimal symptoms (occasional mild foot discomfort after prolonged activity), and functional status (not significantly limited).

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional capacity detail: runs, plays, participates normally. Mild fatigue only after >20 minutes walking. No gait asymmetry or compensatory patterns.
clues.rewrite: The child is fully ambulatory and participates in all playground activities without limitation or observable gait deviation. No pain with normal play or walking for typical childhood durations.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Timeline (present since early infancy, consistent with normal development), absence of trauma or swelling, and positive family history of asymptomatic flexible flatfeet in parents.
clues.rewrite: Deformity noted since infancy with no antecedent trauma, swelling, or progressive pain. Parents have flexible flatfeet and remain fully asymptomatic and functional into adulthood.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Weight-bearing versus non-weight-bearing arch behavior (absent when standing, reconstitutes when sitting/suspended). No rigidity, tenderness, or toe deformities. This is the KEY DISCRIMINATOR between flexible and rigid pes planus.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Quantitative radiographic angles (increased talocalcaneal angle, decreased talonavicular angle). Explicit exclusion of rigid pathology (tarsal coalition, abnormal ossification). Confirms flexibility with arch reconstitution on non-weight-bearing films.

Flat Feet in Children_310.notes: PROGRESSIVE REVEAL PROBLEM: Clues 1, 2, and 3 are heavily redundant. By Clue 1, the reader already knows the child is minimally symptomatic, functionally active, and has a long-standing deformity. Clues 2 and 3 only elaborate on the same facts without adding diagnostic discriminators. The differential at Clue 3 is still broad (flexible pes planus, rigid pes planus, tarsal coalition, other structural foot pathology). The puzzle only truly narrows at Clue 4 (flexible vs rigid distinction via weight-bearing test). Clue 5 then confirms with imaging. Rewrite Clues 2 and 3 to introduce NEW clinical information: e.g., Clue 2 could focus on specific gait mechanics or balance observations; Clue 3 could introduce developmental milestones, associated systemic conditions, or prior failed conservative management. This would build a proper clinical funnel.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, symptom triad (dizziness, tinnitus, blurred vision), timing (10-30 sec episodes), trigger (cervical rotation to one side), and resolution pattern (quick with position change). Establishes vestibular/neurological complaint with cervical movement link.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional specificity: head rotation + extension (looking upward) trigger symptoms; sudden movements worsen; sustained rotation problematic. Relief with supine/neutral position. Adds movement pattern detail but does not substantially narrow beyond Clue 1's already-clear cervical movement link.
clues.rewrite: Patient reports difficulty with overhead tasks at work, particularly sustained looking upward during classroom instruction. Symptoms worse with driving (head checks) and confined spaces where rotation is frequent. Symptom-free when reclining or neck immobilized.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Chronic neck pain (2 years, ergonomic), bilateral stiffness, recent MVA (1 month ago, mild strain), negative neurology. Adds timeline and mechanical context but the MVA detail is somewhat weak given 'mild strain' descriptor; doesn't sharply differentiate from benign mechanical causes or establish trauma-related vascular concern.
clues.rewrite: Two-year history of neck pain and progressive bilateral stiffness from desk work. Motor vehicle accident one month ago with significant deceleration-type injury; initially treated conservatively. Symptoms of dizziness began insidiously 3 weeks post-accident and have progressively worsened with head movements. No prior episodes of vertigo or neurologic deficits.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Positive Dix-Hallpike bilaterally with nystagmus (vestibulocerebellar finding); limited cervical ROM; vertebral artery test (extension + rotation) reproduces dizziness; normal upper extremity neuro. The vertebral artery test is the critical new finding that links cervical movement to vascular compromise. Dix-Hallpike positivity is somewhat contradictory (typically unilateral; bilateral suggests non-BPPV cause).
clues.rewrite: Cervical active ROM limited in rotation and extension bilaterally. Dizziness and blurred vision reliably reproduced with combined cervical extension and ipsilateral rotation (positive vertebral artery provocation test). Nystagmus present during maneuver. Upper extremity strength, reflexes, and sensation intact. No focal neurologic deficits. Postural stability normal in supine.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI shows disc-osteophyte complexes at C5-C6/C6-C7 with foraminal stenosis on symptomatic side (mechanical explanation); angiography shows patent vertebral artery with no dissection or severe stenosis (rules out occlusive disease). Confirms mechanical cervical pathology but the normal angiography findings somewhat weaken a vascular diagnosis—the imaging is equivocal and doesn't definitively explain the mechanism.
clues.rewrite: Cervical spine MRI demonstrates osteophytic changes and disc bulging at C5-C6 and C6-C7 with foraminal stenosis and lateral recess narrowing on the right side. Vertebral artery appears patent on angiography. However, dynamic imaging (MRA with head rotation) reveals significant compression of the right vertebral artery lumen during ipsilateral rotation, consistent with extrinsic vascular compromise from osteophytes.

Cervical Arterial Dysfunction_311.notes: The puzzle attempts to present Cervical Arterial Dysfunction (CAD) with a mechanical cervical component, but has coherence problems. Clue 1 is appropriately broad but already strongly suggests a cervical-vestibular link. Clue 2 rephrases functional limitations without adding spatial or patient-impact specificity beyond Clue 1. Clue 3 adds MVA context but describes it as 'mild', which doesn't strongly support a vascular injury mechanism and creates narrative inconsistency with Clue 4's findings. Clue 4 introduces the critical vertebral artery provocation test but includes bilateral Dix-Hallpike positivity, which is atypical for CAD and suggests a competing diagnosis (BPPV or central cause)—this muddies rather than clarifies the clinical picture. Clue 5's normal angiography contradicts a strong vascular diagnosis unless dynamic imaging is added. Overall, the clues don't build a tight diagnostic funnel; instead, they introduce competing possibilities (mechanical stenosis vs. vascular compression vs. vestibular) without clear resolution. Recommend: tighten Clue 2 for functional specificity unrelated to movement (e.g., symptom impact on work/ADLs); strengthen Clue 3 with more trauma severity; revise Clue 4 to remove bilateral Dix-Hallpike and focus on vertebral artery test; enhance Clue 5 with dynamic imaging findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Progressive weakness in hands affecting fine motor control (writing, buttoning) over several months. Establishes acute-on-chronic timeline and body region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: impaired grip, coordinated movement loss, occupational disability. States pattern 'spreads to involve multiple limb regions over time'.
clues.rewrite: Patient reports progressive loss of grip strength and fine dexterity, now affecting ability to write and perform occupational tasks. Functional decline is functionally limiting but patient remains cognitively intact and independent in self-care.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Focal onset spreading over 6-12 months; preserved sensation and cognition; excludes trauma/infection. Clarifies progressive vs. static course and rules out structural/infectious causes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Visible fasciculations, measurable atrophy, asymmetric weakness, hyperreflexia, possible cranial nerve involvement (tongue). These are the hallmark motor neurone disease signs.
clues.rewrite: Examination reveals asymmetric weakness predominantly in intrinsic hand muscles with visible muscle fasciculations in forearms and shoulders. Muscle bulk is reduced in affected regions. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk. Sensory examination remains intact throughout.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: EMG shows denervation (fibrillations, positive sharp waves), motor unit changes, and MRI excludes structural mimics. Confirms anterior horn cell pathology.
clues.rewrite: Needle EMG demonstrates acute denervation potentials with fibrillations and positive sharp waves in weak muscles, alongside reduced motor unit recruitment. MRI of cervical spine and brain are structurally normal with no evidence of compression or signal abnormality.

Motor Neurone Disease_312.notes: Major issues: Clue 2 largely rephrases Clue 1 ('spreads to multiple limbs' is already in Complaint). Clue 4 is nearly diagnostic on its own (fasciculations + atrophy + hyperreflexia + tongue involvement is almost pathognomonic), leaving little work for Clue 5. Clue 5 explicitly mentions 'anterior horn cell loss' which is too direct. The differential should not collapse until Clue 4. Rewrite Clue 2 to focus on functional capacity/cognition preservation (to rule out ALS variants with cognitive involvement), rewrite Clue 4 to describe signs without clustering them so obviously, and rewrite Clue 5 to confirm motor unit loss pattern without naming the pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Localised posterior heel pain, gradual onset, aggravated by shoe contact. Establishes body region and temporal character.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Pain with plantarflexion and firm heel counter compression; walking aggravates it. Narrows to ankle/heel region and suggests mechanical irritation pattern.
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot tolerate firm-heeled shoes and struggles with prolonged walking. Pain worsens significantly with activities requiring repetitive ankle plantarflexion or calf loading.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Recent training intensity increase or footwear change; no acute trauma; chronic compression from tight shoes. Mechanism aligns with repetitive overload rather than sudden injury.
clues.rewrite: Recent change in training volume or shoe type triggered symptoms. No history of acute injury. Athlete reports chronic irritation from heel counter pressure, suggesting gradual mechanical irritation rather than single insult.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Swelling and tenderness between Achilles tendon and calcaneus; pain with passive plantarflexion and bursal compression. Localises pathology to retrocalcaneal space specifically.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Anechoic fluid collection in retrocalcaneal space confirmed on ultrasound; possible associated Haglund exostosis on MRI. Confirms bursal inflammation and identifies structural predisposing factors.

Retrocalcaneal Bursitis_313.notes: Clue 2 is significantly over-specified for an activity clue — it already includes compression mechanism and footwear details that belong in History (Clue 3). This disrupts the progressive reveal. The player learns the mechanism and footwear link in Clue 2, then Clue 3 merely repeats these points without adding new information. Clue 3 should focus on temporal/training context instead. After these rewrites, the funnel will work: broad complaint → functional loss → predisposing factors (training/equipment changes) → focal examination findings → imaging confirmation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Pain in tailbone region, sitting-aggravated, recent onset
clues.rewrite: A patient reports posterior lower back and buttock pain that worsens with sitting. Symptom onset was recent. The pain is described as localised and constant.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: prolonged sitting intolerable, difficulty rising from chair, pain with position changes
clues.rewrite: Patient reports inability to sit for more than 15–20 minutes without severe discomfort. Difficulty rising from a chair is pronounced. Patient resorts to standing or lying down frequently throughout the day.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism: either direct trauma or insidious from repetitive pressure; recent lifestyle change (increased sitting)
clues.rewrite: Patient reports no recent fall or direct blow to the buttock area. Symptom onset coincided with a new job requiring 8+ hours daily sitting. No prior episodes of similar pain. Symptoms have steadily worsened over the past 4 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Coccyx palpation tenderness; passive coccygeal movement reproduces pain; pain with sitting reproduced in clinic
clues.rewrite: Palpation of the coccyx elicits point tenderness. Coccygeal mobilisation (gentle anteroposterior movement) reproduces the patient's familiar pain. Hip flexion and rotation are full and painless. Straight leg raise is negative bilaterally. No lower limb neurological signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Coccygeal deviation, angulation, fracture, or abnormal mobility on dynamic imaging; soft tissue inflammation on MRI

Coccydynia_314.notes: Major progressive reveal failure: Clue 1 explicitly names 'tailbone' and 'coccygeal region' — this is essentially giving away the anatomy. Clue 2 adds very little beyond 'sitting hurts' (already established). Clue 3 repeats the sitting mechanism again without adding new discriminatory history. Clue 4 is repetitive palpation and movement findings that only confirm what Clues 1–2 already stated. Only Clue 5 provides genuinely new information (imaging specifics). For coccydynia to work as a Physiodle puzzle, Clue 1 must be much broader (e.g., 'lower back and gluteal region pain'), Clues 2–3 must distinguish from sacroiliac, lumbar, and buttock referred pain diagnoses, and Clue 4 must provide examination findings that isolate the coccyx (e.g., negative SLR, negative FABER, intact neurology). Currently the puzzle telegraphs the answer by Clue 2.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient is post-MI (weeks to months prior) with reduced exercise capacity compared to baseline.
clues.rewrite: Middle-aged to older adult reports marked fatigue and breathlessness with minimal physical exertion, significantly worse than before a recent acute cardiac event.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations: dyspnea and fatigue at low exertion, exercise intolerance at previously tolerable levels.
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot walk more than one flight of stairs without severe dyspnea and chest discomfort. Previously could climb two flights without symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Risk factors (hypertension, hyperlipidemia, smoking) and deconditioning post-MI.
clues.rewrite: ECG at time of acute event showed ST elevation in anterior leads with peak troponin elevation confirming full-thickness MI. Revascularization was performed via PCI with stent placement. Patient completed basic cardiac rehab phase but remains severely deconditioned.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Stress testing shows reduced exercise capacity and blunted vital sign response. Physical exam may show peripheral edema or elevated JVP suggesting cardiac dysfunction.
clues.rewrite: Six-minute walk test: patient achieves 280 m with dyspnea at 4 minutes (distance well below predicted for age). At rest: heart rate 92 bpm, BP 138/88 mmHg. No peripheral edema. Lung bases clear.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Echocardiography shows regional wall motion abnormality, reduced ejection fraction, and extent of myocardial damage.
clues.rewrite: Transthoracic echocardiogram: left ventricular ejection fraction 35% (severely reduced). Anterior wall akinesia with dyskinetic segment at apex consistent with anterior MI territory. Mild mitral regurgitation secondary to LV dilatation.

Cardiac Rehabilitation Post-MI_315.notes: This puzzle has critical flaws in progressive reveal. Clue 1 essentially names the answer ('recovering from acute MI') and removes all differential reasoning—a player reads 'post-MI' and immediately guesses the condition. The subsequent clues then repeat and elaborate the same clinical picture (exercise intolerance, deconditioning, cardiac dysfunction) without adding meaningfully distinct information. There is no progressive narrowing because the diagnosis was already revealed. For a valid Physiodle puzzle, Clue 1 must describe symptoms only (e.g., 'marked dyspnea and fatigue with minimal exertion') without mentioning the cardiac event or MI history. Only Clue 3 should introduce the acute cardiac event mechanism. Clues 2–5 currently all reinforce 'post-MI with reduced LV function' without differentiating it from other post-cardiac-event presentations (myocarditis, DCM, post-surgery, etc.). The puzzle needs a complete structural rebuild to create a true clinical funnel.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient age (adult), prior polio diagnosis, progressive weakness and fatigue decades later, functional decline
clues.rewrite: An adult reports progressive muscle weakness and fatigue that has worsened over the past few years. The patient recalls a severe neurological illness in childhood requiring hospitalization and prolonged recovery.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations in previously affected muscles; fatigue disproportionate to activity; reduced endurance
clues.rewrite: The patient can no longer perform tasks requiring sustained exertion. For example, they tire rapidly with walking or repetitive arm movements, though rest does not fully resolve fatigue between days. Previously able activities now become impossible within hours.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Explicit confirmation of prior poliomyelitis diagnosis; timeline of initial recovery followed by 2-5 year insidious worsening
clues.rewrite: The patient had achieved near-complete functional independence following the childhood illness, remaining stable for several decades. Worsening began insidiously 2-5 years ago without new infection, injury, or clear trigger. New joint pain and muscle cramps have also emerged.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Asymmetric weakness in original polio distribution; measurable atrophy; diminished reflexes; fasciculations
clues.rewrite: Weakness is confined to muscles originally affected by polio and is asymmetrically distributed. Fasciculations are visible in affected muscles. Sensation is intact. Cranial nerves and unaffected limbs are normal. Reflexes are brisk in healthy limbs but diminished or absent in weakened muscles.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Muscle atrophy matching polio distribution on MRI; EMG shows chronic motor neuron loss and denervation

Post-Polio Syndrome_316.notes: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 explicitly names poliomyelitis, which essentially gives away the diagnosis immediately — a player reading 'childhood history of poliomyelitis' followed by 'decades later' weakness has almost no differential to work through. Clues 2 and 3 then simply rephrase and elaborate the same information already provided in Clue 1 without adding genuinely new discriminatory information. A proper sequence should start with broad symptoms (weakness + fatigue in an adult) and gradually narrow toward the polio history as a key historical finding, NOT lead with it. Clues 4 and 5 are clinically sound but by then the answer is long decided. Recommend complete restructuring: Clue 1 should describe only symptoms and general age/timeline; Clue 2 should hint at pattern of weakness (asymmetric, proximal-predominant, specific distribution); Clue 3 should introduce the prior severe illness in childhood; Clue 4 should confirm motor neuron signs (fasciculations, denervation); Clue 5 should confirm polio etiology via imaging/EMG pattern.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes rotatory vertigo triggered by head position changes, with nausea and variable onset in older adults. Casts a broad net across vestibular and cervical pathologies.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specifies the exact movement triggers (looking up, rolling over, bending down) and functional avoidance behavior. Narrows from general vertigo to positional vertigo affecting ADLs.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Adds temporal pattern (acute onset, intermittent recurrence), possible precipitants (head trauma, viral illness), and explicitly rules out vestibular hearing loss by noting absence of tinnitus/hearing changes. Differentiates from Ménière's and post-viral vestibulopathy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Provides the key discriminatory findings: Dix-Hallpike positivity with characteristic latency (2-10 sec), rotatory-vertical nystagmus with similar delay, fatigue on repetition, and positive supine roll test. These findings are BPPV-specific and rule out central causes and non-positional vertigo.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Clarifies that diagnosis is clinical (positional maneuvers), adds caloric testing finding (reduced function on affected side, suggesting peripheral vestibular dysfunction), and notes normal MRI excludes central pathology. Confirms peripheral rather than central diagnosis.
clues.rewrite: MRI of the brain and internal auditory canals is normal, excluding retrocochlear or central lesions. Caloric testing shows reduced vestibular function on the affected side, consistent with peripheral vestibular origin.

Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo_317.notes: This is a well-structured progressive reveal. Clue 1 is appropriately broad, Clues 2–3 narrow progressively to positional vertigo in an older adult without hearing loss, and Clue 4 provides the definitive clinical examination findings (Dix-Hallpike with characteristic latency and nystagmus pattern) that essentially confirm BPPV. Clue 5 is slightly redundant because the diagnosis is already essentially made by Clue 4; the rewrite tightens it by specifying MRI findings and caloric interpretation to reinforce the peripheral vestibular picture and exclude mimics. Overall, the differential narrowing is logical and clinically sound.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Exercise-induced chest tightness and breathing difficulty. Establishes symptom type and trigger (exercise).
clues.rewrite: A young athlete reports chest tightness and difficulty breathing that develops during or shortly after intense training sessions. Symptoms resolve quickly with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional trigger specificity (high-intensity running, cold/dry conditions) and rapid reversibility with bronchodilators.
clues.rewrite: Symptoms are reliably triggered by sustained high-intensity aerobic exercise, particularly in cold or dry air. The patient notes wheezing during these episodes.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Temporal correlation with training intensity (not fitness level) and atopic background (rhinitis, dermatitis, asthma history).

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Normal resting exam but post-exercise wheezing and acute FEV1 decline. Demonstrates need for provocation testing to unmask findings.
clues.rewrite: Resting respiratory examination and lung auscultation are completely normal. Baseline spirometry is normal. However, examination performed immediately post-exercise reveals wheezing and audible breathing difficulty.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Quantifies FEV1 decline post-exercise (>10%) and confirms with bronchial challenge. Confirms normal resting spirometry.
clues.rewrite: Post-exercise spirometry shows FEV1 decline of >12% from baseline, measured 5-10 minutes after standardised treadmill running. Methacholine bronchial challenge test is positive. Resting spirometry between episodes remains within normal limits.

Exercise-Induced Bronchoconstriction_318.notes: The core problem: Clues 1 and 2 both establish exercise-induced breathing symptoms and reversibility — they are functionally redundant. Clues 3, 4, and 5 then repeat the same finding (normal baseline, abnormal post-exercise) in three different formats. There is minimal progressive narrowing. After Clue 1 alone, a physio student has already narrowed the differential to exercise-induced asthma/bronchoconstriction vs. vocal cord dysfunction vs. deconditioning. By Clue 3, nothing substantially new has been added to discriminate between these. Clues 4 and 5 essentially document the same pathophysiology twice. The puzzle needs restructuring: Clue 2 should pivot to a new functional finding or a red herring that eliminates alternatives (e.g., symptoms NOT present with non-exercise exertion, or NOT present in humid environments). Clue 3 should clearly establish atopic background and normal baseline function. Clue 4 should provide the critical exam finding (wheezing post-exercise). Clue 5 should provide the objective confirmatory test (spirometry/challenge test). Current structure is suitable for a 2-3 clue puzzle, not a 5-clue one.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes vertigo triggered by head position changes, with nausea, in an older adult. Sets the broad category of vestibular/positional symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specifies functional triggers (looking up, rolling in bed, bending) and avoidance behaviour. Confirms positional mechanism but largely restates Clue 1 rather than narrowing meaningfully — the diagnosis is already heavily implied by 'positional vertigo.'
clues.rewrite: Episodes are triggered by head movements, particularly extension and rotation. The patient reports severe dizziness lasting seconds to minutes, with fear of falling leading to reduced head mobility and activity avoidance.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Adds temporal pattern (acute onset, sometimes post-trauma/viral, intermittent recurrence) and specifically notes absence of hearing loss/tinnitus. The latter is genuinely useful for ruling out inner ear pathology (e.g., Menière disease). This narrows the differential meaningfully.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Provides the critical diagnostic maneuver (Dix-Hallpike) with characteristic latency, nystagmus morphology (rotatory + vertical), and fatigue. This is highly specific and strongly differentiates BPPV from other vestibular disorders. Strong clue in the correct position.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: States that diagnosis is clinical (positional maneuvers) rather than imaging-based, and notes normal MRI excludes central causes. This is appropriate but slightly weak — imaging is not typically needed for BPPV diagnosis in routine practice. The clue correctly reflects this but feels somewhat generic/explanatory rather than confirmatory.
clues.rewrite: MRI of the brain and internal auditory meatus is normal, excluding central vertigo, structural lesions, or retrocochlear pathology. Videonystagmography (VNG) during Dix-Hallpike shows characteristic nystagmus with rotatory and vertical components, confirming peripheral origin.

Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo_319.notes: Good progressive reveal overall. Clue 1 appropriately broad for vestibular complaints. Clue 2 is somewhat redundant with Clue 1 (both emphasize positional trigger) and adds minimal new info beyond Clue 1 already established. Clues 3 and 4 are strong: Clue 3 effectively rules out Menière (hearing loss absent), and Clue 4 provides the diagnostic gold standard. Clue 5 is serviceable but slightly weak — imaging is not typically diagnostic for BPPV and feels like it just confirms negatives. Consider rewriting Clue 2 to emphasize functional impact and duration of episodes rather than just restating position triggers, and Clue 5 to include objective vestibular testing (VNG) results that confirm the nystagmus pattern.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Acute dyspnoea and chest discomfort without obvious physical exertion; sudden onset. Establishes respiratory complaint as presenting symptom.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Stress/anxiety trigger, rapid shallow breathing pattern, dizziness, perioral paresthesia, spontaneous resolution. Adds functional context and specific symptom cluster.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Unpredictable recurrence in high-stress situations, cardiac investigations normal, diagnosed anxiety/panic disorder. Adds temporal pattern and psychiatric history.
clues.rewrite: Symptoms recur unpredictably over weeks to months. Patient has been investigated for cardiac causes with normal results. Known history of generalised anxiety disorder diagnosed 2 years ago. No recent life changes or trauma; symptoms worse during work pressure.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Reduced end-tidal CO2 during episodes, hyperventilation reproduces symptoms including paresthesia and dizziness. Cardiovascular and respiratory exam otherwise unremarkable. Adds objective physiological evidence of hyperventilation.
clues.rewrite: During observation of an acute episode: rapid respiratory rate (24-28 breaths/min), shallow breathing pattern. Manual hyperventilation for 2 minutes reproduces exact symptom complex including perioral tingling and lightheadedness. Chest auscultation clear bilaterally. Heart rate elevated but regular; blood pressure normal. No focal neurological deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Capnography shows reduced ETCO2; cardiac imaging normal excluding structural/ischaemic disease. This directly confirms hyperventilation physiology and rules out organic cardiac pathology.
clues.rewrite: Capnography during a triggered episode shows ETCO2 of 28 mmHg (normal 35-45 mmHg), with rapid, shallow waveform. 12-lead ECG and transthoracic echocardiogram normal; troponin negative. Chest X-ray shows no acute cardiopulmonary pathology.

Anxiety-Related Hyperventilation_320.notes: The puzzle has a coherent clinical story and appropriate broad-to-narrow trajectory. However, Clues 2 and 3 overlap considerably on the anxiety/stress trigger and recurrence pattern. Clue 4 introduces ETCO2 reduction which Clue 2 already mentioned conceptually (rapid shallow breathing → hyperventilation). Clue 5 largely reiterates what Clue 4 established (low ETCO2) and repeats cardiac exclusion from Clue 3. Rewrites should sharpen the differentiation: Clue 3 should focus on timeline/onset pattern and prior anxiety diagnosis without repeating the stress trigger; Clue 4 should emphasize reproducibility of hyperventilation provocation and detailed vital signs; Clue 5 should provide specific gas values and cardiac biomarkers to firmly exclude organic disease. The differential narrowing is present but clues could be more distinct.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Acute onset severe lower back pain with bilateral leg pain and progressive neurological dysfunction
clues.rewrite: A middle-aged adult presents with acute onset severe lower back pain and bilateral leg pain. Symptoms began suddenly without prior warning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitation including difficulty walking, standing, and bowel/bladder control; rapid progression
clues.rewrite: Patient reports progressive difficulty with normal bowel and bladder function. Urinary retention and loss of normal continence control are emerging.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Acute onset following heavy lifting/trauma; prior lumbar disc disease/stenosis; rapid progression over hours to days
clues.rewrite: Prior history of intermittent lower back complaints and spinal imaging showing disc bulges. Current event followed heavy lifting 12 hours ago with very rapid deterioration of symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Bilateral lower extremity weakness (proximal), bilateral sensory loss in lower extremities and saddle region, bowel/bladder dysfunction, reduced anal tone
clues.rewrite: Severe bilateral lower extremity weakness affecting hip flexion and knee extension. Saddle region sensory loss (L4-S4 dermatomes). Reduced anal sphincter tone on digital examination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI shows massive central disc herniation compressing cauda equina at single or multiple levels

Cauda Equina Syndrome_321.notes: Major progressive reveal failure. Clue 1 (Complaint) explicitly names 'bilateral leg pain,' 'neurological dysfunction,' and 'progressive' — essentially giving away CES before any differentiation is attempted. Clue 2 repeats bowel/bladder dysfunction already mentioned in Clue 1 and adds no new discriminating information. Clue 3 repeats 'acute onset' and 'rapid progression' from Clue 1, then adds mechanism/history which should have been in Clue 1 or 2, not 3. Clue 4 repeats 'bilateral weakness' and 'bowel/bladder' yet again. The entire puzzle collapses into redundancy by Clue 3; a player learns the diagnosis from Clues 1-2 alone and Clues 3-4 add nothing diagnostically meaningful. Rewrite should: (1) Make Clue 1 genuinely broad — remove 'bilateral,' 'neurological,' and 'progressive' to open differential; (2) Use Clue 2 to add mechanism/timing; (3) Use Clue 3 to introduce bowel/bladder emergencies; (4) Use Clue 4 to confirm saddle anesthesia and anal tone loss; (5) Keep Clue 5 as imaging confirmation. This will create a proper clinical funnel.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient presents with chest pain, limited arm movement, reduced exercise tolerance, in post-operative recovery phase.
clues.rewrite: A patient reports chest wall pain with exertion and difficulty with upper limb tasks. Pain is worse with deep breathing and coughing. Recovery is in the early weeks post-operatively.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — already established limited arm movement and reduced exercise tolerance. No new functional information beyond what complaint already specified.
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot reach overhead or behind back. Cannot perform bed mobility independently. Fatigue limits walking to <100 metres. Activities of daily living such as dressing and hygiene are severely restricted.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism clarified — thoracotomy weeks prior for lung pathology. Establishes surgical origin and timeline, narrowing differential from general post-op to chest surgery specifically.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: chest wall tenderness, restricted shoulder ROM, guarded breathing pattern, scar tissue. However, these findings are predictable from Clue 2 (limited arm movement, respiratory function) and Clue 3 (thoracotomy weeks prior). No unexpected examination findings that distinguish this from straightforward post-thoracotomy recovery.
clues.rewrite: Chest wall scar is firm and adherent. Scapular dyskinesis present bilaterally. Breathing pattern shallow and predominantly upper-chest; diaphragmatic excursion reduced on palpation. Intercostal muscles tender and restricted. Shoulder external rotation markedly reduced compared to internal rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Excludes post-operative complications (pneumothorax). Confirms normal healing. Generic confirmation of expected post-operative course — adds little diagnostic value beyond ruling out obvious surgical failure.
clues.rewrite: Chest X-ray shows stable lung parenchyma with no pneumothorax or pleural effusion. Rib cage alignment normal. Healing of incision line is complete without evidence of dehiscence.

Post-Thoracotomy Rehabilitation_322.notes: This puzzle has a fundamental problem: Clue 1 nearly names the answer ('post-operative recovery phase' + 'thoracic surgery' + 'chest pain' + 'limited arm movement'). A physio student would immediately generate 'post-thoracotomy rehabilitation' as the top differential. Clues 2 and 3 are largely redundant reiterations of information already provided. Clue 4 describes entirely predictable examination findings. There is almost no progressive narrowing — the differential should collapse to a single diagnosis after Clue 1, not after Clue 5. To fix this: rewrite Clue 1 to describe only symptoms without mentioning post-op status; use Clue 3 to reveal the surgical history; use Clue 4 to distinguish this from other post-op chest conditions (e.g., cardiac surgery, pneumonia recovery) through specific movement patterns and scar characteristics. The puzzle currently fails the progressive reveal principle badly.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Neck pain radiating into one arm, with gradual or acute onset following minor trauma or activity. Establishes symptoms and timeline.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Pain triggered by extension and ipsilateral rotation; arm activities limited; posture maintenance difficult. Narrows to cervical spine region with motor/functional impact.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Prior cervical spine history may exist; symptoms activity-dependent. However, 'history of neck pain' and 'minor trauma' already stated in Clue 1. Minimal new narrowing.
clues.rewrite: Long hours at desk work. Symptoms worse with repeated neck extension during driving or overhead tasks. No significant prior cervical pathology, but occasional stiffness before onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific neuro findings: dermatomal weakness, diminished reflexes, Spurling's test positive. These are the key discriminators separating disc herniation with nerve root compression from other cervical conditions (e.g., facet syndrome, muscle strain).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI confirms disc herniation with nerve root compression and foraminal narrowing. This is specific and definitive for the diagnosis.

Cervical Disc Herniation_323.notes: The puzzle is structurally sound but Clue 3 (History) adds almost nothing new beyond Clue 1, which already mentions 'minor trauma or activity' as the onset mechanism. The rewrite should introduce distinct historical context (occupational or behavioral triggers) that patients with other cervical conditions (facet-mediated pain, myofascial syndrome) might not share. This would improve the progressive reveal funnel and avoid redundancy at a critical mid-game point.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Acute onset unilateral lower leg weakness, foot drop, burning foot pain, foot inversion weakness — establishes lower limb neurological presentation but commits too early to specific nerve territory findings.
clues.rewrite: A 58-year-old male presents with acute unilateral lower leg weakness and foot drop that developed over several days. He reports burning foot pain, worse on the affected side, and difficulty with foot control.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: inability to dorsiflex foot, requires high-stepping gait, difficulty with stairs and uneven surfaces. HOWEVER, clue 1 already explicitly stated foot drop and inversion weakness — this clue merely elaborates on functional consequences without adding mechanistically distinct information. Sensation findings belong in examination, not activity.
clues.rewrite: Walking and standing significantly limited due to inability to dorsiflex the foot, requiring high-stepping gait pattern. Climbing stairs and walking on uneven surfaces are particularly difficult.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Recent knee arthroscopy (3 weeks prior) with meniscal repair, no perineural blocks used, no diabetes/PVD/prior neuropathy, no preceding trauma. This is strong mechanistic information — establishes iatrogenic injury context and timeframe. Good clue that narrows differential significantly.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific motor findings (dorsiflexion 2/5 bilateral, inversion 2/5 affected side, plantarflexion/eversion 5/5) and sensory findings in posterior tibial distribution. However, clue 1 already stated inversion weakness and dorsiflexion deficit is implied by foot drop. The bilateral dorsiflexion weakness is NEW but weakens the post-arthroscopy etiology theory. Sensory findings in posterior tibial distribution are NEW and useful.
clues.rewrite: Motor examination shows dorsiflexion strength reduced on affected side (1/5) with plantarflexion intact at 5/5. Sensory testing reveals diminished sensation in the sole and medial foot (posterior tibial nerve distribution) on the affected side only.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI shows tibial nerve signal changes at popliteal fossa, ultrasound shows focal swelling, EMG confirms tibial involvement. This is definitive structural and electrophysiological confirmation — appropriate for final clue. However, the specific mention of 'popliteal fossa' combined with history of knee arthroscopy essentially names the answer.
clues.rewrite: MRI shows signal intensity changes in the tibial nerve consistent with compression or ischemia. Ultrasound demonstrates focal nerve swelling at the site of injury. Electrodiagnostic studies confirm denervation pattern consistent with complete tibial nerve involvement.

Tibial Nerve Palsy_324.notes: Progressive reveal is severely compromised. Clue 1 over-commits by naming inversion weakness (a tibial nerve finding) and foot drop (which could be deep peroneal but the combination with inversion weakness = tibial). Clue 2 adds minimal new information beyond elaborating Clue 1's functional consequences and misplaces sensory findings (belongs in exam). Clue 3 is strong and adds valuable history. Clue 4 partially redundant with Clue 1 but usefully specifies posterior tibial sensory loss. Clue 5 names the location and essentially confirms diagnosis before imaging findings are even needed. Rewrite should: broaden Clue 1 (remove inversion weakness mention), genuinely advance Clue 2 (remove sensation), sharpen Clue 4 (focus on sensory specificity), and soften Clue 5 (remove popliteal fossa location hint).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient has trauma-related injuries AND PTSD symptoms
clues.rewrite: Patient reports physical pain and stiffness following a significant injury event several months ago. Symptom severity fluctuates unpredictably.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — already stated 'both physical and emotional trauma' and 'avoidance of triggering activities' simply restates the PTSD component already named
clues.rewrite: Patient avoids certain positions and movements that were present during the injury. Reports difficulty progressing rehabilitation exercises independently.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Timeline (recent, within months) — the only genuinely new detail. However, PTSD diagnosis is explicitly named, which should not occur in Clue 3
clues.rewrite: Injury occurred following a motor vehicle accident 4 months ago. Patient underwent initial surgery and has completed basic wound healing. Reports interrupted sleep and heightened startle response since the incident.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Observation of anxiety and hypervigilance — but these are already stated in Clue 1 ('emotional trauma', 'anxiety with physical activity') and Clue 2. The 'movement-related anxiety' concept was already introduced in Clue 2
clues.rewrite: Visible muscle guarding and bracing during passive range of motion testing. Heart rate elevation noted when patient attempts previously injured region. Pain catastrophizing observed during functional tasks.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging documents physical injury; psychological assessment confirms diagnosis — but this explicitly names PTSD, which should never appear in any clue. The imaging component is appropriately final but weakened by naming the answer
clues.rewrite: CT imaging reveals healed fracture of left tibia with mild post-traumatic osteoarthritis at fracture site. Psychological screening questionnaires (PCL-5, GAD-7) show elevated scores consistent with trauma-related anxiety disorder with somatic features.

Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder - Physica.notes: This puzzle has critical flaws: (1) The diagnosis is explicitly named in Clue 1 ('PTSD symptoms') and Clue 3 ('PTSD diagnosis'), completely removing the deduction element. (2) Clues 1–3 are heavily repetitive — they keep restating the same dual physical-emotional trauma concept without adding substantive new clinical information. (3) The differential is never narrowed because the answer is given away immediately. (4) By physio standards, 'PTSD' is a psychiatric diagnosis, not a physio diagnosis — the reframes above focus on the PHYSICAL rehabilitation presentation (guarding, catastrophizing, movement-related anxiety, altered sleep/arousal) which is what a physio would actually assess. The puzzle should force deduction through physical findings and functional limitations, not explicit psychiatric labeling.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes age group (school-aged child), symptom presentation (hip pain/limp, insidious onset), and that pain can radiate to knee. Appropriately broad for starting clue.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specifies functional limitations during walking, running, sports, and PE. However, this is largely implied by 'limp' and 'functional limitation with walking' from Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: The child reports pain with hip abduction and internal rotation movements. Difficulty with climbing stairs, getting in/out of car seats, and sitting cross-legged. Antalgic gait is visible.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Provides age range (4-8 years), male predominance, idiopathic avascular necrosis pathophysiology, and absence of trauma. The 'idiopathic femoral head avascular necrosis' is essentially naming the answer.
clues.rewrite: Insidious onset over weeks to months. Male child. No preceding trauma, infection, or surgery. Parents report gradual onset of limp without clear cause. No systemic illness or relevant medical history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Provides specific ROM losses (internal rotation and abduction), positive Trendelenburg, pain provocation with internal rotation, and muscle findings. This meaningfully narrows differential and discriminates from other pediatric hip conditions (e.g., hip dysplasia, slipped capital femoral epiphysis).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Radiographic findings (femoral head flattening, fragmentation, joint space widening) and MRI findings (necrosis, loss of epiphyseal blood supply) are highly specific. Sequential imaging documents progressive changes characteristic of the condition.

Legg-Calvé-Perthes Disease_326.notes: Main issues: Clue 1 and 2 are redundant — Clue 2 merely elaborates on functional limitation already stated in Clue 1 without adding region-specific or movement-pattern information that narrows the differential. Clue 3 explicitly names 'avascular necrosis' which is tantamount to naming the answer in a pediatric hip pain context. Rewriting Clues 2 and 3 is essential to restore the progressive reveal funnel. After rewrite, the funnel should be: (1) pediatric hip/knee pain with limp; (2) movement-specific losses and antalgic gait pattern; (3) gradual onset, male, no trauma/infection; (4) specific ROM and Trendelenburg findings; (5) imaging confirms femoral head necrosis and remodeling.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes location (behind ankle, sole of foot) and character (gradual onset, activity-reproducible pain). Appropriately broad for Clue 1.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specifies functional triggers (prolonged standing/walking, shoe compression at medial ankle) and relief pattern (rest/elevation). Adds functional context beyond Clue 1.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Establishes NO trauma history and links onset to activity increase or swelling. However, 'gradual onset' was already stated in Clue 1. The ankle swelling detail is new but vague — unclear if primary or secondary.
clues.rewrite: No significant trauma history. Symptom onset correlates with increased activity, standing tolerance, or recent ankle swelling from unrelated cause. No prior ankle surgery or chronic ankle instability.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Provides specific findings: posteromedial malleolar tenderness, positive Tinel sign at tarsal tunnel, pain on passive eversion, and sensory changes on sole. This is appropriately specific and meaningfully narrows differential.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Describes flexor retinaculum thickening, posterior tibial nerve compression, and nerve signal changes on MRI/ultrasound. However, this is somewhat redundant with Clue 4's Tinel sign and sensory changes — imaging merely confirms what examination findings already suggested.
clues.rewrite: MRI shows flexor retinaculum thickening and posterior tibial nerve compression at tarsal tunnel level with associated nerve edema and T2 signal hyperintensity. Ultrasound may show reduced nerve mobility with ankle plantarflexion.

Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome_327.notes: Progressive reveal is mostly sound: Clue 1 casts wide net (posterior ankle/sole pain), Clue 2 narrows to medial ankle with activity/compression link, Clue 3 should add mechanism/timeline but mostly rephrases, Clue 4 provides strong discriminatory exam findings, Clue 5 confirms but overlaps with Clue 4. Clue 3 needs rewriting to add distinct history info (comorbidities, prior treatments, or contextual factors). Clue 5 could be strengthened by adding imaging specificity that exam doesn't provide (e.g., nerve mobility, fluid signal intensity).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes age group (middle-aged), body region (anterior shoulder), and trigger pattern (shoulder movement, arm lifting). Broad enough for differential but appropriately anchors to shoulder.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specifies movement patterns that reproduce pain (overhead activities, repetitive elbow flexion) and functional impact (lifting/reaching limitation). This adds movement-specific detail beyond generic 'shoulder pain' but still applies to multiple shoulder conditions.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Adds timeline (gradual onset), absence of trauma, and activity-related exacerbation. Helps exclude acute tears or post-trauma presentations. Narrows differential toward overuse/degenerative vs. inflammatory.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Provides specific objective findings (bicipital groove tenderness, positive Speed's and Yergason's tests) that directly implicate the biceps tendon. ROM preservation rules out advanced rotator cuff pathology or capsular restriction. This is highly discriminatory — moves from broad shoulder differential to biceps-specific diagnosis.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Confirms structural pathology of the biceps tendon (thickening, hypoechoic changes, signal abnormality, partial tear). Imaging findings are specific to biceps tendinopathy and exclude other shoulder diagnoses. This is appropriately confirmatory and near-definitive.

Biceps Tendinopathy_328.notes: This is a well-constructed puzzle with strong progressive reveal. Clue 1 casts a wide net (shoulder pain in middle-aged adult with movement triggers). Clue 2 narrows to overhead/repetitive elbow flexion pattern. Clue 3 adds non-traumatic, gradual timeline. Clue 4 provides the key discriminator: bicipital groove tenderness + Speed's/Yergason's positivity = biceps. Clue 5 confirms with imaging. Each clue meaningfully advances the differential. The puzzle strikes a good balance between accessibility (not solvable from Clue 1-2 alone) and appropriate difficulty. Recommended for daily play.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age group (older adult), chronic neck pain, progressive stiffness, occasional arm radiation

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitation: activities requiring neck mobility are affected; symptoms fluctuate with activity level
clues.rewrite: Patient struggles with overhead reaching and turning the head while driving. Prolonged reading or computer work triggers exacerbations. Symptoms are inconsistent day-to-day but generally worse with cooler weather or after poor sleep.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Long-standing onset (middle age), degenerative progression over years, prior acute exacerbations
clues.rewrite: Neck pain began in the patient's 50s after years of desk work. Symptoms have slowly worsened over the past decade. The patient has had 2–3 episodes of sudden sharp pain with arm tingling that resolved within weeks. No significant trauma or whiplash history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Cervical ROM restriction (especially extension), preserved strength, possible Lhermitte sign, possible myelopathic signs (gait, hand clumsiness)
clues.rewrite: Cervical extension is severely limited (10–15° loss). Lateral rotation to both sides is restricted. Upper limb strength is 5/5 throughout. Grip strength is mildly reduced and asymmetrical. Walking shows no ataxia. Reflexes are brisk in the lower limbs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI confirms multilevel degenerative changes, spinal canal stenosis, possible cord compression and T2 signal changes

Cervical Spondylosis_329.notes: Progressive reveal is weakened by Clue 2 and Clue 3. Clue 2 is too vague and generic (could describe almost any chronic neck condition with functional impact). Clue 3 largely reiterates information already present in Clue 1 (chronic, progressive, older adult, onset in middle age) without adding discriminatory detail. A player wouldn't learn much new after Clue 3 that narrows the differential meaningfully beyond what Clue 1 already suggested. Clue 4 is clinically sound but the mention of 'myelopathic signs' is somewhat redundant with Clue 1's 'arm radiation' and gets closer to naming the answer. Rewriting Clue 2 to specify functional deficits (overhead, driving, sustained desk work) and Clue 3 to emphasize the chronic slow progression with episodic exacerbations and occupational history will improve the funnel effect.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, gradual onset, chronic polyarticular pain in large joints (knees, hips, shoulders), progressive functional decline
clues.rewrite: A 72-year-old retired businessman reports chronic, progressively worsening pain in multiple joints. Onset was insidious over decades. He experiences stiffness that partially improves with activity and temporary relief with heat and anti-inflammatories.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Pattern of morning stiffness (30-45 min), functional limitations with repetitive motion, symptom relief with rest/heat/NSAIDs
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot walk more than short distances or climb stairs without significant pain and fatigue. Overhead reaching (e.g., getting items from shelves) is severely restricted. Symptoms are worst at end of day after activity.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Prior knee injury (30 years), family history of arthritis, sedentary lifestyle post-retirement, absence of systemic inflammatory features

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: bilateral knee crepitus, palpable osteophytes, specific ROM limitations (knee flexion 100°, hip internal rotation 20°, glenohumeral abduction 120°), scapular compensation at shoulder

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Radiographic confirmation of degenerative changes (joint space narrowing, marginal osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis) across multiple joints. Normal inflammatory markers exclude systemic inflammatory arthropathy.

Osteoarthritis_330.notes: Clue 1 is too revealing — phrases like 'chronic joint pain and stiffness affecting multiple large joints, predominantly the knees, hips, and shoulders' narrows immediately to OA and largely gives away the answer before any guessing occurs. Clue 2 largely restates functional limitations already implied by Clue 1 without adding substantive new discriminative information; it should pivot to describe the temporal pattern of symptoms (end-of-day worsening vs. morning stiffness character more clearly) to help differentiate from inflammatory arthropathies. Clues 3, 4, 5 are well-structured and properly sequenced. Consider making Clue 1 much broader (e.g., age + polyarticular pain without specifying joints) and using Clue 2 to narrow to large weight-bearing joints and establish the activity-dependent pain pattern that distinguishes mechanical from inflammatory causes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Distance runner with midfoot arch pain triggered by running/impact. Establishes broad differential of midfoot injuries in athlete.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Pain with running and weightbearing; functional limitation. However, this is already implied by Clue 1 ('develops during or after running activity'). No new narrow of differential.
clues.rewrite: Patient reports pain is worse with repetitive impact and high mileage. Able to walk slowly on flat ground but cannot jog or run. Pain increases with each subsequent day of training.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Recent training load increase and mention of amenorrhea/female athlete risk. This narrows to stress injury in high-risk female athlete, moves differential away from acute trauma or acute fracture.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Single-leg stance pain, navicular compression test positive, tenderness over navicular. This is highly specific and essentially names the answer. Clue 5 (imaging) becomes almost redundant.
clues.rewrite: On examination, midfoot is tender to palpation over the arch. Pain reproduced with resisted plantarflexion and inversion. Mild swelling noted over midfoot. Single-leg stance is painful but tolerated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI confirms navicular stress fracture with bone marrow edema and periosteal reaction. However, Clue 4 already named 'navicular' and 'compression test', so this merely confirms what Clue 4 already revealed.
clues.rewrite: MRI shows bone marrow edema and periosteal reaction in the midfoot tarsal region. CT or radiographs reveal stress fracture line with early callus formation.

Navicular Stress Fracture_331.notes: The puzzle has a major flaw in progression: Clue 1 and 2 are redundant (both describe running-triggered pain), and Clue 4 is far too specific—it names 'navicular' and includes 'navicular compression test', which essentially gives away the diagnosis. This violates the progressive reveal principle. A skilled clinician with only Clues 1-3 should have navicular stress fracture high on differential (based on midfoot pain + amenorrhea + training load), but Clue 4 makes Clue 5 almost pointless. Reorder and rebalance: Clue 2 should provide new functional/temporal detail, and Clue 4 should use non-anatomically-specific palpation findings that differentiate midfoot stress fracture from other midfoot pathologies (e.g., plantar fasciitis, cuboid syndrome) without naming navicular.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographics (young to middle-aged), symptom pattern (pain, instability, catching), and activity context (overhead activities).
clues.rewrite: Young to middle-aged adult with shoulder pain and a catching or clunking sensation during arm movement. Symptoms are intermittent and activity-related.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitation with overhead activities and rotation; sports-specific impact (throwing). Largely rephrases Clue 1's overhead activity context.
clues.rewrite: Cannot perform overhead reaching or throwing without increased symptoms. Pain is reproducible with specific arm positions rather than constant throughout the day.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism (repetitive overhead or prior dislocation) and timeline (gradual onset). Adds context to activity but lacks specificity about trauma vs. overuse distinction.
clues.rewrite: Insidious onset over months with repetitive overhead loading, or acute onset following a prior shoulder dislocation or subluxation event. No recent direct trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific special tests (crank, anterior slide, O'Brien's) and physical signs (apprehension, relocation, external rotation weakness) differentiate labral tear from other shoulder pathologies.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI with arthrography confirms labral pathology and characterizes tear location, size, and associated rotator cuff involvement. Definitive diagnosis.

Shoulder Labral Tear_332.notes: Clues 1 and 2 are heavily redundant—both emphasize overhead activities and instability/catching. Clue 2 should shift focus to specific functional losses (position-dependent pain, throwing inability) rather than re-stating the activity context. Clue 3 adds some mechanism detail but remains vague about whether this is overuse vs. traumatic onset, which is clinically important. Clues 4 and 5 are strong discriminators. The puzzle collapses too early; a player could plausibly guess the answer after Clue 1 alone ('young overhead athlete with shoulder catching and instability' screams labral tear to an experienced clinician).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient is a runner with lateral knee pain during running activity. Establishes sport, body region, and activity trigger.
clues.rewrite: A 28-year-old runner reports lateral knee pain that begins during training sessions and persists for several hours afterward.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1. Already established pain is triggered by running. Clue 2 only rephrases: running causes pain, rest helps. No new functional limitation beyond what Complaint already stated.
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot complete long-distance running (>5km). Pain worsens with downhill running and does not occur during cycling or swimming.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Recent training load increase and hip weakness. This adds mechanism and underlying biomechanical context, narrowing to overuse conditions with proximal chain involvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Three specific tests (Ober, palpation, Noble compression) and their findings. This adds objective discriminatory findings that differentiate IT band syndrome from other lateral knee conditions (e.g., lateral ligament sprain, patellofemoral pain, knee osteoarthritis).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging confirms IT band thickening, bursal fluid, and inflammation at distal IT band. This is near-definitive and supports IT band syndrome diagnosis over differentials.

Iliotibial Band Syndrome_333.notes: Clue 1 is too specific and narrowing (names 'outer side of knee', 'lateral knee region', and running all at once—essentially gives away IT band syndrome). Clue 2 is redundant with Clue 1; both only reinforce 'running causes pain, rest helps' without adding functional detail. After rewriting Clue 1 to be more generic and Clue 2 to focus on specific functional limitations (e.g., can't do long-distance running, downhill running), the progressive reveal would improve significantly. Clues 3, 4, and 5 are well-constructed and appropriately specific for their positions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, occupation, timeline (3 months progressive), symptom character (dull aching), location (anteromedial knee), and triggers (prolonged sitting, stair climbing). Establishes broad differential for anterior knee pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Introduces CATCHING sensation (key plica symptom) and links to specific activity pattern (repetitive flex-extension, inclined surfaces). Distinguishes from simple mechanical pain—adds neuromotor/mechanical catching component. Demonstrates activity-dependent behavior with 24-hour relapse pattern.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism (hyperextension 4 months ago, hiking) that may have precipitated plica irritation. Prior history of anterior knee pain in early 20s (suggests predisposition). Recent activity escalation (increased walking) correlates with symptom flare. Adds temporal coherence and mechanical context not implied by Clues 1–2.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Point tenderness at medial femoral condyle at joint line in 30° flexion (anatomically specific to plica insertion). MEDIAL PLICA TEST reproduces catching and pain—this is the key special test for plica syndrome. McMurray negative and no effusion rule out meniscal pathology and joint effusion, narrowing differential away from meniscal tears or inflammatory arthritis.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI demonstrates thickened, fibrotic medial plica (4mm) with adjacent cartilage signal changes. Absence of meniscal pathology or loose bodies confirms plica syndrome as primary pathology and excludes alternative diagnoses already raised in Clue 4. Definitive structural confirmation.

Plica Syndrome_334.notes: This is a well-structured puzzle with excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 is appropriately broad (anterior knee pain, common triggers, no anatomy-specific language that gives away the answer). Clue 2 adds the critical 'catching' symptom and activity pattern specificity that begins to narrow the differential toward mechanical internal derangement. Clue 3 adds mechanism and timeline that support a chronic irritation pattern. Clue 4 is strongly discriminatory: the medial plica test is pathognomonic, and negative McMurray/no effusion rule out competing diagnoses. Clue 5 provides definitive imaging confirmation. The differential at each stage is reasonable: after Clue 1 (patellofemoral pain, meniscal tear, ligament sprain, plica, etc.); after Clue 2 (mechanical catching narrows to meniscal pathology, plica, loose body); after Clue 3 (chronic irritation pattern favors plica over acute tear); after Clue 4 (medial plica test + point tenderness at joint line + negative McMurray = plica syndrome highly likely); after Clue 5 (confirmed). No redundancy, no premature naming, no generic language. Clinically coherent and diagnostically sound.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient has a foot wound with delayed healing and painlessness due to neuropathy. Establishes diabetes context and neuropathic phenotype.
clues.rewrite: An adult with type 2 diabetes presents with a foot wound that is not healing well. The patient reports the wound is painless.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitation with weightbearing and footwear tolerance. Narrows to lower limb, weight-bearing surface issue.
clues.rewrite: The patient reports difficulty walking on the affected foot and cannot tolerate normal shoes. Gait is antalgic or avoidant.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Long-standing diabetes with poor control, documented neuropathy, repetitive pressure mechanism. This heavily narrows to diabetic foot pathology.
clues.rewrite: Long-standing diabetes with suboptimal glycemic control. Patient reports numbness in the feet. The ulcer appeared insidiously without recalled trauma or injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Full-thickness ulcer on plantar surface, loss of protective sensation on monofilament testing, absent erythema. This is discriminatory from acute infection/cellulitis and confirms neuropathic phenotype.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Radiographs exclude osteomyelitis; MRI characterises bone involvement if present. Helps rule out complicating secondary infection.
clues.rewrite: Plain radiographs show no bony erosion or osteomyelitis. Soft tissue depth and extent of ulcer are confirmed on imaging.

Diabetic Foot Ulcer_335.notes: Major issues: Clue 1 is far too explicit—it uses 'ulcer', 'diabetic', 'neuropathy', and 'painless' which together give away the answer immediately. Clue 2 adds minimal new info beyond 'foot problem with functional limitation', which was already strongly implied by Clue 1. Clue 3 repeats 'diabetic neuropathy' and essentially confirms the diagnosis. By Clue 3, any clinician knows the answer is diabetic foot ulcer; no diagnostic uncertainty remains. The progressive reveal has collapsed. Clues 1–3 need significant rewriting to broaden Clue 1 (remove 'diabetic' and 'neuropathy' from the complaint), add genuinely new functional or mechanistic info in Clue 2, and save the neuropathy context for Clue 3 as a distinguishing historical feature. Clues 4 and 5 are appropriate in specificity but arrive too late because the diagnosis is already obvious.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Shoulder pain in overhead athlete with abnormal scapular movement
clues.rewrite: Overhead athlete with insidious-onset shoulder pain during throwing or pressing movements. Pain worsens with continued activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — already established overhead athlete with overhead activity limitation. No new functional detail beyond 'overhead activities'.
clues.rewrite: Unable to sustain loaded overhead positions or throwing without compensatory shoulder hiking or trunk rotation. Cannot perform push-ups without scapular winging.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Gradual onset with repetitive overhead activity or prior injury. Slightly more specific than Clue 1, but largely predictable given the complaint already implies overhead athlete.
clues.rewrite: Symptoms began after returning to sport following a prior rotator cuff strain 6 months ago. Pain initially resolved but recurred when resuming full training. No significant trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Scapular dyskinesis observed during arm elevation; reduced scapular muscle strength. This is the first clue that explicitly describes the examination finding that defines the diagnosis.
clues.rewrite: Scapula cannot maintain neutral position during arm elevation — medial border lifts away from ribcage. Serratus anterior and lower trapezius strength 4/5. Scapulohumeral rhythm abnormal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Associated rotator cuff or labral pathology on imaging. Confirms the diagnosis indirectly through structural findings rather than directly confirming dyskinesis itself.
clues.rewrite: MRI shows supraspinatus tendinopathy without full-thickness tear. No labral pathology. Plain films normal. Imaging findings support functional scapular origin of symptoms rather than primary rotator cuff tear.

Scapular Dyskinesis_336.notes: This puzzle has critical flaws in progressive reveal. Clue 1 essentially names the answer ('abnormal scapular movement pattern' + 'dyskinesis' is the diagnosis). Clues 2 and 3 add almost no new discriminatory information — they simply rephrase the overhead athlete context already implied by Clue 1. Clue 4 finally provides the examination detail that should have been withheld until then, but by naming 'dyskinesis' in Clue 1 and Clue 4, the puzzle gives away the answer far too early. The differential after Clue 1 collapses from 10+ shoulder conditions to essentially one. A proper sequence should progress: (1) Shoulder pain in overhead context only, (2) Specific movement limitation (e.g., loss of scapular positioning during overhead press), (3) Timeline and prior injury history, (4) Examination findings showing scapular winging/dyskinesis with strength deficits, (5) Imaging ruling out primary structural pathology. Rewrite required.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes wrist region, repetitive mechanism, pain on movement — foundational but overly specific. Already names 'wrist' and implies tendon involvement by saying 'related to movement'.
clues.rewrite: Patient reports wrist pain that worsens with certain daily activities. Onset was gradual, not sudden.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specifies gripping and wrist mobility as aggravating factors. Adds functional limitation dimension.
clues.rewrite: Cannot grip or pinch with full strength. Fine motor tasks requiring sustained wrist control (writing, buttoning) are avoided due to pain.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Clarifies repetitive cause (typing, sports) and gradual onset over time. Adds timeline and activity context.
clues.rewrite: Keyboard worker, 8+ hours daily for 2 years. Symptoms began 6 months ago and have worsened. No prior injury or trauma. No systemic inflammatory history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Provides specific palpation tenderness, resisted movement pain reproduction, and crepitus. These are concrete discriminators.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound and MRI findings confirm structural tendon pathology (thickening, hypoechoic changes, intrasubstance edema). Provides near-definitive confirmation.

Wrist Tendinopathy_337.notes: Major problem: Clues 1, 2, and 3 are heavily redundant. All three cluster around 'repetitive wrist activity causing pain with movement.' Clue 1 already uses anatomically specific language ('wrist region') and hints at mechanism (repetitive). Clue 2 just restates the aggravating factor. Clue 3 elaborates on the repetitive cause but adds little differentiating value. After Clue 1 alone, a clinician has already narrowed to wrist/hand pathology; after Clue 3, they're still not meaningfully closer to differentiating wrist tendinopathy from de Quervain's, ECU subluxation, intersection syndrome, or other wrist tendon disorders. The differential should tighten more between Clues 1–3. Clues 4 and 5 are appropriate and specific. Recommend: Clue 1 becomes broader (remove 'wrist region', just say 'upper limb pain with activity'). Clue 2 focuses on which specific movements/grips fail (e.g., pinching, sustained gripping). Clue 3 adds timeline and occupational detail to create temporal/contextual clarity. This would create a real funnel effect.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Young athlete with hip pain and reproducible snapping sensation
clues.rewrite: Young athlete with hip pain during or after sport. Pain is intermittent and activity-dependent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Snapping triggered by specific hip movements (flexion/extension/rotation); functional limitation with certain activities
clues.rewrite: Unable to perform high-intensity movements—running, kicking, or cutting activities provoke symptoms. Pain worsens with repetitive hip motion over time.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Onset related to repetitive activity and increased training load; underlying muscle tightness present
clues.rewrite: Gradual onset over weeks of increased training. Patient reports tightness in hip flexors and lateral hip. No prior hip injury or surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Snapping reproducible on exam with pain; hip flexibility abnormalities noted
clues.rewrite: Snapping elicited on hip flexion–adduction test or during single-leg stance. Pain reproduced consistently. Marked limitation in hip internal rotation; iliopsoas and TFL tightness on palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging (ultrasound or MRI) shows anatomical cause: IT band snapping, iliopsoas snapping, or intra-articular loose body; dynamic ultrasound visualizes snap

Snapping Hip Syndrome_338.notes: Major progressive reveal problem: Clues 1 and 2 both explicitly mention 'snapping'—this gives away the diagnosis immediately, violating the funnel principle. Clues 1, 2, and 4 are near-identical (all describe reproducing the snap).
