clues.rewrite: A 62-year-old man presents 6 weeks after hand surgery for a finger contracture. Reports excellent initial progress with restored movement, but over the past week has noticed increasing tightness returning and range of motion declining.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific joints affected (MCP vs PIP) and pattern of restriction; scar healing status; functional impact on hand extension
clues.rewrite: Unable to fully flatten hand and extend fingers completely. PIP joint of ring finger particularly stiff despite good recovery in early weeks. Recent increased hand use with therapy and work contributing to symptom progression.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Degree of pre-operative contracture (50 degrees); surgical approach (full fascial excision); timing and type of post-operative activity (PT and work return)
clues.rewrite: Original contracture measured 50 degrees pre-operatively. Surgical plan included complete fascial excision. Increased activity with physical therapy and return to work commenced 4-5 weeks post-op, coinciding with symptom return.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective ROM measurements showing specific improvement at MCP but limitation at PIP; palpable scar firmness increasing; exclusion of infection/wound breakdown

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound confirmation of intact fascial resection planes; evidence of recurrent disease (hypoechoic nodules at operative margins); scar in proliferative phase; exclusion of fluid/hematoma

Dupuytren's Disease Post-Fasciectomy_551.notes: Major flaw: Clue 1 immediately names the condition ('Dupuytren contracture' and 'fasciectomy') eliminating any diagnostic uncertainty from the start. Clue 2 largely reiterates ROM and scar status already stated in Clue 1 without adding distinct functional or contextual information. The puzzle becomes a confirmation exercise rather than a diagnostic challenge. Rewrite Clue 1 to describe symptoms only (progressive tightness, loss of extension) without naming the procedure or diagnosis. Clue 2 should then narrow to the hand/finger region and specific movement loss. This would make the progressive reveal more meaningful.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, occupation, acute onset during routine activity, severe sharp anterior shoulder pain, night pain with sleep disruption

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitation pattern (abduction and internal rotation worse, cross-body reach limited) and movement-dependent guarding. Pain descriptor 'sharp and well-localized anteriorly' is redundant with Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: Severe limitation with shoulder abduction and internal rotation; reaching across body markedly painful. Pain prevents active motion through guarding; passive range of motion not yet assessed. Discomfort worse with lying on affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Explicitly frames presentation as 'inflammatory process' and names 'acute phase' and 'calcific tendinitis'—this is a major problem. Also redundantly states 'acute onset without injury' which Clue 1 already established.
clues.rewrite: No preceding trauma or overuse history. Office work involves minimal overhead demands. Pain intensity markedly disproportionate to mechanism (routine desk task). Similar acute presentations often self-resolve over weeks despite severity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific palpation finding (supraspinatus tenderness), passive vs. active ROM comparison, strength testing interpretation (pain-limited, not true weakness), and two special tests (Dugas negative, Hawkins positive). Good discriminatory information.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Radiographic confirmation of calcium deposit (size, density, borders), soft tissue swelling, and MRI evidence of active inflammation/resorption phase. Appropriately specific for final clue.

Calcific Tendinitis_552.notes: The main problem is Clue 3, which explicitly names the diagnosis ('calcific tendinitis acute phase') and the pathophysiological process ('inflammatory process'). This defeats the purpose of a guessing game and removes diagnostic reasoning challenge. The clue should focus on timeline and lack of mechanical provocation (which distinguishes calcific tendinitis from rotator cuff tear or impingement), not on naming the pathology. Clue 2 also carries redundant pain descriptors from Clue 1. After the rewrite of Clue 3, the progressive reveal will work properly: Clue 1 casts a wide net (acute shoulder pain in office worker), Clue 2 narrows to specific movement patterns and passive-active difference, Clue 3 establishes disproportionate severity and no trauma (suggesting inflammatory rather than mechanical origin), Clue 4 confirms supraspinatus region with provocative special test, and Clue 5 confirms calcification on imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 58-year-old diabetic with 2-year progressive numbness, tingling, and burning pain in feet affecting sleep. Establishes patient demographics, disease duration, and symptom character.
clues.rewrite: 58-year-old man with 12-year diabetes history presents with progressive symmetrical numbness and burning pain in the feet over the past 2 years, affecting sleep quality.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Bilateral symmetric distal lower limb symptoms with functional impact (tripping, temperature sensation loss). Nocturnal pain exacerbation and pattern of ascent.
clues.rewrite: Patient reports loss of protective sensation in feet — recently slipped in shower because unable to sense hot water temperature. Symptoms worse at night, interfering with sleep. No upper limb involvement noted.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Poor glycemic control (HbA1c 8.5%), sedentary lifestyle, obesity, hypertension, smoking, and recent statin use. Establishes metabolic/vascular risk profile and prior management gaps.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific objective findings: diminished vibratory sensation, monofilament loss, absent ankle reflexes bilaterally, mild ankle dorsiflexion weakness, decreased proprioception. Localises to distal, length-dependent pattern affecting sensory and motor components.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: NCS/EMG confirms axonal sensory and motor nerve damage with chronic denervation. Fasting glucose and MRI exclude alternative spinal/metabolic causes. Near-definitive confirmation.

Peripheral Neuropathy_553.notes: The puzzle struggles most in Clues 1 and 2. Clue 1 is too explicit — it already names 'neuropathy' by mentioning diabetes + progressive numbness + tingling + burning pain in feet for a known diabetic. A physio student reading only Clue 1 would narrow immediately to diabetic neuropathy, defeating the progressive reveal. Clue 2 then largely rephrases the same distal, symmetric, nocturnal pattern without adding functionally distinct information — it elaborates rather than narrows. Clues 3–5 are strong: History adds metabolic context, Examination provides discriminating signs, and Imaging confirms. Recommend: Clue 1 should be vaguer about diabetes specificity and avoid 'burning pain'; Clue 2 should focus on a single functional deficit (e.g., the hot-water temperature loss) and perhaps introduce an alternative clue (stocking-glove distribution, or gait unsteadiness) that hints at length-dependent pattern without naming it.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Postpartum status, midline abdominal wall separation, weakness, functional limitation, cosmetic concern
clues.rewrite: A 5-month postpartum woman reports persistent abdominal weakness and difficulty with functional activities. Notices visible bulging along midline when exerting herself.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations: exercise intolerance, pain with coughing/Valsalva, core stability deficits, posture maintenance issues
clues.rewrite: Cannot return to pre-pregnancy exercise intensity. Reports pain and bulging with coughing, sneezing, and straining. Difficulty maintaining upright posture for prolonged periods.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Twin pregnancy, significant weight gain, vaginal delivery 5 months ago, breastfeeding, no formal rehabilitation, high parity

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective palpable findings: 3.5 finger separation at umbilicus, visible bulging with contraction, reduced abdominal wall tension, lateral bulging on rectus contraction, no hernia signs

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Quantified diastasis rectus: 4.2 cm interrectal distance, thin linea alba, no fascial dehiscence, confirmed bilateral separation on cross-sectional imaging

Diastasis Rectus Abdominis_554.notes: Clue 1 gives away the answer far too explicitly by naming 'midline abdominal wall separation' — this is essentially the diagnosis. A physio student reading only Clue 1 has already narrowed to one condition. Clue 2 provides functional information already heavily implied by Clue 1's mention of 'weakness' and 'functional limitation' — there is minimal new information here. The clues should build from broad postpartum complaints → functional movement deficits → timeline/risk factors → specific physical findings → imaging confirmation. Currently, Clues 1 and 2 are too descriptive of the same problem without meaningful narrowing between them.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 42-year-old recreational runner, 8-week buttock pain worse over 5km runs, localized to lower buttock near ischial tuberosity, recent training volume/intensity increase. This establishes age, activity context, onset timeline, symptom location, and aggravating factor (distance/intensity).

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Pain worsens specifically with running hills and sprinting (not just distance); pain with sitting on hard surfaces; radiation to posterior thigh (non-radicular, stops at knee); morning stiffness. NEW INFO: functional pattern of aggravation (hills/sprinting), sitting intolerance (suggests ischial load), non-neural radicular pattern. Narrows from generic buttock pain to load-dependent posterior thigh region issue.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Gradual onset correlating with mileage increase (40→55 mpw), no trauma, prior lower back/hip flexor tightness, weak gluteal activation, recent cambered surface running. NEW INFO: clear training load correlation and mechanism (overload vs trauma), biomechanical context (weak glutes, tight hip flexors, uneven surfaces). This eliminates acute injuries and suggests chronic overload tendinopathy pattern. Differential now narrowed to hamstring, gluteal, or sciatic-related conditions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Palpable tenderness at ischial tuberosity, pain on hamstring contraction (isometric), positive Puranen-Orava test (specific for proximal hamstring), SLR 70° positive, negative slump (rules out neural involvement), limited hip IR vs ER. NEW INFO: specific special test (Puranen-Orava) directly indicates proximal hamstring tendinopathy; isometric contraction pain confirms musculotendinous unit; hip ROM asymmetry provides biomechanical context. This should confidently differentiate proximal hamstring tendinopathy from gluteal tendinopathy or lumbar pathology.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI: increased T2 signal in proximal hamstring tendons at ischial attachment, thickened (8mm vs 5mm), no full tear, inflammation around tuberosity. Ultrasound: hypoechoic tendon, loss of fibrillar pattern, increased Doppler vascularity at attachment. NEW INFO: structural confirmation of tendinopathy at the specific anatomical site (ischial attachment), grade/severity of degeneration, excludes full tear. This definitively confirms proximal hamstring tendinopathy.

Proximal Hamstring Tendinopathy_555.notes: Excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 establishes broad context (buttock pain, runner, training load). Clue 2 adds functional pattern and sitting intolerance (ischial load specificity). Clue 3 provides mechanism (training overload) and biomechanical factors (weak glutes, tight hip flexors). Clue 4 contains the key discriminator (Puranen-Orava test, isometric pain, hip ROM asymmetry) that confidently differentiates proximal hamstring from nearby conditions. Clue 5 seals it with imaging. Differential narrows logically at each step. No redundancy, no premature revelation, no gaps.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Progressive dyspnea on exertion, fatigue, functional limitation (can't climb stairs). Sets the broad picture of cardiopulmonary or systemic illness.
clues.rewrite: A 68-year-old man with a cardiac history presents with progressive shortness of breath on exertion and increasing fatigue over recent weeks. Exercise tolerance has declined noticeably. Reports difficulty keeping up with daily activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Orthopnea (3 pillows), bilateral ankle swelling, NYHA Class III, nocturnal cough, weight gain. This is examination-level detail mixed with functional limitation.
clues.rewrite: Cannot lie flat at night without significant dyspnea. Bilateral lower limb swelling noted. Nocturnal cough present. Weight gain of 5 pounds over 2 weeks reported.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Prior anterolateral MI 3 years ago treated with PCI; cardiac risk factors (HTN, hyperlipidemia, smoking); medication non-compliance. This appropriately contextualizes cardiac vulnerability.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Hypertension, tachycardia, bilateral basal crackles, elevated JVP, peripheral edema to mid-shin, hepatomegaly, S3 gallop. These are objective signs of fluid overload and cardiac decompensation.
clues.rewrite: Elevated blood pressure (150/95) and heart rate (102 bpm). Bilateral crackles at lung bases. S3 gallop audible. Raised jugular venous pressure. Hepatomegaly present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ejection fraction 35% (HFrEF), LV dilation, anterior wall motion abnormality (prior MI), moderate mitral regurgitation, elevated BNP. Confirms systolic dysfunction and structural remodeling.

Heart Failure_556.notes: CRITICAL ISSUE: This puzzle is essentially a clinical vignette for heart failure from Clue 1 onwards. The diagnosis is too obvious too early. Clue 1 explicitly names 'myocardial infarction 3 years ago' and describes classic HF symptoms (orthopnea, fatigue, dyspnea on exertion), which any clinician recognizes as heart failure immediately. Clues 2–5 then over-elaborate the same story with clinical sign redundancy (orthopnea mentioned again as 'can't lie flat'; swelling mentioned as 'ankle edema' then 'peripheral pitting edema'; crackles described in both Clue 2 and Clue 4). The differential is never genuinely open. For Physiodle to work, Clue 1 must be MUCH broader and not telegraph 'post-MI patient with HF symptoms.' Consider reframing Clue 1 to describe only dyspnea and fatigue without MI history, then reveal cardiac risk factors in Clue 3. Clue 2 should focus on the orthopnea/swelling functional impact WITHOUT listing vital signs or crackles. The examination and imaging clues are solid but come too late to have diagnostic impact.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, mechanism (fall FOOSH), immediate onset, clinical signs (swelling, deformity). Establishes acute traumatic shoulder injury in elderly patient with risk factors for fracture.
clues.rewrite: A 75-year-old woman with known osteoporosis falls from standing height onto her outstretched right arm. She reports immediate severe shoulder pain and is unable to move the arm. Staff at the scene note visible swelling and altered shoulder contour.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: any shoulder movement provokes severe pain. Rapid swelling progression suggests significant soft tissue injury or vascular involvement.
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot attempt any active shoulder movement. Rapid swelling extends into the upper arm over minutes. Pain severity out of proportion to typical soft tissue injury alone.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Establishes systemic risk factors (osteoporosis with documented T-score, chronic corticosteroid use, prior fracture history). These explain fracture susceptibility and narrow differential to structural fracture rather than soft tissue injury or dislocation alone.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: palpable crepitus (indicates fracture), specific axillary nerve deficit (sensory + motor), preserved vascular integrity. This differentiates proximal humerus fracture from other shoulder injuries and identifies associated nerve injury.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Defines fracture pattern (4-part, comminuted, displaced), anatomical extent (greater tuberosity displacement, humeral head retropulsion), and confirms axillary nerve involvement with CT and angiographic findings. This confirms diagnosis and severity.

Proximal Humerus Fracture_557.notes: Primary issue: Clue 1 is too specific and essentially names the diagnosis. A 75-year-old osteoporotic woman with FOOSH fall, immediate severe pain, deformity, and swelling almost certainly has a proximal humerus fracture — the differential is already collapsed before Clue 2. A skilled clinician would guess 'proximal humerus fracture' on Clue 1 alone. Clue 2 adds functional language but doesn't meaningfully narrow further since the mechanism and presentation already point to fracture. The puzzle needs Clue 1 rewritten to be more non-specific: present only the complaint (shoulder pain, inability to move) without the mechanism, age, osteoporosis history, or deformity. Reserve mechanism and risk factors for Clue 3. This would force the player to work through differential diagnoses (rotator cuff tear, dislocation, contusion, fracture) before imaging confirms. Clues 3–5 are well-sequenced and appropriately specific.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Widespread musculoskeletal pain pattern, 5-year duration, multiple body regions affected, constant burning/aching quality, prior normal investigations
clues.rewrite: A 48-year-old woman presents with widespread musculoskeletal pain for 5 years. Pain described as burning and aching throughout the body, affecting multiple regions. Patient reports pain seems out of proportion to what physical examination and imaging would explain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Disproportionate pain-to-findings ratio, allodynia, environmental triggers (stress, weather), sleep disruption, cognitive symptoms (brain fog)
clues.rewrite: Patient reports light touch causes significant pain (allodynia). Sleep severely disrupted with non-restorative pattern. Symptoms worsen with stress and weather changes. Concurrent memory and concentration difficulties reported.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Gradual insidious onset after minor neck strain (not major trauma), extensive normal workup over time, comorbid anxiety and depression, recent psychosocial stressors, pain catastrophizing

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Benign objective findings despite severe pain complaint, widespread tender points (11+ of 18 fibromyalgia tender points), allodynia confirmed on testing, absence of neurological deficits, no structural abnormalities on exam, cognitive testing abnormalities
clues.rewrite: Physical examination shows widespread tender points (11+ of 18 tender points positive). Allodynia confirmed to light touch across multiple body areas. No neurological deficits, no synovitis, no muscle atrophy. Objective findings notably disproportionate to severity of pain complaints.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: All inflammatory and metabolic labs normal (ESR, CRP, CBC, metabolic panel, thyroid), structural imaging normal (cervical and lumbar MRI), sleep architecture abnormality (reduced deep sleep), quantitative sensory testing confirms central sensitisation (lowered thresholds, wind-up amplification)

Central Sensitisation Syndrome_558.notes: Progressive reveal structure is compromised by Clue 1 being too specific and Clue 2 being nearly diagnostic. Clue 1 mentions 'pain disproportionate to findings' which is a hallmark red flag for central sensitisation—this should come later. Clue 2 explicitly names 'allodynia' and lists sleep/cognitive symptoms with stress/weather triggers, which collectively narrow the differential dramatically after only 2 clues. By Clue 3, the diagnosis is essentially given away. Clue 4 adds the tender points exam which feels redundant with the allodynia and widespread pain already established. Rewrite Clue 1 to be purely symptomatic (pain distribution, quality, duration) without comparative language about findings. Rewrite Clue 2 to focus only on allodynia and sleep without cognitive symptoms—reserve those for Clue 3. This will preserve a proper differential funnel and allow genuine diagnostic reasoning to occur across the sequence.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient identity, diagnosis, and entire clinical pathway (amputation reason, type, timing, rehabilitation phase). This is maximally specific — essentially names the answer.
clues.rewrite: 52-year-old man presenting 8 weeks post-operative from lower limb surgery. Reports pain at surgical site, cramping sensations in the missing limb, and difficulty with early mobilization. Expresses frustration and denial about functional changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations (early mobilization restricted, balance impaired, requires assistance with transfers) and psychological features (denial, frustration). However, phantom limb pain and stump pain were already mentioned in Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: Cannot independently transfer or mobilize. Requires contact guard or moderate assistance for all positional changes. Trunk balance severely compromised without upper limb support. Demonstrates reluctance to engage in rehabilitation activities and expresses uncertainty about recovery timeline.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Medical context (poorly controlled diabetes, peripheral neuropathy, sepsis, antibiotic treatment, sedentary lifestyle, poor social support, housing instability). While this adds detail, the mechanism and severity were already fully disclosed in Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: Type 2 diabetes with HbA1c 9.2% and long-standing peripheral neuropathy. Infection required aggressive sepsis management. Pre-morbidly sedentary with limited family support. Recent social disruption affecting engagement in rehabilitation program.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective physical findings (stump edema, incision healing, hip strength 3+/5, trunk balance fair, transfer dependence, depression screening PHQ-9=18). This is the first clue providing concrete examination-level discrimination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Structural confirmation (consolidated bone, no bony protrusions, mature scar, adequate vascular supply ABI 0.7, prosthetic prescription). Appropriate final confirmation level, though by this point the diagnosis is already certain from Clue 1.

Post-Amputation Rehabilitation_559.notes: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 names the answer directly by stating 'transtibial amputation' and explicitly labeling the patient as 'in rehabilitation phase with prosthetic fitting planned.' A player reading only Clue 1 knows the diagnosis with certainty. Clues 2–3 then redundantly elaborate on information already given (phantom pain, stump pain, diabetes complications, sepsis all mentioned in Clue 1). Clue 4 finally provides genuinely new examination data, but it arrives far too late — the game is already won. The puzzle needs complete restructuring: Clue 1 should withhold the amputation diagnosis entirely and present only broad functional complaint language; Clues 2–3 should progressively narrow toward lower-limb loss without naming it; Clue 4 should clinch the lower-limb amputation diagnosis; Clue 5 should confirm prosthetic readiness. In its current form, this is a single-clue puzzle disguised as a five-clue puzzle.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Acute anterior knee pain, traumatic mechanism (pivot/plant), sensation of tearing, immediate swelling and loss of function. Establishes acute traumatic onset in young athlete.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Inability to perform straight leg raise and active knee extension; severe pain with extension attempts; localised tenderness over tibial tubercle. Adds functional deficit and anatomical localisation.
clues.rewrite: Severe anterior knee pain with any attempt at active knee extension. Unable to perform straight leg raise. Rapid swelling developed within 30 minutes of injury. Guarding of entire knee joint.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Young male, high-demand cutting sport, acute traumatic mechanism during cutting, recent growth spurt (age 18), no prior injury/surgery. Adds developmental context (growth plate vulnerability) and sport-specific mechanism.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Loss of active knee extension strength (0/5), gross swelling with hematoma over tibial tubercle, severe localised tenderness at tubercle, palpable quadriceps but non-contractile, effusion present. Critically, inability to extend establishes loss of extensor mechanism function.
clues.rewrite: Complete loss of active knee extension strength (0/5 against gravity). Gross anterior knee swelling with visible hematoma localised to tibial tubercle region. Severe point tenderness over tuberosity. Knee effusion present. Quadriceps muscle palpable but unable to generate contraction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: X-ray shows avulsion fracture of tibial tuberosity with 1.5 cm displacement, fracture extends into articular surface, MRI confirms complete patellar tendon disruption at bone insertion, bone marrow edema, extensive soft tissue hematoma. Confirms exact diagnosis and extent.

Tuberosity Avulsion Fracture_560.notes: Main issues: (1) Clue 2 contains examination-level specificity (palpation over tibial tubercle, palpable quadriceps, effusion assessment) that belongs in Clue 4, creating overlap and violating progressive reveal. (2) Clue 4 repeats much of what Clue 2 already established (tibial tubercle tenderness, extension weakness, swelling) without adding substantially new discriminatory information. The 'palpable but non-contractile' quadriceps detail and effusion are already implied. (3) Recommend moving palpatory findings and objective assessment details from Clue 2 to Clue 4, and tightening Clue 2 to focus purely on movement limitation and functional loss. This creates clearer separation: Clue 2 = what movement is lost; Clue 4 = why (exam findings that prove extensor mechanism failure). Progressive reveal would then flow more naturally from symptom → function → history → objective mechanism of failure → imaging confirmation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, sex, symptom character (sharp deep buttock pain), duration (3 months), functional impact (limited walking to 500m). Establishes deep hip/buttock region and non-radiating pattern.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Gait-phase specificity (worse during hip adduction phase), positional relief (sitting/lying), transition difficulty, absence of radiculopathy, occasional groin involvement. Narrows to hip-specific movement dysfunction.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Insidious gradual onset without trauma, occupational sitting (risk factor), prior groin strain 2 years ago, hip tightness and limited internal rotation. Adds mechanism/risk context and prior hip pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Positive resisted adduction, ischiofemoral stretch test (highly specific), asymmetric IR limitation (25 vs 45°), deep infrapiriformis tenderness, hamstring tenderness, antalgic gait. These are specific clinical findings that differentiate ischiofemoral impingement from other deep hip conditions (e.g., FAI, labral pathology, pure sciatica).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: REDUNDANT with Clue 4 and Clue 3. The MRI findings (narrowed ischiofemoral space 18mm, QF edema, hamstring tendinopathy) are the anatomical confirmation, but the examination findings already named 'ischiofemoral stretch test' and described the specific regional tenderness pattern. The imaging adds quantitative measurements and rules out FAI/labral pathology, which is confirmatory but the diagnosis is essentially already given by Clue 4's examination findings.
clues.rewrite: MRI pelvis shows ischiofemoral space narrowing (18mm, normal >24mm) with quadratus femoris muscle edema. Hamstring origin tendinopathy at ischial tuberosity. No femoroacetabular impingement, labral tears, or nerve compression identified. Findings consistent with chronic deep hip impingement syndrome.

Ischiofemoral Impingement_561.notes: Progressive reveal is generally sound through Clue 4, which provides highly specific examination findings (ischiofemoral stretch test, IR asymmetry, infrapiriformis tenderness) that strongly narrow to ischiofemoral impingement. However, Clue 4 is so specific that Clue 5 becomes confirmatory rather than revelatory — by that point, a skilled clinician should already be confident in the diagnosis. The imaging findings in Clue 5 are important for ruling out mimics (FAI, labral pathology) but the rewrite suggested above should make this distinction more prominent rather than just elaborating measurements. Consider whether a subtle alternative diagnosis (e.g., deep gluteal syndrome, hamstring origin syndrome) might still be on the differential after Clue 4 before Clue 5 lands — if not, the clues may be too easy overall despite good structure.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, symptom onset (preschool), and three specific motor concerns (pencil grip, cycling, balance). Establishes broad developmental motor difficulty.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: fine motor (writing, drawing, buttoning) and gross motor (running, jumping, frequent falls). But these are largely implied by Clue 1 already — pencil grip difficulty obviously involves writing/drawing; poor balance obviously means falling.
clues.rewrite: Difficulty with playground skills: cannot skip or hop, trips over own feet during play. Also struggles with self-care tasks like tying shoes and fastening clothing. Noticeably slower at sports and climbing compared to peers.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Timeline (delayed milestones vs. siblings/peers), excludes prenatal/prematurity causes, normal hearing/vision/cognition/seizures. This adds important rule-outs (not cerebral palsy, not primary intellectual disability, not sensory deficit).

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific quantified findings: 9-hole peg test (2.5 vs 1 min), tandem stance (5 sec), asymmetric gait. Objective measures that confirm motor coordination deficit across domains. Neurological exam normal (rules out acquired neurological pathology).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Standardized motor assessment (MABC <5th percentile), normal neuroimaging and ancillary testing. This is near-definitive for DCD and rules out other causes. However, the clue mixes assessment (MABC) with imaging (MRI, audiometry, cognition) — these are appropriate but somewhat heterogeneous content for a single 'Imaging' clue.

Developmental Coordination Disorder_562.notes: The main weakness is Clue 2, which largely rephrases Clue 1 without adding substantive new information. Clue 1 already establishes poor pencil grip (fine motor), poor cycling/balance (gross motor), and clumsiness. Clue 2 then elaborates on writing, drawing, buttoning (fine motor) and running, jumping, falling (gross motor) — but the player learns nothing diagnostically new; they're just seeing the same motor deficit described twice. Clue 2 should instead pivot to functional impact in a specific domain not yet covered (e.g., self-care, play, sports-specific skills) or to social/emotional consequences (e.g., social withdrawal, frustration, school refusal). The progressive reveal is thus weak at the 1→2 transition. Clues 3, 4, and 5 work well — they progressively narrow from history/rule-outs to objective findings to formal assessment. After a rewrite of Clue 2, this puzzle would flow much better.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes acute severe hip pain with inability to bear weight, external rotation deformity, and risk factor (corticosteroid use, osteoporosis). Narrows to hip pathology.
clues.rewrite: A 62-year-old woman on long-term corticosteroid therapy for rheumatoid arthritis falls from standing height onto her left side. Acute severe left hip pain with inability to bear weight. Left leg held in external rotation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Describes functional loss (transfer inability, pain with movement) and swelling in upper thigh region. Confirms hip region involvement.
clues.rewrite: Severe pain with any hip movement. Cannot transfer bed-to-chair or ambulate. Left leg adopts external rotation and flexion posture. Marked swelling in proximal thigh with visible deformity.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Adds critical context: osteoporosis from long-term corticosteroids, low-energy mechanism (suggesting fragility fracture vs traumatic fracture in healthy bone), prior fracture history, postmenopausal status, vitamin D deficiency. Strongly suggests fracture.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: 2cm limb shortening (typical of proximal femur fracture), external rotation deformity, specific tenderness pattern, groin pain with internal rotation. These findings distinguish fracture from other causes (e.g., dislocation, muscle strain).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Defines specific fracture type and location (transverse subtrochanteric with lateral comminution, valgus angulation, medial cortex disruption). Distinguishes subtrochanteric from intertrochanteric or femoral neck fracture. Confirms osteoporosis severity.

Subtrochanteric Fracture_563.notes: The puzzle has a critical weakness at Clue 1: it essentially announces the answer by naming 'deformity with external rotation' and stating the mechanism as a fall in an osteoporotic patient on corticosteroids. Any competent clinician reading only Clue 1 would immediately think 'hip fracture' — the differential is not meaningfully broad. Clue 2 then adds minimal new discriminatory information beyond restating the regional pain and movement loss. By Clue 3, the answer is almost certain. Clues 4 and 5 are well-structured and genuinely specific, but they arrive too late to matter for gameplay. Rewrite Clue 1 to present only the mechanism and acute symptoms without the deformity descriptor or osteoporosis context; save the risk factors for Clue 3. This forces the player to reason through a broader differential (other causes of acute hip pain: dislocation, muscle tear, referred pain from spine) before the history narrows it. Clue 2 is then redundant with Clue 1 as currently written; suggest rephrasing for functional/postural detail rather than pain rephrasing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age (60), chronic neck pain, progressive upper extremity symptoms, stiffness, neurological progression
clues.rewrite: A 60-year-old patient with a 10-year history of intermittent neck pain now reports progressive tingling and weakness in both arms over the past 6 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Exacerbation with prolonged sitting and neck extension; functional limitations with sustained positioning
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot work at a desk for more than 30 minutes without symptom escalation. Activities requiring overhead reaching and downward gaze (reading, cooking) trigger acute worsening. Symptoms do not improve with rest alone.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Redundant: 'long-standing cervical degenerative disc disease' and 'age-related joint degeneration' already heavily implied by clue 1 (60-year-old, chronic, progressive). 'Spinal cord compression may be developing' repeats clue 1's neurological progression.
clues.rewrite: Patient reports morning stiffness lasting 45 minutes. No prior trauma or neck injury. No imaging performed previously. Symptoms gradually worsened despite two courses of physiotherapy (6 months apart). Intermittent episodes of grip weakness and difficulty with fine motor tasks (buttoning, writing).

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Limited neck ROM in multiple planes; upper extremity strength deficits; pathological reflexes (hyperreflexia, Babinski)
clues.rewrite: Neck flexion limited to 30°. Bilateral hand intrinsic weakness (grip strength 3+/5 bilaterally). Brisk reflexes in upper extremities (3+) with sustained clonus at ankles. Positive Lhermitte sign (electric shock down spine with neck flexion). Lower limb weakness present (hip flexion 4/5 bilaterally). Gait ataxic.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI findings: multi-level disc degeneration, osteophytes, spinal canal narrowing, cord compression, signal changes
clues.rewrite: MRI cervical spine shows osteophyte-mediated stenosis at C4–C6 with canal diameter reduced to 8 mm (normal >13 mm). T2 hyperintense signal within spinal cord at C5 level. Cord compression ratio >60% with flattening evident.

Cervical Spondylosis_564.notes: This puzzle fails the progressive reveal principle severely. Clues 1–3 essentially repeat the same information (progressive neurological symptoms, cervical degeneration, age, chronic course). The differential is already crushed by clue 1 alone, leaving almost no room for clinical reasoning. Clue 1 uses near-diagnostic language ('progressive upper extremity symptoms,' 'neurological symptoms,' 'functional limitation') that only fits a handful of conditions, and clue 3 explicitly names 'cervical degenerative disc disease' and 'spinal cord compression.' Clues 2 and 3 add almost no new information—they elaborate on what's already stated. Clue 4 is also generic (could describe many upper motor neuron lesions) and doesn't meaningfully discriminate myelopathy from, say, motor neurone disease. Only clues 4–5 together approach adequate specificity. A rewrite must broaden clue 1 dramatically, ensure clue 2 adds functional detail not implied by clue 1, clue 3 adds timeline/mechanism not yet mentioned, clue 4 discriminates with specific neuro findings, and clue 5 seals with imaging. This puzzle currently plays more like a trivia question than a diagnostic reasoning game.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Sudden onset severe vertigo upon waking; recent viral illness 5 days prior. Sets up vestibular syndrome in broad terms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: constant disabling vertigo worse with head movement and eye closure; nausea/vomiting; improves with immobilization. Confirms peripheral vestibular pattern.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: No prior vertigo episodes; hearing preserved; no trauma/surgery. Excludes recurrent/BPPV and audiovestibular pathology.
clues.rewrite: No prior episodes of vertigo. Patient denies hearing changes or tinnitus. No recent head trauma, otologic procedures, or autoimmune history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Positive head impulse test with catch-up saccade; negative Dix-Hallpike; caloric asymmetry (50% unilateral paresis); positive Romberg with ipsilateral fall. Identifies unilateral peripheral vestibular deficit and excludes BPPV.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI normal (no neuroma/lesion); no CN enhancement; audiometry normal; electrography shows vestibular asymmetry. Confirms peripheral vestibular lesion, excludes central and structural causes.
clues.rewrite: MRI inner ear structures and cerebellopontine angle normal, excluding vestibular schwannoma or brainstem lesion. No abnormal cranial nerve enhancement. Differential diagnosis now narrows to viral-inflammatory vestibular nerve pathology.

Vestibular Neuritis_565.notes: The puzzle has a solid funnel structure through Clue 3, but Clue 3 itself is largely redundant—it restates information already embedded in Clues 1 and 2 (viral prodrome, sudden onset, absence of hearing loss) without adding genuinely new discriminatory details. Clue 5 also feels somewhat repetitive: after Clue 4's definitive examination findings (positive HIT, caloric asymmetry), the imaging clue mostly confirms what is already strongly suspected rather than providing a critical new discriminator. The puzzle would be stronger if Clue 3 added distinct historical or demographic context (e.g., recurrence pattern, systemic illness, medication history, or occupational factors), and if Clue 5 provided imaging that actively ruled out a close mimicker (e.g., 'internal auditory canal patent; no evidence of labyrinthitis or meningitis involvement'). Overall coherence is good, but progressive narrowing could be tighter.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes acute traumatic mechanism (door catch, hyperflexion), anatomical location (ring finger), immediate pain over distal phalanx, and loss of DIP extension. Sets the stage broadly.
clues.rewrite: 34-year-old man with acute injury to ring finger sustained 1 day ago when caught in door. Reports immediate pain over tip of finger and inability to straighten the end joint. Minimal swelling, no visible deformity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Confirms active loss of DIP extension (fixed flexion) and notes pain with passive extension. Sensory intact. Mild swelling DIP joint.
clues.rewrite: Unable to actively straighten the distal interphalangeal joint — finger held in slight flexion at rest. Passive extension is possible but painful. No weakness or numbness elsewhere in hand.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Timeline (1 day post-injury), no prior hand injuries, no arthritis, no connective tissue disorder, occupational context (office worker). Confirms acute vs. chronic nature and reduces likelihood of degenerative or systemic causes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific quantified ROM (30° flexion deformity at DIP), clarification that flexor digitorum superficialis is intact, no lacerations, normal sensation. However, contains anatomical error and redundancy with earlier clues.
clues.rewrite: DIP joint held in approximately 30-degree flexion deformity. No laceration over dorsal aspect of finger. Flexor tendons (FDP and FDS) intact and functional. Sensation and circulation intact. No evidence of compartment syndrome or vascular compromise.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: X-ray shows small avulsion fracture at dorsal base of distal phalanx (15% articular surface, 4mm fragment). This definitively identifies the underlying pathology — bony mallet vs. tendinous mallet. Confirms diagnosis.

Mallet Finger_566.notes: The puzzle has significant structural problems. Clue 1 already names 'hyperflexion injury' with explicit DIP loss of extension and distal phalanx pain — this is nearly pathognomonic for mallet finger and gives away the answer prematurely. Clue 2 essentially rephrases the same findings (DIP flexion, inability to extend) without meaningful new functional information. Clue 4 contains an error (FDS is not tested in mallet finger assessment) and again repeats ROM and neurovascular findings. The differential narrowing happens in Clues 1–2, leaving Clues 3–4 feeling redundant. Clue 3 is appropriate and adds timeline context. Clue 5 is appropriately specific but arrives after the diagnosis is already obvious. Rewrite should make Clue 1 broader (acute finger injury with loss of extension, minimal detail on exact mechanism or joint), allow Clue 2 to establish the isolated DIP loss of active extension, let Clue 3 narrow mechanism and timeline, use Clue 4 for detailed exam and ROM quantification, and reserve Clue 5 for the avulsion fracture confirmation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 32-year-old male with polyarticular joint swelling/pain, systemic fever and malaise, 2-week onset post-diarrhea, morning stiffness pattern.
clues.rewrite: A 32-year-old man presents with acute-onset painful swelling in knees and ankles 2 weeks after an acute diarrheal illness. Reports fever (101°F), fatigue, and malaise. Morning stiffness lasts approximately 2 hours.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Associated urethritis with dysuria and penile discharge; conjunctivitis with eye redness. Symmetric polyarthritis worse in morning and with activity.
clues.rewrite: Unable to walk comfortably due to knee and ankle pain. Also experiencing dysuria and penile discharge. Complains of eye redness and discomfort bilaterally.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Specific trigger: 3-day bloody diarrhea (self-limited), timeframe of 2 weeks prior. Recent unprotected sexual contact 4 weeks ago. No prior joint disease, negative family history for autoimmune disease. Previously healthy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Keratoderma blennorrhagicum (psoriasiform papules on palms/soles), oral ulceration on palate, asymmetric arthritis pattern (contradicts clue 2 claim of symmetry), warm effusions, urethral discharge confirmed, bilateral conjunctivitis with mucoid discharge.
clues.rewrite: On examination: warmth and effusion in knees and ankles; asymmetric polyarthritis noted. Urethral discharge visualized. Bilateral conjunctivitis with mild mucoid discharge. Distinctive psoriasiform papules (keratoderma blennorrhagicum) present on palms and soles. Shallow oral ulcers noted on palate.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Serological confirmation: HLA-B27 positive, stool culture positive for Shigella flexneri, chlamydial serology positive. Lab markers: elevated ESR (68), elevated CRP, negative RF/anti-CCP (rules out RA). Synovial fluid analysis: inflammatory non-septic pattern.

Reactive Arthritis_567.notes: CRITICAL ISSUES: Clue 1 is diagnosis-giveaway territory — a physio student reading only the complaint immediately thinks 'reactive arthritis' (post-GI infection + urethritis + conjunctivitis = classic triad). The complaint should NOT include urethritis, conjunctivitis, or post-diarrhea framing; these belong in Activity/History. Clue 2 uses language ('acute polyarthritis', 'urethritis', 'conjunctivitis') that is EXAMINATION or HISTORY-level specificity, not Activity/Functional. Clue 4 contradicts Clue 2 by reporting ASYMMETRIC arthritis when Clue 2 stated SYMMETRIC — this is incoherent. The progressive reveal is severely compromised because diagnostic clues are frontloaded (Clue 1) rather than unfolding across the sequence. After Clue 1 alone, the answer is nearly certain. Reordering and redistribution of information is essential.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, comorbidity (diabetes), symptom pattern (triggering/locking), bilateral involvement, temporal onset (morning worse, sudden).
clues.rewrite: A 62-year-old man with Type 2 diabetes reports sudden-onset triggering sensation in fingers bilaterally, with difficulty extending the digits in the morning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitation to gripping and fine motor tasks. Pain with forced extension. Temporal pattern: daytime > nocturnal.
clues.rewrite: Functional impact worsens with repetitive gripping and activities requiring fine motor control. Significant functional limitation by evening, though symptoms partially resolve overnight.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Diabetes control status (HbA1c 8.5%). Absence of prior hand pathology. Timeline: 1 month, rapid bilateral onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective palpable findings (nodular thickening at A1 pulley level), reproducible triggering phenomenon, pain with triggering, secondary grip weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound confirmation of flexor tendon nodular thickening, pulley hypertrophy, inflammatory changes, absence of tendon tearing. Bilateral involvement confirmed.

Trigger Finger_568.notes: Clue 1 is too specific and names the cardinal finding ('triggering') explicitly, which essentially gives away the answer to anyone familiar with trigger finger. Clue 2 largely rephrases symptom information already given in Clue 1 (locking, extension difficulty, pain) without adding meaningful functional distinction — the nocturnal pattern is the only novel element. Clues 3–5 are well-structured and progressively narrow the differential appropriately. Rewrite Clue 1 to be symptom-only without the word 'triggering' and remove 'bilateral' (reserve for later clues). Rewrite Clue 2 to emphasize the functional impact (activity intolerance during the day, recovery overnight) rather than restating symptom mechanics.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Mechanism (rugby tackle, finger caught in jersey), acute onset with sharp pain, immediate loss of finger flexion, swelling in proximal forearm
clues.rewrite: A 26-year-old rugby player sustained an acute injury to the ring finger during a tackle. Sharp pain and sudden inability to flex the ring finger. Swelling developed rapidly in the forearm within minutes of injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — loss of DIP flexion already stated. Clue 2 adds only the anatomical name (flexor digitorum profundus avulsion) which names the answer. PIP joint function adds marginal new info but does not meaningfully narrow differential from Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: Ring finger held in fixed extension. Unable to make a fist with the ring finger. PIP joint can bend slightly but with significant pain and weakness. Pain worse with any attempt to grip or squeeze.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Timeline (2 hours ago), sport context (high-contact), patient demographics (young, athletic, full mobility demands), prior trauma history (none)

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific strength grades (DIP 0/5, PIP 4/5), localized tenderness in palm and volar forearm near wrist, fullness along FDP tendon region, intact sensation and vascular status, no skin disruption

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Confirms FDP tendon rupture at wrist level with 3cm retraction gap, edema in forearm, hematoma/fluid collection tracking proximally. Excludes bony mallet (no avulsion fracture).

Jersey Finger_569.notes: The puzzle has a critical failure: Clue 1 essentially names the answer ('jersey finger' is the colloquial term for FDP avulsion from traction injury; 'ring finger caught in jersey' + 'loss of finger flexion' + 'swelling over proximal forearm' is almost pathognomonic). Clue 2 then explicitly states 'flexor digitorum profundus avulsion' — naming the diagnosis outright. After reading Clue 1 alone, an experienced clinician would confidently guess the answer before seeing Clues 2-5. The progressive reveal principle is completely violated. Clues 3-5 are well-sequenced and appropriate in content, but Clues 1-2 must be rewritten to remove the obvious mechanism and the anatomical diagnosis name. Clue 1 should describe a functional complaint (sudden inability to flex) without mentioning how it happened or which forearm region is swelling. Clue 2 should describe observable functional loss without naming the tendon or using the word 'avulsion'.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, symptom triad (pain/numbness/weakness), upper extremity radiation, acute onset from position change, functional impact on work
clues.rewrite: A 48-year-old reports acute onset of neck pain with numbness and weakness in one arm following a position change during lifting. Symptoms began 2 weeks ago and are affecting work capacity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Dermatome pattern of radiation; neck flexion and rotation aggravate; traction and positioning relieve. This narrows to nerve root irritation/compression.
clues.rewrite: Pain radiates distally in a linear pattern down the arm. Neck movements in certain directions provoke symptoms; brief rest and gentle traction provide temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism (position change/lifting); underlying degenerative changes as predisposition; progressive course suggesting ongoing compression
clues.rewrite: Patient has chronic neck stiffness from prior years of desk work. Current acute flare coincided with moving furniture. No prior imaging of the neck. Similar but milder episodes resolved within weeks previously.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: ROM limitations (flexion/rotation); dermatome-specific sensory loss; motor weakness corresponding to nerve root; diminished reflex. These are objective findings that discriminate nerve root involvement from other neck conditions.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI confirms disc herniation or spondylotic osteophyte at a specific level compressing the nerve root, matching the clinical dermatome/myotome pattern. This is confirmatory and definitively excludes other diagnoses.

Cervical Radiculopathy_570.notes: Major problem: Clues 1, 2, and 3 all explicitly name or strongly imply 'nerve root compression' and 'dermatome pattern' — the core diagnostic feature. By Clue 2, any physio student knows this is radiculopathy. Clues 1 and 2 are too specific too early and eliminate the differential narrowing process. Clue 1 should describe only symptoms (pain, numbness, weakness) without specifying radiation pattern or mechanism. Clue 2 should add the functional/activity detail that reveals the radicular distribution pattern. Clue 3 should provide history that distinguishes cervical radiculopathy from other causes of arm pain (trauma, spondylosis history, progressive course). The puzzle collapses the differential far too early.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient is 54F, 6 weeks post-ACDF at C5-6 for cervical myelopathy. Reports stiffness, residual arm numbness, good pain control.
clues.rewrite: 54-year-old woman presents 6 weeks after elective neck surgery. Reports persistent neck stiffness and mild arm numbness that is gradually improving. Pain is well-controlled.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: reduced neck mobility, dysphonia, paresthesias improving, increasing functional capacity.
clues.rewrite: Hoarseness of voice present since procedure. Neck movement remains restricted but progressing. Patient reports gradual recovery of arm sensation and overall functional improvements with therapy.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Redundant: Clue 1 already stated ACDF at C5-6 for myelopathy. This clue only rephrases the pre-operative diagnosis and surgical approach already disclosed.
clues.rewrite: Pre-operative presentation included progressive hand weakness and gait disturbance concerning for myelopathy. Anterior surgical approach selected for direct decompression and fusion. Patient is engaged with rehabilitation and has realistic recovery expectations.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific objective findings: ROM values, incision healing, strength gains (3+/5 → 4+/5), dysphonia status, gait improvements.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging confirmation: fusion incorporation, correct hardware position, cord decompression, resolution of signal change, no recurrence.

Cervical Spine Surgery_571.notes: FUNDAMENTAL FAILURE: Clue 1 completely names the answer — it states the exact diagnosis ('anterior cervical discectomy and fusion at C5-6 for cervical myelopathy'). This destroys the game. Clue 2 adds functional color but is secondary to what was already disclosed. Clue 3 is almost entirely redundant with Clue 1, merely restating pre-operative findings and surgical rationale without new discriminatory information. Even if Clue 1 were rewritten to be vague (e.g., 'patient reports stiffness and arm numbness 6 weeks after elective neck surgery'), Clues 2 and 3 still fail the progressive reveal principle — they don't narrow a differential, they elaborate on a single known case. The puzzle conflates 'case detail progression' (which is narrative elaboration) with 'diagnostic narrowing' (which is what Physiodle requires). For this to work: Clue 1 must describe only symptoms (stiffness, numbness, hoarseness) without mentioning surgery. Clue 2 must reveal the surgical context. Clue 3 must differentiate between post-op complications (e.g., nerve injury, infection, implant failure vs. normal recovery). Clues 4–5 should confirm the specific post-operative status. As written, this puzzle is unplayable — there is no diagnostic reasoning required.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, gender, symptom onset timeline (4 weeks), location (lateral hip), aggravating factors (walking, side-lying), and functional impact (sleep disruption, 1-block walk tolerance)

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional movement provocations: stairs, uneven surfaces, leg crossing, hip internal rotation. Response to conservative measures (rest, ice). Distinguishes weight-bearing movements from positional pain.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Gradual onset without trauma (NOT acute injury), recent activity increase 6 weeks ago, occupational risk factor (sedentary sitting), prior spinal history, and objective finding of bilateral abduction weakness (though affected side worse).
clues.rewrite: Gradual onset 6 weeks ago coinciding with new exercise program focused on walking/hill work. No traumatic event. Sedentary desk job. Prior episodes of lower back pain (resolved). Mild bilateral hip abductor weakness noted, worse on affected side. No previous hip injections or formal physio.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific palpation finding (greater trochanter tenderness), functional tests (Trendelenburg, Noble compression), measurable ROM/strength deficit (3+/5 abduction), balance impairment (single-leg stance). These discriminate gluteal dysfunction from other lateral hip pain sources.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Structural confirmation: gluteus medius tendinopathy with signal/structural changes, bursal thickening, vascularity on Doppler. Rules out OA, labral pathology, and other structural mimics. Confirms soft tissue/tendinous origin.

Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome_572.notes: This is a well-structured puzzle with good progressive narrowing. Clue 1 opens with classic GTPS presentation but broad enough for hip OA, bursitis, or labral conditions. Clue 2 adds movement-specific provocations (stairs, crossing legs) that narrow toward abductor/external rotator pathology. Clue 3's only redundancy is repeating the weakness finding already implied in Clue 4; suggest reframing to add timeline coherence and occupational context instead. Clue 4 is excellent — special tests (Trendelenburg, Noble) and palpation directly differentiate GTPS from other causes. Clue 5 is appropriately definitive. Minor rewrite needed for Clue 3 to avoid restating weakness already covered in examination.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, progressive bilateral hearing difficulty, high-frequency loss pattern, social context (wife's observation). Sets broad differential for hearing loss.
clues.rewrite: A 68-year-old man reports progressive difficulty hearing over the past 5 years. Wife notes he frequently asks for repetition and keeps television volume loud. Denies ear pain, drainage, or balance problems.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1. 'Difficulty hearing conversations in noisy environments' and 'speech discrimination affected' are already implied by Clue 1's complaint. Tinnitus presence adds minimal new specificity. Social withdrawal elaborates impact but doesn't narrow differential.
clues.rewrite: Struggles most in noisy settings (restaurants, social gatherings). Tinnitus present as high-pitched ringing in both ears. No vertigo or balance loss. Declining participation in group activities.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Occupational noise exposure (45 years as machinist without protection) is NEW and critical — directly explains mechanism. Family history of hearing loss adds genetic risk. Smoking history, absence of ototoxic drugs, and absence of trauma/surgery are clinically relevant new context.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Contains major inconsistencies and redundancy. Otoscopy normal is reasonable. BUT Weber/Rinne findings conflict with later imaging interpretation: clue 4 states 'air-bone gap' and 'conductive component' suggesting mixed/conductive loss; Clue 5 then says 'sensorineural with mixed component' and normal tympanometry contradicts the conductive suggestion. Speech discrimination 68% is clinically relevant but partially foreshadowed by Clue 2. Cerumen impaction removal is clinical action, not diagnostic finding.
clues.rewrite: Otoscopy: clear bilateral tympanic membranes, no cerumen impaction. Weber test: no lateralization. Rinne test: air conduction > bone conduction bilaterally (normal pattern). Speech discrimination score 68% at 65 dB. No otologic signs of trauma, infection, or mass.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Audiometry confirms bilateral high-frequency sensorineural pattern. Elevated bone conduction thresholds at >2000 Hz. Tympanometry normal type A confirms normal middle ear. These findings should definitively discriminate sensorineural from conductive or mixed causes. However, Clue 4's air-bone gap statement contradicts Clue 5's pure sensorineural interpretation — major incoherence.
clues.rewrite: Audiometry: bilateral symmetrical sensorineural hearing loss, worse at frequencies >2000 Hz. Air-bone gaps absent bilaterally. Bone conduction thresholds elevated 50–60 dB at high frequencies; air conduction similarly affected. Tympanometry type A bilaterally; acoustic reflexes present. Speech discrimination 68%.

Hearing Loss_573.notes: CRITICAL ISSUES: (1) Clue 1 essentially names the answer ('hearing loss' + 'hearing difficulty' + high-frequency pattern + age 68 = age-related sensorineural hearing loss). A physio student guesses this on attempt 1. (2) Clue 2 adds almost no new diagnostic information beyond Clue 1 — it elaborates functional impact but doesn't narrow differential further. (3) MAJOR INCOHERENCE: Clue 4 suggests air-bone gaps and mixed/conductive loss; Clue 5 then contradicts this with pure sensorineural findings and normal tympanometry. This breaks the clinical story and confuses players. (4) Clue 4 includes cerumen removal (a procedure/action) rather than pure examination findings. The puzzle needs significant restructuring: make Clue 1 much broader (remove 'hearing' terminology if possible, keep only age/progressive symptoms), ensure Clue 4 and 5 tell a coherent story, and ensure audiometric findings actually discriminate sensorineural from conductive (or mixed).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Initial presentation: blurred vision right eye, progressive leg weakness over 4 weeks, recent viral prodrome 2 months ago, fatigue and cognitive fog
clues.rewrite: 34-year-old woman presenting with sudden vision loss in right eye with pain on eye movement, and progressive lower limb weakness over 4 weeks. Reports fatigue and mild cognitive difficulties affecting work. Recent viral illness-like symptoms 2 months prior.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: reduced visual acuity, weakness affecting gait and stair climbing, balance/coordination problems, bladder urgency, sensory loss in feet
clues.rewrite: Unable to climb stairs independently due to leg weakness and imbalance. Frequent falls secondary to coordination loss. Bladder symptoms (urgency, frequency) interfering with social activities and sleep. Stocking-glove sensory loss in feet.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Relapsing pattern with previous transient neurological episodes, waxing and waning symptoms suggesting multiple separate events, recent URI, family history of autoimmune disease
clues.rewrite: Patient reports 3-4 episodes of unexplained neurological symptoms over past 2 years, each lasting weeks to months then partially resolving. Current episode began 4 weeks ago following recent upper respiratory infection. No significant past medical history. Strong family history of rheumatoid arthritis and lupus.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific objective findings: afferent pupillary defect right eye, central scotoma right eye, 4/5 strength bilateral LE with hypertonia and hyperreflexia, positive bilateral Babinski signs, spastic gait with ataxia, dysmetria

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI brain demonstrates multiple periventricular demyelinating lesions with gadolinium enhancement in acute lesions; spinal cord T2 hyperintense lesion at C4-5 with enhancement; CSF oligoclonal bands positive; visual evoked potentials show prolonged latency confirming demyelination

Multiple Sclerosis_574.notes: Critical failures in progressive reveal structure. Clue 1 is far too specific and essentially names the diagnosis through optic neuritis + progressive leg weakness + viral prodrome + relapsing pattern — this combination is classic MS teaching material and leaves almost no differential work for the player. Clues 2 and 3 then largely restate or elaborate details already implied by Clue 1 without meaningfully narrowing the field further (stocking-glove distribution, relapsing pattern, autoimmune family history are all mentioned or strongly implied in Clue 1). The puzzle collapses after Clue 1; a clinician with MS on the differential after reading only the complaint will find little surprise in subsequent clues. Clues 4 and 5 are appropriate for their positions and provide genuine diagnostic specificity, but by then the case is already solved. Recommend rewriting Clues 1-3 to be genuinely broader and less name-giving, with Clue 2 focusing purely on functional limitations and Clue 3 on temporal pattern/prior history without overloading it with autoimmune flags.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, occupation, symptom profile (hand clumsiness, gait unsteadiness), timeline (6 months, progressive), and functional impact (buttoning, stumbling). Establishes broad differential including upper limb nerve/cord pathology, cerebellar, Parkinsonian, or general neurological conditions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional specificity: dexterity > strength dissociation, wide-based gait pattern, reduced arm swing, stair/uneven surface difficulty, symptom progression with activity. This narrows to spinal cord or central nervous system pathology affecting balance/coordination, eliminating peripheral nerve and pure motor conditions.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Prior cervical stiffness (suggests pre-existing spondylosis), remote trauma (5 years prior), gradual progression over months (chronic rather than acute). This adds mechanism context and timeline detail that begins to point specifically toward cervical cord compression rather than other CNS pathologies.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Lhermitte sign (highly specific for cord pathology), bilateral hyperreflexia and pathologic Hoffman's reflex, lower extremity hyperreflexia with spasticity, bilateral gait stiffness, mild-moderate intrinsic hand weakness with loss of fine coordination. These objective findings are the key discriminators: Lhermitte + hyperreflexia + spasticity + preserved strength essentially lock in cervical myelopathy over other differential considerations (Parkinson's, cerebellar ataxia, peripheral neuropathy).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Specific anatomical confirmation: multilevel spondylosis with cord compression at C5-6 and C6-7, myelopathic T2 signal change (hyperintensity), severely reduced sagittal diameter (10 mm). This provides structural imaging proof of cord compression and rules out mimics. The T2 changes confirm myelopathic cord involvement.

Cervical Myelopathy_575.notes: Excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 casts a wide neurological net (hand clumsiness + gait unsteadiness could be Parkinson's, cerebellar ataxia, peripheral neuropathy, myelopathy, etc.). Clue 2 narrows meaningfully to CNS spinal cord/balance pathology via the dissociation pattern and gait quality. Clue 3 adds cervical spine history and mechanism that points toward cervical pathology specifically. Clue 4 provides the critical examination cluster (Lhermitte + hyperreflexia + spasticity + bilateral findings) that discriminates myelopathy from Parkinsonian, cerebellar, or peripheral mimics. Clue 5 confirms with anatomical specificity and imaging biomarkers. The funnel is well-constructed; each clue adds genuinely new information that narrows the differential progressively. No redundancy, no early giveaways, no vague later clues.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, sex, symptom location (anterior knee), triggering activity (running), and pain behavior (volume-dependent). Establishes broad differential for anterior knee pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Extends to stairs and squatting; confirms activity avoidance pattern. However, 'pain with stairs and squatting' was already strongly implied by 'anterior knee pain limiting running' — these are highly overlapping presentations in the differential.
clues.rewrite: Cannot perform single-leg squat or step down without anterior knee pain. Pain worse with prolonged sitting (movie sign).

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Training error (volume increase without recovery) and recurrent pattern (prior episode 2 years ago, resolved with modification). This meaningfully narrows to overuse/load-management conditions and supports PFPS specifically.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: VMO weakness, knee valgus gait, and patellar mobilization test findings. However, these are relatively generic for any knee pain condition and don't strongly discriminate from other anterior knee pain diagnoses (e.g., patellar tendinopathy, OA). 'Pain reproducible with functional activities' is circular — already stated in Clue 1.
clues.rewrite: VMO weakness confirmed on manual test. Knee valgus noted during single-leg squat. Pain with patellar compression during weightbearing activities. Q-angle elevated. No focal tendon tenderness; patellofemoral joint line tender to palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging is normal (X-ray, MRI); diagnosis is clinical. This is appropriate and correctly emphasizes that PFPS is a clinical diagnosis. However, the mention of 'VMO dyskinesis on dynamic analysis' is a rephrase of Clue 3 (VMO weakness) and Clue 4 (valgus pattern) — adds no new imaging finding.
clues.rewrite: X-rays normal; no OA. MRI normal; no cartilage damage, no tendinopathy, no ligamentous pathology. Diagnosis confirmed clinically — structural imaging excludes alternative causes.

Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome_576.notes: Progressive reveal is weakened by Clue 2 failing to narrow beyond Clue 1 (stairs/squatting pain is already implied by anterior knee pain in runners), and Clue 4 using generic objective findings that could fit multiple knee pain diagnoses. Clue 5's mention of VMO dyskinesis is redundant with earlier clues. The puzzle is moderately easy because a confident differential (patellar tendinopathy vs. PFPS) isn't clearly discriminated until too late. Rewrite Clue 2 to add specific functional limitations (single-leg control, sitting knee pain) and Clue 4 to emphasize patellofemoral localization and absence of tendon/soft-tissue pathology signs.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Middle-aged woman, intermittent hip pain triggered by return to dancing, long-standing hip tightness predating current episode

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific movement pattern (adduction + internal rotation, flexion patterns) consistently provokes pain; functional limitation is reproducible with combined movements
clues.rewrite: Patient reports pain worsens with hip flexion combined with internal rotation and adduction. Struggles with lunging, pivoting movements, and crossing legs. Pain is predictable and reproducible with these combined movement planes.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Anatomical predisposition (proximal femoral or acetabular morphology); mechanism is activity-triggered after period of inactivity; lifelong mobility restriction as underlying factor
clues.rewrite: Patient has had long-standing hip stiffness since adolescence but was previously asymptomatic. Returned to dancing 3 months ago after 2-year break. No previous imaging. No hip trauma or surgery history. Symptoms gradually worsened over first 4 weeks of renewed activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Hip flexion-adduction-internal rotation (FAIIR) reproduces pain; combined plane ROM restriction; hip flexor tightness; positive 'impingement tests'
clues.rewrite: Hip flexion ROM 110°, internal rotation 15° (restricted bilaterally, more so on symptomatic side). Pain reproduced at end-range when combining flexion with adduction and internal rotation. Hip flexor tightness noted in prone lying. Pain anterolateral hip region, not groin. No catch or giving way. Neurology intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI/CT shows specific morphological abnormalities (proximal femur or acetabulum); labral pathology may be present
clues.rewrite: MRI demonstrates cam-type morphology at femoral head-neck junction with associated labral tear. No hip dysplasia. Mild synovitis present.

Femoroacetabular Impingement_577.notes: Major progressive reveal problem: Clue 1 already contains 'lifelong hip tightness' and 'functional limitations with lower extremity mobility' — Clue 2 then just rephrases the same movement restrictions without adding NEW functional or mechanism info. Clue 3 explicitly names 'proximal femoral or acetabular morphology' and 'impingement mechanism' — this is effectively naming the answer before Clue 4. Clue 4 uses terminology like 'impingement sign' and 'impingement tests' which are synonymous with FAI diagnosis and should not appear before imaging. The differential should still be open after Clue 3 (e.g., labral tear, hip capsular restriction, adductor pathology all possible). Clue 4 should discriminate between these using exam findings, and Clue 5 should confirm via imaging. Current sequence collapses the diagnostic certainty too early.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, sport, duration, location (superior patellar pole), mechanism (jumping/hitting), and physical sign (swelling). Establishes anterior knee pain in a jumping athlete.
clues.rewrite: A 38-year-old recreational volleyball player reports anterior knee pain for 6 weeks that worsens with jumping and explosive movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific movement patterns (explosive/eccentric loading), pain characteristics (sharp), recovery pattern (improves with rest, returns with sport), and temporal stiffness. Confirms functional limitation to dynamic activities.
clues.rewrite: Cannot perform repetitive jumping or spiking without pain. Eccentric loading during deceleration significantly aggravates symptoms. Pain settles quickly with rest but reliably returns when returning to sport participation.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Gradual onset, training volume increase, prior patellar tendon history, and recent decline in flexibility. Establishes overuse mechanism and chronicity.
clues.rewrite: 6-week history correlating with increased training frequency (4x weekly volleyball). No acute injury event. Prior similar patellar tendon discomfort 2 years ago (self-resolved). Recent decrease in hamstring and quadriceps flexibility noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Palpation localization (proximal patellar tendon at superior pole), isometric resistance pain, single-leg squat findings (valgus/femoral IR), ROM limitation (pain at 120-140°), and swelling. Differentiates quadriceps tendon from patellar tendon pathology and identifies biomechanical dysfunction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Structural confirmation: tendon thickening (8mm vs normal 5-6mm), loss of fibrillar architecture, increased vascularity (Doppler), and intrasubstance degeneration without tear. Definitively confirms tendinopathy diagnosis and rules out full-thickness rupture.

Quadriceps Tendinopathy_578.notes: Major issue: Clue 1 is far too specific and essentially gives away the diagnosis. It names the exact location (superior patellar pole), the sport, the mechanism, and even describes swelling at the tendon insertion — all strong indicators of quadriceps tendinopathy. A experienced clinician would have very few differential diagnoses after Clue 1 alone. Clue 2 largely repeats information already established in Clue 1 (jumping aggravates, rest helps, explosive movements problematic) without adding meaningful new dimension — it elaborates mechanically but doesn't narrow the differential further. Clue 3 compounds this by again discussing jumping, training volume, and prior tendon history all already implied in Clues 1-2. By Clue 3, a clinician should have reached high confidence; instead, the puzzle is still layering foundational information. Clues 4 and 5 are appropriately specific and do differentiate quadriceps from patellar tendon pathology. Rewrite Clue 1 to remove anatomical location specificity and swelling description. Rewrite Clue 2 to focus on functional impact beyond what Clue 1 already establishes (e.g., recovery timeline, activity tolerance ceiling, daily function impact). This will create a genuine funnel effect.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographics (26F overhead athlete), symptom onset (gradual 4 weeks), primary complaint (scapular winging and shoulder dysfunction), functional impact on sport (throwing mechanics affected).
clues.rewrite: 26-year-old female volleyball player with 4-week gradual onset of shoulder weakness and dysfunction during overhead activities. Reports difficulty with throwing mechanics and general shoulder fatigue with training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations specify movement patterns (overhead pressing, pushing, reaching forward) and specific exercise intolerance (bench press, push-ups). Pain location clarified as shoulder during overhead activities.
clues.rewrite: Unable to perform overhead pressing without fatigue. Difficulty maintaining control during push-ups. Weakness worsens with repeated reaching forward or upward. Bench press and throwing activities provoke discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism (repetitive overhead sports), timeline (gradual overuse from increased training volume), associated findings (thoracic kyphosis hinted). However, explicitly names 'long thoracic nerve injury' — this is essentially the answer.
clues.rewrite: Overhead athlete with progressive training load increase over 4 weeks without adequate deload. No acute trauma or nerve injury event. Previous shoulder issues in past season. Trains 5–6 days per week with minimal rest days.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings that confirm scapular dysfunction: visibly prominent medial border, failed scapular control on wall push-up test, weakness in specific myotomes (shoulder abduction/internal rotation 3+/5), postural finding (thoracic kyphosis). These actively differentiate long thoracic nerve palsy from other shoulder pathology.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: EMG confirms active denervation pattern (fibrillations, positive sharp waves in serratus anterior). NCS shows reduced amplitude (50% reduction) with preserved velocity — confirms axonal motor nerve lesion of long thoracic nerve. Imaging rules out structural shoulder or chest pathology.

Serratus Anterior Injury_579.notes: The puzzle has critical issues in Clues 1 and 2. Clue 1 explicitly names 'scapular winging' which is pathognomonic for long thoracic nerve/serratus anterior injury — this gives away the answer before players have made any guesses.
