clues.new_info: MRI confirms bursal fluid collection (12mm) with edema and rules out meniscal/ligamentous/articular pathology and bone involvement. This is the definitive discriminator: fluid in pes anserine bursa + normal joint structures = bursitis diagnosis confirmed.

Pes Anserine Bursitis_693.notes: This is a well-structured puzzle with strong progressive reveal. Each clue adds distinct, non-redundant information. Clue 1 is appropriately broad (could suggest meniscal, ligamentous, or soft tissue pathology). Clue 2 narrows to soft tissue inflammation with activity specificity. Clue 3 adds mechanism (overuse + biomechanics) and eliminates degenerative pathology. Clue 4 pinpoints the anatomical structure via palpation and special tests while excluding confounders (Noble test). Clue 5 provides imaging confirmation. The answer (pes anserine bursitis) is never named until confirmation, and a skilled clinician would likely have high confidence after Clue 4. No significant issues identified.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Post-operative period, surgical site pain/swelling, scar tenderness, sensory changes
clues.rewrite: Patient reports hand paresthesias and grip weakness that began insidiously over months prior to presentation. Symptoms are worst at night and with repetitive gripping activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — both describe post-operative improvement, swelling reduction, and progressive recovery
clues.rewrite: Patient unable to perform fine pinch tasks and reports dropping objects. Grip strength significantly reduced compared to contralateral side. Activities requiring sustained wrist extension or forearm supination exacerbate symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clues 1 and 3 — restates compression, numbness, tingling, weakness, and nerve regeneration already covered
clues.rewrite: Surgical release performed 3 weeks ago via open palmar approach. Pre-operative symptoms refractory to conservative management lasting 8+ months. No history of diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, or pregnancy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Redundant with prior clues — restates healing, swelling reduction, and progressive strength improvement already established
clues.rewrite: Incision well-healed without erythema or drainage. Two-point discrimination reduced in median nerve distribution compared to ulnar nerve. Phalen's test negative; Tinel's sign mildly positive at thenar eminence. Abductor pollicis brevis strength 4/5.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound/MRI, nerve conduction studies — confirmatory imaging of decompression and healing (redundant with the diagnosis already given in Clue 1)
clues.rewrite: Nerve conduction studies show slowed median motor latency (7.2 ms, normal <4.2 ms) and prolonged distal motor latency with reduced amplitude. Sensory conduction severely reduced. Findings confirm focal demyelination across carpal tunnel with axonal loss.

Post-Carpal Tunnel Release_694.notes: CRITICAL FLAW: Clue 1 explicitly names 'post-operative carpal tunnel release' — this defeats the entire game. The puzzle has no mystery; the answer is given away on reveal 1. Additionally, Clues 1–5 are severely redundant; they repeatedly emphasize post-operative recovery, swelling reduction, and progressive improvement without adding discriminatory information. There is no progressive narrowing of a differential — instead, all clues reinforce the same single narrative already established by Clue 1. A rewrite must: (1) Remove 'post-operative' and 'carpal tunnel release' from Clue 1; (2) Present pre-operative carpal tunnel syndrome as the initial complaint, not post-operative status; (3) Reserve post-operative complications (e.g., scar hypersensitivity, prolonged pain, recurrent compression, or conversion to post-operative reflex sympathetic dystrophy) as the actual puzzle answer; OR (4) Redesign entirely to present a post-operative complication (e.g., recurrent compression, pillar pain, complex regional pain syndrome) as the diagnostic challenge rather than straightforward post-operative healing. As written, this puzzle cannot function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient is an amputee with pain in the absent limb; establishes the core complaint and patient context.
clues.rewrite: Patient reports pain in a limb that feels present despite visible absence. Quality varies from cramping to burning to sharp stabbing. Symptoms are distressing and persistent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Triggers and exacerbating factors: emotional stress, weather changes, residual limb activity, prosthetic use, nighttime and inactivity.
clues.rewrite: Pain worsens with emotional stress and weather changes. Prosthetic use and residual limb activity modulate intensity. Symptoms peak at night and during prolonged inactivity.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Temporal relationship to amputation; pre-operative pain as risk factor; natural history of gradual improvement but chronic persistence.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Physical exam shows no objective pathology in the missing limb; distinction between stump pain and phantom pain required; emphasis on subjective trigger identification.
clues.rewrite: Residual limb inspection reveals no acute inflammation or skin changes. Pain cannot be reproduced by palpation of the absent limb. Careful history distinguishes phantom sensations from local stump tenderness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Neuroimaging findings: cortical reorganization, altered sensory cortex representation on fMRI, metabolic changes on PET in pain processing regions.

Phantom Limb Pain_695.notes: Major problems: Clue 1 essentially names the answer by saying 'pain in the absent limb' and 'amputee' — a physio student reading only this clue already knows it's phantom limb pain. Clue 2 adds triggers but these were already somewhat implied by the post-amputation context in Clue 1. Clue 3 is the strongest and most appropriate for its position. Clue 4 is too vague ('no pathology in the missing limb' is obvious and doesn't discriminate) and doesn't distinguish this from other post-amputation presentations. The progressive reveal fails because the answer is revealed in Clue 1 rather than emerging gradually. To fix: Clue 1 should describe only pain characteristics (cramping, burning, stabbing) without mentioning amputation or absent limbs; Clue 2 should focus on functional impact in daily life; Clue 3 then reveals the amputation history; Clue 4 should distinguish phantom pain from residual limb pain via examination findings; Clue 5 should provide imaging confirmation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient reports finger deformity with specific joint positioning (PIP flexion, DIP extension) and functional impact on gripping/fine motor.
clues.rewrite: A patient reports progressive finger deformity affecting hand function. Gripping and fine motor control have deteriorated over recent weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1. Clue 1 already stated 'functional limitation occurs with gripping and fine motor control.' Clue 2 merely rephrases this without adding mechanism, timing, or context.
clues.rewrite: The patient cannot actively straighten the affected finger at the middle joint, even with assistance. Attempts to extend the PIP joint fail despite intact sensation and strength in the intrinsic muscles.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism of injury (trauma/laceration) and temporal relationship (acute injury → progressive deformity if untreated). This adds meaningful clinical history and narrows differential toward traumatic causes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective joint positions and central slip dysfunction are confirmed, but this largely rephrases Clue 1's descriptor ('button-hole appearance' = 'PIP flexion with DIP extension'). The addition of 'swan neck deformity develops if untreated' is prediction, not examination finding.
clues.rewrite: On examination, the PIP joint is held in flexion and resists passive extension. The DIP joint lies in hyperextension. Palpation reveals a palpable gap or defect over the dorsal PIP joint. The Fowler test (passive flexion at MCP with extended DIP) shows loss of DIP extension when PIP is relaxed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging findings are vague and generic ('may reveal underlying fracture', 'demonstrates tendon injury'). No specific imaging pathognomonic for boutonniere is described. This clue fails to be definitive or even particularly discriminating.
clues.rewrite: MRI shows disruption or attenuation of the central slip of the extensor tendon at the level of the PIP joint. Associated soft tissue swelling and possible avulsion fracture at the dorsal base of the middle phalanx confirm the diagnosis.

Boutonniere Deformity_696.notes: This puzzle has severe problems with the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 essentially names the answer by describing the pathognomonic 'button-hole appearance' and 'characteristic' deformity — a skilled clinician would guess boutonniere from Clue 1 alone. Clues 2 and 4 redundantly restate the same joint positioning without adding new discriminatory information. Clue 3 works well, adding mechanism and temporal context. Clue 5 is too vague to be useful. The puzzle needs major restructuring: Clue 1 should be broadened to non-diagnostic language, Clues 2 and 4 should be rewritten with distinct examination/functional findings, and Clue 5 should specify MRI or ultrasound findings that actually confirm tendon pathology. Currently the game is too easy and too repetitive.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Swimmer with shoulder pain during/after training, anterior location, overhead movements limited
clues.rewrite: Overhead athlete reports shoulder pain during training sessions. Onset was gradual over recent weeks with increasing training intensity. Pain is diffuse around the shoulder with overhead movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific activity exacerbation (freestyle, butterfly) and relationship to training load
clues.rewrite: Patient unable to complete full training sets due to pain. Symptoms worsen proportionally with session duration and frequency. Rest days provide temporary relief but symptoms recur with return to activity.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism (repetitive overhead), timeline (rapid volume increase), and impairments (rotator cuff/scapular weakness)
clues.rewrite: Recent addition to competitive squad led to doubled training volume 3 weeks ago. No prior shoulder injuries. Previous physiotherapy for similar complaints in other overhead athletes suggests predisposition. No neurological symptoms or night pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific weakness pattern (external rotation), scapular dysfunction, and positive impingement signs

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging confirmation of rotator cuff tendinopathy, specifically supraspinatus involvement

Swimmer's Shoulder_697.notes: The puzzle has severe structural problems. Clue 1 names 'swimmer' and describes 'swimmer's shoulder' mechanics directly—this is essentially a giveaway. Clues 2 and 3 then become redundant, both elaborating on the same core information (repetitive overhead motion, training volume, weakness) without adding clinically distinct discriminators. The answer should be guessable by Clue 3 at the latest, leaving Clues 4-5 irrelevant. To fix: (1) Remove 'swimmer' from Clue 1; present as generic overhead athlete. (2) Make Clue 2 about functional capacity impact rather than sport-specific stroke names. (3) Make Clue 3 add new temporal/contextual information (e.g., sudden volume increase timing). Clues 4-5 are well-structured but are wasted because the answer is already obvious earlier.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Sudden acute joint pain, swelling, erythema in big toe; severe pain limiting weight bearing
clues.rewrite: Patient reports sudden-onset severe joint pain in the foot, worse with weight bearing. Pain began overnight and is limiting mobility significantly.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — weight bearing exacerbates pain (already stated), movement triggers discomfort (already stated), rest provides relief (already implied)
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot tolerate footwear or pressure on the affected joint. Symptoms are worst in early morning and evening; daytime rest provides temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism (crystal deposition from hyperuricemia) and risk factors (male, alcohol, diet, CKD, diuretics)
clues.rewrite: Middle-aged male with history of similar acute episodes. Recent heavy alcohol use and consumption of red meat. Takes a diuretic for hypertension. Episodes resolve spontaneously over 1-2 weeks. No prior diagnosis established.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — erythema (stated), swelling (stated), warmth (implied by acute inflammation), extreme tenderness (stated), fever may be present (new but non-specific)
clues.rewrite: Marked erythema and swelling over the first MTP joint with warmth to palpation. Exquisite point tenderness at the joint line. Pain reproduced with passive dorsiflexion and plantarflexion. No signs of instability. Strength and sensation intact distally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Serum uric acid (confirming hyperuricemia), joint aspiration (needle-shaped monosodium urate crystals), radiographs (chronic changes)

Gout_698.notes: This puzzle fails the progressive reveal principle critically. Clue 1 essentially names the answer by stating 'sudden acute joint pain, swelling, erythema' with 'big toe most commonly affected (podagra)' — podagra is gout-specific terminology. Clue 2 repeats functional limitations already clear from Clue 1 without narrowing the differential. Clue 3 explicitly describes the pathophysiology (monosodium urate crystal deposition) and gout-specific risk factors, which is far too specific for the history clue and belongs after examination findings. Clue 4 largely rephrases Clue 1's examination findings without adding discrimination. By Clue 1, a clinician already knows the answer is gout — there is no funnel effect, no meaningful narrowing. A rewritten version should: (1) Clue 1 focus on acute foot pain with systemic features, avoiding 'podagra' and red flags for gout; (2) Clue 2 add functional/temporal pattern NOT obvious from pain location; (3) Clue 3 provide epidemiologic and behavioral history that shifts suspicion toward gout over other acute arthritides; (4) Clue 4 provide clinical signs that differentiate gout from acute septic arthritis, pseudogout, etc.; (5) Clue 5 confirm with definitive lab/imaging findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Location (medial ankle/arch), character (mild pain), and activity response (increases with activity, improves with rest and support).
clues.rewrite: A patient reports mild pain on the medial side of the foot during weight-bearing activities. Pain is vague in onset and worsens progressively through the day with standing or walking. Rest provides relief.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — same activity triggers (prolonged walking/standing) and same response pattern (pain with activity, relief with rest and support).
clues.rewrite: Patient reports difficulty maintaining prolonged walking or standing. Single-leg activities and dynamic weight shifts increase symptoms. Running is not tolerated well.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Explicitly names 'tibialis posterior tendon dysfunction Stage 1' and describes the pathological process. This is the diagnosis name itself.
clues.rewrite: Insidious onset over weeks to months. No clear traumatic event. Patient reports gradual worsening with increased activity demand or change in footwear. No prior foot injuries or significant comorbidities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Tenderness behind medial ankle, ability to perform single-leg heel rise with mild discomfort, arch height maintained. These are genuinely new objective findings.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI specificity: tendon inflammation with thickening and signal changes, normal arch alignment. This is specific enough for confirmation.

Posterior Tibial Tendon Dysfunction Grad.notes: Major progressive reveal failure. Clue 1 includes too much specific clinical language ('behind and below the medial ankle,' 'arch support') that points toward the foot/ankle region and posterior tibial anatomy. Clue 2 adds no new functional information beyond Clue 1 — same activities, same response. Clue 3 is catastrophic: it explicitly names 'tibialis posterior tendon dysfunction' and 'Stage 1,' essentially giving away the answer before examination findings. A player reading Clue 3 would immediately know the diagnosis. The puzzle lacks proper clinical funneling: the differential should narrow from broad symptoms (Clue 1) → functional movement pattern (Clue 2) → temporal/mechanism history (Clue 3) → specific exam findings (Clue 4) → imaging confirmation (Clue 5). Currently, the answer is revealed at Clue 3. Rewrites suggested for Clues 1–3 to restore meaningful progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 35-year-old with 6-month persistent moderate pain after work-related low back strain; imaging shows only minor disc bulge; pain has plateaued despite normal tissue healing timelines.
clues.rewrite: A 35-year-old reports persistent moderate low back pain six months after a work-related strain. Pain has not resolved despite reassurance and normal healing timelines for the initial injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional avoidance of bending, lifting, and sitting due to fear of re-injury; family reports escalating avoidance even for light tasks; pain catastrophizing beliefs limiting participation.
clues.rewrite: Patient actively avoids bending, lifting, and prolonged sitting. Family reports progressive restriction of activities, including avoidance of movements previously tolerated. Patient expresses catastrophic thoughts about movement causing harm.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Pre-existing anxiety disorder predating injury; high-stress work environment with job dissatisfaction; prior pain episodes resolved with activity resumption (suggests capacity for recovery).

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Pain response disproportionate to tissue pathology; excessive guarding; inconsistent strength testing; poor tolerance to repeated movements; elevated Tampa Scale of Kinesiophobia score.
clues.rewrite: Physical examination reveals pain responses and guarding that exceed findings on imaging. Strength testing is inconsistent and variable. Patient demonstrates difficulty tolerating repeated movement trials. Mood and anxiety screening indicates elevated distress.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI shows resolved inflammation and normal neural structures; findings do not correlate with the degree of disability reported; supports diagnosis via pattern of disproportionality.
clues.rewrite: MRI demonstrates resolution of inflammatory changes from the initial injury with normal spinal cord and nerve root anatomy. Imaging findings do not explain the extent of functional limitation and pain reported by the patient.

Kinesiophobia_700.notes: CRITICAL ISSUE: This puzzle heavily over-names the answer by explicitly mentioning 'pain-related disability syndromes', 'fear-avoidance', 'pain catastrophizing', and 'Tampa Scale of Kinesiophobia' in Clues 1–4. Clues 2 and 4 essentially state the diagnosis using clinical terminology. The progressive reveal is broken: a clinician reading Clue 1 already has 'kinesiophobia' as the leading diagnosis; Clues 2–4 then confirm it with increasingly specific naming rather than clinical narrowing. For a strong Physiodle puzzle, Clues 1–3 should establish the clinical picture WITHOUT naming fear-avoidance or screening tools. Clue 4 should introduce objective evidence (e.g., disproportionate pain response, inconsistent findings) that distinguishes kinesiophobia from organic pathology. Clue 5 should confirm via imaging normalcy. Rewrite all clues to strip explicit naming of psychological constructs and fear-based language until Clue 4 examination findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age (10yo), activity context (competitive soccer), pain location (posterior heel with plantar radiation), temporal pattern (worsens during/after training)
clues.rewrite: A 10-year-old competitive athlete presents with posterior heel pain that worsens during and after training sessions. Pain is localized to the back of the heel. Onset was gradual over the past 4-5 weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Movement-specific aggravation (running, jumping, COD); response to rest/ice; morning stiffness after rest
clues.rewrite: Patient experiences morning stiffness and pain in the heel upon weight-bearing. Pain resolves quickly with rest but returns with any running or jumping activity. Symptoms are worse after high-volume training days.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Recent growth spurt (3-4 months); increased training volume/intensity (past 6 weeks); no trauma

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific palpation findings (midline calcaneal tenderness, medial-lateral compression pain); ROM limitation (dorsiflexion); soft tissue finding (gastrocnemius tightness); mild swelling

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Calcaneal apophyseal fragmentation and sclerosis; absence of fracture/displacement; confirms apophysitis

Sever's Disease_701.notes: The major problem is Clue 1 explicitly names 'growth plate pathology' and describes the clinical presentation so specifically that a knowledgeable player can guess Sever's disease immediately. This defeats the purpose of progressive reveal. Clue 2 adds minimal new information beyond what Clue 1 already established (activity aggravation and relief patterns are already implied by 'worsens during/after training'). Clues 3-5 have good progressive specificity and proper order. Rewrite Clue 1 to broaden the presentation and remove the pathology hint. Clue 2 should emphasize the temporal pattern (morning stiffness, quick relief) rather than just restating activity triggers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, menopausal status, height loss, thoracic spine pain, recent fragility fracture
clues.rewrite: A 72-year-old woman presents with progressive height loss over several years and chronic mid-back pain. She sustained a wrist fracture from a minor fall 2 weeks ago. Reports increasing stooped posture.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations from wrist injury and thoracic pain; postural change limiting activities; fragility fracture mechanism
clues.rewrite: Unable to bear weight through wrist for 3 weeks post-injury. Thoracic pain prevents lying flat and limits walking distance to <10 minutes. Difficulty rising from low chairs and bed.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Menopause timing, HRT non-use, dietary insufficiency, sedentary behavior, family history, sun exposure

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective measurements (height loss confirmed, kyphosis quantified, grip strength 3/5, point tenderness T7-8, TUG prolonged)
clues.rewrite: Height loss 2 inches confirmed. Thoracic kyphosis ~50 degrees on measurement. Grip strength 3/5 bilateral. Percussion tenderness over T7 spinous process. Timed Up and Go 18 seconds (prolonged). No lower limb neurological deficit.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: DEXA T-scores (osteoporotic range), vertebral fracture confirmed at T7 with degree of compression, bone turnover markers elevated

Osteoporosis_702.notes: Major issue: Clue 1 is far too specific and essentially names the diagnosis by explicitly stating 'estrogen deficiency,' 'progressive height loss,' 'thoracic spine pain,' 'fragility fracture,' and menopausal status all together. A physio student reading only this would immediately think 'osteoporosis' — there is no genuine differential. Clue 1 should present vague symptoms only (height loss, back pain, recent fracture) without the mechanistic explanation. Clue 2 largely repeats what Clue 1 already established (wrist fracture, thoracic pain, kyphosis, fragility mechanism) without adding meaningful functional or mechanistic insight beyond elaboration. The clues begin too specific and fail the progressive reveal principle: after Clue 1, the diagnosis is already obvious, and Clues 2–5 merely confirm details rather than narrow a differential. Rewrite Clue 1 to be genuinely broad (could fit multiple diagnoses: rheumatoid arthritis, multiple myeloma, metastatic cancer, etc.) and rewrite Clue 2 to focus on functional impact rather than reiterating the same clinical findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, osteoporosis history, sudden-onset sharp chest wall pain post-minor trauma, lateralized unilateral presentation
clues.rewrite: A 52-year-old with a history of mild osteoporosis presents with sudden-onset sharp chest wall pain following a minor fall from standing height. Pain is strictly localized to one side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Movement/breathing pattern correlation: pain with deep inspiration, coughing, trunk flexion/rotation; splinting behavior; positional intolerance (lying on affected side)

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Long-term corticosteroid use (polymyalgia rheumatica) as fracture risk factor; significant smoking history (30 pack-years); no prior rib injury history

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Precise point tenderness at posterolateral rib at one level; palpation reproduces pain; intercostal muscle guarding; shallow splinted breathing pattern
clues.rewrite: Precise point tenderness elicited over the posterolateral chest wall at one rib level. Direct palpation reproduces sharp pain. Intercostal muscle guarding is marked.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Linear lucency through cortical bone of one rib with minimal displacement; no flail segment; confirms fracture location and excludes pulmonary complications
clues.rewrite: High-resolution CT chest demonstrates a linear cortical break through one rib with minimal displacement. No associated flail segment or pulmonary compromise identified.

Rib Fracture_703.notes: Clue 1 is overly specific for a 'Complaint' clue — it already mentions osteoporosis, trauma mechanism, and even flags the differential (musculoskeletal vs cardiac/pulmonary). This borders on naming the answer before the game starts. A physio student reading only Clue 1 would immediately narrow to rib fracture or severe musculoskeletal contusion; the differential should be much broader. Clues 3, 4, and 5 are clinically coherent and each adds distinct information, but Clue 1 undermines the progressive reveal principle. Clues 4 and 5 are somewhat redundant — both describe the same fracture (point tenderness → imaging confirmation); Clue 4 could emphasize additional examination findings (e.g., specific ROM loss, crepitus if present, or negative cardiac/pulmonary signs) to better differentiate from other causes of acute chest wall pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Chronic knee pain with morning stiffness improving with activity; worse with prolonged standing/walking, stairs, uneven ground
clues.rewrite: A 62-year-old patient reports gradual-onset knee pain over 2 years. Pain is worse in the morning but eases with light activity. Pain returns with prolonged walking or standing. Patient reports difficulty with stairs.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — repeats prolonged walking/standing, stairs (descending specifically), and symptom relief with rest. Adds minor detail: swelling.
clues.rewrite: Patient reports knee swelling by end of day after activity. Pain is mechanical: worse with weight-bearing but improves with rest overnight. Morning stiffness typically lasts 15–20 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism and risk factors: prior knee injury, meniscal pathology, obesity, repetitive impact. Introduces PATHOLOGY terminology ('cartilage loss', 'degeneration') that hints at diagnosis too early.
clues.rewrite: Patient sustained a meniscal tear in right knee 5 years ago; mild surgery was avoided. BMI is 31. Works in construction with repetitive squatting. Progressive onset; no acute injury precipitated current worsening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific objective findings: effusion, ballottement, crepitus, limited ROM with end-range pain in flexion and extension. These are legitimate examination findings that distinguish degenerative joint disease.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Radiographic features: joint space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis. These are pathognomonic for OA. Final clue is appropriately specific.
clues.rewrite: Weight-bearing AP radiographs show medial compartment joint space narrowing with marginal osteophytes and subchondral sclerosis. Lateral and skyline views show mild patellofemoral changes.

Knee Osteoarthritis_704.notes: Major problem: Clue 2 adds almost no new information beyond Clue 1 — it simply restates the same functional limitations and adds only 'swelling', which is already implied by 'morning stiffness improves with activity'. Clue 3 is more problematic: it names the pathology ('cartilage loss', 'degeneration') far too early, essentially giving away the answer before examination findings are presented. Risk factors belong in History, but the explicit mention of degenerative mechanisms breaks the progressive reveal. The sequence should funnel like: broad symptoms → functional impact → risk factors and timeline → examination-specific findings → imaging confirmation. Currently, Clue 3 jumps to diagnosis-level language. Clue 1 is also slightly too easy (morning stiffness + worse with activity is classic OA), but is acceptable for opening. Rewrite Clues 2 and 3 to respect the reveal order.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, occupation (desk worker), symptom profile (upper back/neck tension + cervical headaches), onset (gradual over 3 years), and acknowledgment that multiple patterns may contribute.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional relationship to computer work, that movement/stretching helps, and that forward shoulder posture is provocative. Also hints at postural compensation (thoracic extension and scapular retraction provide relief).

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Specific occupational ergonomics (9-10 hours daily, poor monitor/keyboard positioning), that prior physio was only temporarily helpful, no trauma history, and long-standing postural habits since adolescence.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective postural findings (forward head posture, rounded shoulders), specific muscle tightness (pectoralis major, upper trapezius), specific muscle weakness (serratus anterior, lower trapezius), and limited cervical extension.
clues.rewrite: Postural assessment reveals forward head posture and rounded shoulders. Palpation confirms significant upper trapezius and pectoralis tightness. Scapular dyskinesis noted on movement screening. Lower trapezius and serratus anterior strength testing shows grade 3-4/5 bilaterally. Cervical extension ROM limited to 35 degrees. No neural signs on upper limb neurodynamic testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Negative cervical imaging rules out structural pathology. Confirms diagnosis is postural/functional rather than structural.
clues.rewrite: Cervical and thoracic spine X-rays negative for disc herniation, spondylosis, or subluxation. Postural analysis using plumb-line assessment shows head projection 3 cm anterior to acromion. Scapulohumeral rhythm is asymmetrical with restricted upward rotation bilaterally. Findings confirm postural dysfunction without underlying structural disease.

Upper Crossed Syndrome_705.notes: MAIN PROBLEM: This puzzle attempts to diagnose a postural syndrome (Upper Crossed Syndrome) that is fundamentally clinical—not structural or radiographic. This creates an inherent friction with the Physiodle format. Clue 4 (Examination) is the CORE of diagnosis here, but it reads exactly like a summary of Clue 1 + Clue 2 combined. The muscle tightness (pec major, upper trap) was already heavily implied by 'forward shoulder posture' and 'thoracic extension relieves symptoms.' The weakness pattern (serratus anterior, lower trapezius) was already telegraphed by 'scapular retraction is limited/weak.' Clue 5 then adds almost nothing—it just confirms what Clue 4 already showed. For a postural diagnosis like this to work in Physiodle, Clue 4 must include MORE SPECIFIC, DISCRIMINATORY findings (e.g., scapulohumeral rhythm abnormalities, precise ROM deficits, presence/absence of neural signs) that distinguish UCS from similar presentations (e.g., cervicogenic headache with local muscle strain, thoracic outlet syndrome, or even simple tension-type neck pain with poor posture). Clue 5 could then add quantified postural measurements (head projection distance, scapular positioning angles) that CONFIRM the pattern. Consider whether UCS is the right answer for a Physiodle game, as the diagnosis hinges on synthesizing multiple clues into a pattern—it's less 'guess the condition' and more 'recognize the syndrome,' which may frustrate players.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, occupation, bilateral neck stiffness, upper trapezius localization, 4-week timeline post-transition
clues.rewrite: A 38-year-old office worker presents with bilateral posterior neck stiffness that emerged 4 weeks ago. Symptoms are worse at end of day and improve with rest on weekends.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Diurnal pattern (progressive through workday, minimal on weekends/vacation); transient relief with stretching and breaks

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Suboptimal ergonomics (laptop screen positioning); no prior trauma/neck history; increased stress; temporal relationship to environmental change

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Palpable bilateral upper trapezius tightness and trigger points; full but uncomfortable ROM; forward head posture; no neurological deficits or radiculopathy
clues.rewrite: Bilateral upper trapezius muscle tightness with palpable trigger points. Cervical ROM is full but restricted by muscle guarding. Forward head posture noted. No neurological signs, nerve tension, or radicular pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Normal cervical spine imaging rules out structural pathology; confirms diagnosis via clinical-temporal correlation
clues.rewrite: Cervical X-rays normal—no degenerative changes, fracture, or alignment abnormality. Imaging ruled out structural disease; diagnosis confirmed by symptom resolution with ergonomic correction over 2 weeks.

Neck Tension - Desk Work_706.notes: The puzzle has a critical flaw: Clue 1 (Complaint) already names or strongly implies the answer by explicitly stating 'upper trapezius region' and 'bilateral neck stiffness' in the context of desk work transition. A player reading only Clue 1 would likely guess 'occupational neck tension' or similar immediately. The clue should remove anatomical specificity and the explicit mention of the work transition trigger. Additionally, Clue 4 mostly rephrases what Clue 1 and 3 already established (upper trapezius, no serious pathology). Clue 5 is also generic—normal imaging could apply to dozens of musculoskeletal conditions. The rewritten Clue 1 removes the 'upper trapezius' anatomical label and keeps the differential broad (could be thoracic outlet, cervicogenic headache, etc.). Clue 5 is rewritten to provide a concrete resolution timeline that actually narrows the diagnosis by showing rapid improvement with ergonomic change, which is specific to occupational postural dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographics, smoking history, and diagnosis name (COPD) with acute exacerbation symptoms.
clues.rewrite: 71-year-old with long-standing cigarette smoking history presents with acute worsening of chronic dyspnea, productive cough, and wheezing. Yellow-tinged sputum noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact — dyspnea at rest and minimal activity tolerance; acute decline from baseline.
clues.rewrite: Unable to walk more than 20 metres without severe breathlessness. Previously could manage light household tasks; now struggling with self-care activities.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Redundant: clue 1 already stated 'long-standing COPD for 8 years' and 'current smoker'. This clue merely rephrases and adds non-specific 'recent URI' and 'possible infection trigger'.
clues.rewrite: URI symptoms began 1 week ago with fever and malaise. Recent hospitalisation 2 months ago for similar exacerbation treated with antibiotics and steroids.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective vital signs and clinical signs: tachypnea, accessory muscle use, diminished breath sounds with wheezes, productive cough, cyanosis, alert mental status.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Chest X-ray hyperinflation with possible infiltrate; sputum culture positive for bacteria; spirometry obstructive pattern; ABG hypoxemia and hypercapnia.

Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease_70.notes: This puzzle has a critical flaw: Clue 1 literally names the diagnosis (COPD) and the clinical scenario (acute exacerbation), making the answer obvious from the start. A Wordle-style game requires players to work through differential diagnosis progressively — they should NOT see 'COPD' in Clue 1. Clue 2 and 3 then provide minimal new information beyond dyspnea and a URI trigger. Clues 4 and 5 are appropriately specific but come too late — the game is already solved. Rewrites should: (1) remove the diagnosis name from Clue 1, (2) make Clue 2 functionally more specific, (3) reframe Clue 3 around infection triggers and prior treatment response to support differential narrowing. The differential before diagnosis reveal should include acute asthma exacerbation, pneumonia, acute heart failure, and other causes of acute dyspnea/cough — COPD exacerbation should emerge only after examination and imaging clues.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient is 6 weeks post-TKR with persistent pain and stiffness; mentions 'post-surgical complications must be ruled out' which is meta-commentary rather than clinical information
clues.rewrite: A 68-year-old presents 6 weeks post-operatively with persistent knee pain and stiffness that has not improved as expected. Pain is present at rest and with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Extension lag during ambulation, stair difficulty (especially descent), walking tolerance >30 min with post-activity swelling
clues.rewrite: Patient is unable to fully extend the knee during walking and struggles with stair descent. Knee swelling increases with activity lasting longer than 30 minutes. Quadriceps control is noticeably weak.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Pre-operative contracture of 15 degrees, CPM use for 10 days post-op, suboptimal exercise compliance

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific ROM measurements (ext -10°, flex 85°), quadriceps strength 4/5, anterior swelling with positive effusion sign, exclusion of infection/DVT

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Component position and alignment normal, no loosening, malalignment, fracture, dislocation, or hardware failure
clues.rewrite: Post-operative radiographs show correct component alignment and positioning. No evidence of hardware failure, loosening, or fracture. Mechanical integrity of the reconstruction is sound.

Post-Total Knee Replacement_708.notes: Major issue: Clue 1 directly names 'total knee replacement' in the complaint, essentially giving away the answer on the first guess. The puzzle should start with a post-operative patient with knee stiffness/pain WITHOUT naming the surgery or diagnosis. Clue 2 largely rephrases Clue 1's functional limitations without adding substantially new information—the extension lag and stair difficulty are already implied by 'pain and stiffness persist.' The progressive reveal funnel should sharpen more aggressively: after Clue 1 (broad post-op knee complaint), Clue 2 should narrow to specific movement deficits, Clue 3 should add historical context that eliminates infection/DVT (contracture history, CPM use, suboptimal compliance), Clue 4 should discriminate between arthrofibrosis vs. other complications (extension lag + flexion ROM + effusion), and Clue 5 should rule out mechanical causes. Currently, Clue 1 removes too much ambiguity too early.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Patient demographics, post-mastectomy status with axillary dissection, timeline (8 weeks), and mention of potential complications (seroma, lymphedema)
clues.rewrite: A 54-year-old female presents with shoulder and upper chest wall symptoms 8 weeks following left-sided surgical intervention. Functional limitations with daily activities are worsening. No acute pain or fever.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations with overhead tasks, internal rotation/adduction deficit, and pain threshold with arm elevation above 90 degrees

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Surgical details (incision healed, no adjuvant therapy yet, extent of lymph node dissection, dominance), which contextualise the timeline and reduce likelihood of infection/radiation changes
clues.rewrite: Left axillary lymph node dissection (levels 1–2) performed 8 weeks ago. Surgical wound has healed without breakdown. No chemotherapy or radiation initiated. Right-hand dominant.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Quantified ROM measurements (internal rotation 30° vs 65° contralateral; forward flexion 110°), palpable scar tissue with mobility restriction, exclusion of infection/seroma/severe lymphedema

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound findings rule out seroma/fluid collection and confirm soft tissue adhesions as the primary structural finding; excludes mechanical complications requiring surgery

Post-Mastectomy Rehabilitation_709.notes: The puzzle has a significant flaw in Clue 1: it explicitly names the surgical procedure (mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection), which is far too specific for an opening clue and essentially narrows the answer to post-mastectomy rehab immediately. This violates the principle of casting a wide net. The complaint clue should mention only symptoms (shoulder restriction, chest tightness, reduced upper limb function) and timeline, without disclosing the surgical context or complication risks. This would allow players to consider other causes of post-surgical shoulder dysfunction (rotator cuff repair, CABG, thoracic surgery, etc.) before narrowing to mastectomy rehab. Clue 3 is also slightly redundant—the healing status and absence of adjuvant therapy are useful but don't add as much discriminatory value as they could. Otherwise, Clues 2, 4, and 5 form a reasonable progressive funnel from functional limitation → ROM/palpation findings → imaging confirmation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, sport, location (anterolateral shoulder), activity type (overhead throwing/spiking), onset timeline (8 weeks, progressive), and explicitly names the differential (rotator cuff vs scapular dysfunction)
clues.rewrite: A 26-year-old overhead athlete presents with progressive anterolateral shoulder pain during explosive overhead activities. Onset was 8 weeks ago and has worsened despite activity modification. Pain is reproducible with sport-specific movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific phase of movement (late cocking and acceleration), pattern of pain (explosivity-dependent, phase-dependent), and temporal response (partial relief with rest, immediate recurrence)

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Training volume increase as trigger (2 months prior), no trauma, prior episodes resolved with rest, history of lower crossed syndrome
clues.rewrite: Training volume increased significantly 2 months prior to onset. No traumatic event. History of postural dysfunction (lower crossed syndrome). Previous shoulder episodes responded to rest but this presentation is more persistent.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Visual scapular winging during arm elevation, weakness of serratus anterior and lower trapezius, posterior and pectoral tightness, positive supraspinatus sign (80-120 degrees)
clues.rewrite: Scapular medial border winging noted during active arm elevation. Serratus anterior and lower trapezius weakness on strength testing. Tightness in posterior shoulder and pectoralis. Supraspinatus sign positive (pain 80–120 degrees abduction).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Mild supraspinatus tendinopathy (no full-thickness tear), normal rotator cuff otherwise, no labral pathology, subacromial impingement from scapular dyskinesis
clues.rewrite: MRI: mild supraspinatus tendinopathy without full-thickness tear. Rotator cuff otherwise intact. No labral pathology or glenohumeral joint abnormality. Subacromial space narrowing consistent with impingement secondary to scapular positioning.

Scapular Dyskinesis_710.notes: Progressive reveal is severely compromised. Clue 1 explicitly names the answer ('scapular dysfunction') in the differential statement, eliminating any mystery. Clue 4 redundantly confirms scapular dyskinesis (visual observation of winging + weak stabilizers) after it was already obvious from Clue 1's prompt. Clue 3 adds little beyond what Clues 1–2 established (we already knew onset was recent and activity-dependent). The differential funnel should narrow *silently* through objective findings in Clues 3–4, not be pre-stated. Rewrite Clue 1 to remove the diagnostic hint, and Clue 4 to focus on findings that actively distinguish scapular dyskinesis from rotator cuff-primary pathology (e.g., emphasis on scapular positioning during movement, strength asymmetry, lack of full-thickness pathology).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 32-year-old, 12 weeks post-partum, vaginal delivery with first-degree perineal tears, pain with intercourse, difficulty with flatus continence
clues.rewrite: A 32-year-old presents 12 weeks post-partum with pain during intercourse and occasional loss of control of intestinal gas. She reports these symptoms began after delivery but have not fully resolved.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: unable to resume sexual activity, stress urinary incontinence with cough/sneeze/exercise, avoids high-impact activities
clues.rewrite: Stress urinary incontinence occurs with coughing, sneezing, and running. The patient has avoided exercise and high-impact activities for 12 weeks due to fear of leakage and pelvic discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Labor 16 hours, instrumental assistance second stage, immediate repair of tears, exclusively breastfeeding, no episiotomy

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Palpable pain over perineal scar, weak voluntary pelvic floor contraction (2/5), severe fatigue with rapid muscle onset, tissue restrictions in perineal body, scar tissue visible but no dehiscence
clues.rewrite: Pelvic floor muscle strength is reduced (2/5 on manual assessment). Voluntary contraction fatigue occurs rapidly within 2–3 repetitions. Palpation reveals tender scar tissue and fascial restrictions in the perineal body. Anal sphincter tone is intact and symmetric.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Intact external anal sphincter with symmetry, no structural defects in perineal body, decreased pelvic floor muscle thickness vs. norms, rules out significant complications
clues.rewrite: Transperineal ultrasound shows intact external anal sphincter and no defects in the perineal body. Pelvic floor muscle thickness is reduced compared to age-matched controls, consistent with muscular atrophy rather than structural trauma.

Postnatal Pelvic Floor Dysfunction_711.notes: MAJOR ISSUES WITH PROGRESSIVE REVEAL: Clue 1 essentially names the diagnosis outright — 'postnatal pelvic floor dysfunction' is already explicit in the complaint statement (age, timeframe, mechanism, symptoms, and even 'pelvic floor assessment is necessary'). This eliminates the need for a differential. Clue 2 adds stress incontinence specificity but only reinforces information already in Clue 1. Clue 3 (History) is good and adds the trauma detail (instrumental delivery, tear repair) which was missing from Clue 1's excessive detail. Clues 4 and 5 both describe findings that do NOT actively distinguish postnatal pelvic floor dysfunction from other postpartum pelvic conditions (e.g., pudendal neuralgia, pelvic girdle pain, or myofascial pelvic pain). The imaging (Clue 5) is particularly weak — saying 'sphincter intact' and 'no defects' could apply to many non-structural pelvic floor conditions and does not confirm postnatal dysfunction specifically. Rewrite Clue 1 to remove the mechanism (perineal tears, postpartum context) and focus only on symptoms. Clue 2 needs to narrow to incontinence type and activity avoidance. Clues 4–5 need stronger discrimination between pelvic floor weakness/scar pain versus other differential diagnoses.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, activity context (tennis), pain character (insidious, anterolateral, activity-related, overhead-triggered), timeline (3 months)
clues.rewrite: A 48-year-old recreational athlete presents with insidious-onset anterolateral shoulder pain over 3 months, worsened by activity and overhead movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations (overhead activities, serving, reaching across body) and response to rest/sleep positioning
clues.rewrite: Pain is worse with overhead reaching and cross-body adduction. Sleeping on the affected side aggravates symptoms. Rest provides gradual relief over days to weeks.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Training volume increase (competitive tennis 3x/week), laterality (dominant side), mechanism (no acute trauma), and prior history (none)

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Positive impingement signs (Hawkins, Neer), supraspinatus weakness pattern, palpation finding (suprascapular notch tenderness), and normal scapular mobility
clues.rewrite: Positive Hawkins and Neer impingement tests. Supraspinatus weakness present but no complete loss of strength. Scapular kinematics normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound confirmation: supraspinatus tendon thickening and heterogeneity with intact fibers (tendinopathy not tear), plus secondary bursal involvement

Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy_712.notes: The main problem is Clue 1 explicitly naming 'rotator cuff pathology' as a primary consideration—this gives away the answer category too early and violates the principle that Clue 1 should cast a wide net. The phrase 'rotator cuff pathology' narrows from ~20 shoulder diagnoses to ~5 immediately. Clues 2 and 3 are coherent and appropriately positioned. Clue 3 (History) could be strengthened slightly by clarifying the training load change more explicitly as a predisposing factor. Clue 4 is specific but slightly over-detailed with the palpation finding (suprascapular notch tenderness)—this is a minor issue since examination-level detail is appropriate here, though 'suprascapular notch' is anatomy-specific language that may feel redundant with 'supraspinatus' weakness already established. Clue 5 is well-calibrated and appropriately definitive. Overall, the puzzle is clinically coherent but Clue 1 needs rewriting to avoid naming the pathology class.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, symptom character (chronic pain and stiffness), location (anteromedial joint space), timeline (5-year progression with recent acceleration), and significant risk factor (30-year spray history).
clues.rewrite: A 62-year-old with chronic ankle pain and stiffness progressing over 5 years, particularly with recent acceleration. Long history of recurrent ankle injuries in the same ankle.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations (uneven ground, pivoting movements, decreased walking tolerance from 45 to 20 minutes) and presence of morning stiffness—indicates inflammatory/degenerative rather than purely structural issue.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism of injury (cumulative damage from recurrent sprains causing joint surface and ligamentous damage), absence of acute trauma, and high activity loading history. Establishes post-traumatic pattern rather than primary OA.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific ROM deficits (dorsiflexion 5°, plantarflexion 35° vs normal), joint line tenderness localization, gait pattern (circumduction, stiffness), and weight-bearing calf raise pain. These findings discriminate ankle OA from ankle instability, chronic ankle sprain, or other ankle pathologies.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging findings (joint space narrowing, marginal osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis, no instability) confirm osteoarthritis diagnosis and rule out structural instability or other pathology.

Ankle Osteoarthritis_713.notes: Clue 1 is problematic. It mentions 'anteromedial joint space' and '30-year history of recurrent ankle sprains'—both are anatomy-specific and give away the post-traumatic OA diagnosis too early. A player reading only Clue 1 would likely guess ankle OA immediately rather than generating a broad differential. Clues 2–5 form a solid narrowing sequence, but Clue 1 should be rewritten to be much vaguer: retain age, symptom character, and timeline, but remove the joint space localization and reduce emphasis on the spray history count. This would make Clue 1 consistent with plausible alternatives like ankle instability, chronic ankle pain syndromes, or early OA, and allow Clues 2–5 to do the differential work.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Deep groin pain with hip flexion/internal rotation; mechanical symptoms (catching/clicking); functional loss in running/pivoting
clues.rewrite: A patient reports deep groin or anterior hip pain during athletic activities. Onset was gradual over several weeks. Pain is intermittent but reproducible with certain movements. Mechanical symptoms may accompany pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — same movement pattern (hip flexion + internal rotation), same sports (running, cutting, pivoting), same positional aggravator (prolonged sitting/standing)
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot tolerate combined movements of hip flexion and adduction (e.g., bringing knee to opposite shoulder). Pain worsens with prolonged sitting. Walking is tolerated but high-impact activities are limited.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Mechanism (trauma vs. microtrauma); risk factor context (FAI, rotation-based sports); athlete demographic

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: FABER test, anterior impingement test, internal rotation in flexion provocation, hip ROM deficits — all specific objective findings
clues.rewrite: On examination, the hip shows restricted internal rotation in flexion (less than 10 degrees). Pain is reproduced with combined flexion, adduction, and internal rotation. No significant capsular end-feel. Palpable psoas and adductor tenderness may be present. Hip abductor strength is intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: MRI arthrography confirms labral tear with contrast extravasation; tear morphology visualised; guides surgical planning

Hip Labral Tear_714.notes: Major progressive reveal failure: Clue 1 is TOO SPECIFIC and already names the movement pattern and mechanical symptoms that define labral pathology, making the answer guessable from complaint alone. Clue 2 is almost identical to Clue 1 — it repeats the same movement pattern, same sports, and same functional loss without adding new clinical information. This violates the narrowing funnel: Clue 2 should establish WHICH BODY REGION or MOVEMENT PATTERN is affected (which Clue 1 already did), not just rephrase it. Clue 4 overlaps significantly with both Clue 1 and 2 by reiterating the flexion+internal rotation provocation — the NEW content (specific ROM measurement, capsular quality, muscle strength) gets buried in generic test names. Rewrite Clue 1 to cast a much wider net (groin pain, athletic, gradual onset — no movement patterns), Clue 2 to narrow to the movement category (flexion-adduction combined movement intolerance, prolonged sitting), and Clue 4 to use precise ROM/strength/end-feel language that distinguishes labral tear from FAI, capsular tightness, or muscle injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Sudden onset severe calf pain during plantarflexion with sharp, tearing sensation; acute functional loss noted.
clues.rewrite: A 44-year-old recreational athlete reports sudden-onset severe pain in the posterior lower leg during a tennis match. A sharp, tearing sensation was felt, followed by immediate difficulty with weight-bearing. No prior calf symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional limitations: unable to plantarflex against resistance, cannot stand on affected leg without pain/instability, cannot climb stairs or run.
clues.rewrite: Patient reports severe swelling has developed over the first 24-48 hours post-injury. Bruising is becoming visible extending from mid-calf toward the ankle. Morning stiffness and pain with any weight-bearing are significant.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Acute mechanism during sport; adequate warm-up; prior similar acute calf injury 10 years ago that resolved; mentions Achilles assessment as important differential.
clues.rewrite: Patient reports an episode of acute calf pain 10 years prior that self-resolved without intervention. Current injury occurred during explosive movement in tennis. No chronic calf problems or previous Achilles issues. No recent corticosteroid use or systemic illness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: significant calf swelling and bruising; severe plantarflexion weakness (1-2/5); Thompson test negative (rules out Achilles rupture); palpable muscle defect possible; substantial ecchymosis.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound confirms hypoechoic disruption in gastrocnemius-soleus complex with edema/hemorrhage; partial tear (grade 2-3) localized to medial gastrocnemius; Achilles tendon intact.

Tennis Leg_715.notes: Clue 1 is far too specific and essentially names the answer ('midsubstance calf tear,' 'tennis leg' context, plantarflexion mechanism all present). This destroys the progressive reveal principle. Clue 2 is largely redundant with Clue 1 — both focus on plantarflexion weakness and functional loss, just from different angles. Clue 3 adds some history but the mention of 'Achilles tendon assessment is important' introduces examination-level thinking prematurely. Clues 4 and 5 are well-structured and do their job, but by that point the answer has already been given. Rewrites aim to: (1) make Clue 1 genuinely broad (posterior lower leg pain, tearing, acute onset — could be calf strain, Achilles rupture, plantaris rupture, DVT, etc.); (2) shift Clue 2 to focus on NEW functional info like swelling/bruising timeline (adds inflammation pattern without immediately naming muscle); (3) improve Clue 3 to focus on mechanism and risk factors (prior episode, sport context, age) rather than anatomical specifics.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Age, occupation, symptom distribution (medial hand), timeline (4 months progressive), nocturnal pattern, functional impact (grip strength). Establishes broad differential for hand paresthesias.
clues.rewrite: A 38-year-old desk worker presents with progressive numbness and tingling in the medial hand (ring and little fingers) over 4 months. Symptoms are worse at night and have begun affecting grip strength.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations tied to elbow flexion posture (typing, sleeping position, phone holding). This narrows differential to nerve compression at elbow rather than wrist or central causes.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Occupational ergonomic risk factor (low armrest, high keyboard). Remote medial elbow fracture history contextualises potential anatomical predisposition but is explicitly ruled out as acute cause.
clues.rewrite: Patient works with poor ergonomic setup: armrest too low, keyboard too high, elbow flexed for prolonged periods. No recent trauma to the elbow.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific ulnar nerve compression signs (Tinel's at cubital tunnel, Froment test positive, intrinsic atrophy, sensory loss in little finger). Confirms ulnar nerve involvement and distinguishes from proximal/distal neuropathy or other elbow pathology.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Electrophysiology (NCS/EMG) confirms conduction slowing across elbow segment with denervation in intrinsic muscles. MRI localises compression to cubital tunnel level. Definitively confirms diagnosis.

Cubital Tunnel Syndrome_716.notes: The puzzle has a solid progressive reveal structure overall, but Clue 1 explicitly states 'Ulnar nerve compression at the elbow must be confirmed,' which names the anatomy and mechanism of the answer diagnosis too early. This collapses the differential immediately and removes diagnostic uncertainty. Clue 3 adds minimal new narrowing—it elaborates on the ergonomic setup without adding clinically distinctive information beyond what Clues 1 and 2 established. The puzzle would be stronger if Clue 1 focused solely on symptoms without the confirmatory statement, allowing Clues 2 and 3 to build the clinical picture toward ulnar nerve entrapment at the elbow.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Mechanism (caught in jersey), acute trauma, loss of finger flexion, location (ring finger), swelling in proximal forearm
clues.rewrite: 26-year-old rugby player. Ring finger caught forcefully during tackle. Sharp pain and immediate inability to bend finger at one joint. Swelling developed quickly in the forearm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Redundant with Clue 1 — already stated 'loss of finger flexion.' Clue 2 merely specifies which joint (DIP) and which muscle (FDP) — this is examination-level specificity, not functional limitation.
clues.rewrite: Patient cannot actively straighten the ring finger at rest; finger held in bent position. Pain worsens when attempting to bend the finger. Foregrip and gripping activities severely limited.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Timing (2 hours ago), sport context, young athletic patient, no prior trauma
clues.rewrite: Injury occurred 2 hours ago during competitive contact sport. No prior hand or finger injuries. Patient is fully active athlete with high functional demands.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific ROM grades (0/5 DIP, 4/5 PIP), location of swelling (volar forearm proximal to wrist), palpation findings (tender palm, fullness along FDP region), neurovascular status (intact)

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging confirms complete FDP rupture with proximal retraction (3cm gap), hematoma tracking in forearm, rules out bony avulsion

Jersey Finger_717.notes: Major problem: Clue 1 essentially names the answer ('Jersey Finger' is defined by FDP avulsion from traction during tackle). The mechanism, acute loss of DIP flexion, and proximal forearm swelling in an athlete during a tackle-induced catch is pathognomonic. A second-year physio student would guess the diagnosis immediately. Clue 2 compounds this by explicitly stating 'flexor digitorum profundus avulsion' — this is diagnostic terminology, not functional description. The progressive reveal collapses; Clues 1 and 2 together leave almost no differential. Clues 3, 4, and 5 are competent but arrive after the diagnosis is already obvious. Rewrite Clue 1 to remove mechanism specifics and anatomy names. Rewrite Clue 2 to describe functional limitation (inability to maintain grip, finger posture) rather than anatomical diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Broad symptom presentation: mixed incontinence types (urgency and stress), high frequency (12+ voids/day), and nocturnal symptoms in older woman

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional impact: social withdrawal, travel limitation, sleep disruption from nocturia
clues.rewrite: Unable to engage in social outings, avoid travel. Sleep severely disrupted by nocturia (wakes 4+ times nightly). Anxiety about public situations due to incontinence episodes.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Risk factors and timeline: multiple vaginal deliveries, prior hysterectomy (15 years), menopause (12 years), progressive worsening, previous failed pelvic floor therapy attempt

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective findings: weak pelvic floor contraction, pelvic heaviness, palpable laxity, mild prolapse, possible guarding
clues.rewrite: Weak voluntary pelvic floor contraction (grade 2/5 strength, poor endurance). Pelvic heaviness on Valsalva. Palpable anterior vaginal wall laxity. Unable to isolate deep core muscles. No significant pain-related guarding noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Imaging confirms mixed etiology: weakened support, detrusor overactivity plus stress component, elevated post-void residual, posterior compartment prolapse
clues.rewrite: Pelvic ultrasound: Grade 1-2 cystocele on straining. Urodynamics: detrusor overactivity (small contractions during filling) superimposed on hypermobility at urethrovesical junction (stress component evident). Post-void residual 80 mL. Defecography: rectocele present with incomplete evacuation.

Pelvic Floor Dysfunction_718.notes: The puzzle has a structural problem in the middle clues. Clue 2 largely reiterates the functional consequences already clearly stated in Clue 1 (incontinence → social limitation, frequency → sleep disruption). It doesn't add genuinely new discriminatory information — a clinician learns little beyond what the complaint already told them. Clue 4 is too generic and includes 'possible' qualifiers that blur the clinical picture; it should contain specific, confident examination findings that differentiate pelvic floor weakness from other causes of urgency/frequency (e.g., overactive bladder without weakness, or pure stress incontinence). Clue 5 improves specificity well but could be slightly more precise in describing the mixed urodynamic pattern. Rewriting Clues 2 and 4 will sharpen the progressive reveal: Clue 2 should focus on severity/pattern of functional limitation (e.g., timing of leakage, severity of nocturia frequency); Clue 4 should provide objective findings that confirm pelvic floor weakness specifically (strength grade, endurance, ability to isolate deep stabilizers) rather than vaguer descriptors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes location (3rd–4th intermetatarsal space), pain character (burning), radiation pattern (into toes), and aggravating factor (tight shoes). Essentially names the answer by specifying exact anatomical location and calling it 'plantar interdigital space'.
clues.rewrite: A 52-year-old woman presents with 6 months of burning pain in the ball of the foot. Pain radiates into the toes and is notably aggravated by tight footwear and prolonged standing. Symptoms are intermittent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Functional limitations: prolonged standing, tight/heeled shoes, push-off loading. Relief with barefoot walking. However, these details are already heavily implied by Clue 1 (tight shoes already mentioned as aggravating factor). The NEW functional impact is minimal.
clues.rewrite: Walking on hard surfaces and ascending stairs exacerbate symptoms. Patient reports a sharp shooting sensation into specific toes during gait, particularly in the push-off phase. Activities requiring forefoot gripping (e.g., standing on tiptoes) are avoided.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Occupational exposure (high heels 5 days/week), prior history of similar condition on opposite foot treated successfully, no trauma. The prior history is useful context, but the occupational detail is already heavily implied by Clue 1 (tight shoes). Does narrow differential somewhat by supporting a biomechanical/pressure-based mechanism, but could be sharper.
clues.rewrite: Patient has a documented history of the same condition on the contralateral foot 8 years ago, resolved with conservative measures. Symptoms are recurrent in the same occupation. No history of foot trauma or inflammatory arthropathy. No sensory disturbance elsewhere in the foot.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Specific special test findings (Mulder's click), palpation tenderness, and critical negative finding (normal sensation). This is where the differential should narrow dramatically. Mulder's test is highly specific for Morton's neuroma. However, the examination clue should differentiate between remaining candidates — after Clues 1–2, a player might still consider intermetatarsal bursitis, metatarsalgia, or stress fracture. This clue should rule those out more effectively.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Ultrasound and MRI both confirm a nodular mass in the exact location with specific signal characteristics. This is confirmatory rather than diagnostic at this stage — imaging findings match the clinical and examination picture established in Clues 1–4. Appropriate as final confirmation, but could be more specific about how imaging rules out differential diagnoses (e.g., rules out plantar fasciitis, metatarsal fracture, or bursal inflammation).

Morton's Neuroma_719.notes: Clue 1 is a critical failure: it names the anatomical diagnosis by specifying '3rd and 4th metatarsal heads' and 'plantar interdigital space,' which immediately points skilled clinicians to Morton's neuroma. This clue should be much broader — forefoot pain with radiation, without giving away the exact interspace location. Clue 2 is heavily redundant with Clue 1 (tight shoes already mentioned; functional impact of standing/footwear already established). The puzzle needs to use its early clues to establish broad differential (forefoot pain could be metatarsalgia, stress fracture, bursitis, nerve compression), then progressively narrow. Clues 3–5 are clinically sound but cannot compensate for the early give-away. Rewrite Clues 1 and 2 to broaden the initial differential.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes acute post-operative cognitive changes with fluctuating confusion, disorientation, and agitation/lethargy in an elderly patient. Sets age, context, and symptom pattern.
clues.rewrite: A 72-year-old admitted 4 days ago following hip fracture repair. Family reports sudden onset of confusion and disorientation that seems to come and go. Patient alternates between agitation and lethargy. Baseline cognition was borderline—family notes he was becoming more forgetful before admission.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Describes functional impact—inability to cooperate with rehab, reversed sleep-wake cycle, safety concerns. Adds the temporal pattern (daytime sleepiness, nocturnal agitation).
clues.rewrite: During the day, patient is drowsy and difficult to engage in therapy. At night, he becomes agitated and tries to get out of bed unsupervised. Staff report he doesn't seem to know where he is or what time of day it is. Mood and alertness shift unpredictably hour to hour.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Provides mechanism and risk factors: baseline dementia, post-operative medication changes (opioids, sedatives), rule-out of delirium mimics (infection, electrolyte, dehydration). Hospital environment stressor noted.
clues.rewrite: Pre-admission cognition: early dementia with slow decline. Post-operatively: opioids introduced for pain, two sleep aids prescribed. Medical team has investigated and ruled out infection, electrolyte abnormality, and dehydration. Patient has no prior delirium episodes. This is his first time in hospital.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective cognitive testing with CAM criteria positive, formal documentation of inattention and disorientation, no focal neurology. Vital signs stable.
clues.rewrite: Bedside cognition testing: cannot name the date or hospital location. Digit span: struggles to repeat 4 digits forward. Attention test (months backwards): stops after 3 months. No focal weakness, sensation intact, cranial nerves normal. Heart rate 78, BP 138/82, temperature 37.1°C.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Rules out structural mimics (stroke, subdural, intracranial lesion). Confirms delirium is clinical diagnosis; imaging negative except chronic small vessel changes.
clues.rewrite: CT head performed to exclude acute intracranial pathology. No acute stroke, no subdural hematoma, no bleed. Only finding: chronic small vessel ischemic changes consistent with age. Structural causes ruled out. Clinical diagnosis of delirium confirmed.

Delirium - Rehabilitation Phase_720.notes: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 literally names the answer ('Delirium superimposed on dementia must be recognized') and describes its defining features (fluctuating confusion, disorientation, agitation/lethargy) in clinical detail. By Clue 1, an experienced physiotherapist would have already diagnosed delirium. Clues 2, 3, and 4 then largely rephrase the same information—fluctuating cognition, inattention, medication/environment triggers—without meaningfully narrowing a differential that was already collapsed. Clue 5 merely documents that imaging was done to exclude mimics, which was already implicit in Clue 3's statement that infection and metabolic causes were ruled out. The game offers no clinical reasoning challenge: there is no differential to narrow, no moment where the player thinks 'ah, NOW I can rule out X diagnosis.' A successful rewrite must remove the diagnostic term from Clue 1, broaden it to plausible mimics (stroke, infection, medication side effect, pain, sleep deprivation), and then use Clues 2–5 to progressively narrow toward delirium as a specific pattern of acute, fluctuating, inattentive confusion in a vulnerable host post-operatively.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: 42-year-old male, 12 weeks post-traumatic hip dislocation, presenting with progressive loss of motion, stiffness, and functional decline in hip region
clues.rewrite: A 42-year-old man presents with progressive stiffness and loss of motion in the hip, developing over the past 3 months. Pain and swelling in the hip region limiting daily activities like dressing and walking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Specific functional pattern: loss of hip rotation (internal/external), developing flexion contracture, limited abduction/adduction; concrete ADL impact (dressing, toileting)

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Trauma mechanism (posterior hip dislocation + femoral head fracture 12 weeks ago); complex soft tissue injury; delayed rehab; male sex and age 42 as HO risk factors; prolonged immobilization
clues.rewrite: Hip posterior dislocation with femoral head fracture sustained 12 weeks ago from high-energy trauma. Significant soft tissue injury. Prolonged immobilization in acute phase followed by delayed mobilization.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Objective ROM measurements (flexion contracture present, IR/ER <15° each, abduction 10°); firm end-feel on passive motion; palpable stiffness; tenderness
clues.rewrite: Hip flexion contracture (unable to achieve neutral extension). Severe restriction of internal rotation and external rotation (<15° each direction). Firm, non-painful end-feel on passive range. Palpable stiffness in periarticular tissues. No neurological deficit. Antalgic gait.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: X-ray: Brooker Grade III-IV heterotopic ossification with bridging bone; CT: mature bone in soft tissues around joint capsule and bridging across articular space; MRI: ossified bridges with mature marrow signal
clues.rewrite: X-ray hip demonstrates extensive bone formation in soft tissues surrounding the hip joint with bridging across the articulation, graded as severe. CT imaging shows mature ossified bridges in capsular and periarticular tissues. MRI confirms ossification signal consistent with mature bone.

Heterotopic Ossification_721.notes: Critical issues: Clue 1 includes trauma history and risk factors (12 weeks, posterior dislocation, femoral head fracture, age/sex) that should appear in Clue 3 (History), making it too specific for Complaint stage. Clues 3 and 4 substantially overlap — both list mechanism, soft tissue injury, ROM restrictions, and firmness/stiffness findings. Clue 5 explicitly names 'heterotopic ossification' and 'bridging bone formation,' which are diagnostic terminology that essentially gives away the answer. The progressive reveal funnel is broken: by Clue 2, differential is already very narrow (post-traumatic hip stiffness with specific ROM pattern); Clue 3 should differentiate HO from other post-traumatic sequelae (arthrofibrosis, contracture, adhesions) but instead just re-confirms what Clue 1 already implied; Clue 4 provides ROM data already stated in Clue 2. Clue 5 should confirm with imaging — this is done, but the terminology used ('heterotopic ossification,' 'bridging bone') is too explicit.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.new_info: Establishes diabetic patient with progressive distal burning/electrical pain in feet and lower legs
clues.rewrite: A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes for 12 years presents with progressive pain in the feet and lower legs over the past 8 months. Pain is described as burning, electrical, or 'pins and needles' with a distal-to-proximal pattern.

clues.label: Activity
clues.new_info: Establishes functional limitation (standing/walking exacerbates); introduces allodynia and temporal pattern (worse at night)
clues.rewrite: Light touch or clothing contact triggers sharp pain out of proportion to stimulus severity. Symptoms are notably worse at night and wake the patient from sleep. Prolonged standing or walking significantly exacerbates discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.new_info: Adds glycemic control status (suboptimal) and hypertension; confirms insidious 8-month onset; rules out trauma
clues.rewrite: Glycemic control has been inconsistent (HbA1c 7.8–8.5% over 2 years). Patient is also hypertensive. No preceding injury or trauma to the feet. Symptoms began insidiously without clear trigger.

clues.label: Examination
clues.new_info: Provides objective sensory deficits (sharp-dull, vibration, monofilament, temperature) in stocking distribution; diminished reflexes; normal motor strength

clues.label: Imaging
clues.new_info: Confirms demyelination pattern on NCS/EMG; DN4 score; definitively establishes peripheral neuropathy diagnosis
clues.rewrite: Nerve conduction studies show reduced motor and sensory conduction velocities in bilateral lower extremities consistent with demyelination. EMG demonstrates mild denervation in distal foot muscles. DN4 neuropathic pain questionnaire score is elevated, supporting neuropathic origin.

Neuropathic Pain_722.notes: Major structural problem: Clue 1 immediately names 'diabetic neuropathy' and 'neuropathic pain,' eliminating the diagnostic mystery entirely. A physio student reading only Clue 1 already knows the answer. Clues 2–3 largely reinforce what was already established (diabetic patient with neuropathic pain in a stocking distribution).
